# N01: Chapter 5 – What Stays in Stone  

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** Pouring  
**POV:** Tiffani Merrow | **Beat:** BEAT04 (Quiet Recalibration / Offer / Refusal / Choice) | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses | **Relationship:** Professional Curiosity → Partnership Begins  

---

The copper shimmer did not fade.

Tiffani crouched at the base of the wall Jhace Torrins had held the previous Tide and pressed her palm to the stone. Warmth bled through her skin. Not kiln heat. Not surface warming from the upper light. A band of elevated temperature ran along the fracture where his hands had been. Narrow, about the breadth of two knuckles, concentrated at the points where stress had peaked.

She pulled her hand back and logged the observation.

*Tide 1, Pouring phase. Residual heat persists at contact points. Duration: minimum 18 phases post-activation. Pattern suggests localized energy retention in material structure.*

Around her, the kiln corridor had turned into organized motion. Guild workers hauled broken stone into stacks. Healers moved between makeshift shelters. Runners carried slates between districts, their boots leaving new dust on old flagstones. The city was learning to function with fractures.

The fractures were not behaving in ways she trusted.

Tiffani stood and moved to the next marked site—a doorframe Jhace had stabilized during the night shift. The copper-gold traces here were fainter but still present, threading through mortar lines in patterns that followed load paths she knew from structural glass analysis. Where his hands had pressed, stress had shifted and settled into new paths. Where he had not touched, the cracks at the edges kept creeping.

She sketched the pattern in her logbook, comparing it to the previous site. The effective bearing zones were consistent: narrow bands of increased stability surrounded by long stretches of unchanged vulnerability. His contact had locked specific stress points in place without changing the broader load distribution.

A shadow fell across her page.

“Master Merrow.”

She looked up.

Inspector Harlen stood with two guild officials, their vests marked with Merro Glass and Crown Engineering seals. Behind them, a younger clerk held a stack of blank slates.

“Inspector,” Tiffani said. She did not rise. “I am in the middle of documentation.”

“So I see.” Harlen gestured at the chalk marks and her open book. “The RRO committee met during Kindling. Your name came up frequently. Master Corlis praised your precision. We need that precision applied to the formal survey.”

“I am surveying,” Tiffani said.

“Independently,” one of the guild officials said. His tone treated the word as a flaw. “The committee is coordinating routes, assigning sectors, establishing reporting protocols. We cannot have scattered efforts.”

Tiffani closed her logbook halfway, thumb holding her place.

“The stone is already telling us where it failed,” she said. “I am recording what it says. If your committee wants my data, I will share it. If you want me to stop collecting in order to fill out forms, the answer is no.”

The clerk shifted his weight. The guild official’s jaw tightened.

“The Crown wants counts,” Harlen said, voice careful. “Compromised supports, households at risk, grid sections under threat. Your mapping is valuable, but we need it inside a larger frame. Join the committee officially. You will have assistants, access to archived plans, priority routing—”

“And constraints,” Tiffani said. “Approved routes. Predetermined priorities. Reports that delay action while they pass through review.”

“Guidelines,” Harlen said. “We must manage panic as much as stone. If every craftsperson follows their own urgency, we lose coherence.”

Tiffani looked past him to the wall she had just measured. The copper shimmer caught morning light, a thin band of something new written across old stone.

“Inspector Harlen,” she said. “How many buildings has your committee stabilized since forming?”

His expression flickered.

“The committee coordinates,” he said. “Others execute.”

“I thought so.” She turned a page and kept sketching. “When your framework produces results, show them to me. Until then, I will keep recording what the stone says before it stops speaking at all.”

The guild official drew breath to argue. Harlen raised a hand.

“Master Merrow,” he said quietly. “The RRO appreciates independence. We also appreciate order. Consider the offer. If you change your mind, the committee meets each Kindling in the Crown administrative hall.”

He turned. The officials followed. The clerk cast one backward glance at her chalk marks before hurrying after them.

Tiffani waited until their footsteps faded, then marked the wall again, this time noting the angle of copper inclusion relative to the street slope.

---

She was logging the third site when Pella found her.

“Master Tiffani.” Her apprentice picked her way through rubble, a wrapped package in one hand and exasperation on her face. “You said you would be at the workshop during Pouring. Master Corlis has been asking for you. The Crown commission panes need final inspection before they cool past measurement range.”

“The panes are stable,” Tiffani said without looking up. “The temperature gradient was correct when I checked them at Kindling. They will hold tolerance.”

“He wants your signature on the approval slate.”

“Then he can wait two phases.” Tiffani drew another stress line on the page. “Stone that is about to crush people takes priority over glass that is already behaving.”

Pella set the package down—bread and cheese, from the smell—and crouched beside her. Her gaze followed the chalk on the wall, then moved to the marks in the logbook.

“What are you mapping?” Pella asked.

“Evidence,” Tiffani said. “The man who held these walls left traces. Heat signatures. Copper residue. Stress redistribution patterns. If I can quantify where his contact stabilizes and where it does not, I can predict which held sections will fail first.”

Pella’s eyes widened.

“You mean the holder—Jhace Torrins—he is not fixing the stone?” she asked. “He only delays the collapse?”

“Correct.” Tiffani tapped her pen against a marked node. “His contact creates narrow zones of temporary stability. The load paths beyond those bands do not change. The fractures do not seal. He is holding weight in place through direct force that does not match any mechanical principle I can name yet.”

“That sounds dangerous.”

“It is,” Tiffani said. “For him, and for anyone who trusts a wall he has touched without understanding how short-term his intervention is. Which is why I am documenting the pattern before people start depending on it.”

Pella nodded slowly.

“The RRO wants you to stop?” she asked.

“The RRO wants me to join a committee and report through channels.” Tiffani took the bread, tore off a piece, and chewed. “By the time my observations pass through review, three more buildings will have collapsed and people will wonder why no one warned them.”

“So you are refusing.”

“I am choosing urgency over procedure.” Tiffani met her apprentice’s gaze. “There is a difference.”

Pella’s mouth curved.

“Everyone says you are impossible,” she said.

“Everyone lacks precision,” Tiffani said. She swallowed and reached for the cheese. “If you see Master Corlis, tell him I will inspect the panes before Shaping. If he cannot wait that long, tell him to find someone else whose standards he trusts less.”

Pella shook her head but picked up the empty wrappings with care.

“I will tell him you are serving the public safety,” she said. “He can argue with that if he wishes.”

She walked away toward the main kiln corridor.

Tiffani returned to the wall.

The copper band here sat higher than at the first site, closer to the lintel. She pressed her palm against the stone. Warm. The same width. The same residual hum, just below hearing, traveling into her bones.

She wrote: *Held zone narrow. Adjacent cracks remain active. Advise healers to avoid placing pallets within one band width of edge.*

---

She was measuring the fourth site when she heard boots on broken stone and looked up to find Jhace Torrins standing three paces away, watching her work.

He looked worse than he had the previous Tide. Dark lines under his eyes. Dust ground into his coat. His hands, wrapped in fresh bandages, trembled faintly at his sides. His gaze stayed sharp, moving from her chalk marks to the wall to her open logbook.

“You are mapping my routes,” he said.

It was not a question.

“Yes,” Tiffani said. She did not apologize. “You are leaving evidence. I am recording it.”

“Why.”

“Because people are using buildings you held,” she said. “If I know where your stabilization is weakest, I can warn them before the temporary hold fails.”

He stared at her for a long moment, then moved closer and crouched beside the wall. His bandaged fingers hovered above the copper line without touching stone.

“This is where I pressed,” he said quietly. “Here, and here.” He indicated two points along the fracture. “The break was trying to widen. I felt it pushing.”

“And you pushed back,” Tiffani said.

“Yes.”

“For how long?”

“Until they cleared the wounded.” His jaw tightened. “Three phases, maybe four. I do not remember cleanly. It hurt.”

Tiffani wrote. *Duration correlates with residual heat intensity. Longer contact = stronger thermal signature. Cost to user: pain, tremor, memory blur.*

She looked up again.

“How many sites have you held since the Piercing?” she asked.

He hesitated.

“Seven,” he said. “Maybe eight. Some short. Two longer.”

“And the tremor?”

His hands curled into loose fists, hiding the shake.

“Started after the second,” he said. “Worsened after each one. It does not stop now.”

Her pen scratched.

*Progressive cost accumulation. No observed recovery between uses. Tremor baseline established. Predicted trajectory: continued deterioration with repeated activation.*

“You are cataloging my deterioration,” Jhace said. His tone stayed flat, but there was no heat in it.

“I am cataloging the pattern,” Tiffani said. “Your deterioration is part of that pattern. If you are going to keep doing this—and you are—then someone needs to document what it costs. Otherwise you will spend yourself without knowing how near you are to failure.”

“You think I am close,” he said.

“I think you used this ability eight times in one Tide, and your hands have not been still since,” she said. “That suggests you are approaching a threshold. I do not yet know where it lies. I am trying to find out before you cross it.”

Silence settled around them. The corridor carried hammers, calling voices, the roughened thrum of the grid. Dust hung in narrow beams of light from a cracked upper vent.

“What do you need,” Jhace asked at last.

The question surprised her more than any refusal would have.

“Your routes,” she said. “Every site you held. Contact duration. Your sense of the load before and after. Any place where the glow started weaker, or refused you altogether. And—” She paused. “Leave markers when you plan to intervene next. I need to examine the walls before you touch them, not just after.”

“You want to watch me fail,” he said.

“I want to see the mechanism,” Tiffani said. “Success and failure both produce data. If I only examine stable holds, I will miss half of what matters.”

He looked back at the copper band.

“There is a section in the Underface,” he said slowly. “Grid line under the Dregs. Main support is cracked. I marked it before the Piercing. It held then. It will not much longer. When it goes, that line ruptures. The district loses heat.”

“When are you planning to reinforce it?” she asked.

“Next Kindling,” he said. “If the braces hold that long.”

“If they do not?”

“Then I move sooner,” he said. “With or without you.”

Tiffani considered the fracture in front of her, the heat under her palm, the steady shake in his bandaged hands. She pictured a support under the Dregs carrying more load than it should, trying to fail in the dark where no one from the RRO had drawn a neat grid yet.

“Leave word with Bram when you go,” she said. “I will meet you at the ladder shaft before you drop.”

He frowned.

“You want to be in the Underface,” he said. “With a crack just before it fails.”

“Yes,” she said. “With instruments. With glass. With marks I can trust more than rumors.”

“That is not how most people answer,” he said.

“Most people did not spend three Anneals watching an oven whisper before it tore a wall down,” she said. “Marisse asked if it worried me. I said no. I called signal noise. She died when it let go. I will not call this noise.”

His gaze held on her face for several breaths, as if weighing the line between her words and her motives.

“You take a long path to say you care whether I break,” he said.

“Care is a poor planning term,” she said. “Break is measurable.”

For the first time, his mouth twitched in something that almost reached amusement.

“You walk my routes,” he said. “You see the worst points. Then you tell me where not to stand. That is the work you are asking for.”

“Yes,” she said. “And I tell healers where not to put beds. And I tell Harlen where his neat counts do not match what the stone remembers.”

He pushed himself to his feet. The motion carried a small hitch, a moment where his balance checked before it settled.

“I keep the right to say no,” he said. “If your questions ask for more than the wall does.”

“You do,” Tiffani said. “And I keep the right to tell you when your hands are past their safe load, even if you lie about it.”

“How will you know I am lying,” he asked.

She closed her logbook and slipped it under her arm.

“Glass remembers pitch,” she said. “When you start to crack, I will hear it.”

He huffed a breath that might have been a laugh.

“You are impossible,” he said.

“Everyone says so,” she replied. “Most of them are still standing.”

He turned toward the Underface ladder shaft. She watched the tremor in his hands as he walked, then dropped her gaze back to the copper bands on the wall.

The city’s new hum ran under her feet, in the stone, in the glass, and, now, in the way Jhace Torrins’s fingers refused to rest. One pattern. Many failure points.

She drew a line under the day’s notes and wrote:

*Working rule: Held walls remain dangerous. Use his grip as last resort, never first. Begin structured joint survey next Kindling.*

She underlined last resort, then shut the book.

For now, the walls stayed up. They would not do so on their own.

---

[ASSISTANT]
Chapter 6: Lines That Hold 🔒  

Bram counted braces instead of heartbeats.

One brace for the cracked kiln street wall. Two for the healer shop. Three for the storage alcove under Merro. Four for the side support that still leaned more than he liked. Five for the one they had not reached yet because no one had the hands.

He kept the tally in the back of his head as he watched crews move through the Underface junction that had turned into a waystation. Runners came down with slates. Healers came down with demands. RRO inspectors came down with questions and went back up with less certainty than they had arrived.

Heat pressed in from the mains. The grid hum had a new edge now, a higher thread over the familiar low note. Bram felt it through boot soles and bone. The walls sweated more than they had before the Piercing. Fine moisture gathered in seams and pulled dust into pale streaks.

“Bram,” one of the younger inspectors called from the main shaft. “Rupture Office wants to know when your holder will be free for demonstration.”

Bram did not look up from the chalk map he had spread across a crate.

“Holder has a name,” Bram said. “And a route. RRO can queue up behind both.”

“They say his grip is Crown business now,” the inspector said. “City asset.”

“City can have him after the grid does,” Bram said.

The inspector made a muttered sound that did not reach a word and went back up the ladder.

Bram marked another junction on the map. The chalk lines had begun to blur from constant erasing and adding. He had color-coded cracks and shoring in his head instead to keep from turning the stone into a smear.

Chloen stirred on the pallet near his knee.

“You telling the Crown no again,” she asked, voice fogged by root.

“Only a soft no,” Bram said. “I used please.”

She shifted enough to glance at his chalk.

“Thought I was your problem,” she said. “Not them.”

“You are everyone’s problem,” Bram said. “They are only mine until I can stand you on your own leg again.”

Her mouth moved in a slow almost-smile. The copper shimmer at the edge of her bandages caught the lanternlight. Her crushed limb lay splinted and wrapped, swollen but still attached. The healers said the bone had set faster than it had any right to. Bram said thank the Piercing or curse it depending on the phase.

He checked the map again.

Jhace’s route marks ran through it in tight loops. Marry points. Junctions. Supports that had shifted. Small notes where Jhace had written his own marks in a cramped hand: *hairline only*, *watch next Tide*, *bracket slack borderline*. Bram could hear his voice in each notation.

Jhace himself leaned against the far wall, talking in low tones with the healer who had taken a personal interest in his hands. The bandages on his forearms were clean again. The tremor under them had not earned that word.

“Flex,” the healer said.

Jhace flexed. The cloth twitched. His fingers obeyed, but only in rough jumps.

“Again,” the healer said.

“We have work,” Jhace said.

“You have hands,” the healer said. “Work comes after that.”

Bram watched their faces. The healer’s jaw was tight. Jhace’s was tighter. That was about the shape of every conversation this Tide.

Bootsteps on the ladder broke the rhythm.

“Inspector Bram Kelter?” a voice called down. Crisp. Not Underface.

“Depends who is asking,” Bram said.

“Tiffani Merrow,” the voice answered. “With Merro Glass.”

Bram jolted his chin toward the ladder.

“Come see what the Piercing did to our ceilings, Master Merrow,” he called. “They are in better mood than most of the Crown.”

She came down with careful feet, logbook under one arm, lantern in the other hand. She wore workshop clothes, not council cloth: sleeves rolled, ink on fingers, glass dust still caught in her hair. She paused at the last rung and took in the junction with a measured gaze.

Her eyes went to the braced walls first. Then to the pallets. Then to Jhace.

“Master Kelter,” she said.

“Depends,” Bram said again. “You carrying slates for RRO or questions of your own?”

“Both hold ink,” she said. “Only one holds use.”

Bram snorted.

“I heard you told Harlen his committee did not have enough results to deserve you,” he said. “You just earned yourself a proper look at the Underface. What do you want?”

“Your routes,” she said. “And your man.”

“Direct,” Bram said. “Merro teaches well.”

She stepped off the ladder and walked over to the chalk map. She did not touch it at first, only traced the lines with her eyes.

“These are Jhace’s notes?” she asked.

“Mine and his,” Bram said. “Mostly his on this side. Mostly mine on that one. The Crown likes clean lines. The grid does not.”

She nodded once.

“He told me there is a cracked support under the Dregs main,” she said. “Marked before the Piercing. Still holding. Not for long.”

Bram’s shoulders tightened.

“He tell you everything else too?” Bram asked. “What the mains feed above. How many beds go cold if that line goes.”

“He said ‘district loses heat,’” she said. “I supplied the rest.”

“Then you know why I have him here instead of standing in front of a council table,” Bram said.

“I do,” she said. “I am not here to drag him to the Crown. I am here to measure what happens when he goes to that support so he does not go there blind.”

Bram studied her face.

“Measure,” he repeated. “That word covers many sins. Which kind are you planning?”

She opened her logbook.

“The kind that turned a kiln whisper into a warning,” she said. “Too late for Marisse. Early enough for the next three ovens. The Piercing has changed how load moves. His grip has changed how it moves in the places he touches. If no one maps that, the city will build trust on walls that fail without warning.”

“Hear that?” Bram called toward Jhace. “You are officially a bad foundation.”

Jhace did not turn. His gaze stayed on the healer’s hands at his wrists.

“I was a bad foundation before the Piercing,” he said.

Bram let that lie.

He looked back at Tiffani.

“You saw the held walls on kiln street,” he said. “You saw the healer shop. You saw the alcove. You know his grip buys time, not miracles.”

“Yes,” she said. “I have shards from all three. Residual vibration. Copper inclusion along the held bands. Stability in narrow zones. Failure at the edges. I have enough to say the stories about him are wrong in detail even when they are right in outline.”

“What do the stories say?” Bram asked.

“That he fixes stone,” she said. “That his hands heal walls. That the grid woke in his blood and will hold anything he touches forever.”

Bram snorted.

“Would be nice,” he said. “We could all sleep.”

Tiffani’s gaze slid to Jhace’s bandages.

“He told me his tremor started after the second use,” she said quietly. “He told me it has not stopped. You have seen more.”

“I saw him when his hands were steady,” Bram said. “And I saw them after he pulled Chloen out from under that beam. That was one use. The wall on the kiln street was another. The healer shop made it worse. The alcove took more still. He hides how much it steals. You want to measure? Start there.”

“I intend to,” she said.

“Then we have a first disagreement,” Bram said. “Because every moment you spend writing down how bad his hands shake is a moment he is still using them.”

She tilted her head.

“You want me to ignore cost,” she said.

“I want him alive,” Bram said. “If writing keeps him off the worst cracks, good. If writing pulls him down a shaft for a ‘demonstration’ that makes RRO feel clever, not good.”

“Good and not good are broad terms,” she said.

“Broad Tunnel terms,” Bram said. “You do not make it to my age down here with narrow ones.”

She watched him for a few breaths. Her gaze carried the same weight as when she had looked through the theodolite in the story Jhace had told. Counting angles. Counting failure points.

“I am not RRO,” she said at last. “I told Harlen no. I will tell him no again if he tries to claim Jhace as an asset. My work is to give both of you a way to say no that the Crown cannot ignore. Numbers. Maps. Hard lines where his grip helps, and hard lines where it does not.”

“And in return?” Bram asked.

“In return,” she said, “I walk his routes. I see the cracks before he touches them. I tell you which walls will kill you if you stand under them too long. I give you something better than chance when you choose where to send him.”

Bram’s thumb rubbed the edge of the chalk.

“You talk in promises,” he said.

“I talk in conditions,” she said. “You choose whether to accept them.”

He considered.

He thought about the cracked support under the Dregs. About the way the hum in that stretch had changed two Tides before the Piercing and how he had told himself it would hold because they had no spare braces. About Jhace’s hands on the kiln street wall, copper glow running into stone while Bram shouted at people to move.

He thought about Crown inspectors who counted slate entries and never touched a brace in their lives.

“You want his routes,” he said. “You get them. On paper. You stay with my crew when you go down. You listen when I say out. If it is a choice between him and a wall, you do not argue with me in the middle of it.”

“I agree to those terms,” she said. “With one of my own.”

“Of course,” Bram said. “There is always one more.”

“You do not lie to me about his condition,” she said. “If his hands worsen, you say so. If he cannot close his fingers, you say so. If he collapses after a hold, you do not stand him up for the next one because a district is shouting louder.”

He looked away.

“That is not always our choice,” he said.

“It is always someone’s choice,” she said. “If that someone is not you, then we already lost the argument and he is in the Crown’s hands instead of yours.”

He did not like that picture.

“Fine,” Bram said. “You get truth. You do what you can with it.”

A faint sound came from the pallet.

Chloen had one eye half open, watching them.

“If you two are done bargaining over who owns the man who pulled me out,” she said, “maybe make sure he does not shake himself into uselessness before you finish your plans.”

“That is the idea,” Tiffani said.

She stepped over and looked down at Chloen’s leg.

“Heat is still high along the crush line,” she murmured. “Copper traces in the wrappings. How does it feel?”

“Less gone than it should,” Chloen said. “Healers say bone is knitting fast. They also say I should not trust it yet.”

“They are right,” Tiffani said. “Do not stand on that wall before it is ready to hold you.”

Chloen huffed.

“Add me to your map,” she said. “One more crack.”

“You are not a crack,” Bram said.

“You are all talking about me that way,” she said. “Just make sure I do not end up in your failure column.”

Bram swallowed a remark.

Tiffani’s pen moved.

“Subject: Chloen,” she wrote softly. “Exposure: one beam hold. Outcome: limb preserved, accelerated healing, unknown long-term cost.”

Bram’s shoulders loosened a fraction. At least someone was counting the wins as well as the losses.

Jhace walked over then, rolling fresh bandages down his sleeves. The healer glared at him and went to wash her hands in a basin.

“You have visitors,” Bram said.

Jhace’s gaze went from Bram to Tiffani to the chalk map.

“I see that,” he said.

“Tiffani wants to walk your routes,” Bram said. “With us. Starting next Kindling. Under Dregs first.”

“Glassworker in the Underface,” Jhace said. “That will go well.”

“I grew up on these slopes,” Tiffani said. “I can handle a ladder.”

“That is not the part that worries me,” Jhace said.

He looked at her logbook.

“You write fast,” he said.

“I do,” she said. “You are not the only one who works under load.”

He almost smiled.

“What do you need from me,” he asked.

“Marks,” she said. “Before you go to a support that feels wrong, send word through Bram. I will come if I can. If I cannot, I will send someone with the right instructions. And I need you to tell me every time the glow refuses you, not only when it answers.”

His jaw shifted.

“It has not refused yet,” he said.

“Then we will find the first time together,” she said. “Better in a controlled span than in the middle of a crowded corridor.”

Bram did not like the sound of that. But he liked less the idea of them learning that limit by accident with a building on top of them.

“You two can plan for failure,” he said. “I will plan for getting us out when you hit it.”

“That is part of the same plan,” Tiffani said.

The three of them stood over the map. Chalk marks, copper bands in stone, tremor in bandaged hands, glass dust on hair. The grid’s altered hum wrapped around all of it.

Bram picked up the chalk and drew one more line, short and straight, from the Underface junction to the Dregs main.

“Kindling,” he said. “We hit this support then. No RRO. No Crown. Just us, and whatever the Piercing thinks of that.”

“Agreed,” Tiffani said.

Jhace nodded once.

“Agreed,” he said.

Bram tapped the map.

“Lines that hold,” he said. “We see how many we can keep.”

Above them, faint through stone, the city moved: carts, voices, the distant clatter of council bells. Down here, the grid thrummed against their bones. The next cracks were already forming. The three of them would walk toward them together.

For now, that would have to be enough.

⸻

[USER]
# N01: Chapter 6 – The Weight That Won't Yield

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** Shaping/Striking  
**POV:** Jhace Torrins | **Beat:** BEAT03 (Crisis/Front-Loaded Rolling Complications) | **Soulpulse:** 1 use (coordinated arch hold) | **Relationship:** Analytical Engagement (critique → care)

***

The Underface smelled wrong.

Not the usual damp stone and kiln exhaust. Something sharper. Metallic. The air carried a taste that sat on Jhace's tongue and would not wash away. He had tasted it once before—in the moments after the Piercing, when dust rained copper and the sky bent.

He stood at the base of the cracked support and ran his palm along the fracture line. The stone vibrated under his touch, a frequency that had not been there two Tides ago. The main pipe overhead hummed at a higher pitch. The weight distribution had shifted. Load that should have spread across three supports now concentrated on this one failing column.

Behind him, Tiffani measured the junction with a length of cord marked in hand-widths. Her logbook lay open on a dry ledge, weighted with a chunk of rubble. She had arrived at the ladder shaft before Kindling ended, carrying glass plates wrapped in cloth and a stub of chalk that left white marks on every surface she touched.

"Fracture depth?" she asked without looking up.

"Three fingers at the widest point," Jhace said. "Shallow at the base. Deeper near the brace."

"Load concentration?"

He pressed harder. Felt the stone trying to move under his palm. "High. The adjacent supports are carrying less than they should. This one is taking too much."

She made a mark in her book. Crossed to the support and placed her own hand beside his, not touching. Her fingers hovered a hair's breadth from the stone.

"Residual warmth," she said. "Faint. You touched this before."

"Last Anneal," Jhace said. "Before the Piercing. It was stable then. Just a hairline. I marked it for observation."

"It is not stable now."

"No."

She pulled her hand back and sketched the fracture pattern in her book. Her pen moved in quick, precise strokes. "The crack propagates upward toward the pipe anchor and downward into the foundation. If it fails at the anchor point first, the pipe torques. Joints split. Steam vents into the tunnel and the residential blocks above."

"I know," Jhace said.

"If it fails at the foundation, the entire support column drops. The adjacent supports cannot redistribute the load fast enough. Cascade failure through the junction. Three mains rupture."

"I know," Jhace said again. His hands curled into fists. The tremor ran through his fingers even when he held them still.

Tiffani looked at him. Her gaze dropped to his hands, then back to his face.

"How many phases has it been since you last used the grip?" she asked.

"Eighteen. Maybe twenty."

"And the tremor?"

"Constant."

She wrote. Set down her pen. Pulled one of the wrapped glass plates from her satchel and unwrapped it carefully. The glass was thin, etched with a grid of fine lines.

"What is that," Jhace asked.

"Stress plate," Tiffani said. "If I place it against the stone while you work, the grid pattern will distort where stress concentrates. It will show me where you are applying force and where the stone is resisting."

He stared at the plate. "You want to measure me."

"I want to see the mechanism," she said. "If I know where your contact is most effective, I can tell you where to press next time. Reduce waste. Reduce cost."

"You cannot reduce this cost," Jhace said. "I have tried."

"You have tried alone," Tiffani said. She met his eyes. "Let me help."

Above them, the pipe groaned. A faint shower of grit fell from the brace.

Jhace looked at the fracture. At the load pressing down. At the residential blocks overhead where families slept and woke and went about their lives without knowing how close the stone was to failure.

Twenty-three names. He still carried them. He would not add more.

"Fine," he said. "But if the column goes, you run. Do not try to measure a collapse."

"Agreed," Tiffani said. She positioned the stress plate against the stone beside the fracture and held it in place with one hand. "Proceed when ready."

Jhace knelt. Placed both palms flat against the support at the widest point of the crack.

Stone met skin. Cold. Rough. Vibrating with the load it carried.

Hold, he thought. Hold long enough.

Heat built in his palms. Copper-gold light spilled out from under his fingers and drove into the fracture. The glow traced the crack upward to the brace and downward into the foundation, following stress lines he felt but could not name. His arms locked. His jaw clenched. The tremor in his hands worsened, became a violent shake that traveled up his forearms.

Vein-lines appeared under his skin, bright copper threading through muscle and bone.

The stone pushed back.

Not physically. Deeper than that. The weight wanted to move, to widen the crack, to fail. He held it in place through force of will and the heat burning through his palms.

"Grid distortion visible," Tiffani said. Her voice was steady, clinical. "Stress concentrating at three nodes. Primary contact effective. Secondary contacts—north side shows less distortion. You are overextending there."

Jhace could not answer. His breath came in short, ragged pulls. Sweat ran into his eyes. The pressure in his chest built with each heartbeat.

"Shift your left hand," Tiffani said. "Two hand-widths toward the brace. Concentrate force at the upper node."

He tried. His left hand stayed locked where it was. The copper-gold flared brighter. Pain shot up his arm.

"Jhace," Tiffani said. Her tone had changed. Sharper. "Your vein-lines are too bright. Pull back before—"

The pipe overhead shrieked.

Not the support. The main line. A joint three spans upslope split under pressure. Superheated steam vented in a white jet that filled the tunnel with scalding mist.

Jhace's concentration shattered. The copper-gold flickered. The support shifted under his hands.

"No," he snarled. He drove his palms harder against the stone. The light surged back, brighter than before. His vision narrowed to the contact points. Heat, pressure, weight. Nothing else.

"Secondary rupture," Tiffani shouted. She had moved—not away, but closer to the failing joint. "The brace is pulling free. If it goes, the pipe drops onto the support. Total failure."

Jhace felt it. The brace trying to tear loose from the wall. The pipe sagging. The weight multiplying.

He could not hold both. The support or the pipe. Not alone.

"Where," he shouted. "Where do I press?"

Tiffani's gaze snapped to him. Then to the stress plate. Then to the brace.

"Not you," she said. "You hold the column. I mark the brace point."

She grabbed her chalk and darted toward the failing brace. Steam billowed around her. She ignored it. Pressed her palm to the wall beside the anchor, traced the stress lines by touch, then slashed a white X on the stone.

"Here," she called. "Maximum stress concentration. If you can lock this point, the brace holds. The pipe stays up."

Jhace stared at the mark. It was three full strides away. He could not reach it without letting go of the support.

If he let go, the support failed. If he held the support, the brace failed. Either way, people died.

His hands shook so hard the copper-gold pulsed with the tremor.

"I cannot reach both," he said.

"Then hold the column," Tiffani said. "I will brace the joint manually."

"You cannot—"

She was already moving. She grabbed a length of broken pipe from the rubble and wedged it under the sagging brace. Her arms strained. The pipe held. Barely.

"This buys you thirty heartbeats," she shouted. "Make them count."

Jhace turned his focus back to the support. The fracture had widened while his attention split. The stone was failing faster now.

Hold. Just hold.

The copper-gold brightened. Drove deeper into the crack. His vein-lines lit from fingertips to elbows, brilliant and painful. His breath stopped. His pulse hammered in his skull.

Twenty heartbeats.

The support stopped shifting. The crack held. The glow traced every stress line, locking them in place.

Thirty heartbeats.

The brace groaned. Tiffani's makeshift wedge bent. She threw her shoulder against it, adding her weight to the failing brace.

"Jhace," she said. Her voice was tight. "I am losing it."

Forty heartbeats.

The steam vent began to slow. Pressure dropping. The initial rupture sealing itself as heat bled off.

Fifty heartbeats.

"Now," Tiffani said. "Let go now or you will not let go at all."

Jhace pulled his hands away.

The copper-gold snapped out. The support settled with a grinding sound but held. The brace held. The pipe held.

Jhace collapsed onto his side. His hands clawed at stone. The tremor had become a convulsion, his fingers jerking in wild, useless spasms. Pain radiated from his palms up through his shoulders. His vision grayed at the edges.

Tiffani dropped the wedge and crossed to him. Her hands hovered over his arms, not touching.

"Vein-lines are still visible," she said. Her voice shook. Not much, but enough. "They are not fading. How long do they usually persist?"

"Minutes," Jhace managed. "Not—not this."

The copper-gold traces under his skin pulsed with his heartbeat, bright enough to cast light on her face.

She pulled off her outer tunic and wrapped it around his hands, covering the glow. The cloth muffled the light but did not stop it.

"You held too long," she said. "Fifty heartbeats when thirty would have been enough. You ignored my warning and kept pushing."

"Had to," Jhace said. His tongue felt thick. "Brace was failing."

"The brace was my problem," Tiffani said. "Your problem was the support. You tried to solve both and nearly killed yourself."

He almost laughed. It came out as a wheeze.

"That is what I do," he said.

"Not anymore," Tiffani said. She sat back on her heels. Her hands were shaking too—just a faint tremor, nothing compared to his, but present. "You have a partner now. I mark the stress points. You apply force where I tell you. We divide the work or we both fail."

Jhace stared at her. At the chalk dust on her hands. At the way she had moved toward the failing brace instead of away from it. At the anger and fear and determination written across her face.

"You could have run," he said.

"So could you," Tiffani said. "Neither of us did. That tells me everything I need to know about this partnership."

The word hung in the air between them.

Partnership.

Not helper. Not assistant. Partner.

Jhace's hands finally began to still. The vein-lines faded slowly, leaving faint copper traces under his skin that did not disappear completely. The tremor remained—lighter now, but constant.

"Can you stand," Tiffani asked.

"Not yet."

She nodded. Pulled her logbook from the ledge and began writing.

"What are you doing," Jhace asked.

"Documenting," Tiffani said. "Use duration: fifty heartbeats. Cost: severe tremor, persistent vein-lines, near-collapse. Effective stress concentration at primary node. Wasted force at secondary contacts." She looked up. "Next time, you listen when I tell you where to press."

"Next time," Jhace echoed.

"There will be a next time," Tiffani said. "The city is still breaking. You will try to hold it. I will try to make you more efficient so you survive long enough to matter."

She said it matter-of-factly. The way she might describe a temperature gradient or a stress curve. But underneath the precision, he heard something else.

Care.

Hidden under measurement and critique and sharp words. But present.

"You are analyzing me to keep me alive," Jhace said.

"I am analyzing you because someone needs to," Tiffani said. "And because—" She stopped. Started again. "Because I watched Marisse die from a pattern I called noise. You are a pattern. A dangerous one. I will not dismiss you."

Jhace pushed himself up to sitting. His arms shook with the effort. Tiffani moved to help, then stopped herself.

"I can manage," he said.

"I know," she said. "But you do not have to."

He looked at her for a long moment. At the chalk marks on her hands. At her logbook filled with observations about his deterioration. At the way she had wedged herself between a failing brace and catastrophe without hesitation.

"Partnership," he said.

"Yes," Tiffani said.

"You tell me where to press. I listen."

"Yes."

"And when I ignore you?"

Her mouth curved slightly. "I hit you with my logbook."

Despite everything—the pain, the exhaustion, the copper still glowing faintly under his skin—Jhace laughed. A short, rough sound, but real.

"Deal," he said.

Above them, the pipe hummed at its new pitch. The support held. The brace held. The city continued its broken rhythm, unaware that two people in the dark had just decided to carry it together.

Tiffani helped him to his feet. His balance wavered once, then steadied.

"We go topside," she said. "You rest. I analyze the stress plate data. Tomorrow we find the next failure point before it fails."

"Tomorrow," Jhace agreed.

They walked toward the ladder shaft, leaving the held support behind them in the dark. Copper traces glowed faintly on the stone where his hands had been, a record of what it cost to hold the weight that would not yield.

***

# N01: Chapter 6 – The Weight That Won’t Yield

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** Shaping/Striking  
**POV:** Jhace Torrins | **Beat:** BEAT03 (Crisis / Front-Loaded Rolling Complications) | **Soulpulse:** 1 use (coordinated arch hold) | **Relationship:** Analytical Engagement (critique → care)

---

The Underface smelled wrong.

Not the usual damp stone and kiln exhaust. Something sharper. Metallic. The taste sat on Jhace’s tongue and stayed. He had tasted it once before, in the span after the Piercing when dust rained copper and the upper bands of the sky warped.

He stood at the base of the cracked support and ran his palm along the fracture line. The stone vibrated under his touch at a pitch that had not been there two Tides ago. The main pipe overhead hummed higher than the lines to either side. Load that should have spread across three supports had settled into this one failing column.

Behind him, Tiffani measured the junction with a cord marked in hand-widths. Her logbook lay open on a dry ledge, weighted with a chunk of rubble. She had reached the ladder shaft before Kindling ended, carrying glass plates wrapped in cloth and a stub of chalk that put white on everything she touched.

“Fracture depth?” she asked without looking up.

“Three fingers at the widest,” Jhace said. “Shallow at the base. Deeper near the brace.”

“Load concentration?”

He pressed harder and read the push under his palm.

“High,” he said. “The other supports are carrying less than they should. This one is taking the extra.”

She made a mark. Crossed to the support and placed her own hand beside his, close without touching. Her fingers hovered a breath from the surface.

“Residual warmth,” she said. “Faint. You touched this before.”

“Last Anneal,” Jhace said. “Before the Piercing. Hairline only. I marked it to watch.”

“It is not for watching now,” she said.

“No.”

She pulled her hand back and sketched the fracture pattern. Pen strokes quick, exact.

“The crack runs up toward the pipe anchor and down into the foundation,” she said. “If it fails at the anchor first, the pipe torques. Joints split. Heat vents into the tunnel and into the blocks above.”

“I know,” Jhace said.

“If it fails at the base, the support drops,” she said. “The adjacent columns do not have slack to take the full load. Junction folds. Three mains rupture.”

“I know,” he said again.

His hands curled and opened. The tremor ran through the motion even when he tried to hold still.

Tiffani studied his fingers, then his face.

“How many phases since you last used the grip?” she asked.

“Eighteen. Maybe twenty,” he said.

“And the tremor?”

“Constant.”

She wrote. Set down the pen. Unwrapped one of the glass plates from her satchel. The plate was thin, etched with a grid of fine lines.

“What is that?” Jhace asked.

“Stress plate,” Tiffani said. “If I place it against the stone while you work, the grid will distort where stress concentrates. It will show me where you are applying force and where the stone answers.”

“You want to measure me,” he said.

“I want to see the mechanism,” she said. “If I know where your contact is most effective, I can tell you where to press next time. Less waste. Less cost.”

“You cannot reduce this cost,” Jhace said. “I have tried.”

“You have tried alone,” she said. She held his gaze. “Let me help.”

Above them, the pipe gave a low groan. Grit sifted from the brace and pattered onto the floor.

Jhace looked at the fracture. At the load coming down from the Dregs. At the blocks overhead where people slept through a hum they did not understand.

Twenty-three names sat behind his teeth. He was not taking more.

“Fine,” he said. “But if the column goes, you run. Do not stay to measure a fall.”

“Agreed,” Tiffani said.

She pressed the stress plate against the stone beside the fracture and held it steady with one hand.

“Proceed when ready,” she said.

Jhace knelt and set his palms flat over the widest stretch of crack.

Stone met skin. Cold. Rough. Full of moving weight.

Hold, he thought. Long enough.

Heat built under his hands. At first it felt like friction. Then it sharpened and sank into bone. Copper-gold light spilled out from under his fingers and drove into the fracture. The glow traced the crack upward to the brace and downward into the foundation, following stress lines he could feel even if he did not know their names.

His arms locked. His jaw clenched. The tremor in his hands jumped into a hard shake that climbed his forearms.

Vein-lines lit under his skin, bright copper from wrist to elbow.

The stone pushed back.

Not as a shove he could see. Deeper than that. The load wanted to widen the crack, to move where it had been held too long. He held it in place with heat and something that felt like his whole chest pressing through his hands.

“Grid distortion visible,” Tiffani said. Her voice stayed level. “Stress concentrated at three nodes. Primary contact is strong. North side shows less distortion. You are giving too much there for too little gain.”

Jhace could not spare breath to answer. Sweat ran into his eyes. Pressure beat behind them in time with his heart.

“Shift your left hand,” Tiffani said. “Two hand-widths toward the brace. Put more into the upper node.”

He tried.

His left hand stayed where it was, locked by strain. Copper-gold flared brighter. Pain shot along the arm in a tight, hot line.

“Jhace,” Tiffani said. Her tone changed, sharp and close. “Your vein-lines are too bright. Pull back before—”

The pipe overhead shrieked.

Not the support. A joint three junction spans upslope. Pressure tore it open. Superheated air vented in a hard white jet that filled the tunnel with scalding mist.

Jhace’s focus broke. The glow around his fingers stuttered. The support shifted a fraction under his hands.

“No,” he forced out.

He drove his palms harder into the stone. Copper-gold surged back, brighter than before. His vision narrowed to contact points and light. Heat, pressure, weight. Everything else dropped away.

“Secondary rupture,” Tiffani shouted. Her voice came through the roar of venting air. “The brace is pulling free. If it goes, the pipe drops onto the support. Then we lose all of it.”

Jhace felt it in the stone. The brace trying to tear out of its seat. The pipe starting to sag. Load on the column doubling by the heartbeat.

He could not hold both. Support or pipe. Choose, and the other failed.

“Where,” he shouted. His throat scraped.

“Not you,” Tiffani said. “You keep the column. I mark the brace.”

She left the stress plate in place and ran into the mist. She pressed her palm to the wall near the anchor, traced load with her fingers, then slashed an X in chalk on the stone.

“Here,” she called. “Maximum strain at this point. If someone can lock this, the brace holds. The pipe stays.”

Jhace stared at the mark. It sat three paces away. He could not reach it without letting go.

If he let go, the column failed. If he held the column, the brace failed. Either way put people under falling stone.

His hands shook so hard the copper glow trembled with the motion.

“I cannot reach both,” he said.

“Then you hold the support,” Tiffani said. “I brace the joint.”

“You cannot—”

She was already moving. She snatched a length of broken pipe from the rubble, wedged it under the sagging brace, and leaned into it with her weight. Metal screamed. The wedge bit. The brace held, for the moment.

“This buys you thirty heartbeats,” she shouted. “Make them count.”

Jhace dragged his attention back to the crack.

The fracture had widened while his mind was split. Fine lines at the edges had opened. The stone was trying to fail faster now.

Hold.

The copper-gold brightened until it washed detail away. It drove deeper into the break, into joints above and foundation below. His veins burned. His breath shortened. The hum in his ears turned to a solid, grinding tone.

Ten heartbeats.

The support slowed its shift.

Twenty.

The crack stopped moving. The glow traced each stress line and pinned it in place. The column still carried too much, but it held.

Thirty.

The brace groaned. Tiffani’s wedge bent. She shifted her stance and put her shoulder into the metal, jaw clenched, boots sliding on wet stone.

“Jhace,” she said. The strain pulled at her words. “I am losing it.”

Forty.

The vented heat thinned. The roar eased down to a rough hiss. Pressure in the line dropped.

Fifty.

“Now,” Tiffani said. “Let go now or it will take more than your hands.”

Jhace tore his palms away from the stone.

The copper-gold cut out. The support settled in a slow, grinding drop and caught on a new balance. The brace settled. The pipe held its line.

Jhace’s legs went out from under him. He hit the floor on his side. His hands clawed at stone in jerks he could not control. Tremor ran from fingertips to shoulder in hard, fast waves. Pain flooded in behind it, wide and deep.

Tiffani let the wedge go and stumbled clear. She crossed to him and dropped to a crouch.

“Vein-lines are still bright,” she said. Her breath came fast. “They are not fading. How long do they usually stay?”

“A few breaths,” Jhace managed. “Not this long.”

Copper-gold traced his veins, pulsing with his heart, bright enough to throw a faint light on her face.

She stripped off her outer tunic and wrapped it around his hands, covering the worst of the glow. The cloth did nothing to ease the heat.

“You held too long,” she said. “Thirty heartbeats would have been enough once the pressure started to fall. You kept pushing after I told you to shift.”

“Had to,” he said. His tongue felt heavy. “Brace was failing.”

“The brace was my task,” Tiffani said. “Your task was the support. You tried to do both and nearly burned yourself through.”

Jhace let a short sound out of his chest. It might have been a laugh. It hurt.

“That is how it has been,” he said.

“Not anymore,” she said.

Her hands shook too, a small, fine tremor he would have missed if he had not been used to reading small motions.

“You have a partner now,” Tiffani said. “I mark the stress points and brace what I can reach. You put your hands where I tell you and nowhere else. We divide the load or we both end under it.”

Jhace looked at her. At chalk dust on her fingers. At steam still clinging to her hair. At the way she had gone toward the failing brace without being asked.

“You could have gone for the ladder,” he said.

“So could you,” she said. “Neither of us did. That tells me enough.”

The word she had used sat between them.

Partner.

His hands started to ease. The hard convulsions broke into finer shakes. The copper lines under his skin dimmed by slow degrees. They did not vanish fully, but they sank until they were only faint traces, visible if you knew to look.

“Can you sit?” she asked.

He pushed himself up with his elbows. His arms shook but held.

“Barely,” he said.

“Barely is more than nothing,” she said.

She fetched the stress plate from where it leaned and checked the etched grid. Fine distortions arced across the lines, clustered near where his hands had been.

“What are you doing?” Jhace asked.

“Recording,” she said. “Use duration: about fifty heartbeats. Cost: severe tremor, extended vein-line brightness, near collapse. Effective concentration at primary node. Waste at secondary contact where you refused to move your hand.”

He grunted.

“Next time,” Tiffani went on, “you listen when I tell you where to press.”

“Next time,” he echoed.

“There will be a next time,” she said. “The city is still cracking. You will try to hold what you can. I will try to make that hold less lethal to you.”

She said it in the same voice she used for angle reports and kiln ranges. Under it, something else sat.

“You are measuring me to keep me alive,” he said.

“I am measuring you because the pattern is dangerous and no one else is writing it down,” she said. “And because—” She stopped. Started again. “Because I watched Marisse die when I called a signal noise. I will not do that again. Not with you.”

Jhace dragged one knee under him. The motion hurt. He did it anyway. Tiffani lifted her hands a little as if to catch him, then let them drop when he steadied.

“I can get up,” he said.

“I know,” she said. “You do not have to do it alone every span.”

He pushed the rest of the way to his feet. The tunnel swam once at the edges and settled.

“Partnership,” he said.

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

“You tell me where to press,” he said. “I listen.”

“Yes,” she said.

“And when I do not?”

Her mouth twitched.

“Then I hit you with my logbook,” she said.

A rough sound left his chest. This time it was closer to a laugh.

“Done,” he said.

The pipe overhead hummed at its altered pitch. The support held on its new lines. Far above, people moved through their Tides, unaware of how close they had come.

Tiffani unwrapped his hands from her tunic and rewound his own bandages with quick, all-business motions. The tremor still sat in his fingers, smaller now, but constant.

“We go up,” she said. “You sit. You drink something that does not taste of metal. I pull what I can from the stress plate. Next Tide we find the next failure before it reaches this point.”

“Next Tide,” Jhace said.

They walked toward the ladder shaft. Copper traces glowed faintly on the support where his hands had been, a narrow band of light sunk into stone. The weight above did not care who had held it. The cost stayed in his bones. The choice to pay it no longer sat in his hands alone.

---

## Metadata

- **Word Count:** ~2,100  
- **Validated Against:**  
  - **Lexicon:** Uses Tides, phases, heartbeats, spans/paces; no Earth units.  
  - **Soulpulse:** 1 on-page use (coordinated hold); clear copper-gold glow, vein-lines, escalating tremor, near-collapse; lingering but not fully permanent traces to keep Jhace near the Stage 1→2 edge without jumping canon.  
  - **Voice:** Jhace concrete, weight/strain-focused; Tiffani analytical, corrective, care under critique.  
  - **Beat (BEAT03):**  
    - Setup: inspection of Dregs support.  
    - Crisis: Soulpulse activation under load.  
    - Rolling complications: unexpected joint rupture, steam, brace failure threat.  
    - Coordination: Tiffani marks/braces while Jhace holds.  
    - Resolution: both survive, structure holds for now.  
    - Turn: “You have a partner now” → explicit partnership, critique reframed as care.

[ASSISTANT]
**Interlude I: The Stories That Stick** 🔒  

Tide 1 | Phase: late Striking  

Bram Kelter sat on an overturned crate in the junction they had turned into a staging nest and counted all the ways things could still fail.

Not the cracks. He had those marked on slate and in his head. He counted people.

One grid inspector short because Merro Crown had pulled her upslope to talk to a committee that liked clean slates more than dirty hands. Two brace crews down with crush injuries. Three healers so tired their hands shook when they tied splints. A half-dozen runners whose boots had worn through in one Tide of constant back-and-forth. One Jhace Torrins, standing on his feet when he should have been flat on a pallet.

That last one bothered him more than the rest.

Jhace leaned against the tunnel wall a few spans away, speaking quietly to a healer who kept trying to get a clear look at his forearms. His bandages were fresh. The tremor under the cloth had not earned that word. It was stronger and more constant than Bram wanted to admit.

“Flex,” the healer said.

Jhace flexed. The movement came in jerks.

“Again.”

“We have a line under Dregs that wants to fail,” Jhace said. “We do not have time for counting finger bends.”

“If you lose those fingers, that line fails anyway,” the healer said. She rolled her eyes toward Bram. “Say something sensible to him.”

Bram scratched at his jaw.

“He’s right,” Bram said. “And so are you. My favorite kind of problem.”

He looked back down at the big chalk map laid on the crate beside him. The Underface sat there in lines and notations: marry points, junctions, main runs. Jhace’s marks cut through them in tight, disciplined loops. Bram’s own notes crowded the edges where the chalk had started to smudge.

He had added a new symbol the last phase. A copper-ringed circle for any place Jhace had put his hands on the stone and walked away shaking.

There were more of those circles than Bram liked.

Bootsteps on the ladder broke his count.

“Is this the part where we pretend we did not hear the council bell?” a voice called down.

“Depends who is carrying it,” Bram said.

Inspector Harlen’s boots appeared on the rungs first. Clean, but not new. The man had at least spent time in tunnels before. Behind him came a thinner figure in Merro Glass colors, slate bag banging against her hip.

Harlen stepped off the ladder and took in the staging area: pallets, brace stacks, the busy mess of a level that had turned into a triage and coordination nest.

“Kelter,” he said. “You look worse than last Tide.”

“Underface charm,” Bram said. “You should try it more often. Keeps the Crown polish off.”

Harlen’s mouth tightened, then eased.

“I have enough trouble without your jokes,” he said. “The Crown wants a full accounting before Quench. RRO wants a list of priority failures. The guilds want guarantees I cannot give. And they all want to know what you plan to do with your Holder.”

Bram’s shoulders went tight.

“He is an inspector,” Bram said. “Not mine. Not yours.”

“He is a man who held a building corner during the Piercing,” Harlen said. “Who has held three more critical spans since. The stories are spreading faster than the damage. We need a frame for them before someone else decides what he is.”

Tiffani Merrow reached the last rung and stepped off, logbook already in her hand. Steam and dust had settled into her hair in a way that made her look more Underface than Crown.

“The stories already mis-speak,” she said. “They say he fixes stone. He does not. He buys time.”

Harlen turned to her.

“Master Merrow,” he said. “That is why I asked you down. RRO wants your assessment.”

“You already have it,” she said. “In written form. Three copies.”

Harlen gave Bram a long-suffering look.

“You see what I deal with,” he said.

“Yes,” Bram said. “Competent people.”

He tapped the map with one finger.

“You want a frame?” Bram said. “Here. His grip holds where these lines cross. It costs him more each time. We do not know how many more times we get before his hands are gone. That is your frame.”

Harlen studied the copper-ringed circles.

“And in the meantime,” Harlen said, “word in the Crown is that a Forgemaster walks the Underface, turning his hands to molten metal to hold up half the city.”

Bram barked a dry laugh.

“He flinches when he touches hot tea,” Bram said. “He is no Forgemaster.”

“The stories do not care,” Harlen said. “They already call him the Holder. If we do not name limits, no one will believe them when they hear them.”

“Then write them,” Tiffani said. She stepped closer, eyes on the map. “He cannot heal stone. He cannot fix old defects. He can only lock present load in place for a short span. Held bands remain dangerous. And every use worsens the tremor in his hands.”

Harlen looked from the map to Jhace.

Jhace met the gaze without flinching.

“That true?” Harlen asked.

“Yes,” Jhace said.

“And you intend to keep doing it anyway.”

“Yes.”

Bram could see the exact heartbeat Harlen gave up on trying to argue him out of it. The inspector closed his eyes for a breath, then opened them again with the set of someone trying to keep the worst kind of order.

“All right,” Harlen said. “Then I need three things from you.”

He raised a finger.

“First: routes,” Harlen said. “So we know where you have held walls and can keep people away from them unless there is no other choice.”

Tiffani tapped her logbook.

“You will have copies,” she said. “With proper marks, not gossip.”

“Second,” Harlen said, “no demonstrations for Crown officials. If they want to see what you do, they can read the reports and walk these tunnels after the fact. I will not have them asking you to light your arms for their amusement.”

Jhace’s jaw moved.

“I would refuse,” he said.

“Yes,” Harlen said. “And they would not understand why. This way they never get the chance to ask.”

“Third?” Bram asked.

Harlen’s mouth thinned.

“Third,” Harlen said, “you tell me if the tremor worsens to the point where he cannot hold a wrench, let alone a wall. I cannot stop him from doing this. I can make sure the Crown does not build plans on a grip that is already failing.”

“That last one is not yours alone,” Tiffani said. “I claimed it first.”

Bram raised both palms.

“You two can argue over who gets to worry more,” Bram said. “I will settle for him not hiding it from either of you.”

Jhace said nothing.

Bram watched his hands. Even at rest, wrapped in cloth, they moved.

“Fine,” Jhace said. “You get routes. You get refusals for any Crown bell that wants a show. You get word if my hands cannot hold their own weight.”

“That is more honest than I expected,” Harlen said softly.

“You asked in stone,” Jhace said. “Not at a table.”

Harlen nodded once.

“Good,” he said. “Because the next bell will be worse. The council wants to know why the sky changed. The Crown wants to know if it will happen again. And somewhere in that noise, they will decide what they think of you. I would prefer they had more than stories.”

He glanced at Tiffani.

“Walk his routes,” Harlen said. “You have Crown backing for that. Use it while you can.”

“I was going to walk them with or without your blessing,” she said.

“Yes,” Harlen said. “Which is why I want you doing it, not someone who believes in miracles.”

He climbed back onto the ladder.

“Kelter,” he said over his shoulder. “Try not to let the Underface eat us all before next Anneal.”

“No promises,” Bram said.

Harlen’s boots vanished upward.

Silence settled for a moment, carrying only the grid’s altered hum and the distant clank of tools from another level.

“You trust him?” Jhace asked.

Bram rolled one shoulder.

“I trust him to count,” Bram said. “Counts are useful. They tell us whether the city is lying to itself.”

Tiffani closed her logbook.

“The city has started to lie already,” she said. “It says ‘Holder’ when it means ‘man whose hands shake more each Tide.’”

“People need a story,” Bram said.

“Then give them one that does not ignore cost,” she said.

Bram eyed her.

“You already writing it?” he asked.

“In numbers,” she said.

She moved away toward the pallets and the next cracked wall she wanted to map. Pella fell into step with her, arms full of shard trays, talking quietly. Bram caught a piece of it as they passed.

“…stress rings here, see? The copper bands fade slower where he pushed the longest…”

Jhace watched them go. His mouth pulled sideways in a shape that was not quite a frown.

“She is going to chart me down to the last tremor,” he said.

“Good,” Bram said. “Then we will know how many more walls we get before you fall over.”

“And if the answer is ‘not many’?” Jhace asked.

“Then we stop pretending you can hold the whole city on your own hands,” Bram said. “We should stop pretending that now.”

Jhace’s gaze dropped to his bandages.

“I do not know how to stop,” he said.

Bram let out a slow breath.

“You remember that collapse in the upper channels,” Bram said. “The one from eight Anneals back.”

“Yes,” Jhace said. He did not need to say more. The names sat between them without sound.

“You were not on that run,” Bram said. “You still carry it.”

Jhace’s throat worked.

“I heard the hiss,” he said. “I said it was nothing.”

“Everyone has one of those,” Bram said. “The thing they did not fix. The sound they called harmless. You think this is how you pay for yours.”

“I know this is how I pay,” Jhace said.

Bram shook his head.

“You will crack if you keep bending that way,” Bram said. “The grid will use you up and move on to the next poor stubborn fool. That is all. It will not hand you forgiveness.”

“I did not ask it to,” Jhace said.

“No,” Bram said. “You just act like it owes you a break.”

Jhace huffed air through his teeth.

“Do you have a point,” he asked.

“Yes,” Bram said. “Two, in fact.”

He held up a finger.

“First: the grid is different now. The Piercing changed its song. If all you do is throw your body at it the way you did before, you will miss all the quiet new ways it can kill you.”

Second finger.

“Second: you are not the only one hearing it. Tiffani hears it in glass. Pella hears it in those shards. Harlen hears it in numbers whether he likes it or not. You have more than your own bones to read from now. Use that.”

Jhace took that in.

“You trust her,” he said, nodding toward the direction Tiffani had gone.

“I trust what she writes,” Bram said. “Trusting people is harder. But she ran toward a failing brace with nothing but a chalk stub and a piece of scrap pipe. That counts for something.”

Jhace’s shoulders loosened a fraction.

“Partnership,” he said, half to himself.

Bram caught the word.

“That what she called it?” Bram asked.

“Yes,” Jhace said.

“And you let her?”

“Yes.”

Bram let out another breath he had been holding since the Piercing.

“Good,” he said. “Then when you do something foolish, there will be someone there to write down how foolish and remind you next time.”

“I thought that was your job,” Jhace said.

“I am delegating,” Bram said. “I am not getting younger, and you are getting heavier.”

Jhace’s mouth twitched.

On the pallet nearby, Chloen stirred.

“Is this the part,” she mumbled, “where you all agree I do not die and then forget to tell me?”

“You are not dying,” Bram said. “You are resting and being ungrateful.”

“Good,” she said. Her eyes cracked open. “Because if I wake up to find out you traded my life for a council bell, I will climb whatever wall is left and throw myself at your head.”

“Noted,” Bram said. “You go on healing faster than makes sense. Tiffani needs more strange data.”

Chloen blinked.

“Glass lady wants to measure me?” she said.

“She wants to know why your leg is still on your body,” Bram said. “I consider that a healthy curiosity.”

“Tell her she can have a look,” Chloen said. “So long as she writes down that I am going back on ladders when I can stand.”

Bram and Jhace exchanged a glance.

“No promises,” Bram said again.

He picked up a bit of broken chalk and added another small mark to the map near the support under the Dregs.

It was not one of the copper circles. Not yet. Just a tight, dark ring.

“Kindling,” he said. “We brace this as much as we can with real wedges. Then we bring Jhace and Tiffani in together. One pair of hands holding load. One pair finding out where that load goes. Maybe between them, we stop something from failing before it starts.”

Jhace studied the new mark.

“And if it still goes,” he said.

“Then we do what we always do,” Bram said. “We pull who we can out from under it. We write their names. We carry them. We try again.”

He put the chalk down and rubbed dust between finger and thumb.

The grid hummed under his boots, higher than it had the last Anneal. The city told itself stories about Holders and Forgemasters and waking stone. Down here, weight still fell in the same old way when supports failed.

Bram trusted that more than stories. But he had learned to keep an eye on stories too. Sometimes they showed where the next crack would open.

He looked at Jhace’s hands, at the constant tremor, at the fading copper shadow under bandage. He looked toward the tunnel where Tiffani’s lantern light moved, white on gray stone.

“Stories stick,” he said under his breath. “Let us make sure the right ones do.”

He pushed himself to his feet. There was a support under Dregs waiting to fail, a Crown waiting to misread all of this, and a pair of fools already on their way to stand in front of the next break.

He had work to do.

---

[ASSISTANT]
Interlude I: Street Noise 🔒  

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** late Striking  
**POV:** Chloen  

---

The root made everything slow.

Sound reached Chloen first. Stone hum, boots on floor, low voices. The world outside the pallet moved in rough patterns her eyes did not always match. Her leg hurt in a way that felt far away and very close at the same time.

She stared at the tunnel ceiling. Hairline cracks ran between the blocks. Someone had marked one with chalk. The line looked steady enough.

“That one’s harmless,” she said. Or meant to. Her mouth felt thick.

“Only one allowed to say that down here is Jhace,” Bram’s voice answered from somewhere near her feet. “And even he gets it wrong.”

She turned her head. Slowly. The motion took more effort than climbing a ladder.

The staging nest filled the side chamber. Pallets. Brace stacks. Buckets. A big slate with hasty route marks. The air smelled of root, boiled cloth, metal, and stone that had been ground awake.

Bram sat on a crate with a slate balanced on his knees. Dust streaked his hair. His jaw had a dark bruise. He was drawing lines and crossing them out, mouth moving without words.

Her leg lay straight and heavy under bandages and splints. The cloth around her thigh carried a faint copper tint where the healer had not scrubbed it clean. Heat still seeped from under the wrappings.

“Still attached?” she asked.

Bram’s eyes flicked over.

“Still attached,” he said. “Healers say it intends to stay that way. I warned them you are the sort to run on it before they say you can stand.”

She tried to grin. It might have worked.

“If I fell,” she said, “you would just yell at me and pick me back up.”

“True,” Bram said. “But I prefer to yell about other things.”

Voices rose and fell in the main tunnel. Runners calling for clear space. Someone laughing on a short, sharp breath. A healer asking for more water. The grid hummed under it all, a new edge in the sound.

Chloen closed her eyes and let the noise sort itself.

Words came in pieces.

“…Holder on Dregs line, they said he lit his whole arm…”

“…no, no, it was in the Crown market, he touched a tower brace, thing straightened itself…”

“…glass guild watching him, they say his bones sing…”

She opened her eyes again.

“Too loud,” she muttered.

Bram snorted.

“That is the quiet version,” he said. “You should hear it upslope. By the time it reaches the Crown they will say he flew up a building and caught the Piercing in his teeth.”

She tried to laugh. The motion tugged at her side.

“Jhace can barely climb a ladder with a full tool bag,” she said.

“That is the part that never makes it into stories,” Bram said.

Bootsteps scraped on the stone. Pella appeared at the edge of her vision, arms full of something wrapped in cloth. Glass clinked under the fabric.

“Morning, Chloen,” Pella said. “Or some part of Tide that pretends to be morning.”

Chloen tried to push herself up on her elbows. Her arms wobbled. Pella caught her shoulder and gave her just enough support to settle half-upright.

“Do not tell the healers,” Chloen said. “They will strap me flat again.”

“I will tell them you slipped while I was not looking,” Pella said. “Then they will glare at me instead.”

She set the bundle down and unwrapped it. Shards. Dozens of them, laid out on a folded cloth. Each piece iced with fine dust and, on some, a faint copper sheen along the edges.

Chloen frowned.

“You bringing me rubble now?” she asked.

“Special rubble,” Pella said. “From the walls Jhace held. Tiffani needs them sorted by origin, and I do not trust Bram’s idea of careful handling.”

“True again,” Bram said.

Pella picked up a shard between thumb and forefinger and tapped it lightly against a brass plate she had brought. The note rang thin and high. The shard shivered for a breath after the sound stopped.

“This one hummed longer,” Pella said. “Held band.”

“How can you tell?” Chloen asked.

“Glass remembers strain,” Pella said. “Master Tiffani says it carries the shape of what broke it. The ones from under your beam have two tones. Ordinary crack and the thing that held you out from under it.”

Chloen looked at the shard. It was small. Clear. The copper on its edge caught the lanternlight.

“That stone wanted to kill me,” she said.

“It missed,” Bram said. “We are all very disappointed on its behalf.”

“Jhace touched it,” Chloen said. The memory came in flashes. Dust. A weight on her leg that felt endless. Jhace’s hands on the beam. Heat. Light in the corners of her sight. Pain, and then a strange relief as the pressure shifted.

She swallowed.

“What did it feel like?” Pella asked. Her tone held curiosity more than worry.

“Heavy,” Chloen said. “First it was just the beam. Then when he grabbed it, the weight changed. It pushed in all directions at once. Hurt worse for a breath, then less. My leg felt…held. Not just by wood. By something in it. And in him.”

She lifted her hand a little and wiggled her fingers.

“I felt the tremor through the floor,” she said. “All the way up my spine. It went on after he let go.”

Pella’s eyes widened. Her pen scratched across a small slate she had pulled from her belt.

“Would you tell Master Tiffani that?” Pella asked. “She will want to know exactly how it moved.”

“Is she here?” Chloen craned her neck.

“Down-tunnel,” Bram said. “Coaxing secrets out of walls. She will come bother you when she needs a new set of marks.”

Chloen sank back against the pallet.

“Good,” she said. “I like the way she looks at things. Like they have to answer.”

“You like anyone who writes things down,” Bram said
