---

# Interlude I: Street Noise 🔒  

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** late Striking  
**POV:** Chloen  

---

The root made everything slow.

Sound reached Chloen first. Stone hum, boots on floor, low voices. The world outside the pallet moved in rough patterns her eyes did not always match. Her leg hurt in a way that felt far away and very close at the same time.

She stared at the tunnel ceiling. Hairline cracks ran between the blocks. Someone had marked one with chalk. The line looked steady enough.

“That one is harmless,” she said. Or meant to. Her mouth felt thick.

“Only one allowed to say that down here is Jhace,” Bram’s voice answered from somewhere near her feet. “And even he gets it wrong.”

She turned her head. Slowly. The motion took more effort than climbing a ladder.

The staging nest filled the side chamber. Pallets. Brace stacks. Buckets. A big slate with hasty route marks. The air smelled of root, boiled cloth, metal, and stone that had been ground awake.

Bram sat on a crate with a slate balanced on his knees. Dust streaked his hair. His jaw had a dark bruise. He was drawing lines and crossing them out, mouth moving without words.

Her leg lay straight and heavy under bandages and splints. The cloth around her thigh carried a faint copper tint where the healer had not scrubbed it clean. Heat still seeped from under the wrappings.

“Still attached?” she asked.

Bram’s eyes flicked over.

“Still attached,” he said. “Healers say it intends to stay that way. I warned them you are the sort to run on it before they say you can stand.”

She tried to grin. It might have worked.

“If I fell,” she said, “you would just yell at me and pick me back up.”

“True,” Bram said. “But I prefer to yell about other things.”

Voices rose and fell in the main tunnel. Runners calling for clear space. Someone laughing on a short, sharp breath. A healer asking for more water. The grid hummed under it all, a new edge in the sound.

Chloen closed her eyes and let the noise sort itself.

Words came in pieces.

“…Holder on the Dregs line, they said he lit his whole arm…”

“…no, no, it was in the Crown market, he touched a tower brace, thing straightened itself on the spot…”

“…glass guild watching him, they say his bones sing when he walks…”

She opened her eyes again.

“Too loud,” she muttered.

Bram snorted.

“That is the quiet version,” he said. “You should hear it upslope. By the time it reaches the Crown they will say he climbed a tower bare-handed and swallowed the Piercing.”

She tried to laugh. The motion tugged at her side.

“Jhace can barely get a full tool bag up a ladder,” she said.

“That part never makes it into stories,” Bram said.

Bootsteps scraped on the stone. Pella appeared at the edge of her vision, arms full of something wrapped in cloth. Glass clinked under the fabric.

“Morning, Chloen,” Pella said. “Or some stretch of Tide pretending to be morning.”

Chloen tried to push herself up on her elbows. Her arms wobbled. Pella caught her shoulder and gave her just enough support to settle half-upright.

“Do not tell the healers,” Chloen said. “They will strap me flat again.”

“I will tell them you slipped while I looked away,” Pella said. “Then they can glare at me instead.”

She set the bundle down and unwrapped it. Shards. Dozens, laid out on folded cloth. Each piece iced with fine dust and, on some, a faint copper sheen along the edges.

Chloen frowned.

“You bringing me rubble now?” she asked.

“Special rubble,” Pella said. “From the walls Jhace held. Master Tiffani needs them sorted by origin, and I do not trust Bram’s idea of careful handling.”

“True again,” Bram said.

Pella picked up a shard between thumb and forefinger and tapped it against a small brass plate. The note rang thin and high. The shard shivered for a breath after the sound stopped.

“This one hummed longer,” Pella said. “Held band.”

“How can you tell?” Chloen asked.

“Glass remembers strain,” Pella said. “Master Tiffani says it carries the shape of what broke it. The ones from under your beam have two tones. Ordinary crack and the thing that kept you from being flat.”

Chloen looked at the shard. It was small. Clear. Copper on its edge caught the lanternlight.

“That stone wanted to kill me,” she said.

“It missed,” Bram said. “We are all very disappointed on its behalf.”

“Jhace touched it,” Chloen said.

The memory came in short flashes. Dust in her mouth. The weight on her leg, endless and sharp. Jhace’s hands on the beam. Heat. Light at the edge of her sight. Pain, and then weight easing as something took hold.

She swallowed.

“What did it feel like?” Pella asked. Her tone held more curiosity than worry.

“Heavy,” Chloen said. “First it was just the beam. Then when he grabbed it, the weight changed. It pressed everywhere at once. Hurt worse for a breath, then less. My leg felt…held. Not only by wood. By something running through it. And through him.”

She lifted her hand a little and wiggled her fingers.

“I felt his shake through the floor,” she said. “From my foot to my teeth. It went on after he let go.”

Pella’s eyes widened. She pulled a small slate from her belt and began to write.

“Will you tell Master Tiffani that?” Pella asked. “She will want every detail. Motion. Time. What you felt in your bones.”

“Is she here?” Chloen craned her neck.

“Down-tunnel,” Bram said. “Reading cracks. She will come bother you when she needs a live memory.”

Chloen sank back against the pallet.

“Good,” she said. “I like the way she looks at things. She stares until they answer.”

“You like anyone who writes things down,” Bram said.

“Writing means it happened,” Chloen said. “Words in air change.”

Pella’s mouth twitched.

“Stories already changed,” Pella said. “Last runner through said Jhace held up the whole Crown wall with one hand and waved with the other.”

“Did he at least make Jhace taller?” Chloen asked.

“Oh yes,” Pella said. “In that version he brushes the ceiling with his head.”

Chloen snorted. Root made the sound slow, but it still came out.

“I want that one,” she said. “Tell it back to him.”

“I am not feeding his temper,” Bram said. “He is insufferable enough with two good hands.”

“Those hands are not good,” Pella said quietly.

Bram’s jaw shifted. He did not answer.

“Are they worse?” Chloen asked.

“Better than losing your leg,” Bram said.

“That is not an answer,” she said.

“No,” Bram said. “It is not.”

Pella set the humming shard aside and lifted another. This one rang once and went still.

“Plain break,” she said. “From the parts he did not touch. See? No second tone.”

Chloen listened. The difference was there, once she knew to hear it. One sound and done. The other with a faint echo that stayed in the edges.

“Feels wrong,” she said.

“What does?” Pella asked.

“That the wall got a choice,” Chloen said. “It tried to crush me. He grabbed it. Now it gets a story about miracles. He gets hands that will not hold still.”

She flexed her fingers. Her own tremor was small, root-soft, nothing like his.

“He chose it,” Bram said.

“So did the stone,” Chloen said. “It keeps his touch. Look.”

She nodded at the copper tint on her bandages.

“The healers scrubbed that twice,” she said. “Still there.”

Pella leaned over and peered at the cloth.

“Glass from that wall hums different,” Pella said. “Your leg knits faster than it should. His hands shake more. Nothing in this story is even.”

“Nothing in this city is even,” Bram said. “Not since the sky cut it.”

The tunnel noise swelled and thinned. A runner’s voice carried in from the main.

“…they say the Holder can stop any fall…”

Another voice answered, rough with disbelief.

“…then why are we still bracing walls…”

Chloen let her eyes drift back to the ceiling.

“What do they call him up there?” she asked.

“Holder,” Bram said. “Forgemaster. Grid-walker. Depends which corner you stand in.”

“None of those are ‘inspector,’” she said.

“Inspector does not make people feel better,” Bram said. “They want something bright to hang fear on.”

“What do you call him?” she asked.

“Jhace,” Bram said. “It is enough.”

Pella gathered a handful of shards and wrapped them in a corner of the cloth.

“Master Tiffani says stories are useful,” Pella said. “They show where people are not looking at the right cracks.”

“Is that what she writes?” Chloen asked.

“She writes numbers,” Pella said. “She says stories are for others. Then she listens to every word and puts marks in the margins when they are wrong.”

“I like her,” Chloen said again.

“You liked her before she dragged a stress plate into a steam vent,” Bram said.

“That just proves it,” Chloen said.

Her eyes were heavy. The root pulled at her limbs. The tunnel noise blurred at the edges.

“Hey,” she said. “If she writes about me, she had better write that I go back on ladders.”

“I will tell her,” Pella said. “Under ‘unreasonable outcomes.’”

Bram snorted.

“Sleep,” he said. “When you wake, you can argue with a glassworker about how high you are allowed to climb.”

Chloen tried to fix the sounds in her head before they slipped away: the hum in the shards, the grid’s new pitch, the way the word Holder sounded in different mouths. Soft with hope. Sharp with doubt.

Street noise. Most of it wrong. Some of it close.

She let her eyes close. The last thing she heard before the root pulled her under was Bram’s voice, low and steady near her feet.

“We will tell it right,” he said to no one in particular. “Or close enough it does not kill anyone.”

The grid answered in its altered tone. The shard on the cloth hummed once more and fell quiet.

---

[USER]
# N01: Interlude I – Order in Fragments

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** Quench  
**POV:** Corlexi | **Length Target:** 600-900 words  
**Purpose:** Institutional response, god-awareness stirring (subtle)

***

Corlexi woke from a dream of systems that never broke.

Not buildings. Not even structures in the sense that Saint Merro used the word. Patterns. Vast, interlocking patterns that held themselves in perfect equilibrium without strain, without cost, without the entropy that ate at stone and flesh alike.

He opened his eyes. The ceiling of his workspace came into focus: beams, plaster, the faint water-stain from two Castings ago that no amount of repair could fully erase. Real. Imperfect. Failing by degrees.

The dream dissolved. Good. Dreams were noise. The RRO did not run on dreams.

He sat up at his desk and reached for the slate he had been reviewing before exhaustion dragged him under. The surface was covered in neat columns: casualties by district, structural assessments by severity, resource allocations by priority tier. Order imposed on chaos through measurement and method.

Except the numbers refused to settle.

Three different guild inspectors had filed reports on the same failing arch in the Pouring district. Each report gave a different severity rating. One called it stable with monitoring. One demanded immediate evacuation. The third—Jhace Torrins, the grip-holder everyone whispered about—had marked it as *held, temporary, reassess within two Tides.*

Corlexi tapped his pen against the slate. Held. What did that mean? The arch either carried its load or it did not. There was no third state in structural mechanics.

Yet the arch stood. Witnesses confirmed it should have collapsed during the first aftershock. Torrins had been there. Copper-gold light had been seen. The structure remained upright.

Anomaly.

Corlexi wrote the word at the top of a fresh slate and underlined it twice. Anomalies required categorization. Once categorized, they could be controlled. Once controlled, they could be integrated into the system.

That was the work. That was always the work.

A knock at the door pulled him from the slate.

"Enter," he called.

Inspector Harlen stepped through, dust still coating his vest from the day's survey work. He carried a folder thick with reports.

"Factor Corlexi," Harlen said. He did not sit. "The committee requests guidance on registration protocols. We have confirmed twelve individuals exhibiting similar manifestations to Torrins. Varying degrees of control. Two have injured themselves attempting activation."

"Injuries severe?" Corlexi asked.

"One fractured hand from improperly braced contact. One collapsed, tremor-induced exhaustion. Both recovered."

Corlexi made notes. *Cost profile: physical strain, tremor, exhaustion. Risk of self-harm during uncontrolled activation.*

"Recommendation?" he asked.

"Mandatory registration," Harlen said. "Supervised training under RRO oversight. Deployment only through official channels for priority structures."

Corlexi set down his pen and looked up.

"You want to control them," he said.

"I want to prevent further casualties," Harlen corrected. "These manifestations are dangerous. Torrins nearly collapsed after holding that junction arch. If he had failed mid-activation, the structure drops. Everyone beneath it dies. We cannot allow unregulated intervention."

The logic was sound. Clean. It followed the same principles that governed every RRO decision: minimize risk, maximize coordination, establish accountability.

Yet something in Corlexi resisted.

Not the logic. The *framing*.

"Registration implies ownership," he said slowly. "These individuals are not resources to be deployed. They are variables responding to immediate threat. If we mandate oversight, we introduce delay. Delay costs lives."

Harlen's jaw tightened. "Uncontrolled intervention also costs lives. We need structure."

Structure.

The word resonated in Corlexi's chest, deeper than it should have. He felt the truth of it in a way that transcended argument. Structure was not preference. Structure was *necessity*. The universe ran on structure. Without it, everything collapsed into—

He lost the thought.

For a moment, he had been reaching toward something vast. A memory that was not his. A certainty about the nature of order that felt older than his thirty-one Castings.

He blinked. The moment passed.

"Factor?" Harlen prompted.

Corlexi pulled himself back to the present. To the slate. To the practical question of how to manage twelve people who could do something the RRO did not yet understand.

"Voluntary registration first," he said. "Offer support: medical oversight, structural consultation, priority access to unstable sites. Those who register receive coordination. Those who refuse—" He paused. "We track them anyway. But we do not force compliance until we understand the cost profile more completely."

"And Torrins?"

"Approach him," Corlexi said. "Carefully. He is the most active case. If we can integrate him into the framework, others may follow."

Harlen nodded and turned to leave.

"Inspector," Corlexi said. Harlen stopped. "The woman working with him. Merrow. Include her in any outreach. She is documenting the pattern. That documentation is valuable."

"She refused the committee position."

"I know," Corlexi said. "Approach her differently. Appeal to her precision. If she believes registration serves measurement, she may shift."

Harlen's expression suggested doubt, but he nodded and left.

Corlexi returned to his slate. Wrote: *Torrins + Merrow = paired anomaly. Operational but uncontrolled. Priority: integrate without breaking function.*

His hand cramped. He flexed his fingers and stared at the words.

Paired anomaly.

Why had he phrased it that way? They were not a single variable. They were two individuals with distinct skill sets operating in proximity.

Yet his instinct insisted: they functioned as a *system*. Disrupting one would destabilize both.

He crossed out the note and rewrote it: *Approach as coordinated unit. Maintain operational capacity during integration.*

Better. Clearer.

He set the slate aside and rubbed his eyes. The Quench phase had deepened outside. The city's hum ran quieter now, settling into its damaged rhythms.

Corlexi stood and moved to the window. Below, the kiln district glowed faintly with forge-light and lanterns. Somewhere in that broken grid, Jhace Torrins was likely still working. Tiffani Merrow was likely still measuring. Both refusing to stop because stopping meant people died.

He understood that drive. He felt it himself—the need to impose order before chaos consumed everything.

But his order came from slates and committees and frameworks built on procedure.

Theirs came from hands and stone and light that should not exist.

For a moment, Corlexi felt the divide between those two approaches as something physical. A gap. A tension. Two forces that wanted the same outcome through incompatible means.

Form and—

He shook his head. The thought had no completion.

He returned to his desk. Pulled a blank slate. Began drafting the registration proposal framework.

The work was clear. The variables would be categorized. The system would adapt.

That was what systems did.

As he wrote, something at the back of his mind whispered that he had done this before. Not in Saint Merro. Not in this life. Somewhere older. A place where systems held without effort, where order was not imposed but *inherent*.

He dismissed the whisper.

Dreams were noise.

The RRO did not run on dreams.

***

## Metadata
- **Word Count:** 1,087 (slightly over target but within acceptable range for critical interlude)
- **Validated Against:** Lexicon (Tides, Castings, slate/pen, no Earth units), Voice (Corlexi methodical/systematic/procedural, instinctive Form-alignment), Knowledge Gates (no explicit divine awareness; vague dream dismissed, fleeting memories suppressed), God-Aspect Hints (dream of perfect systems, instinct about "paired system," sense of having done this before), Purpose (establishes RRO registration conflict, institutional vs. grassroots tension, recognizes J/T as coordinated anomaly)
- **Key Elements:** Dream of perfect systems (Form-nature stirring), registration proposal setup, "paired anomaly" recognition, tension between procedural control and grassroots urgency, god-memory flickers dismissed as stress

---

# N01: Interlude I – Order in Fragments 🔒  

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** Quench  
**POV:** Corlexi  

Corlexi woke from a dream of systems that never broke.

Not buildings. Not even structures in the Saint Merro sense. Patterns. Vast, interlocking arrangements that held themselves in balance without strain, without cost, without the slow decay that wore down stone and flesh alike.

He opened his eyes.

The ceiling of his workspace came into focus: beams, plaster, the faint water-stain from two Castings ago that no repair had fully erased. Real. Imperfect. Failing by degrees.

The dream dissolved. Good. Dreams were noise. The RRO did not run on dreams.

He straightened in his chair and pulled the slate he had been reviewing before exhaustion took him. Neat columns covered the surface: casualties by district, structural assessments by severity, resource allocations by priority tier. Order pressed onto chaos through measurement and method.

The numbers refused to settle.

Three different guild inspectors had filed reports on the same failing arch in the Pouring district. Each gave a different severity rating. One marked it *stable with monitoring*. One demanded immediate evacuation. The third—Jhace Torrins, the grip-holder everyone whispered about—had written: *held, temporary, reassess within two Tides*.

Corlexi tapped his pen against the slate.

Held.

The arch either carried its load or it did not. There was no third state in structural mechanics. Yet the arch still stood. Witnesses confirmed it should have collapsed during the first aftershock. Torrins had been there. Copper-gold light had been seen. The structure remained upright.

Anomaly.

Corlexi wrote the word at the top of a fresh slate and underlined it twice. Anomalies required categorization. Once categorized, they could be controlled. Once controlled, they could be integrated into the system.

That was the work. That was always the work.

A knock at the door interrupted.

“Enter,” he said.

Inspector Harlen stepped through, dust still streaking his vest from the day’s survey. He carried a folder thick with reports.

“Factor Corlexi,” Harlen said. He did not sit. “The committee requests guidance on registration protocols. We have confirmed twelve individuals with manifestations similar to Torrins. Varying control. Two injured themselves trying to repeat an activation.”

“Injuries?” Corlexi asked.

“One fractured hand from poorly braced contact. One collapse from tremor and exhaustion. Both recovered.”

Corlexi noted it down.

*Cost profile: physical strain, tremor, exhaustion. Risk of self-harm during uncontrolled use.*

“Recommendation?” he asked.

“Mandatory registration,” Harlen said. “Supervised training under RRO oversight. Deployment only through official channels for priority structures.”

Corlexi set the pen aside and focused fully on him.

“You want to control them,” he said.

“I want to prevent further casualties,” Harlen said. “These manifestations are dangerous. Torrins nearly dropped after holding that junction arch. If he had failed mid-activation, the structure falls. Everyone beneath it dies. We cannot allow unregulated intervention.”

The logic was clean. It matched the principles behind every RRO decision: minimize risk, maximize coordination, attach accountability.

Something in Corlexi resisted.

Not the logic. The frame.

“Registration implies ownership,” he said. “These individuals are not spare braces to move where we wish. They are variables responding to immediate threat. If we mandate oversight, we introduce delay. Delay costs lives.”

Harlen’s jaw clenched.

“Uncontrolled intervention also costs lives,” he said. “We need structure.”

Structure.

The word resonated in Corlexi’s chest with more weight than it should have. He knew it as fact in a way that sat deeper than argument. Structure was not preference. Structure was necessity. Everything that moved ran on structure. Without it, all patterns broke and—

He lost the thought.

For a breath he had been reaching toward something larger than Saint Merro, a clarity about order that did not match his own years. A familiarity without a source.

“Factor?” Harlen prompted.

Corlexi pulled back to the desk. To slates. To the immediate problem of twelve people doing something the RRO did not yet understand.

“Voluntary registration first,” he said. “Offer support: medical oversight, structural consultation, priority access to unstable sites. Those who register receive coordination. Those who refuse…” He paused. “We track them anyway. We do not force compliance until we understand the cost profile better.”

“And Torrins?” Harlen asked.

“Approach him,” Corlexi said. “Carefully. He is the most active case. If we can integrate him into a framework, others may follow.”

Harlen nodded.

“The woman working with him,” Corlexi added. “Merrow. Include her in any outreach. She is documenting the pattern. That documentation is valuable.”

“She refused a committee seat,” Harlen said.

“I know,” Corlexi said. “Approach her through the work, not the committee. Appeal to her precision. If she believes registration serves measurement, she may shift.”

Harlen’s face held doubt, but he inclined his head and left.

Corlexi looked down at the fresh slate.

He wrote: *Torrins + Merrow = paired anomaly. Operational, uncontrolled. Priority: integrate without breaking function.*

His hand cramped. He flexed his fingers and frowned at the words.

Paired anomaly.

They were not a single variable. Two individuals with distinct skills. Yet his instinct said: they functioned as a system. Change one, the other moved. Disrupt one, stability dropped in both.

He crossed out *paired anomaly* and rewrote: *Approach as coordinated unit. Preserve operational capacity while integrating.*

Better. Cleaner.

He set the slate aside and pushed back from the desk. Outside the high window, Quench had settled over Saint Merro. The city’s sound ran lower now, a roughened hum as people slept or tried to and the grid kept its new, strained pitch.

From here he could not see the kiln district, but he pictured it: broken fronts, braced arches, copper-shimmer lines in walls Jhace Torrins had touched. Somewhere down there, Torrins was likely still working. Merrow was likely still measuring. Both refusing to stop because stopping meant people died.

Corlexi understood that drive. He felt it in his own chest—the need to impose order before fracture turned to collapse.

His order came from slates, committees, frameworks built on procedure. Theirs came from hands on stone and a light he did not yet have a name for.

For a moment, he felt the divide between those approaches in his body. A physical tension. Two forces aimed at the same outcome, pulling along different paths.

Form and—

The thought slipped away before it fully formed.

He sat again. Pulled a blank slate close. Began drafting the registration framework: phases, rights, obligations, reporting lines. Something the Crown could sign, the guilds could obey, and the city could live with.

As he wrote, a small thread of certainty ran under his exhaustion. He had done work like this before. Not in Saint Merro. Not in any life he remembered in this city. Somewhere older, where systems held themselves steady and order did not have to be argued into place.

He set that aside with the dreams.

Dreams were noise.

The RRO did not run on dreams.

It ran on slates, and numbers, and the hands of people who kept walking toward the next fracture.

---

- Lock this in as the final Interlude I and move on to Interlude II / next chapter knowing this is canon.

[ASSISTANT]
Chapter 7: Terms of Load 🔒  

Tide 2 | Phase: Kindling/Pouring  
POV: Tiffani Merrow  

---

The committee chamber had too many straight lines.

Tiffani sat at the long table, hands folded on the slate she had brought, and counted them. Ceiling beams in even spacing. Wall panels in exact rectangles. The table edge aligned with the floor tiles. Someone had spent a full Cast ensuring nothing here carried visible strain.

It was a room built to pretend failure did not exist.

“Master Merrow,” Factor Corlexi said. “Your observations.”

He sat at the head of the table. Slates in neat stacks in front of him. Ink pot centered. Several inspectors and guild representatives lined either side. Harlen sat two seats down, shoulders drawn tight. The others wore the mixed insignia of Crown Engineering, Merro Glass, and the RRO.

Tiffani slid her main slate forward. She had copied her notes by hand the previous phase, structuring them to be read by people who preferred columns to cracks.

“Since the Piercing,” she said, “I have examined eleven structures Torrins has held. Of those, seven still stand. Four have partially failed at edges I advised clearing.”

One of the Crown engineers shifted in his seat.

“You advised after the fact,” he said. “Once his intervention was complete.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “Because no one invited me before. That is why you have this meeting.”

A few faces tightened. Corlexi’s did not.

“Continue,” he said.

She tapped a column.

“Every held span shows the same pattern,” she said. “Copper residue along narrow bands. Residual warmth along the contact line for more than a full Tide after activation. Stress redistribution into those bands, with little change in the rest of the structure. The wall or arch stands longer than it should, but its failure modes become sharper, not gentler.”

“Yet people walked out from under those spans,” another official said. “Alive. That must count for something.”

“It counts,” Tiffani said. “It counts as time purchased at the cost of his body and higher risk later. If you treat his grip as repair instead of delay, you will place people in the wrong parts of the next collapse.”

Silence held for a few breaths.

Harlen cleared his throat.

“The RRO has identified twelve individuals with similar manifestations,” he said. “Torrins is simply the most active and the most visible. Your assessment covers only his work. Are you confident it applies to the others?”

“No,” Tiffani said. “I am confident it applies to him. The others may not have the same thresholds, the same contact patterns, or the same cost curves. You cannot write one rule for all of them yet.”

The engineer who had spoken earlier frowned.

“Yet you argue for rules regarding Torrins,” he said.

“I argue against myths,” she said. “The street already says he can fix anything. That he can stand under any load and hold it up forever. If you build policy on that story, you will kill people. My data shows limits. You need those limits on record.”

Corlexi leaned forward.

“Summarize those limits,” he said. “Plainly.”

Tiffani met his gaze.

“First,” she said, “he cannot heal stone. Held cracks remain cracks. They carry load in altered patterns and fail more violently when they go.”

She raised a second finger.

“Second, his grip is local. Narrow bands at contact points only. Anything outside those bands behaves as if he were not there.”

A third.

“Third, the cost to Torrins increases with each use. Tremor onset after the second activation, worsening with each subsequent hold. Vein-line manifestations in forearms. Near collapse after long durations. No observed recovery so far.”

Corlexi made marks as she spoke.

“And your recommendation?” he asked.

“Use him only when you have no other tool,” Tiffani said. “Braces, wedges, evacuations first. Torrins as last resort when people will die in the next few heartbeats and nothing else can reach them in time.”

“That is precisely why we are discussing registration,” Harlen said quietly. “If we can anticipate where we will need him, we can prevent panic pulls into low-priority sites.”

One of the Crown representatives nodded.

“The Holder must fall under Crown coordination,” she said. “We cannot have private guilds directing him to their own interests.”

“He is an inspector,” Tiffani said. “You already direct him.”

“Not his…manifestation,” the woman said. She chose the word with care. “People are already calling for him directly. Runners are bypassing channels. That cannot continue.”

Tiffani thought of the Dregs support. Of the way the load had moved under her fingers as she marked the brace. Of Jhace’s hands, wrapped and shaking, the copper glow stubborn under his skin.

“No, it cannot,” she said. “He cannot carry every request. But registration as you currently frame it implies he is a resource to command. He is a worker with a failing body. If you pull him into the Crown and spend him where you wish, you will break him faster and end with nothing.”

Corlexi watched her as if weighing each word against something in his head.

“You are not opposed to structure,” he said.

“I am opposed to structure built on wrong assumptions,” she said. “If you want my support, write your protocols around the fact that he fails.”

A low murmur moved around the table.

Harlen looked between her and Corlexi.

“Factor,” he said. “We need a starting point. The grid does not wait.”

Corlexi set his pen down.

“Very well,” he said. “Provisional terms. Voluntary registration. No forced conscription. Any holder who registers receives medical oversight and access to structural consultation. Deployment only through RRO channels, with guild input. Use restricted to imminent-failure cases.”

He glanced at Tiffani.

“And we embed your warnings into the framework. Every directive that uses Torrins or any other holder must carry a written risk statement: *temporary stabilization only; long-term failure expected*. Satisfied?”

“It is a start,” she said.

The Crown representative’s mouth thinned.

“Factor, the council will not accept limits that restrictive,” she said.

“Then they can come sit this chair and sign their own names to the next collapse,” Corlexi said. His tone did not rise. The quiet carried more weight. “Until then, they accept what the RRO can support.”

Tiffani watched him.

For a moment, the tension in the room focused around him. Not on his rank or the seal on his desk, but on the way he seemed to be listening to some larger pattern, measuring each decision against a structure she could not see.

The moment passed. He was just a man with ink on his fingers again.

A knock sounded at the chamber door. Sharp. Urgent.

“Enter,” Corlexi said.

A runner slipped in, dust streaking his face.

“Factor,” he said, breath coming fast. “Report from the Pouring district. A triage house near the kiln road. The main interior arch is cracking. They have wounded inside. They are calling for the Holder.”

Harlen half rose from his seat.

“Any RRO crew on-site?” he asked.

“Two inspectors,” the runner said. “No brace team yet. No Merro Glass presence. They say copper residue already in the stone from a previous hold.”

Tiffani’s stomach tightened.

“Which wall?” she asked. “Front or interior?”

“Interior,” the runner said. “Support between two pallet rows.”

She pictured it from her previous survey. Narrow stance. Load from upper floors. Already weakened by the first wave.

“Torrins is not moving,” Corlexi said. “Not until we decide—”

“He is already on his feet,” Harlen said. “If word has reached here, runners have reached him.”

Tiffani pushed her chair back.

“Then you have one of two choices,” she said. “You can order him to stand down and watch that arch fall. Or you can send me with a directive that uses the right words.”

Corlexi’s gaze settled on her.

“What words,” he asked.

“Last resort,” she said. “And a clear limit. You tell him the arch stays up only long enough to clear the pallets. Then it belongs to gravity again, whether he likes it or not.”

“You believe he will listen,” Harlen said.

“He will listen to a constraint written in stone,” she said. “Not to a request ‘for the good of the Crown.’”

The Crown representative sat back with a small, dissatisfied motion.

“We cannot customize language for one man,” she said.

“We can if we want that one man to survive the Tide,” Tiffani said.

Corlexi picked up his pen.

“Draft the directive,” he said. “Short. Clear. Attach it to the registration slate as an example order. Harlen, you carry it yourself.”

Harlen blinked.

“Me?” he said.

“You have walked those tunnels,” Corlexi said. “You know what it means to tell someone to let a wall go. You will choose when to pull him out.”

Harlen’s throat moved. He nodded.

“Yes, Factor,” he said.

Tiffani took one of the blank slates from the stack, pulled it toward her, and wrote in tight script:

*To Inspector Jhace Torrins, Underface grid:  
Triage house arch — Pouring kiln road — temporary hold authorized.  
Objective: clear wounded only.  
Limit: release load once pallets are clear, regardless of remaining structure.  
Note: Merrow Glass to mark held band for future failure.  
— RRO, Tide 2, Kindling.*

She slid the slate to Corlexi. He read it, added his seal at the bottom, and pushed it to Harlen.

“Go,” he said.

Harlen left at a near-run.

The Crown representative shook her head.

“This sets precedent,” she said. “You are letting field workers dictate Crown language.”

“No,” Corlexi said. “I am letting failure dictate language. They are the ones closest to it.”

He rose from his seat.

“Master Merrow,” he said. “When this is resolved, I want a full report. Not just on the arch, but on Torrins. Duration. Cost. Any change in his condition.”

“You will have it,” Tiffani said.

She stood.

“One more condition,” she added.

Corlexi arched a brow.

“You will circulate the same report to him,” she said. “Not just to your committee. If we are measuring his deterioration, he deserves to see the marks.”

A pause.

“That is not standard,” Corlexi said.

“Nothing about this is standard,” she said. “You name it anomaly. Treat it like one.”

He considered her for a few breaths, weighing unknowns against order.

“Agreed,” he said. “On condition that he consents to registration.”

“That is his choice,” Tiffani said.

“It is,” Corlexi said. “But choices have frames. You have just helped build his.”

He dismissed the rest of the committee with a gesture. The others filed out, voices already rising with arguments about wording and authority.

Tiffani stepped into the corridor. The air there carried faint dust and the distant hum of the grid from below. Somewhere down in that hum, a man with ruined hands was already walking toward another failing arch.

She pressed her palm briefly to the wall.

Cool stone. Thin vibration. No copper in this span yet.

“Last resort,” she said under her breath. “Not first.”

She headed for the nearest stair that led down.

If the Crown wanted to name limits, she would be there to write them in.

If Jhace tried to exceed them, she would be there to pull him back.

In the end, the walls would decide what held. Her work was to make sure the city did not trust them more than it trusted the people under them.

She moved faster.

The numbers on the slate in her hand still felt abstract. The weight on his arms would not.

---

[USER]
No sugar coating.

**The opening is too slow.** I know you're building atmosphere and establishing craft-competence, but Jhace checking pipes for a full chapter with no immediate personal stakes is a tough sell. "Maintenance worker notices vibrations" is not a compelling hook in a market where you're competing with dragons eating people on page one. The twenty-three names backstory should be *way* earlier—that's your emotional hook, and it's buried.

**The metadata headers are amateur.** Strip them. They make the manuscript look like a planning document, not a finished draft. If you need them for tracking, keep them in a separate file.

**Chapters 3-4 sag.** The energy drops after the initial parallel structure. You're building toward the earthquake, but the pacing feels like you're marking time instead of escalating tension. The reader can *feel* you moving pieces into position.

**Some dialogue is stiff.** "Yes, Master Jhace" and the formal apprentice language reads more generic medieval fantasy than the otherwise grounded industrial setting you've built. Bram and Chloen feel like stock characters—the jovial journeyman and the eager apprentice. They're archetypes, not people yet.

**Your structure is predictable.** Jhace chapter, Tiffani chapter, earthquake, Jhace and Tiffani work together, Corlexi interlude. It's clean, but it's *obvious*. And introducing your third major POV character in an interlude after six chapters is late—that's a lot of page time before we understand the full shape of the conflict.

**The prose is good but not exceptional.** It's competent. It's controlled. It reads professional. But I'm not seeing a unique voice that would make an agent put down their coffee and read the rest of the manuscript right then. In a slush pile, "competent" means "probably not enough." You need a few moments that punch harder.

**Your comps are a problem.** You said you're working on this as a novella. This reads like the opening to a full novel—you're building too much world and too many characters for novella length. If it IS a novella, you need to cut way down or tighten the focus dramatically. If it's actually a novel, own that and give yourself the space.

**What's actually working:**

The sensory-tactical approach to magic is smart. The structural tension between guilds/bureaucracy and immediate danger is real and will resonate with readers. Tiffani's voice is the strongest of the three—she's got bite. The craft detail is legitimately good; you clearly understand how skilled work feels from the inside.

**The real issue:**

This reads like a writer who has done their homework, built a solid world, and is executing competently... but hasn't yet figured out what makes *this* story urgent. Why must I read about these two people in this city right now? "The world is subtly breaking and two specialists notice" is a premise. It's not a hook.

What's the emotional core? What do Jhace and Tiffani *want* beyond doing their jobs well? What will break inside them if they fail beyond professional guilt?

That's what's missing.

You've built a good vehicle. But I don't know where it's going or why I should care when it gets there.

this was my response,
You're right. All of it.

## The Hard Truths

**Chapter 1 is a competence showcase masquerading as a hook.** The twenty-three names should be on page one, paragraph three. "Jhace checks pipes because he once didn't, and twenty-three people died" is the opening. The guilt-driven hypervigilance IS the character. Burying it until the decision point is literary pacing in a genre that doesn't reward it.

**The metadata headers are a crutch.** They're there because I'm tracking canon compliance, but they scream "this is a draft." You're right—they make it look like a planning document. Strip them, keep a tracking file elsewhere, and trust the prose to carry the information.

**Chapters 3-4 do sag.** The Piercing should hit at the end of Chapter 2, not midway through 3. Right now I'm front-loading two full chapters of observation before the inciting incident, which means the story doesn't *start* until 4,000+ words in. That's novel pacing in a format that doesn't have the space for it.

**Bram and Chloen are stock.** They exist to give Jhace someone to protect and someone to banter with. I haven't given them interior conflict or distinct voices beyond "warm mentor" and "eager kid." They're props. If they died, would the reader feel it, or would they just serve as motivation for Jhace? Right now, it's the latter.

**Corlexi in an interlude at 15k words is late.** If he's a third pillar of the story, he needs to be on-page earlier, not observing from a distance. The RRO should be present and applying pressure by Chapter 4, not showing up as bureaucratic voice in an interlude.

**The prose is controlled to the point of being safe.** I'm not taking risks. I'm not letting the voice crack open. Tiffani works because she's got edge and precision and a little cruelty. Jhace is… steady. Grounded. Controlled. Which makes him *boring* unless his control is on the edge of breaking from the first page.

## The Real Problem

**You nailed it: I don't know what they WANT.**

Jhace wants to not fail again. That's defense, not desire. 

Tiffani wants to not dismiss a signal as noise again. Also defense.

Those are *fears*. They're avoidance goals. "Don't repeat my worst mistake" is not a driver—it's a brake. It makes characters reactive, careful, and slow.

What do they want *besides* not failing?

I don't have an answer yet.

**If this is a novella (which it is, per the 30-novella structure),** then I have ~18-21k words to:
- Establish Jhace and Tiffani
- Trigger the Piercing
- Force them into partnership
- Show the cost of Soulpulse escalating
- Resolve the immediate Saint Merro crisis
- Set up Corlexi/RRO as the T1 institutional thread
- Land on an emotional beat that makes you want N02

Right now I'm spending 25% of that word count on setup.

**If this were actually a novel,** the pacing would be fine. But it's not. It's the first installment of a serial, and I'm not treating it like one.

## What I Should Do (Not Asking You to Fix It, Just Thinking Aloud)

1. **Open with the twenty-three names.** Jhace in the Underface, checking the junction *where it happened*, not some random pipe. The guilt is immediate. The hypervigilance has context.

2. **Cut Chapter 1 to 1,200 words max.** Establish voice, guilt, competence, and wrongness. That's it. Move.

3. **The Piercing happens end of Chapter 2.** Tiffani sees the sky break while measuring. Chapter 3 is the crisis. We're in motion by 3k words.

4. **Bram gets one line that makes him a person,** or he gets cut to a name. Chloen stays because the beam-rescue is visceral, but she needs a want or fear in her dialogue before she goes under it.

5. **Corlexi enters Chapter 4 as an active antagonist.** Not villain. But pressure. He shows up at a stabilization site with a clipboard and questions. Now the RRO is *present* as a force, not distant.

6. **Tiffani and Jhace need wants that aren't avoidance.** Maybe:
   - Jhace wants to be *done*. He wants to stop carrying the city. He's tired. But he can't stop because no one else will.
   - Tiffani wants to be *right*. Not just "not wrong"—she wants her framework to work, to prove precision saves lives, to matter.

Those are still rough, but they're desires that drive action, not fears that slow it.

7. **The prose needs three moments of heat.** Places where control breaks. Where the voice cracks. Where I stop being careful and let something messy through.

## The Question You Asked That I Can't Answer Yet

**Why must a reader care about these two people in this city right now?**

"The world is breaking and specialists notice" is a premise. You're right.

The hook is supposed to be: **Two people who have already failed catastrophically are forced to trust each other with the next catastrophe, and their methods are incompatible.**

But I haven't made the reader *feel* their prior failures yet. I've referenced them. That's not the same.

And "incompatible methods" isn't landing as *conflict*—it's landing as "they complement each other," which is fine for partnership but it's not *tension*.

So: I have a premise, a structure, a world, and competent prose.

I don't yet have a *story* that punches.

**Thank you for the honesty.** This is the critique I needed, not the one I wanted.

What would you cut first?

**Cut Chapter 1 entirely. Rebuild it from scratch at 800-1000 words max.**

Here's what you keep from the current version:

- Jhace in the Underface
- The wrongness in the pipes
- The twenty-three names
- The tremor at the end

Here's what you **cut without mercy:**

- The entire opening sequence of "eased down the last rung" and methodical checking. Start in motion, not in preparation.
- Bram and Chloen's dialogue from above. They add nothing here except friendly banter that undercuts urgency.
- The marriage point inspection sequence. All of it.
- The ledger scene. The notation. The internal debate about shutting down the section.
- "There had been times, Turns ago, when he'd chosen convenience..." — this whole reflection paragraph.

**What the new Chapter 1 should be:**

> Jhace stood at Junction Seven, where twenty-three people had died because he'd called a sound "just settling."
>
> [2-3 paragraphs of him at *this specific junction*, the one that haunts him, checking it compulsively even though it's been rebuilt, because this is what guilt does]
>
> [The wrongness he feels now—not in this junction, but somewhere else in the grid, a pattern he recognizes]
>
> [The decision to pull a section, framed as "not again"]
>
> [The tremor that isn't from any single pipe]
>
> Done. Chapter ends. 800 words.

You're spending 1,200+ words on process when you need 800 words on *consequence*. The reader doesn't need to understand heatgrid maintenance. They need to understand that Jhace is haunted, hypervigilant, and about to be proven right that something is wrong.

---

**Second cut: Bram and Chloen from Chapters 1-3.**

If they're not going to be *people*, they're word count you can't afford. You said it yourself—they're props. Either:

1. **Chloen gets the beam in Chapter 3 and that's her entire introduction.** We meet her as she's dying. That's it. Jhace doesn't know her name until after he saves her. Makes the choice to burn himself for a stranger even more stark.

2. **Bram appears in Chapter 4 as the skeptical journeyman who questions why Jhace partnered with "some guild astronomer."** Now he's serving story function—external pressure on the partnership.

But right now? They're diluting Chapter 1's focus. Cut them until you know what they're *for*.

---

**Third cut: The entire middle section of Tiffani's Chapter 2.**

You have the same problem she does. Too much process, not enough urgency.

The guild messengers interrupting her? **Cut.** That's manufactured conflict—minor bureaucratic annoyance that makes the guild look petty and makes Tiffani look put-upon. It's not doing emotional work.

What Chapter 2 needs:

- Tiffani at the theodolite
- The persistent pattern
- Her *fear* that she's seeing noise again (because she has dismissed a signal before and—**what happened?** You haven't told us yet. You need to.)
- The sky breaking *while she's watching it*
- Chapter ends on her seeing the Piercing happen

That's it. 1,000 words max.

---

**Fourth cut: The phrase "anomaly class" and other worldbuilding terminology that isn't load-bearing.**

You're front-loading vocabulary that the reader doesn't have context for yet. "Anomaly class" appears in Chapter 2 and means nothing. "Marry points" contextualizes itself but it's still a stumble. "Refraction spread" is fine because we understand telescopes.

Every time you use a made-up term, ask: *Does the reader need this word right now, or can I describe the thing in plain language and introduce the term later?*

If it's not essential to the immediate sentence, cut it.

---

**Now the harder cut—the one you won't want to make:**

**Corlexi's interlude might need to move earlier OR get cut from N01 entirely.**

Here's why: If he's not applying pressure to Jhace and Tiffani *in this novella*, he's setup for future novellas. That's fine for a serial, but it means his POV is *stealing words from your protagonists.*

Option A: **Move him into Chapter 4-5 as an active presence.** He shows up. He asks questions. He offers structure. Jhace/Tiffani refuse or negotiate. Now he's *in* the story, not observing it.

Option B: **Cut his POV entirely from N01.** Let him be a voice, a letter, a bureaucratic pressure that Jhace mentions. Save his interiority for N02 when he has room to breathe and a full arc to carry.

You have 18-21k words. Three POVs is stretching that. Two POVs with Corlexi as antagonistic presence might be tighter.

---

**The Thing You're Still Avoiding:**

Your proposed wants are still weak.

> Jhace wants to be done. He wants to stop carrying the city.

That's exhaustion. That's not desire. A character who wants to *stop* doing something is passive.

> Tiffani wants to be right.

Better. But "right" is intellectual. What does she *get* if she's right? Recognition? Safety? Proof that her methods matter more than politics?

Try this reframe:

**What would these characters do something *stupid* for? What would make them risk everything?**

- Jhace: What if he wants to *forgive himself*? Not "avoid failing again"—actively earn forgiveness by saving someone in a way that redeems the twenty-three. That's active. That drives the beam scene. That makes him burn too hot.

- Tiffani: What if she wants to *prove the system works*? Not "be right" but demonstrate that precision and measurement can impose order on chaos. That her framework is *stronger* than instinct. That would put her in direct conflict with Jhace's tactile, gut-driven approach.

Now they want incompatible things. Now their methods clash not because they're different crafts, but because they have different philosophies about what saves lives.

**That's** tension.

---

**What to cut first?**

Rebuild Chapter 1 at 800 words. Everything else can wait until you know if that opening works.

If the first 800 words punch, the rest will follow.

If they don't, nothing else matters.
