# N01: The Grip That Holds – Chapters 1–6 & Interlude I

## Chapter 1 – Something Wrong 🔒

**Tide:** 0 | **Phase:** Kindling
**POV:** Jhace Torrins | **Beat:** BEAT01 (Observation / Pattern Recognition) | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses | **Relationship:** Mutual Judgment (Remote)

Jhace eased down the last rung and put both boots on the damp stone ledge. The Underface took the sound of him and gave back the steady thrum of the heatgrid lines running through the dark.

He stood still until his breath slowed. His palm brushed the nearest pipe, not to warm his hand, but to read the vibration through skin and bone. The pipe carried heat from upslope down into the older districts, and it carried the city’s moods with it: a stable thrum when joints were tight, a rasp when a hanger had slipped, a soft chatter when sediment had started to collect.

Jhace moved on a practiced path, lantern hooded low so the light stayed close. He checked the marry points in order, touching metal, then stone, then the iron braces that bit into the wall. Each contact gave him an answer and a memory at the same time—how the joint had sounded last Tide, how the pipe had settled after the last patch, how much slack the bracket allowed before it turned from tolerance into danger.

A voice drifted down from somewhere above, muffled through grates and packed dirt.

“Jhace!” Bram’s tone carried a grin even when the words were blurred by distance. “You still breathing down there, or you finally decided to marry the grid and settle?”

Jhace didn’t look up. “Work first.”

Another voice followed, thinner, eager. Chloen. “Master Kelter says you can tell a cracked pipe by the way the air tastes.”

Jhace paused with his hand on a brace. He could hear them moving topside, boots on planks, the scrape of a tool box. Bram would be leaning over the grate just to make sure he was heard. Chloen would be leaning farther.

“Your master says many things,” Jhace called back. “Keep your weight off the grate.”

“Yes, Master Jhace,” Chloen said, quick and proud.

Jhace kept walking. He didn’t bring apprentices into the Underface. Not because they were useless. Because they were alive. The tunnels took mistakes and kept them.

The route took him through six more junctions without incident. At the seventh—a marry point where three branch lines split from the main—the hum shifted.

Not a crack. Not the high squeal of metal pulling away from stone. The pipe looked clean where his lantern light struck it—no wet line, no rust bloom, no visible bow. But the vibration under his fingertips wasn’t centered. The resonance rode too close to the edge of the metal, as if the load had moved without permission.

He pressed his ear near the brace. The sound came back wrong again, faintly doubled. A join that was starting to talk to itself.

Jhace pulled his ledger from his coat and held it against his knee. The page was already crowded with his tight marks: replacements he’d argued for, patches he’d had to accept, notes on districts where the stone had begun to sweat with stress. He added this junction without hesitation.

The decision came with its own weight. Shutting down a section of line in Kindling meant cold rooms by Quench. Complaints. A foreman with a polished collar telling him the grid had survived worse. Bram would back him in public and tease him in private. Chloen would watch and learn the wrong lesson if the foreman won.

Jhace stared at the brace until his eyes blurred at the edges. There had been times, Turns ago, when he’d chosen convenience. He’d told himself a sound was only a sound, that stone always settled, that the tunnel would be there next Tide. He still carried all twenty-three names from that collapse—every one—even if he never spoke them.

He capped his ink. He ran his thumb along the seam where brace met pipe, feeling for the slightest shift. Nothing moved. The wrongness stayed anyway.

Jhace turned and started back toward the ladder. He would pull the section. He would take the foreman’s anger and keep the grid from deciding for him.

As he walked, the Underface hum settled into its usual pattern for three junctions. Then the entire tunnel gave a tiny tremor that wasn’t from any single pipe. It came through stone and metal together, shallow at first, then gone.

Jhace stopped so fast his lantern swung. He put his palm flat against the wall. The stone felt steady, but his bones remembered the ripple.

It hadn’t come from his junction. It hadn’t come from one district. It had come from everywhere at once.

He listened, breath held tight in his chest. Somewhere above, Bram laughed at something Chloen said, and the sound carried down without knowing it was already late.

---

## Chapter 2 – Glass That Listens 🔒

**Tide:** 0 | **Phase:** Pouring
**POV:** Tiffani Merrow | **Beat:** BEAT01 (Observation / Pattern Recognition) | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses | **Relationship:** Mutual Judgment (Remote)

Tiffani checked the instruments in order. Always in order.

The mounted theodolite first: brass arcs clean, sighting tube clear, clamps tight. She tapped the central spindle with two fingertips and watched the pointer settle on the etched baseline mark in the stone. No drift.

The logbook stand sat to her left, open to a fresh page. The facing sheet held tight script from the last several Tides: phase, cloud bands, refraction spread, color shift, anomaly class. Three entries were circled. She noted them without letting her attention stick.

Prism rack at the platform’s edge: each glass piece resting in its cradle. She lifted one, tilted it into the light, checked for hairlines and ripples. None. The air held a faint trace of kiln-smoke from the workshop below. Everything where it belonged.

Except the numbers said otherwise.

“Kindling, refraction pass,” she murmured. “Baseline confirmation.”

She leaned to the theodolite, eye to the tube. The upper atmosphere sat in neat rings behind the crosshairs. She swept slowly, matching fixed stars to the etched scale, searching for deviation. A quarter subdivision high. Half low.

Once, she would have called that noise and moved on. She did not call it noise now.

Tiffani straightened and reached for the logbook.

Tide 0, Kindling mid-span. Upper rings nominal. Minor fluctuation at star-line Seven-Three. Repeat of last six Tides within one subdivision.

Pattern appears persistent.

She underlined persistent. Persistent meant repeatable. Repeatable meant real. Real meant it could reach a person who depended on glass to be honest.

If it was real, why hadn’t anyone else written it down? Because no one else was assigned to sit on this platform and count these shifts. Because the guild said she was here to test lens clarity for Crown commissions, not watch the sky for trouble.

Bootsteps on the stair broke the thought.

“Merrow?” A man’s voice rose through the hatch. Too brisk to be Pella. “Merrow, you up there?”

“Yes,” she called. “Platform is in use. Come through carefully; mind the stands.”

The hatch pushed open. Two guild messengers edged onto the observatory, seals bright at their throats. Their boots landed too close to a tripod foot.

“The ovens need you,” the older one said. “Master Corlis wants to know when his council panes will be cooled and measured. Promised by Quench, he says. You’re up here staring at clouds.”

“Upper rings,” Tiffani said. Her tone stayed level. “Refraction patterns. The ovens are within schedule. If temperature holds range, the panes move to annealing before Pouring.”

“Then go check them again.” The younger messenger glanced over the instruments with quick, shallow interest. “You were hired for precision glass, not sky work.”

Efficient phrasing. Unhelpful.

“It isn’t a pastime,” Tiffani said. “The pattern has repeated across more than a full set of Tides. The refraction shift is small and compounding. If it continues, it will affect long-range lenses, signal mirrors, tower fires—”

“Has it broken anything yet?” the younger asked.

“No.”

“Then it isn’t our task yet,” the older said. His hands spread in a weary, reasonable gesture. “We work on what has already failed. That is what keeps people warm.”

Corlexi said something close to that once in council: priorities, efficiency, visible damage first. She had gone home afterward and realigned half the workshop’s instruments instead of sleeping.

The messengers watched her, waiting for something simple.

“The ovens are stable,” she said. “Tell Master Corlis I will meet the promised span range. If he wishes to count each breath between now and then, he can climb up and help.”

The younger almost smiled. The older huffed.

“Fine,” he said. “Just remember who pays for this platform.”

They left, boots heavy on the stair.

When the hatch closed, the air on the platform felt easier to pull in.

“Who pays isn’t the same as who needs,” she said under her breath. “Everyone needs the sky to behave.”

She turned back to the theodolite and set the angle to Seven-Three. Outer band. The spot she had circled enough times that her hands could find it without checking the scale.

She leaned in. Sighted. The star should have met the crosshair where it had met it every prior pass.

It missed by more than a subdivision this time. Two full marks and a fraction.

She blinked. Reset. Checked the lens: no dust, no smear. Checked the stand’s feet: all three flat on stone, no grit under the pads. Nothing loose.

Her pulse picked up. Not fear. Not yet. A pattern gaining speed.

“Repeat,” she told herself. “If it repeats under controlled adjustment, it’s real. If not, find your own error first.”

She rotated the scope through a full sweep, then came back to Seven-Three. High again. Same offset.

She wrote with more pressure than before.

Deviation at Seven-Three increased to +2 subdivisions. Instrument base confirmed stable.

Tentative conclusion: source external to instrument.

She underlined tentative twice. A reminder not to run ahead of the data. That habit had a cost.

The hatch creaked again.

“Master Tiffani?” Pella’s head appeared, curls frizzed from kiln heat. A covered bowl sat balanced in one hand, the other tight on the rail.

“You’ve been up here since Kindling opened. I brought stew.”

“That was not necessary,” Tiffani said.

Pella eased onto the platform, placing her feet between stand legs with practiced care. “You say that every Tide. I have gathered evidence. It’s a stable pattern.”

“Dangerous habit,” Tiffani said. “People who notice patterns get tied to impossible mentors.”

“Everyone downstairs says you’re impossible,” Pella said. “I think they lack resolution.” Her gaze dipped to the logbook. “Anything useful?”

“Useful isn’t usually the goal.” Tiffani hesitated. “But yes. The outer refraction ring is drifting. Slowly. Enough to matter for long-distance work if it continues.”

“Matter how?”

“Signal clarity. Star charts. Navigation lenses.” Tiffani flexed ink-stained fingers. “If fixed points slide, errors spread. Ships miss their bearings. Beacon codes blur.”

“Are you going to tell the council?” Pella asked.

“When I have more than two points of drift and some glass humming,” Tiffani said. “For now, I watch. And accept stew.”

“That last part improves all work.”

Pella set the bowl on the lower shelf, careful not to touch any tools. Tiffani took the spoon. The stew was warm, a little too salty, stretched from yesterday’s pot. Heat spread through her chest and eased the tightness under her ribs.

“Thank you,” she said.

“You’re welcome.” Pella waited until Tiffani had swallowed three spoonfuls, then nodded, satisfied. “I’ll come back before Pouring. If you haven’t moved, I reserve the right to complain louder than Master Corlis.”

“You already do,” Tiffani said.

Pella left with a small grin, closing the hatch.

Alone again, the city sound rose: hammer on metal from Underface forges, distant market calls, the deep exhale of the main kiln below as it vented. All ordinary.

Tiffani set the bowl aside and crouched to press her palm to the stone under the theodolite feet. Cool. Steady. No tremor. The wrongness wasn’t in the platform.

“Noise killed Marisse,” she said quietly. “Or my choice to call signal noise.”

That oven wall had whispered for half an Anneal: small heat shifts, fine cracks still buried under glaze. Marisse had asked if it worried her. Tiffani had said no, to keep schedule, to sound certain. The investigation had named it material fatigue. Tiffani had named it the cost of assuming uncertainty meant safety.

She stood and returned her eye to the scope. The deviation had grown again. Seven-Three sat higher. The next star along the ring had shifted too. Together they marked the start of a curve through the upper band. When she traced along that implied path, other stars near it showed slight smear. Brightness stretched by fractions.

Something was pulling at the upper light. Nothing she could see without glass.

Her teeth tasted of metal. She stepped back from the scope. A thin tang sat on her tongue with no source she could name. The hair on her forearms prickled.

Glass around her took on a faint vibration: prisms, spare lens plates, even the ink bottle. The pitch sat just below hearing.

“New condition,” she whispered. “Write. Then move.”

Her hand shook once as she lifted the pen. She braced her wrist on the stand.

Sudden spike. Uniform vibration in all glass on platform. Metallic taste present. Curve of refraction displacement across upper band. Source not visible to eye.

She set the pen down.

A bright line appeared across the upper air, far above any tower. It did not travel straight; it followed the curve her marks had suggested, a narrow white band through blue. Along that band, the sky’s color deepened by a shade.

She watched its motion in fixed steps. White. Fast. Upper band only. No shadow reaching the buildings below. No sound she could separate from the city’s noise.

The vibration in the glass deepened. One prism hopped in its cradle. She caught it with her palm and held it down until the motion eased.

“Enough,” she said aloud. “Seen enough.”

She snapped the logbook shut, tucked it under her arm, checked the ink bottle, then went for the hatch. The metallic taste thickened as she started down the narrow stair.

Above, on the empty platform, the glass still hummed. Over Saint Merro, the sky had changed. She did not have a name for it. She had the marks. She had the taste. She had the decision not to dismiss it.

She did not call it noise.

---

## Chapter 3 – The Sky Breaks 🔒

**Tide:** 0 | **Phase:** Shaping/Striking
**POV:** Jhace Torrins | **Beat:** BEAT03 (Crisis / Inciting Incident) | **Soulpulse:** 2 uses | **Relationship:** Forced Proximity

The wall moved before Jhace did.

Stone shifted under his palm. A deep grind that did not belong. The tunnel’s thrum broke into a jagged pulse. The pipe beside him threw heat sideways hard enough that his fingers slipped.

Then the ground hit.

The Underface lurched. Jhace slammed into the wall. His lantern broke free and swung wild on its hook. Bolts shrieked in their brackets. Somewhere upslope a main line roared as pressure slammed through it, a tearing sound that went through bone.

“Off the ladders!” he shouted. “Now!”

Dust burst from the arch above, a blind cloud. The tunnel narrowed to sound and impact: stone cracking, metal twisting, grit falling in a hard rush that stung his eyes. He tasted old ash and something new under it, a sharp tang on his tongue.

“Chloen!” Bram’s voice came from the ladder shaft. “Move!”

Jhace staggered toward the shaft by habit, one hand on the wall to keep direction. The grid vibration had turned uneven, rolling in waves. A brace ahead of him groaned. He wrapped both arms around it and braced his feet.

The first wave passed. The tunnel did not settle.

Another shudder rolled through. The arch above the shaft gave a hard, hollow crack.

“Off!” Jhace bellowed. “Both of you, off that metal!”

Boots hit stone. A heartbeat later the ladder’s top anchor ripped out of the arch. Iron rungs slammed against the wall, sparks flaring where they struck pipe. The whole frame came down in a rattle of metal, choking the shaft with noise.

Something heavier followed.

A cross-beam tore loose from the upper structure and dropped through the open mouth of the shaft. Jhace saw only a bulked shadow in dust. It hit the tunnel floor with a wet, wrong sound. A short scream cut off.

The dust thinned enough to show Chloen under the beam.

It caught her across one leg above the knee. The rest of her lay clear. Her hands clawed at the floor. She dragged in air and let it out in a hoarse sound that barely reached words.

“Jh—” She bit it off. Breath shook in her chest.

Bram skidded across stone, dropped to his knees at her side. Blood seeped under the beam’s edge, dark against the damp floor.

Jhace reached the timber in three strides. Thick, near his chest-width. One end splintered. The other still buried in ceiling rubble. The weight sat full on her leg.

He planted his feet, set both hands under the beam’s edge, and heaved.

Nothing moved.

Muscle in his back lit with pain. The beam gave no answer. The arch above answered instead—another cracking line, the soft crumble of stone. If he kept forcing, he would bring the rest of the shaft down on all three of them.

“Jhace,” Bram snapped. “Stop. You’ll bring the whole thing.”

Chloen’s fingers dug into the floor. She wasn’t making sound now. Her face had gone gray. Lips pulled back from her teeth.

“How bad,” Jhace said.

“Crushed,” Bram said. No comfort. “We get it off in the next few breaths, she keeps the limb. We fail, she bleeds out or the bone cuts through. Or it all comes down. Good choices.”

Another tremor rolled through. Smaller, but enough to rattle grit from the arch. The beam bounced once and settled heavier.

Chloen bit down on her sleeve. Her eyes found Jhace’s. Fear. And trust he had not earned.

Jhace saw another shaft in his head. A smaller brace. A quiet hiss he had called harmless. Twenty-three names after.

Not again.

“Bram,” he said. “Pull when I tell you. Straight back. Keep her spine clear.”

“Jhace—”

“Now or we lose her.”

Bram moved. He slid his arms under Chloen’s shoulders and upper body and planted his boots wide.

Jhace spat dust, wiped his palms once on his trousers, and put both hands on the beam again. This time he did not try to lift. He let skin settle to rough wood. He felt the crushed fibers where impact had flattened grain. He dropped one knee to stone and lowered his center.

The old grid thrum still ran under everything. A sharper pulse rode on top of it.

He fixed on the weight at one point.

Hold. Long enough.

Heat built in his palms. At first it felt like friction. Then it sharpened and sank through skin into bone. The pressure under his hands changed. Mass turned into resistance. Something pushed back.

Copper-gold light spilled out around his fingers.

Jhace flinched and did not pull away. The light came from his own skin, thin streams running into the wood grain. It spread into the shattered end and into the stone on either side, tracing stress he did not know how to name. The beam vibrated with it, a tight resonance inside the deeper shake of the tunnel.

His arms locked. Shoulder to jaw clenched. Breath left and did not come back clean.

“Now,” he got out.

Bram hauled. The beam did not rise much. It did lighten. Jhace felt the load shift through his hands, up his forearms, into his chest, then out again. Sweat ran into his eyes. His teeth ached. For a few breaths the world narrowed to the hot line where flesh met wood.

Then Chloen slid free.

Her leg came out from under the beam in a rush. It looked wrong—swollen, skin split, blood running—but not as much bone under skin as he expected. Copper-gold traces ran across the bruised flesh for a heartbeat, then sank out of sight.

She screamed once and sagged against Bram’s hold, panting.

“Got you,” Bram said, voice rough. “Got you. Stay with me.”

Jhace let go.

The copper-gold snapped out. The weight threw itself back into the wood. The beam dropped and jolted the stone, then settled, sending a shallow crack through the floor but no full collapse.

Pain hit his hands. Not a cut. A wide burn from fingertips to elbow. His fingers curled without consent. Tremor ran through both arms. Copper-gold lines followed the veins in his forearms for a breath, then faded, leaving only the shake.

“What,” Bram said. He stared at Jhace’s hands. “What did you—”

Another pulse hit.

A deeper shock rolled through the Underface. Not a tremor. A single, enormous surge. The tunnel lights flared and went out. Pipe noise jumped in pitch, then steadied at a harsh new register. A low roar came from everywhere at once—stone and air and the open shaft above.

The air pressed inward for several breaths, then snapped out again.

Jhace hit the floor on one knee. Pain in his hands fought with a heavy pressure in his chest that did not belong to breath.

He looked up.

Through the shaft mouth he saw the sky. Not blue. A slice of white so bright it hurt, cutting across the visible strip. Color at its edges warped. Rooftop outlines bent. Fine dust rained down, glittering faintly copper in the light.

Then rubble slid across the gap and the shaft choked.

He heard screams above. Not one street. Many.

“Out,” Jhace said. The word scraped his throat. “We get out.”

“Chloen can’t climb,” Bram said.

“Then we carry.”

Jhace forced his hands open. Pain flared fresh. Tremor kept going.

“Emergency hatch,” he said. “Two junctions river-side. That run ties to street.”

Bram shifted Chloen’s weight across his shoulders. Her injured leg hung wrong, but when it bumped against his chest she didn’t scream. She hissed and clamped her jaw, eyes unfocused.

Jhace pushed up. The tremor in his hands made the stone edges blur. He set his shoulder to the beam as they passed, testing. It felt stable enough for the breaths it would take to clear.

They ran.

The tunnel had changed shape. Some braces had shifted. Some chalk marks lay under fresh dust. Twice they climbed over fallen pipe. Once a side wall buckled inward, leaving a narrow gap. Jhace shoved at broken stone until it gave them passage, ignoring the way his palms complained.

By the time they reached the hatch, his breath tore in his chest. The metal door had jammed in its frame. He set both hands to it and shoved.

For a breath, a faint copper-gold shimmer traced the hinges. Heat stung up his forearms. The frame groaned and moved. Pain sharpened in his palms.

Bram got a boot into the gap and kicked it wider.

They spilled onto the surface.

The street looked wrong. Stone fractured in long, uneven lines. A building front across the way had peeled open, its face sheared away, rooms exposed. Dust drifted in slow curtains. People ran in broken patterns, some toward the damage, some away.

Above all of it, far higher than any tower, the white line cut the sky.

From here he could see more of it. It did not fall. It passed overhead, impossibly long, light bending along its edges. Fine metallic dust hung in its wake, catching light in a faint copper shimmer that clung to the air.

Jhace had no word for it. He had work.

The building to their right groaned. The lower corner had sunk. Its load had shifted onto a single main support near where street met wall. A knot of people huddled at its base, half in the doorway, half in the open.

“Back!” Jhace shouted. “Away from that wall!”

Some obeyed. Some didn’t. A woman with glass dust in her hair stood near the edge of the crowd, turned upward, staring. A logbook clutched to her chest. Jhace did not know her name.

The support post split with a harsh crack. Stone above it began to fold. Blocks shifted in a chain, strain passing down the line.

If that post went, the whole corner followed. The doorway crushed anyone still within reach. Too many still stood too close.

Jhace’s hands burned. His fingers shook. His body already felt used past what it was meant to do.

Not again.

He ran for the failing post.

“Jhace!” Bram shouted behind him. “You can’t—”

Jhace dropped to his knees at the base of the support, where stone met foundation. Hairline fractures spidered out from the contact point. Dust leaked in slow streams. He pressed both palms flat against the cracked surface. Skin to stone.

Hold. Long enough for them to run.

Heat surged out of him. Copper-gold burst around his fingers, brighter than before. It drove into the fractures, tracing each line through the stone. From his hands it spread upward into the post and sideways into adjoining blocks, chasing stress and filling it with glow.

For a breath he felt every load path. Every place weight had shifted. It was too much. The sense of connection ran from his palms into shoulder and chest and down his spine. Vibration filled his skull.

He dug his fingers into stone. Forearms locked. Breath turned shallow and ragged.

“Move!” Bram bellowed. “Everyone away! Now!”

Feet pounded broken street. The crowd pulled back. The woman with the logbook staggered but did not fall. Her gaze fixed on the glow around his hands, wide and sharp. Glass shards at her feet picked up the copper-gold tint and hummed.

The building’s lean slowed.

The main support still wanted to fail. Cracks pressed against his hands. Stone above tried to fold further. He pushed back with body and heat and will, nothing clean about it.

His vision narrowed at the edges. Sound dulled. Copper-gold brightened until it washed detail away. Only masses and lines remained.

“Jhace,” Bram’s voice came from far off. “That’s enough. They’re clear. Let it go.”

Jhace tried to answer. His jaw would not move.

He held for three more heartbeats.

Then his arms stopped obeying.

The light cut out. The support dropped a small distance, then caught on a new, broken balance. Stone dust cascaded in a fresh curtain. A few blocks fell and shattered in the empty doorway. The building groaned and settled into a shape that did not crush anyone.

Jhace tipped sideways.

He hit the street on his shoulder. His hands twitched against stone. Tremor ran uncontrolled from fingertips to elbow, violent and fine.

The sky tilted. The white line overhead had already passed, leaving haze.

Everything darkened.

—

Sound came back first. Shouts. Weeping. The ring of tools. Someone calling orders. The grid’s low thrum, altered but present.

Then weight: a rough blanket over his chest, stone under his back.

Then his hands. Still shaking.

Jhace opened his eyes.

He lay on broken street, propped against fallen masonry. Dust coated his clothes. Copper-gold smears marked the stone near his hands, fading by the breath. His fingers jerked in small, useless motions.

A shadow crossed his face.

A woman stood over him. Dark hair pulled back in a knot, streaked with fine glass dust and copper shimmer. A logbook clenched to her chest. Her eyes moved over him in quick passes: hands, arms, the stone behind his back, the faint traces of glow still seeping into cracks he had held.

Shock sat on her face. Behind it, calculation.

“What did you do?” she asked.

Her voice held no awe. Only demand.

Jhace swallowed. His throat scraped. His hands would not stop moving.

“I held,” he said. “It should have fallen.”

Their gazes locked for a breath. The city groaned around them. The grid’s hum ran at a new pitch under the noise.

The world had changed. His body knew it. So did her eyes. Neither of them had a name for any of it yet.

---

## Chapter 4 – After the Shudder 🔒

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** Kindling
**POV:** Jhace Torrins | **Beat:** BEAT02 (Trial / Failure / Cost) | **Soulpulse:** 1 use + 1 failed test | **Relationship:** Early Respect

Jhace woke on his back with stone under his shoulders and boiled herbs in his throat.

For a few breaths sound came without shape. Then it sorted: low voices, cloth moving, metal in basins. Somewhere a child crying, then quiet. No collapse roar. Only the roughened hum of the grid under everything.

His hands hurt.

He tried to flex his fingers. Tremor ran from fingertips to elbow in a fine constant shake. Pain followed, a dull burn along bone. Rough cloth bound his palms. The edges rasped.

“Stay down,” Bram said.

Jhace turned his head. He lay on a pallet in a side room off a wider tunnel. Ceiling higher than the work runs, stone blocks set regular. Two rows of makeshift beds on the floor. He recognized the top of a ladder shaft in the next chamber: Bram’s level.

Bram sat on an overturned crate a pace away. Dust streaked his hair. A dark bruise marked his cheek. He held a clay cup in both hands.

“How long,” Jhace said. The words scraped.

“Since late Striking,” Bram said. “We’re into Tide One now. You were out most of that stretch.”

Jhace pushed himself up on an elbow. The room tilted once and steadied.

“Chloen,” he said.

Bram’s mouth twitched.

“Alive,” he said. “Leg’s in bad shape, but not gone. Healer says crushed bone, torn flesh, all the words. Some of the worst breaks were already knitting when they opened it. She’s chewed enough root to sleep through a grid failure.”

Jhace’s gaze tracked past him.

Chloen lay three pallets down, under a cleaner blanket than his. Her leg was splinted from hip to ankle, wrapped in bandage cloth that showed dark stains along the middle. At the edges of the wrappings, a faint copper shimmer clung to the fibers.

Jhace’s fingers twitched. Memory came in pieces: the beam, the heat in his hands, copper-gold streaking over bruised flesh.

“Do not do that,” Bram said quietly. “Do not put her on the same list as the building. She is still breathing because you refused to let that beam have her.”

Jhace looked down at his own hands. The bandages were stained with dust and a pale copper tint. Under the cloth, his fingers jerked in short, useless bursts.

“What do they say happened,” he asked.

“Out there?” Bram jerked his chin toward the tunnel. “Depends who you ask. Some say a corner of Saint Merro learned to stand on its own. Some say the Piercing cracked the rules and the stone is waking. Some say there’s a Forgemaster walking around with molten hands.” His eyes dropped to Jhace’s bandages. “Plenty say an Underface inspector held up a building.”

Jhace’s stomach tightened.

“They’re asking for you,” Bram went on. “The ones who were under that wall. Runners keep sticking their heads in. Want to know when the holder can walk.”

“I held a short span,” Jhace said. The word caught. “It did not fall because loads shifted. They ask for the wrong thing.”

“Maybe,” Bram said. “City’s still cracked. Council’s shouting in the Crown. Guilds are pointing at each other. Stone keeps failing in the middle of it. We have more to do than argue about what they call you.”

Jhace swung his legs off the pallet. Cold floor met his feet. The shift set his arm muscles jumping.

Bram reached out. Jhace shook his head once and pushed himself up without taking the hand. The walls rolled past his eyes and then settled.

“Show me what failed,” he said.

Bram studied him for a few breaths.

“Fine,” Bram said at last. “But if you fall, try not to hit anything that holds weight.”

Jhace loosened the wraps enough to let his fingers move. The cloth pulled at raw skin; pain bit up his forearms. The tremor did not care.

They stepped into the main tunnel.

The level had turned into a passage of constant motion. Runners carrying slates. Guild workers with coil and brace. Healers moving between makeshift nests with baskets of cloth and jars. Blood, hot metal, and root bitter thickened the usual damp-stone smell. The grid hum ran harsher, with edges where mains had been patched quick.

Heads turned as they passed.

“That’s him,” someone said behind them. “The holder from the kiln street.”

Jhace kept his eyes forward.

“Where first,” he asked.

“Nearest break we can reach,” Bram said. “Two marry points took damage. One side support cracked. No spare crew for a full fix yet.”

“Test,” Jhace said. “We see if that thing listens without people under it.”

The first junction had already been wedged and strapped. The main hummed steady. The brace that had sheared away vibrated on its own note. Jhace touched stone beside it.

Stress had moved into the emergency wedges. Imperfect. Stable enough.

“We move,” he said.

The cracked side support sat deeper, in hotter air. Someone had tried to help by jamming broken stone under its base. Gaps showed. The line over it fed a crowded stretch of houses.

“No one on the floors above?” Jhace asked.

“Cleared after the first tremors,” Bram said. “For now.”

The fracture line at the base ran shallow but long. If it failed during the next Tide, the tunnel and part of the building wall would go with it.

Jhace set his palm on the split.

Stone met skin. Cool away from the crack. Warmer where it carried load. He felt the thin gap and its rough edges. His own shake blurred the reading.

Hold, he thought. Hold here. No one is under you yet, but they will be.

Nothing answered.

He pressed harder. Pain flared in his palm, then his wrist. The crack remained a crack. No heat surge. No copper-gold. Only the steady push of weight and his fingers jumping.

“Anything?” Bram asked.

“No,” Jhace said.

“Good,” Bram said. “I was half afraid you were about to say you can fix every hairline in this city by frowning at it.”

Jhace pulled his hand away. His skin tingled. The stone stayed exactly as it was.

“Mark it,” he said. “We reinforce it with tools.”

They turned upslope.

They did not reach the surface before the next break found them.

A runner shot around a bend and nearly collided with them. A boy, sweat bright on his skin, breath tearing.

“You Jhace Torrins?” he gasped. “The one who held the building?”

Jhace’s shoulders went tight.

“Yes,” Bram said. “What’s failing?”

“Shop near the Merro kiln corridor,” the boy said. “Healers using it. Wall cracked. They say if we don’t get you, we lose half the wounded when it drops.”

Jhace started moving before Bram answered.

The kiln street looked worse than it had when they’d first climbed out.

Dust still hung in the air. A whole row of small fronts showed new cracks and missing corners. Someone had turned a narrow shop into a triage room; pallets and tables filled the inside space. The wall facing the street carried a thick fracture from lintel to ground.

People crowded the doorway. Two workers braced the wall with poles. Every shove made fine dust leak faster.

“Back,” Jhace said.

His voice came rough and low. It did not clear the crowd.

Bram stepped up beside him.

“Back,” Bram repeated, louder. “Give him the wall or it will take you with it.”

The crowd shifted, grudging but real. Jhace stepped to the crack.

Closer, it was worse. The roof load had shifted onto two narrow contact points instead of the full length of wall. Inside, ceiling beams sat on that broken line. Several pallets lay under the center span.

Jhace placed both palms flat against the stone.

Cold outer face. Heat from wounded bodies and lamps inside. Heavy pressure from above.

Hold, he thought. They die if you do not hold.

Heat rose under his skin at once. Copper-gold slid across his hands and into the wall, fine streams chasing the fracture up and down. The glow reached the lintel, the base, then spread sideways into the adjoining blocks.

His jaw locked. His arms went rigid.

“Clear the center,” Bram shouted. “Move the pallets to the sides. No arguments.”

Voices and movement answered from inside. Cloth dragged. Someone cried out as they were shifted, then grunted and went quiet.

The wall pressed into his hands, trying to widen the crack. He felt every point where load had narrowed, every place the stone barely held. His pulse beat into the contact, hard enough to make his fingers sting.

His shake worsened. Copper-gold lit the veins along his forearms, bright down to the elbow. Each heartbeat drove pain down those lines.

“Almost,” Bram called. “Hold.”

Jhace held.

The glow ran as far as it could find strain and stopped. It did not heal. It only pinned the worst angles in place.

“Clear,” Bram shouted at last. “No one under the middle. Out.”

Jhace counted three heartbeats more, then let go.

The copper-gold cut off. The wall shifted a short span and caught on a new lean. Dust dribbled. Loose chips fell and bounced on bare floor. The beams above groaned but stayed set.

Jhace’s hands dropped. His fingers clawed and opened in fast, useless grabs.

“Out,” Bram told the street. “No pallets under that span. You two—brace it with poles and proper wedges before next Pouring.”

Jhace stepped back until his shoulders met the opposite wall. Sweat ran cold down his back. The edges of his vision went soft. His hands shook hard enough that his nails clicked together.

“Your arms lit,” Bram said, low. “Vein-lines all the way up. You still with me?”

“For now,” Jhace said. His tongue felt thick.

He looked down. Faint copper-gold lines still traced his forearms, pulsing with the tremor. They faded. The shake did not.

“Well,” Bram said. “We learned the other half.”

“What half,” Jhace asked.

“Whatever this is,” Bram said, “answers when people are about to get crushed. It ignores you when you want the nice clean fixes. And every time it answers, it takes more out of you.”

Jhace stared at his hands until the last trace of color vanished, leaving raw skin and an unsteady twitch.

Someone cleared a throat.

The woman from the street stood near the cracked wall. Glass dust and copper shimmer threaded her hair. A logbook pressed against her chest. Her gaze moved from his hands to the stone.

“Jhace Torrins,” she said.

He narrowed his eyes.

“You know my name,” he said.

“You shouted it yesterday,” she said. “Bram shouted it louder. Runners carried it. May I see where you touched?”

“No,” Jhace said.

She watched him for a breath.

“I need to know what changed in the wall,” she said. “Healers are using buildings you have… helped. If I know where your grip holds strongest, I can tell them where not to put beds.”

That, Jhace could not argue with.

He turned back to the wall. One shaking finger rose to the point where the glow had felt thickest. The tremor made him miss twice. On the third try he hit the right strip.

“Here,” he said. “Then down to here, and across.”

She stepped closer, careful not to brush him. Her hand followed his path, then moved on, checking the stone in small presses.

“Hot band along your line,” she murmured. “Cooler outside it. Stress is concentrated there. Load still carried, but the effective bearing length is small compared to the wall’s full span. It should not stand on that narrow zone. But it does.”

“It stands for now,” Jhace said. “Do not trust it past that.”

“I do not trust anything past that,” she said. “But I prefer numbers to stories. Stories about you are already spreading.”

“Stories are useless,” Jhace said. “Either the brace holds or it does not.”

“And if it only holds when you are here?” she asked.

He had no answer.

Her eyes dropped to his hands. The tremor still ran, fine and constant.

“Is that new?” she asked.

“Old injury,” he said. “Tools.”

She made a quiet sound that was not agreement.

“If you keep lying this badly,” she said, “you make my work harder.”

A voice called from farther up the street.

She glanced that way, then back.

“I am Tiffani Merrow,” she said. “Glasswork and structural optics. I intend to keep people away from the first places your grips will fail. I need your routes and what you feel when you touch stone turned into marks.”

“I have routes,” Jhace said. “I do not have data.”

“You have both,” she said. “You just leave some of it in your bones.”

She moved away, already reading the next damaged fronts. Bram watched her go.

“She will be trouble,” he said.

Jhace did not answer.

A bent nail lay near his boot. He bent and reached for it.

His fingers missed twice. On the third try he caught it between thumb and forefinger. The tremor snapped. The nail jumped from his grip and tapped back onto stone.

He stared at it.

Bram’s gaze slid to his hands.

Jhace straightened. He folded his hands behind his back, hiding the twitch.

“I am fine,” he said.

Bram said nothing.

Across the street, Tiffani looked over. Her eyes dropped once to his hidden hands, then back to his face. She held his gaze for a breath, then turned away.

The grid hummed at its new pitch under their feet. The wall held. Jhace’s grip did not.

---

## Chapter 5 – What Stays in Stone 🔒

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** Pouring
**POV:** Tiffani Merrow | **Beat:** BEAT04 (Quiet Recalibration / Offer / Refusal / Choice) | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses | **Relationship:** Professional Curiosity → Partnership Begins

The copper shimmer did not fade.

Tiffani crouched at the base of the wall Jhace Torrins had held the previous Tide and pressed her palm to the stone. Warmth bled through her skin. Not kiln heat. Not surface warming from the upper light. A band of elevated temperature ran along the fracture where his hands had been. Narrow, about the breadth of two knuckles, concentrated at the points where stress had peaked.

She pulled her hand back and logged the observation.

Tide 1, Pouring phase. Residual heat persists at contact points. Duration: minimum 18 phases post-activation. Pattern suggests localized energy retention in material structure.

Around her, the kiln corridor had turned into organized motion. Guild workers hauled broken stone into stacks. Healers moved between makeshift shelters. Runners carried slates between districts, their boots leaving new dust on old flagstones. The city was learning to function with fractures.

The fractures were not behaving in ways she trusted.

Tiffani stood and moved to the next marked site—a doorframe Jhace had stabilized during the night shift. The copper-gold traces here were fainter but still present, threading through mortar lines in patterns that followed load paths she knew from structural glass analysis. Where his hands had pressed, stress had shifted and settled into new paths. Where he had not touched, the cracks at the edges kept creeping.

She sketched the pattern in her logbook, comparing it to the previous site. The effective bearing zones were consistent: narrow bands of increased stability surrounded by long stretches of unchanged vulnerability. His contact had locked specific stress points in place without changing the broader load distribution.

A shadow fell across her page.

“Master Merrow.”

She looked up.

Inspector Harlen stood with two guild officials, their vests marked with Merro Glass and Crown Engineering seals. Behind them, a younger clerk held a stack of blank slates.

“Inspector,” Tiffani said. She did not rise. “I am in the middle of documentation.”

“So I see.” Harlen gestured at the chalk marks and her open book. “The RRO committee met during Kindling. Your name came up frequently. Master Corlis praised your precision. We need that precision applied to the formal survey.”

“I am surveying,” Tiffani said.

“Independently,” one of the guild officials said. His tone treated the word as a flaw. “The committee is coordinating routes, assigning sectors, establishing reporting protocols. We cannot have scattered efforts.”

Tiffani closed her logbook halfway, thumb holding her place.

“The stone is already telling us where it failed,” she said. “I am recording what it says. If your committee wants my data, I will share it. If you want me to stop collecting in order to fill out forms, the answer is no.”

The clerk shifted his weight. The guild official’s jaw tightened.

“The Crown wants counts,” Harlen said, voice careful. “Compromised supports, households at risk, grid sections under threat. Your mapping is valuable, but we need it inside a larger frame. Join the committee officially. You will have assistants, access to archived plans, priority routing—”

“And constraints,” Tiffani said. “Approved routes. Predetermined priorities. Reports that delay action while they pass through review.”

“Guidelines,” Harlen said. “We must manage panic as much as stone. If every craftsperson follows their own urgency, we lose coherence.”

Tiffani looked past him to the wall she had just measured. The copper shimmer caught morning light, a thin band of something new written across old stone.

“Inspector Harlen,” she said. “How many buildings has your committee stabilized since forming?”

His expression flickered.

“The committee coordinates,” he said. “Others execute.”

“I thought so.” She turned a page and kept sketching. “When your framework produces results, show them to me. Until then, I will keep recording what the stone says before it stops speaking at all.”

The guild official drew breath to argue. Harlen raised a hand.

“Master Merrow,” he said quietly. “The RRO appreciates independence. We also appreciate order. Consider the offer. If you change your mind, the committee meets each Kindling in the Crown administrative hall.”

He turned. The officials followed. The clerk cast one backward glance at her chalk marks before hurrying after them.

Tiffani waited until their footsteps faded, then marked the wall again, this time noting the angle of copper inclusion relative to the street slope.

She was logging the third site when Pella found her.

“Master Tiffani.” Her apprentice picked her way through rubble, a wrapped package in one hand and exasperation on her face. “You said you would be at the workshop during Pouring. Master Corlis has been asking for you. The Crown commission panes need final inspection before they cool past measurement range.”

“The panes are stable,” Tiffani said without looking up. “The temperature gradient was correct when I checked them at Kindling. They will hold tolerance.”

“He wants your signature on the approval slate.”

“Then he can wait two phases.” Tiffani drew another stress line on the page. “Stone that is about to crush people takes priority over glass that is already behaving.”

Pella set the package down—bread and cheese, from the smell—and crouched beside her. Her gaze followed the chalk on the wall, then moved to the marks in the logbook.

“What are you mapping?” Pella asked.

“Evidence,” Tiffani said. “The man who held these walls left traces. Heat signatures. Copper residue. Stress redistribution patterns. If I can quantify where his contact stabilizes and where it does not, I can predict which held sections will fail first.”

Pella’s eyes widened.

“You mean the holder—Jhace Torrins—he is not fixing the stone?” she asked. “He only delays the collapse?”

“Correct.” Tiffani tapped her pen against a marked node. “His contact creates narrow zones of temporary stability. The load paths beyond those bands do not change. The fractures do not seal. He is holding weight in place through direct force that does not match any mechanical principle I can name yet.”

“That sounds dangerous.”

“It is,” Tiffani said. “For him, and for anyone who trusts a wall he has touched without understanding how short-term his intervention is. Which is why I am documenting the pattern before people start depending on it.”

Pella nodded slowly.

“The RRO wants you to stop?” she asked.

“The RRO wants me to join a committee and report through channels.” Tiffani took the bread, tore off a piece, and chewed. “By the time my observations pass through review, three more buildings will have collapsed and people will wonder why no one warned them.”

“So you are refusing.”

“I am choosing urgency over procedure.” Tiffani met her apprentice’s gaze. “There is a difference.”

Pella’s mouth curved.

“Everyone says you are impossible,” she said.

“Everyone lacks precision,” Tiffani said. She swallowed and reached for the cheese. “If you see Master Corlis, tell him I will inspect the panes before Shaping. If he cannot wait that long, tell him to find someone else whose standards he trusts less.”

Pella shook her head but picked up the empty wrappings with care.

“I will tell him you are serving the public safety,” she said. “He can argue with that if he wishes.”

She walked away toward the main kiln corridor.

Tiffani returned to the wall.

The copper band here sat higher than at the first site, closer to the lintel. She pressed her palm against the stone. Warm. The same width. The same residual hum, just below hearing, traveling into her bones.

She wrote: Held zone narrow. Adjacent cracks remain active. Advise healers to avoid placing pallets within one band width of edge.

She was measuring the fourth site when she heard boots on broken stone and looked up to find Jhace Torrins standing three paces away, watching her work.

He looked worse than he had the previous Tide. Dark lines under his eyes. Dust ground into his coat. His hands, wrapped in fresh bandages, trembled faintly at his sides. His gaze stayed sharp, moving from her chalk marks to the wall to her open logbook.

“You are mapping my routes,” he said.

It was not a question.

“Yes,” Tiffani said. She did not apologize. “You are leaving evidence. I am recording it.”

“Why.”

“Because people are using buildings you held,” she said. “If I know where your stabilization is weakest, I can warn them before the temporary hold fails.”

He stared at her for a long moment, then moved closer and crouched beside the wall. His bandaged fingers hovered above the copper line without touching stone.

“This is where I pressed,” he said quietly. “Here, and here.” He indicated two points along the fracture. “The break was trying to widen. I felt it pushing.”

“And you pushed back,” Tiffani said.

“Yes.”

“For how long?”

“Until they cleared the wounded.” His jaw tightened. “Three phases, maybe four. I do not remember cleanly. It hurt.”

Tiffani wrote. Duration correlates with residual heat intensity. Longer contact = stronger thermal signature. Cost to user: pain, tremor, memory blur.

She looked up again.

“How many sites have you held since the Piercing?” she asked.

He hesitated.

“Seven,” he said. “Maybe eight. Some short. Two longer.”

“And the tremor?”

His hands curled into loose fists, hiding the shake.

“Started after the second,” he said. “Worsened after each one. It does not stop now.”

Her pen scratched.

Progressive cost accumulation. No observed recovery between uses. Tremor baseline established. Predicted trajectory: continued deterioration with repeated activation.

“You are cataloging my deterioration,” Jhace said. His tone stayed flat, but there was no heat in it.

“I am cataloging the pattern,” Tiffani said. “Your deterioration is part of that pattern. If you are going to keep doing this—and you are—then someone needs to document what it costs. Otherwise you will spend yourself without knowing how near you are to failure.”

“You think I am close,” he said.

“I think you used this ability eight times in one Tide, and your hands have not been still since,” she said. “That suggests you are approaching a threshold. I do not yet know where it lies. I am trying to find out before you cross it.”

Silence settled around them. The corridor carried hammers, calling voices, the roughened thrum of the grid. Dust hung in narrow beams of light from a cracked upper vent.

“What do you need,” Jhace asked at last.

The question surprised her more than any refusal would have.

“Your routes,” she said. “Every site you held. Contact duration. Your sense of the load before and after. Any place where the glow started weaker, or refused you altogether. And—” She paused. “Leave markers when you plan to intervene next. I need to examine the walls before you touch them, not just after.”

“You want to watch me fail,” he said.

“I want to see the mechanism,” Tiffani said. “Success and failure both produce data. If I only examine stable holds, I will miss half of what matters.”

He looked back at the copper band.

“There is a section in the Underface,” he said slowly. “Grid line under the Dregs. Main support is cracked. I marked it before the Piercing. It held then. It will not much longer. When it goes, that line ruptures. The district loses heat.”

“When are you planning to reinforce it?” she asked.

“Next Kindling,” he said. “If the braces hold that long.”

“If they do not?”

“Then I move sooner,” he said. “With or without you.”

Tiffani considered the fracture in front of her, the heat under her palm, the steady shake in his bandaged hands. She pictured a support under the Dregs carrying more load than it should, trying to fail in the dark where no one from the RRO had drawn a neat grid yet.

“Leave word with Bram when you go,” she said. “I will meet you at the ladder shaft before you drop.”

He frowned.

“You want to be in the Underface,” he said. “With a crack just before it fails.”

“Yes,” she said. “With instruments. With glass. With marks I can trust more than rumors.”

“That is not how most people answer,” he said.

“Most people did not spend three Anneals watching an oven whisper before it tore a wall down,” she said. “Marisse asked if it worried me. I said no. I called signal noise. She died when it let go. I will not call this noise.”

His gaze held on her face for several breaths, as if weighing the line between her words and her motives.

“You take a long path to say you care whether I break,” he said.

“Care is a poor planning term,” she said. “Break is measurable.”

For the first time, his mouth twitched in something that almost reached amusement.

“You walk my routes,” he said. “You see the worst points. Then you tell me where not to stand. That is the work you are asking for.”

“Yes,” she said. “And I tell healers where not to put beds. And I tell Harlen where his neat counts do not match what the stone remembers.”

He pushed himself to his feet. The motion carried a small hitch, a moment where his balance checked before it settled.

“I keep the right to say no,” he said. “If your questions ask for more than the wall does.”

“You do,” Tiffani said. “And I keep the right to tell you when your hands are past their safe load, even if you lie about it.”

“How will you know I am lying,” he asked.

She closed her logbook and slipped it under her arm.

“Glass remembers pitch,” she said. “When you start to crack, I will hear it.”

He huffed a breath that might have been a laugh.

“You are impossible,” he said.

“Everyone says so,” she replied. “Most of them are still standing.”

He turned toward the Underface ladder shaft. She watched the tremor in his hands as he walked, then dropped her gaze back to the copper bands on the wall.

The city’s new hum ran under her feet, in the stone, in the glass, and, now, in the way Jhace Torrins’s fingers refused to rest. One pattern. Many failure points.

She drew a line under the day’s notes and wrote:

Working rule: Held walls remain dangerous. Use his grip as last resort, never first. Begin structured joint survey next Kindling.

She underlined last resort, then shut the book.

For now, the walls stayed up. They would not do so on their own.

---

## Chapter 6 – The Weight That Won’t Yield 🔒

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** Shaping/Striking
**POV:** Jhace Torrins | **Beat:** BEAT03 (Crisis / Front-Loaded Rolling Complications) | **Soulpulse:** 1 use (coordinated arch hold) | **Relationship:** Analytical Engagement (critique → care)

The Underface smelled wrong.

Not the usual damp stone and kiln exhaust. Something sharper. Metallic. The taste sat on Jhace’s tongue and stayed. He had tasted it once before, in the span after the Piercing when dust rained copper and the upper bands of the sky warped.

He stood at the base of the cracked support and ran his palm along the fracture line. The stone vibrated under his touch at a pitch that had not been there two Tides ago. The main pipe overhead hummed higher than the lines to either side. Load that should have spread across three supports had settled into this one failing column.

Behind him, Tiffani measured the junction with a cord marked in hand-widths. Her logbook lay open on a dry ledge, weighted with a chunk of rubble. She had reached the ladder shaft before Kindling ended, carrying glass plates wrapped in cloth and a stub of chalk that put white on everything she touched.

“Fracture depth?” she asked without looking up.

“Three fingers at the widest,” Jhace said. “Shallow at the base. Deeper near the brace.”

“Load concentration?”

He pressed harder and read the push under his palm.

“High,” he said. “The other supports are carrying less than they should. This one is taking the extra.”

She made a mark. Crossed to the support and placed her own hand beside his, close without touching. Her fingers hovered a breath from the surface.

“Residual warmth,” she said. “Faint. You touched this before.”

“Last Anneal,” Jhace said. “Before the Piercing. Hairline only. I marked it to watch.”

“It is not for watching now,” she said.

“No.”

She pulled her hand back and sketched the fracture pattern. Pen strokes quick, exact.

“The crack runs up toward the pipe anchor and down into the foundation,” she said. “If it fails at the anchor first, the pipe torques. Joints split. Heat vents into the tunnel and into the blocks above.”

“I know,” Jhace said.

“If it fails at the base, the support drops,” she said. “The adjacent columns do not have slack to take the full load. Junction folds. Three mains rupture.”

“I know,” he said again.

His hands curled and opened. The tremor ran through the motion even when he tried to hold still.

Tiffani studied his fingers, then his face.

“How many phases since you last used the grip?” she asked.

“Eighteen. Maybe twenty,” he said.

“And the tremor?”

“Constant.”

She wrote. Set down the pen. Unwrapped one of the glass plates from her satchel. The plate was thin, etched with a grid of fine lines.

“What is that?” Jhace asked.

“Stress plate,” Tiffani said. “If I place it against the stone while you work, the grid will distort where stress concentrates. It will show me where you are applying force and where the stone answers.”

“You want to measure me,” he said.

“I want to see the mechanism,” she said. “If I know where your contact is most effective, I can tell you where to press next time. Less waste. Less cost.”

“You cannot reduce this cost,” Jhace said. “I have tried.”

“You have tried alone,” she said. She held his gaze. “Let me help.”

Above them, the pipe gave a low groan. Grit sifted from the brace and pattered onto the floor.

Jhace looked at the fracture. At the load coming down from the Dregs. At the blocks overhead where people slept through a hum they did not understand.

Twenty-three names sat behind his teeth. He was not taking more.

“Fine,” he said. “But if the column goes, you run. Do not stay to measure a fall.”

“Agreed,” Tiffani said.

She pressed the stress plate against the stone beside the fracture and held it steady with one hand.

“Proceed when ready,” she said.

Jhace knelt and set his palms flat over the widest stretch of crack.

Stone met skin. Cold. Rough. Full of moving weight.

Hold, he thought. Long enough.

Heat built under his hands. At first it felt like friction. Then it sharpened and sank into bone. Copper-gold light spilled out from under his fingers and drove into the fracture. The glow traced the crack upward to the brace and downward into the foundation, following stress lines he could feel even if he did not know their names.

His arms locked. His jaw clenched. The tremor in his hands jumped into a hard shake that climbed his forearms.

Vein-lines lit under his skin, bright copper from wrist to elbow.

The stone pushed back.

Not as a shove he could see. Deeper than that. The load wanted to widen the crack, to move where it had been held too long. He held it in place with heat and something that felt like his whole chest pressing through his hands.

“Grid distortion visible,” Tiffani said. Her voice stayed level. “Stress concentrated at three nodes. Primary contact is strong. North side shows less distortion. You are giving too much there for too little gain.”

Jhace could not spare breath to answer. Sweat ran into his eyes. Pressure beat behind them in time with his heart.

“Shift your left hand,” Tiffani said. “Two hand-widths toward the brace. Put more into the upper node.”

He tried.

His left hand stayed where it was, locked by strain. Copper-gold flared brighter. Pain shot along the arm in a tight, hot line.

“Jhace,” Tiffani said. Her tone changed, sharp and close. “Your vein-lines are too bright. Pull back before—”

The pipe overhead shrieked.

Not the support. A joint three junction spans upslope. Pressure tore it open. Superheated air vented in a hard white jet that filled the tunnel with scalding mist.

Jhace’s focus broke. The glow around his fingers stuttered. The support shifted a fraction under his hands.

“No,” he forced out.

He drove his palms harder into the stone. Copper-gold surged back, brighter than before. His vision narrowed to contact points and light. Heat, pressure, weight. Everything else dropped away.

“Secondary rupture,” Tiffani shouted. Her voice came through the roar of venting air. “The brace is pulling free. If it goes, the pipe drops onto the support. Then we lose all of it.”

Jhace felt it in the stone. The brace trying to tear out of its seat. The pipe starting to sag. Load on the column doubling by the heartbeat.

He could not hold both. Support or pipe. Choose, and the other failed.

“Where,” he shouted. His throat scraped.

“Not you,” Tiffani said. “You keep the column. I mark the brace.”

She left the stress plate in place and ran into the mist. She pressed her palm to the wall near the anchor, traced load with her fingers, then slashed an X in chalk on the stone.

“Here,” she called. “Maximum strain at this point. If someone can lock this, the brace holds. The pipe stays.”

Jhace stared at the mark. It sat three paces away. He could not reach it without letting go.

If he let go, the column failed. If he held the column, the brace failed. Either way put people under falling stone.

His hands shook so hard the copper glow trembled with the motion.

“I cannot reach both,” he said.

“Then you hold the support,” Tiffani said. “I brace the joint.”

“You cannot—”

She was already moving. She snatched a length of broken pipe from the rubble, wedged it under the sagging brace, and leaned into it with her weight. Metal screamed. The wedge bit. The brace held, for the moment.

“This buys you thirty heartbeats,” she shouted. “Make them count.”

Jhace dragged his attention back to the crack.

The fracture had widened while his mind was split. Fine lines at the edges had opened. The stone was trying to fail faster now.

Hold.

The copper-gold brightened until it washed detail away. It drove deeper into the break, into joints above and foundation below. His veins burned. His breath shortened. The hum in his ears turned to a solid, grinding tone.

Ten heartbeats.

The support slowed its shift.

Twenty.

The crack stopped moving. The glow traced each stress line and pinned it in place. The column still carried too much, but it held.

Thirty.

The brace groaned. Tiffani’s wedge bent. She shifted her stance and put her shoulder into the metal, jaw clenched, boots sliding on wet stone.

“Jhace,” she said. The strain pulled at her words. “I am losing it.”

Forty.

The vented heat thinned. The roar eased down to a rough hiss. Pressure in the line dropped.

Fifty.

“Now,” Tiffani said. “Let go now or it will take more than your hands.”

Jhace tore his palms away from the stone.

The copper-gold cut out. The support settled in a slow, grinding drop and caught on a new balance. The brace settled. The pipe held its line.

Jhace’s legs went out from under him. He hit the floor on his side. His hands clawed at stone in jerks he could not control. Tremor ran from fingertips to shoulder in hard, fast waves. Pain flooded in behind it, wide and deep.

Tiffani let the wedge go and stumbled clear. She crossed to him and dropped to a crouch.

“Vein-lines are still bright,” she said. Her breath came fast. “They are not fading. How long do they usually stay?”

“A few breaths,” Jhace managed. “Not this long.”

Copper-gold traced his veins, pulsing with his heart, bright enough to throw a faint light on her face.

She stripped off her outer tunic and wrapped it around his hands, covering the worst of the glow. The cloth did nothing to ease the heat.

“You held too long,” she said. “Thirty heartbeats would have been enough once the pressure started to fall. You kept pushing after I told you to shift.”

“Had to,” he said. His tongue felt heavy. “Brace was failing.”

“The brace was my task,” Tiffani said. “Your task was the support. You tried to do both and nearly burned yourself through.”

Jhace let a short sound out of his chest. It might have been a laugh. It hurt.

“That is how it has been,” he said.

“Not anymore,” she said.

Her hands shook too, a small, fine tremor he would have missed if he had not been used to reading small motions.

“You have a partner now,” Tiffani said. “I mark the stress points and brace what I can reach. You put your hands where I tell you and nowhere else. We divide the load or we both end under it.”

Jhace looked at her. At chalk dust on her fingers. At steam still clinging to her hair. At the way she had gone toward the failing brace without being asked.

“You could have gone for the ladder,” he said.

“So could you,” she said. “Neither of us did. That tells me enough.”

The word she had used sat between them.

Partner.

His hands started to ease. The hard convulsions broke into finer shakes. The copper lines under his skin dimmed by slow degrees. They did not vanish fully, but they sank until they were only faint traces, visible if you knew to look.

“Can you sit?” she asked.

He pushed himself up with his elbows. His arms shook but held.

“Barely,” he said.

“Barely is more than nothing,” she said.

She fetched the stress plate from where it leaned and checked the etched grid. Fine distortions arced across the lines, clustered near where his hands had been.

“What are you doing?” Jhace asked.

“Recording,” she said. “Use duration: about fifty heartbeats. Cost: severe tremor, extended vein-line brightness, near collapse. Effective concentration at primary node. Waste at secondary contact where you refused to move your hand.”

He grunted.

“Next time,” Tiffani went on, “you listen when I tell you where to press.”

“Next time,” he echoed.

“There will be a next time,” she said. “The city is still cracking. You will try to hold what you can. I will try to make that hold less lethal to you.”

She said it in the same voice she used for angle reports and kiln ranges. Under it, something else sat.

“You are measuring me to keep me alive,” he said.

“I am measuring you because the pattern is dangerous and no one else is writing it down,” she said. “And because—” She stopped. Started again. “Because I watched Marisse die when I called a signal noise. I will not do that again. Not with you.”

Jhace dragged one knee under him. The motion hurt. He did it anyway. Tiffani lifted her hands a little as if to catch him, then let them drop when he steadied.

“I can get up,” he said.

“I know,” she said. “You do not have to do it alone every span.”

He pushed the rest of the way to his feet. The tunnel swam once at the edges and settled.

“Partnership,” he said.

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

“You tell me where to press,” he said. “I listen.”

“Yes,” she said.

“And when I do not?”

Her mouth twitched.

“Then I hit you with my logbook,” she said.

A rough sound left his chest. This time it was closer to a laugh.

“Done,” he said.

The pipe overhead hummed at its altered pitch. The support held on its new lines. Far above, people moved through their Tides, unaware of how close they had come.

Tiffani unwrapped his hands from her tunic and rewound his own bandages with quick, all-business motions. The tremor still sat in his fingers, smaller now, but constant.

“We go up,” she said. “You sit. You drink something that does not taste of metal. I pull what I can from the stress plate. Next Tide we find the next failure before it reaches this point.”

“Next Tide,” Jhace said.

They walked toward the ladder shaft. Copper traces glowed faintly on the support where his hands had been, a narrow band of light sunk into stone. The weight above did not care who had held it. The cost stayed in his bones. The choice to pay it no longer sat in his hands alone.

---

## Interlude I – Order in Fragments 🔒

**Tide:** 1 | **Phase:** Quench
**POV:** Corlexi

Corlexi woke from a dream of systems that never broke.

Not buildings. Not even structures in the Saint Merro sense. Patterns. Vast, interlocking arrangements that held themselves in balance without strain, without cost, without the slow decay that wore down stone and flesh alike.

He opened his eyes.

The ceiling of his workspace came into focus: beams, plaster, the faint water-stain from two Castings ago that no repair had fully erased. Real. Imperfect. Failing by degrees.

The dream dissolved. Good. Dreams were noise. The RRO did not run on dreams.

He straightened in his chair and pulled the slate he had been reviewing before exhaustion took him. Neat columns covered the surface: casualties by district, structural assessments by severity, resource allocations by priority tier. Order pressed onto chaos through measurement and method.

The numbers refused to settle.

Three different guild inspectors had filed reports on the same failing arch in the Pouring district. Each gave a different severity rating. One marked it stable with monitoring. One demanded immediate evacuation. The third—Jhace Torrins, the grip-holder everyone whispered about—had written: held, temporary, reassess within two Tides.

Corlexi tapped his pen against the slate.

Held.

The arch either carried its load or it did not. There was no third state in structural mechanics. Yet the arch still stood. Witnesses confirmed it should have collapsed during the first aftershock. Torrins had been there. Copper-gold light had been seen. The structure remained upright.

Anomaly.

Corlexi wrote the word at the top of a fresh slate and underlined it twice. Anomalies required categorization. Once categorized, they could be controlled. Once controlled, they could be integrated into the system.

That was the work. That was always the work.

A knock at the door interrupted.

“Enter,” he said.

Inspector Harlen stepped through, dust still streaking his vest from the day’s survey. He carried a folder thick with reports.

“Factor Corlexi,” Harlen said. He did not sit. “The committee requests guidance on registration protocols. We have confirmed twelve individuals with manifestations similar to Torrins. Varying control. Two injured themselves trying to repeat an activation.”

“Injuries?” Corlexi asked.

“One fractured hand from poorly braced contact. One collapse from tremor and exhaustion. Both recovered.”

Corlexi noted it down.

Cost profile: physical strain, tremor, exhaustion. Risk of self-harm during uncontrolled use.

“Recommendation?” he asked.

“Mandatory registration,” Harlen said. “Supervised training under RRO oversight. Deployment only through official channels for priority structures.”

Corlexi set the pen aside and focused fully on him.

“You want to control them,” he said.

“I want to prevent further casualties,” Harlen said. “These manifestations are dangerous. Torrins nearly dropped after holding that junction arch. If he had failed mid-activation, the structure falls. Everyone beneath it dies. We cannot allow unregulated intervention.”

The logic was clean. It matched the principles behind every RRO decision: minimize risk, maximize coordination, attach accountability.

Something in Corlexi resisted.

Not the logic. The frame.

“Registration implies ownership,” he said. “These individuals are not spare braces to move where we wish. They are variables responding to immediate threat. If we mandate oversight, we introduce delay. Delay costs lives.”

Harlen’s jaw clenched.

“Uncontrolled intervention also costs lives,” he said. “We need structure.”

Structure.

The word resonated in Corlex
The file is too long and its contents have been truncated.
