Some say the Piercing cracked the rules and the stone is waking. Some say there's a Forgemaster walking around with molten hands." His eyes dropped to Jhace's bandages. "Plenty say an Underface inspector held up a building."

Jhace's stomach tightened.

"They're asking for you," Bram went on. "The ones who were under that wall. Runners keep sticking their heads in. Want to know when the holder can walk."

"I held a short span," Jhace said. The word caught. "It did not fall because loads shifted. They ask for the wrong thing."

"Maybe," Bram said. "City's still cracked. Council's shouting in the Crown. Guilds are pointing at each other. Stone keeps failing in the middle of it. We have more to do than argue about what they call you."

Jhace swung his legs off the pallet. Cold floor met his feet. The shift set his arm muscles jumping.

Bram reached out. Jhace shook his head once and pushed himself up without taking the hand. The walls rolled past his eyes and then settled.

"Show me what failed," he said.

Bram studied him for a few breaths.

"Fine," Bram said at last. "But if you fall, try not to hit anything that holds weight."

Jhace loosened the wraps enough to let his fingers move. The cloth pulled at raw skin; pain bit up his forearms. The tremor did not care.

They stepped into the main tunnel.

The level had turned into a passage of constant motion. Runners carrying slates. Guild workers with coil and brace. Healers moving between makeshift nests with baskets of cloth and jars. Blood, hot metal, and root bitter thickened the usual damp-stone smell. The grid hum ran harsher, with edges where mains had been patched quick.

Heads turned as they passed.

"That's him," someone said behind them. "The holder from the kiln street."

Jhace kept his eyes forward.

"Where first," he asked.

"Nearest break we can reach," Bram said. "Two marry points took damage. One side support cracked. No spare crew for a full fix yet."

"Test," Jhace said. "We see if that thing listens without people under it."

The first junction had already been wedged and strapped. The main hummed steady. The brace that had sheared away vibrated on its own note. Jhace touched stone beside it.

Stress had moved into the emergency wedges. Imperfect. Stable enough.

"We move," he said.

The cracked side support sat deeper, in hotter air. Someone had tried to help by jamming broken stone under its base. Gaps showed. The line over it fed a crowded stretch of houses.

"No one on the floors above?" Jhace asked.

"Cleared after the first tremors," Bram said. "For now."

The fracture line at the base ran shallow but long. If it failed during the next Tide, the tunnel and part of the building wall would go with it.

Jhace set his palm on the split.

Stone met skin. Cool away from the crack. Warmer where it carried load. He felt the thin gap and its rough edges. His own shake blurred the reading.

Hold, he thought. Hold here. No one is under you yet, but they will be.

Nothing answered.

He pressed harder. Pain flared in his palm, then his wrist. The crack remained a crack. No heat surge. No copper-gold. Only the steady push of weight and his fingers jumping.

"Anything?" Bram asked.

"No," Jhace said.

"Good," Bram said. "I was half afraid you were about to say you can fix every hairline in this city by frowning at it."

Jhace pulled his hand away. His skin tingled. The stone stayed exactly as it was.

"Mark it," he said. "We reinforce it with tools."

They turned upslope.

They did not reach the surface before the next break found them.

A runner shot around a bend and nearly collided with them. A boy, sweat bright on his skin, breath tearing.

"You Jhace Torrins?" he gasped. "The one who held the building?"

Jhace's shoulders went tight.

"Yes," Bram said. "What's failing?"

"Shop near the Merro kiln corridor," the boy said. "Healers using it. Wall cracked. They say if we don't get you, we lose half the wounded when it drops."

Jhace started moving before Bram answered.

The kiln street looked worse than it had when they'd first climbed out.

Dust still hung in the air. A whole row of small fronts showed new cracks and missing corners. Someone had turned a narrow shop into a triage room; pallets and tables filled the inside space. The wall facing the street carried a thick fracture from lintel to ground.

People crowded the doorway. Two workers braced the wall with poles. Every shove made fine dust leak faster.

"Back," Jhace said.

His voice came rough and low. It did not clear the crowd.

Bram stepped up beside him.

"Back," Bram repeated, louder. "Give him the wall or it will take you with it."

The crowd shifted, grudging but real. Jhace stepped to the crack.

Closer, it was worse. The roof load had shifted onto two narrow contact points instead of the full length of wall. Inside, ceiling beams sat on that broken line. Several pallets lay under the center span.

Jhace placed both palms flat against the stone.

Cold outer face. Heat from wounded bodies and lamps inside. Heavy pressure from above.

Hold, he thought. They die if you do not hold.

Heat rose under his skin at once. Copper-gold slid across his hands and into the wall, fine streams chasing the fracture up and down. The glow reached the lintel, the base, then spread sideways into the adjoining blocks.

His jaw locked. His arms went rigid.

"Clear the center," Bram shouted. "Move the pallets to the sides. No arguments."

Voices and movement answered from inside. Cloth dragged. Someone cried out as they were shifted, then grunted and went quiet.

The wall pressed into his hands, the crack widening under load. He felt every point where load had narrowed, every place the stone barely held. His pulse beat into the contact, hard enough to make his fingers sting.

His shake worsened. Copper-gold lit the veins along his forearms, bright down to the elbow. Each heartbeat drove pain down those lines.

"Almost," Bram called. "Hold."

Jhace held.

The glow ran as far as it could find strain and stopped. It did not heal. It only pinned the worst angles in place.

"Clear," Bram shouted at last. "No one under the middle. Out."

Jhace counted three heartbeats more, then let go.

The copper-gold cut off. The wall shifted a short span and caught on a new lean. Dust dribbled. Loose chips fell and bounced on bare floor. The beams above groaned but stayed set.

Jhace's hands dropped. His fingers clawed and opened in fast, useless grabs.

"Out," Bram told the street. "No pallets under that span. You two—brace it with poles and proper wedges before next Pouring."

Jhace stepped back until his shoulders met the opposite wall. Sweat ran cold down his back. The edges of his vision went soft. His hands shook hard enough that his nails clicked together.

"Your arms lit," Bram said, low. "Vein-lines all the way up. You still with me?"

"For now," Jhace said. His tongue felt thick.

He looked down. Faint copper-gold lines still traced his forearms, pulsing with the tremor. They faded. The shake did not.

"Well," Bram said. "We learned the other half."

"What half," Jhace asked.

"Whatever this is," Bram said, "answers when people are about to get crushed. It ignores you when you want the nice clean fixes. And every time it answers, it takes more out of you."

Jhace stared at his hands until the last trace of color vanished, leaving raw skin and an unsteady twitch.

Someone cleared a throat.

The woman from the street stood near the cracked wall. Glass dust and copper shimmer threaded her hair. A logbook pressed against her chest. Her gaze moved from his hands to the stone.

"Jhace Torrins," she said.

He narrowed his eyes.

"You know my name," he said.

"You shouted it yesterday," she said. "Bram shouted it louder. Runners carried it. May I see where you touched?"

"No," Jhace said.

She watched him for a breath.

"I need to know what changed in the wall," she said. "Healers are using buildings you have… helped. If I know where your grip holds strongest, I can tell them where not to put beds."

That, Jhace could not argue with.

He turned back to the wall. One shaking finger rose to the point where the glow had felt thickest. The tremor made him miss twice. On the third try he hit the right strip.

"Here," he said. "Then down to here, and across."

She stepped closer, careful not to brush him. Her hand followed his path, then moved on, checking the stone in small presses.

"Hot band along your line," she murmured. "Cooler outside it. Stress is concentrated there. Load still carried, but the effective bearing length is small compared to the wall's full span. It should not stand on that narrow zone. But it does."

"It stands for now," Jhace said. "Do not trust it past that."

"I do not trust anything past that," she said. "But I prefer numbers to stories. Stories about you are already spreading."

"Stories are useless," Jhace said. "Either the brace holds or it does not."

"And if it only holds when you are here?" she asked.

He had no answer.

Her eyes dropped to his hands. The tremor still ran, fine and constant.

"Is that new?" she asked.

"Old injury," he said. "Tools."

She made a quiet sound that was not agreement.

"If you keep lying this badly," she said, "you make my work harder."

A voice called from farther up the street.

She glanced that way, then back.

"I am Tiffani Merrow," she said. "Glasswork and structural optics. I intend to keep people away from the first places your grips will fail. I need your routes and what you feel when you touch stone turned into marks."

"I have routes," Jhace said. "I do not have data."

"You have both," she said. "You just leave some of it in your bones."

She moved away, already reading the next damaged fronts. Bram watched her go.

"She will be trouble," he said.

Jhace did not answer.

A bent nail lay near his boot. He bent and reached for it.

His fingers missed twice. On the third try he caught it between thumb and forefinger. The tremor snapped. The nail jumped from his grip and tapped back onto stone.

He stared at it.

Bram's gaze slid to his hands.

Jhace straightened. He folded his hands behind his back, hiding the twitch.

"I am fine," he said.

Bram said nothing.

Across the street, Tiffani looked over. Her eyes dropped once to his hidden hands, then back to his face. She held his gaze for a breath, then turned away.

The grid hummed at its new pitch under their feet. The wall held. Jhace's grip did not.

***

## Chapter 5 – What Stays in Stone

The copper shimmer did not fade.

Tiffani crouched at the base of the wall Jhace Torrins had held the previous Tide and pressed her palm to the stone. Warmth bled through her skin. Not kiln heat. Not surface warming from the upper light. A band of elevated temperature ran along the fracture where his hands had been. Narrow, about the breadth of two knuckles, concentrated at the points where stress had peaked.

She pulled her hand back and logged the observation.

Tide 1, Pouring phase. Residual heat persists at contact points. Duration: minimum 18 phases post-activation. Pattern suggests localized energy retention in material structure.

Around her, the kiln corridor had turned into organized motion. Guild workers hauled broken stone into stacks. Healers moved between makeshift shelters. Runners carried slates between districts, their boots leaving new dust on old flagstones. The city was learning to function with fractures.

The fractures were not behaving in ways she trusted.

Tiffani stood and moved to the next marked site—a doorframe Jhace had stabilized during the night shift. The copper-gold traces here were fainter but still present, threading through mortar lines in patterns that followed load paths she knew from structural glass analysis. Where his hands had pressed, stress had shifted and settled into new paths. Where he had not touched, the cracks at the edges kept creeping.

She sketched the pattern in her logbook, comparing it to the previous site. The effective bearing zones were consistent: narrow bands of increased stability surrounded by long stretches of unchanged vulnerability. His contact had locked specific stress points in place without changing the broader load distribution.

A shadow fell across her page.

"Master Merrow."

She looked up.

Inspector Harlen stood with two guild officials, their vests marked with Merro Glass and Crown Engineering seals. Behind them, a younger clerk held a stack of blank slates.

"Inspector," Tiffani said. She did not rise. "I am in the middle of documentation."

"So I see." Harlen gestured at the chalk marks and her open book. "The RRO committee met during Kindling. Your name came up frequently. Master Corlis praised your precision. We need that precision applied to the formal survey."

"I am surveying," Tiffani said.

"Independently," one of the guild officials said. His tone treated the word as a flaw. "The committee is coordinating routes, assigning sectors, establishing reporting protocols. We cannot have scattered efforts."

Tiffani closed her logbook halfway, thumb holding her place.

"The fractures show where the stone failed," she said. "I am recording what they show. If your committee wants my data, I will share it. If you want me to stop collecting in order to fill out forms, the answer is no."

The clerk shifted his weight. The guild official's jaw tightened.

"The Crown wants counts," Harlen said, voice careful. "Compromised supports, households at risk, grid sections under threat. Your mapping is valuable, but we need it inside a larger frame. Join the committee officially. You will have assistants, access to archived plans, priority routing—"

"And constraints," Tiffani said. "Approved routes. Predetermined priorities. Reports that delay action while they pass through review."

"Guidelines," Harlen said. "We must manage panic as much as stone. If every craftsperson follows their own urgency, we lose coherence."

Tiffani looked past him to the wall she had just measured. The copper shimmer caught morning light, a thin band of something new written across old stone.

"Inspector Harlen," she said. "How many buildings has your committee stabilized since forming?"

His expression flickered.

"The committee coordinates," he said. "Others execute."

"I thought so." She turned a page and kept sketching. "When your framework produces results, show them to me. Until then, I will keep recording what the fracture patterns preserve before they stop showing anything at all."

The guild official drew breath to argue. Harlen raised a hand.

"Master Merrow," he said quietly. "The RRO appreciates independence. We also appreciate order. Consider the offer. If you change your mind, the committee meets each Kindling in the Crown administrative hall."

He turned. The officials followed. The clerk cast one backward glance at her chalk marks before hurrying after them.

Tiffani waited until their footsteps faded, then marked the wall again, this time noting the angle of copper inclusion relative to the street slope.

She was logging the third site when Pella found her.

"Master Tiffani." Her apprentice picked her way through rubble, a wrapped package in one hand and exasperation on her face. "You said you would be at the workshop during Pouring. Master Corlis has been asking for you. The Crown commission panes need final inspection before they cool past measurement range."

"The panes are stable," Tiffani said without looking up. "The temperature gradient was correct when I checked them at Kindling. They will hold tolerance."

"He wants your signature on the approval slate."

"Then he can wait two phases." Tiffani drew another stress line on the page. "Stone that is about to crush people takes priority over glass that is already behaving."

Pella set the package down—bread and cheese, from the smell—and crouched beside her. Her gaze followed the chalk on the wall, then moved to the marks in the logbook.

"What are you mapping?" Pella asked.

"Evidence," Tiffani said. "The man who held these walls left traces. Heat signatures. Copper residue. Stress redistribution patterns. If I can quantify where his contact stabilizes and where it does not, I can predict which held sections will fail first."

Pella's eyes widened.

"You mean the holder—Jhace Torrins—he is not fixing the stone?" she asked. "He only delays the collapse?"

"Correct." Tiffani tapped her pen against a marked node. "His contact creates narrow zones of temporary stability. The load paths beyond those bands do not change. The fractures do not seal. He is holding weight in place through direct force that does not match any mechanical principle I can name yet."

"That sounds dangerous."

"It is," Tiffani said. "For him, and for anyone who trusts a wall he has touched without understanding how short-term his intervention is. Which is why I am documenting the pattern before people start depending on it."

Pella nodded slowly.

"The RRO wants you to stop?" she asked.

"The RRO wants me to join a committee and report through channels." Tiffani took the bread, tore off a piece, and chewed. "By the time my observations pass through review, three more buildings will have collapsed and people will wonder why no one warned them."

"So you are refusing."

"I am choosing urgency over procedure." Tiffani met her apprentice's gaze. "There is a difference."

Pella's mouth curved.

"Everyone says you are impossible," she said.

"Everyone lacks precision," Tiffani said. She swallowed and reached for the cheese. "If you see Master Corlis, tell him I will inspect the panes before Shaping. If he cannot wait that long, tell him to find someone else whose standards he trusts less."

Pella shook her head but picked up the empty wrappings with care.

"I will tell him you are serving the public safety," she said. "He can argue with that if he wishes."

She walked away toward the main kiln corridor.

Tiffani returned to the wall.

The copper band here sat higher than at the first site, closer to the lintel. She pressed her palm against the stone. Warm. The same width. The same residual hum, just below hearing, traveling into her bones.

She wrote: Held zone narrow. Adjacent cracks remain active. Advise healers to avoid placing pallets within one band width of edge.

She was measuring the fourth site when she heard boots on broken stone and looked up to find Jhace Torrins standing three paces away, watching her work.

He looked worse than he had the previous Tide. Dark lines under his eyes. Dust ground into his coat. His hands, wrapped in fresh bandages, trembled faintly at his sides. His gaze stayed sharp, moving from her chalk marks to the wall to her open logbook.

"You are mapping my routes," he said.

It was not a question.

"Yes," Tiffani said. She did not apologize. "You are leaving evidence. I am recording it."

"Why."

"Because people are using buildings you held," she said. "If I know where your stabilization is weakest, I can warn them before the temporary hold fails."

He stared at her for a long moment, then moved closer and crouched beside the wall. His bandaged fingers hovered above the copper line without touching stone.

"This is where I pressed," he said quietly. "Here, and here." He indicated two points along the fracture. "The break was trying to widen. I felt it pushing."

"And you pushed back," Tiffani said.

"Yes."

"For how long?"

"Until they cleared the wounded." His jaw tightened. "Three phases, maybe four. I do not remember cleanly. It hurt."

Tiffani wrote. Duration correlates with residual heat intensity. Longer contact = stronger thermal signature. Cost to user: pain, tremor, memory blur.

She looked up again.

"How many sites have you held since the Piercing?" she asked.

He hesitated.

"Seven," he said. "Maybe eight. Some short. Two longer."

"And the tremor?"

His hands curled into loose fists, hiding the shake.

"Started after the second," he said. "Worsened after each one. It does not stop now."

Her pen scratched.

Progressive cost accumulation. No observed recovery between uses. Tremor baseline established. Predicted trajectory: continued deterioration with repeated activation.

"You are cataloging my deterioration," Jhace said. His tone stayed flat, but there was no heat in it.

"I am cataloging the pattern," Tiffani said. "Your deterioration is part of that pattern. If you are going to keep doing this—and you are—then someone needs to document what it costs. Otherwise you will spend yourself without knowing how near you are to failure."

"You think I am close," he said.

"I think you used this ability eight times in one Tide, and your hands have not been still since," she said. "That suggests you are approaching a threshold. I do not yet know where it lies. I am trying to find out before you cross it."

Silence settled around them. The corridor carried hammers, calling voices, the roughened thrum of the grid. Dust hung in narrow beams of light from a cracked upper vent.

"What do you need," Jhace asked at last.

The question surprised her more than any refusal would have.

"Your routes," she said. "Every site you held. Contact duration. Your sense of the load before and after. Any place where the glow started weaker, or refused you altogether. And—" She paused. "Leave markers when you plan to intervene next. I need to examine the walls before you touch them, not just after."

"You want to watch me fail," he said.

"I want to see the mechanism," Tiffani said. "Success and failure both produce data. If I only examine stable holds, I will miss half of what matters."

He looked back at the copper band.

"There is a section in the Underface," he said slowly. "Grid line under the Dregs. Main support is cracked. I marked it before the Piercing. It held then. It will not much longer. When it goes, that line ruptures. The district loses heat."

"When are you planning to reinforce it?" she asked.

"Next Kindling," he said. "If the braces hold that long."

"If they do not?"

"Then I move sooner," he said. "With or without you."

Tiffani considered the fracture in front of her, the heat under her palm, the steady shake in his bandaged hands. She pictured a support under the Dregs carrying more load than it should, trying to fail in the dark where no one from the RRO had drawn a neat grid yet.

"Leave word with Bram when you go," she said. "I will meet you at the ladder shaft before you drop."

He frowned.

"You want to be in the Underface," he said. "With a crack just before it fails."

"Yes," she said. "With instruments. With glass. With marks I can trust more than rumors."

"That is not how most people answer," he said.

"Most people did not spend three Anneals watching an oven whisper before it tore a wall down," she said. "Marisse asked if it worried me. I said no. I called signal noise. She died when it let go. I will not call this noise."

His gaze held on her face for several breaths, as if weighing the line between her words and her motives.

"You take a long path to say you care whether I break," he said.

"Care is a poor planning term," she said. "Break is measurable."

For the first time, his mouth twitched in something that almost reached amusement.

"You walk my routes," he said. "You see the worst points. Then you tell me where not to stand. That is the work you are asking for."

"Yes," she said. "And I tell healers where not to put beds. And I tell Harlen where his neat counts do not match what the fracture patterns preserve."

He pushed himself to his feet. The motion carried a small hitch, a moment where his balance checked before it settled.

"I keep the right to say no," he said. "If your questions ask for more than the wall does."

"You do," Tiffani said. "And I keep the right to tell you when your hands are past their safe load, even if you lie about it."

"How will you know I am lying," he asked.

She closed her logbook and slipped it under her arm.

"Glass resonates at specific pitches," she said. "When you start to crack, I will hear it."

He huffed a breath that might have been a laugh.

"You are impossible," he said.

"Everyone says so," she replied. "Most of them are still standing."

He turned toward the Underface ladder shaft. She watched the tremor in his hands as he walked, then dropped her gaze back to the copper bands on the wall.

The city's new hum ran under her feet, in the stone, in the glass, and, now, in the way Jhace Torrins's fingers refused to rest. One pattern. Many failure points.

She drew a line under the day's notes and wrote:

Working rule: Held walls remain dangerous. Use his grip as last resort, never first. Begin structured joint survey next Kindling.

She underlined last resort, then shut the book.

For now, the walls stayed up. They would not do so on their own.

***

## Chapter 6 – The Weight That Won't Yield

The Underface smelled wrong.

Not the usual damp stone and kiln exhaust. Something sharper. Metallic. The taste sat on Jhace's tongue and stayed. He had tasted it once before, in the span after the Piercing when dust rained copper and the upper bands of the sky warped.

He stood at the base of the cracked support and ran his palm along the fracture line. The stone vibrated under his touch at a pitch that had not been there two Tides ago. The main pipe overhead hummed higher than the lines to either side. Load that should have spread across three supports had settled into this one failing column.

Behind him, Tiffani measured the junction with a cord marked in hand-widths. Her logbook lay open on a dry ledge, weighted with a chunk of rubble. She had reached the ladder shaft before Kindling ended, carrying glass plates wrapped in cloth and a stub of chalk that put white on everything she touched.

"Fracture depth?" she asked without looking up.

"Three fingers at the widest," Jhace said. "Shallow at the base. Deeper near the brace."

"Load concentration?"

He pressed harder and read the push under his palm.

"High," he said. "The other supports are carrying less than they should. This one is taking the extra."

She made a mark. Crossed to the support and placed her own hand beside his, close without touching. Her fingers hovered a breath from the surface.

"Residual warmth," she said. "Faint. You touched this before."

"Last Anneal," Jhace said. "Before the Piercing. Hairline only. I marked it to watch."

"It is not for watching now," she said.

"No."

She pulled her hand back and sketched the fracture pattern. Pen strokes quick, exact.

"The crack runs up toward the pipe anchor and down into the foundation," she said. "If it fails at the anchor first, the pipe torques. Joints split. Heat vents into the tunnel and into the blocks above."

"I know," Jhace said.

"If it fails at the base, the support drops," she said. "The adjacent columns do not have slack to take the full load. Junction folds. Three mains rupture."

"I know," he said again.

His hands curled and opened. The tremor ran through the motion even when he tried to hold still.

Tiffani studied his fingers, then his face.

"How many phases since you last used the grip?" she asked.

"Eighteen. Maybe twenty," he said.

"And the tremor?"

"Constant."

She wrote. Set down the pen. Unwrapped one of the glass plates from her satchel. The plate was thin, etched with a grid of fine lines.

"What is that?" Jhace asked.

"Stress plate," Tiffani said. "If I place it against the stone while you work, the grid will distort where stress concentrates. It will show me where you are applying force and where the stone answers."

"You want to measure me," he said.

"I want to see the mechanism," she said. "If I know where your contact is most effective, I can tell you where to press next time. Less waste. Less cost."

"You cannot reduce this cost," Jhace said. "I have tried."

"You have tried alone," she said. She held his gaze. "Let me help."

Above them, the pipe gave a low groan. Grit sifted from the brace and pattered onto the floor.

Jhace looked at the fracture. At the load coming down from the Dregs. At the blocks overhead where people slept through a hum they did not understand.

Twenty-three names sat behind his teeth. He was not taking more.

"Fine," he said. "But if the column goes, you run. Do not stay to measure a fall."

"Agreed," Tiffani said.

She pressed the stress plate against the stone beside the fracture and held it steady with one hand.

"Proceed when ready," she said.

Jhace knelt and set his palms flat over the widest stretch of crack.

Stone met skin. Cold. Rough. Full of moving weight.

Hold, he thought. Long enough.

Heat built under his hands. At first it felt like friction. Then it sharpened and sank into bone. Copper-gold light spilled out from under his fingers and drove into the fracture. The glow traced the crack upward to the brace and downward into the foundation, following stress lines he could feel even if he did not know their names.

His arms locked. His jaw clenched. The tremor in his hands jumped into a hard shake that climbed his forearms.

Vein-lines lit under his skin, bright copper from wrist to elbow.

The stone pushed back.

Not as a shove he could see. Deeper than that. The load pressed to widen the crack, shifting where it had been held too long. He held it in place with heat and something that felt like his whole chest pressing through his hands.

"Grid distortion visible," Tiffani said. Her voice stayed level. "Stress concentrated at three nodes. Primary contact is strong. North side shows less distortion. You are giving too much there for too little gain."

Jhace could not spare breath to answer. Sweat ran into his eyes. Pressure beat behind them in time with his heart.

"Shift your left hand," Tiffani said. "Two hand-widths toward the brace. Put more into the upper node."

He tried.

His left hand stayed where it was, locked by strain. Copper-gold flared brighter. Pain shot along the arm in a tight, hot line.

"Jhace," Tiffani said. Her tone changed, sharp and close. "Your vein-lines are too bright. Pull back before—"

The pipe overhead shrieked.

Not the support. A joint three junction spans upslope. Pressure tore it open. Superheated air vented in a hard white jet that filled the tunnel with scalding mist.

Jhace's focus broke. The glow around his fingers stuttered. The support shifted a fraction under his hands.

"No," he forced out.

He drove his palms harder into the stone. Copper-gold surged back, brighter than before. His vision narrowed to contact points and light. Heat, pressure, weight. Sound dulled. The world reduced to contact and strain.

"Secondary rupture," Tiffani shouted. Her voice came through the roar of venting air. "The brace is pulling free. If it goes, the pipe drops onto the support. Then we lose all of it."

Jhace felt it in the stone. The brace pulling free of its seat. The pipe starting to sag. Load on the column doubling by the heartbeat.

He could not hold both. Support or pipe. Choose, and the other failed.

"Where," he shouted. His throat scraped.

"Not you," Tiffani said. "You keep the column. I mark the brace."

She left the stress plate in place and ran into the mist. She pressed her palm to the wall near the anchor, traced load with her fingers, then slashed an X in chalk on the stone.

"Here," she called. "Maximum strain at this point. If someone can lock this, the brace holds. The pipe stays."

Jhace stared at the mark. It sat three paces away. He could not reach it without letting go.

If he let go, the column failed. If he held the column, the brace failed. Either way put people under falling stone.

His hands shook so hard the copper glow trembled with the motion.

"I cannot reach both," he said.

"Then you hold the support," Tiffani said. "I brace the joint."

"You cannot—"

She was already moving. She snatched a length of broken pipe from the rubble, wedged it under the sagging brace, and leaned into it with her weight. Metal screamed. The wedge bit. The brace held, for the moment.

"This buys you thirty heartbeats," she shouted. "Make them count."

Jhace dragged his attention back to the crack.

The fracture had widened while his mind was split. Fine lines at the edges had opened. The stone was trying to fail faster now.

Hold.

The copper-gold brightened until it washed detail away. It drove deeper into the break, into joints above and foundation below. His veins burned. His breath shortened. The hum in his ears turned to a solid, grinding tone.

Ten heartbeats.

The support slowed its shift.

Twenty.

The crack stopped moving. The glow traced each stress line and pinned it in place. The column still carried too much, but it held.

Thirty.

The brace groaned. Tiffani's wedge bent. She shifted her stance and put her shoulder into the metal, jaw clenched, boots sliding on wet stone.

"Jhace," she said. The strain pulled at her words. "I am losing it."

Forty.

The vented heat thinned. The roar eased down to a rough hiss. Pressure in the line dropped.

Fifty.

"Now," Tiffani said. "Let go now or it will take more than your hands."

Jhace tore his palms away from the stone.

The copper-gold cut out. The support settled in a slow, grinding drop and caught on a

Sources

[ASSISTANT]
Picking up right where your text cut off in Chapter 6, staying in Jhace’s POV and keeping all the N01 constraints intact.

---

> The copper-gold cut out. The support settled in a slow, grinding drop and caught on a

new bearing line.

Stone shifted under his hands and then held. New hairline cracks formed at the edges. None of them ran deep enough to take the column down. Overhead, the pipe stayed in its seat. The brace carried weight on the bent wedge and whatever strength it had left.

Jhace’s arms gave out.

He fell sideways, catching himself on one elbow. His hands clawed against the floor in hard, useless spasms. The tremor ran from fingertips to shoulder. Copper vein-lines still burned under his skin, bright and slow to fade.

The vent hiss above them thinned to a rough breath. Heat in the tunnel dropped from scalding to merely bad.

Tiffani let go of the brace.

She stepped back, shoulders heaving, hair plastered to her forehead with sweat. For a moment she just stood, eyes on the support, chest rising and falling fast. Then she turned and knelt beside him.

"Vein-lines are still lit," she said. Her voice was steady around the edges but rough in the middle. "They usually fade faster?"

"Yes," Jhace said. The word came out thin. "Breaths. Not… this long."

She took his wrists and turned his hands palm-up. The contact hurt, but not as much as pulling away would have. The skin across his palms felt hot and stretched, marked with faint copper scars that had not been there before the Piercing. The glow under them pulsed with his heartbeat.

"Fifty heartbeats," she said. "You held for fifty. You should have let go ten sooner."

"The column was still moving," he said.

"The column had stopped gaining," she said. "Look."

She shifted so the stress plate stayed in view. The etched grid lines had bent into a tight pattern around three nodes. The distortion at the upper node was deep. At the north side it was shallow.

"You poured too much into the weak node," she said. "You were feeding stone that was already past what it could carry. You cannot prop every fault. You pick the ones that give you the most hold for the least cost."

He shut his eyes. The tremor did not care.

"How much did it cost?" she asked.

"More," he said. "Than the wall. Than the beam. Than anything yet."

He forced his eyes open. The copper along his veins had dimmed, but not gone. His fingers shook without pause. A dull weight sat in his chest, behind bone, where breath should have moved easier.

Tiffani wrapped his hands in her outer tunic, binding fingers together to stop them knocking into each other.

"Can you feel them?" she asked.

"The hands?" he said.

"The marks," she said. "The heat. The lines. Anything beyond pain."

"Yes," he said after a breath. "They feel full. Like there is still… pressure. Pushing out."

She studied his face.

"You have reached a new baseline," she said. "The cost is not dropping back to what it was. If you use this again soon, you will start from here."

"I know," he said.

"Do you?" she asked. "Because you keep pushing past the point where any craftsperson with sense would stop. If you broke a tool this way, you would throw it out and curse the waste."

"I am not a tool," he said.

"Exactly," she said. "You do not get to throw yourself out. You keep working with what is left. That means you plan for the wear you cannot undo."

He gave a short, dry sound that almost counted as a laugh.

"You talk as if you own my hands," he said.

"I do not own them," she said. "But I seem to be the only one counting how fast you are burning through them."

Silence hung between them. The tunnel carried the roughened hum of the grid and the slow drip of condensed steam.

"Can you stand?" she asked at last.

He braced a palm on the floor. His arm shook under the weight of his own torso. Pain flared up to his shoulder.

"Not yet," he said.

She nodded once. Pushed herself to her feet and walked back to the support.

He watched her check the column. She ran her fingers along the crack, tapped near the base, listened. Then she moved to the brace, checked the wedge, tested the pipe for play.

"Support is holding on a smaller bearing than it should," she said. "Brace is still at risk if pressure spikes again. This junction is not safe for casual traffic. We need proper wedges and a second column poured as soon as the Crown stops arguing over who pays."

"Mark it," Jhace said.

She took out her chalk and scribed a clear warning on the wall near the junction mark. Then she came back to him, offered her arm.

"Up," she said.

He hesitated.

"If I lean on you, we both fall," he said.

"If you stay on the floor, we lose the use of you sooner," she said. "Pick."

He took her arm.

She pulled as he pushed. For a breath he thought his legs would not take it. Then his knees locked and held. The world tilted once and settled. The tremor in his hands spread across his shoulders in a fine, constant jitter.

"Better?" she asked.

"No," he said. "Enough."

"Enough is acceptable," she said.

They started toward the ladder shaft. His steps came heavy and shorter than usual. She adjusted her pace without comment, keeping her shoulder close enough that he could grab it if balance failed.

"What did the plate show?" he asked.

"Your contact focuses more than we thought," she said. "Most of the distortion sits at three nodes. If you can learn to aim only at those, you may hold the same loads for fewer heartbeats."

"Can you mark them in time?" he asked.

"If you give me warning before you touch," she said. "Here, I had one plate and one crack. In a more complex junction, I will need more eyes and faster hands."

"You have Pella," he said. "And Bram, if he will listen."

"Bram will listen as long as we do not use RRO words in his hearing," she said. "Pella will listen because she wants to know how the world breaks."

He grunted.

"Harlen will want this," she added. "The counts. The locations. The limits."

"Harlen will want control," Jhace said.

"Then do not give it to him," she said. "Give him numbers. Let him build his frameworks. We keep the choice of when and where you touch."

"You speak as if you stand between us," he said.

"I stand between what you can do and what the city will demand if it hears all of it at once," she said. "Harlen counts. The Crown commands. The guilds complain. In the middle of that you have hands that will not hold still and a grip that eats at them. Someone needs to say no for reasons that live in bone, not on slate."

They reached the ladder shaft.

Jhace looked up at the circle of pale light far above. Climbing with his hands wrapped and shaking would be slow and unpleasant.

"You go first," Tiffani said. "I stay under you in case you slip."

"That is poor safety practice," he said.

"It is better than letting you fall the whole length," she said. "Move."

He moved.

Every rung sent a line of pain up his arms. He used his legs as much as he could, hooking boots hard onto metal and driving with thighs, letting the hands guide more than pull. Twice his grip faltered. Twice Tiffani's shoulder or forearm pressed against his boot, a small solid reminder that the drop was not empty.

At the top, he hauled himself onto the street and lay on his back for a few breaths, staring at the cracked sky band hanging faint in the upper blue. The scar had thinned since the Piercing, but it had not gone.

Tiffani climbed out after him and sat with her back against the nearest wall. Her face was drawn. Her hands shook, though not as badly as his.

"You are worse than yesterday," she said without looking at him.

"So is the grid," he said.

"We will treat both," she said. "In the order that keeps the most people breathing."

He turned his head.

"You said earlier this is not how most people answer," he said. "Running into the Underface when it wants to fail. Holding wedges under shrieking pipe."

"I did say that," she said.

"Why did you?" he asked.

She thought for several breaths.

"Because once I watched a pattern for three Anneals and called it unimportant," she said. "I did not stop work. I did not inconvenience the guild. Marisse died. I carry that. If I see a new pattern now, I go toward it until I know what it does."

He studied her profile. The tight set of her jaw. The way her fingers kept time against her logbook, as if matching some unseen measure.

"You are not here for my sake," he said.

"No," she said. "I am here because you are a moving part in a failure curve that includes the grid, the stone, and the sky. If you break, the curve shifts. I need to know how."

Then she looked at him, and something softened by a fraction.

"And," she said, "because it would be wasteful if the man who held a building and a beam with his bare hands burned them out on the wrong wall."

"That last part sounds like care," he said.

"It sounds like resource management," she said.

"You tell yourself that," he said.

She did not answer.

They sat in silence for a few breaths. Street sounds reached them from farther upslope: carts, shouted orders, the steady ring of tools against stone. Underneath it all, the grid hummed at its altered pitch.

"Next time," Tiffani said, "you listen when I tell you thirty heartbeats is enough."

"Next time," he said, "you mark the nodes faster."

"Agreed," she said.

He pushed himself upright. The tremor in his hands did not ease. The copper scars on his palms still felt too warm.

"Partnership, then," he said.

"Yes," she said.

"You tell me where to press," he said. "I listen."

"And when you ignore me," she said, "I hit you with my logbook."

He huffed air. It almost counted as a laugh.

"Fair," he said.

They stood.

The support under the Dregs held behind them in the dark, load pinned in place for now by light that had already burned itself out of his veins. The city above carried on with cracks, with smoke, with the thin pale mark still hanging across its sky.

Jhace flexed his hands once. The tremor answered, steady and unkind.

"We go topside?" he asked.

"We are already topside," Tiffani said. "We go find Harlen and hand him just enough truth that he does not build his counts on lies."

"And after that?" Jhace asked.

"We find the next place the stone intends to fail," she said. "And we decide whether to pay the cost."

He nodded.

They walked together toward the Crown’s noise, leaving the held weight and the faint copper stain in the Underface behind them.

The weight did not leave him.

It would not.

[USER]
# N01: Chapters 4–6 (SURGICALLY CLEANED)

## Chapter 4 – After the Shudder

Jhace woke on his back with stone under his shoulders and boiled herbs in his throat.

For a few breaths sound came without shape. Then it sorted: low voices, cloth moving, metal in basins. Somewhere a child crying, then quiet. No collapse roar. Only the roughened hum of the grid under everything.

His hands hurt.

He tried to flex his fingers. Tremor ran from fingertips to elbow in a fine constant shake. Pain followed, a dull burn along bone. Rough cloth bound his palms. The edges rasped.

"Stay down," Bram said.

Jhace turned his head. He lay on a pallet in a side room off a wider tunnel. Ceiling higher than the work runs, stone blocks set regular. Two rows of makeshift beds on the floor. He recognized the top of a ladder shaft in the next chamber: Bram's level.

Bram sat on an overturned crate a pace away. Dust streaked his hair. A dark bruise marked his cheek. He held a clay cup in both hands.

"How long," Jhace said. The words scraped.

"Since late Striking," Bram said. "We're into Tide One now. You were out most of that stretch."

Jhace pushed himself up on an elbow. The room tilted once and steadied.

"Chloen," he said.

Bram's mouth twitched.

"Alive," he said. "Leg's in bad shape, but not gone. Healer says crushed bone, torn flesh, all the words. Some of the worst breaks were already knitting when they opened it. She's chewed enough root to sleep through a grid failure."

Jhace's gaze tracked past him.

Chloen lay three pallets down, under a cleaner blanket than his. Her leg was splinted from hip to ankle, wrapped in bandage cloth that showed dark stains along the middle. At the edges of the wrappings, a faint copper shimmer clung to the fibers.

Jhace's fingers twitched. Memory came in pieces: the beam, the heat in his hands, copper-gold streaking over bruised flesh.

"Do not do that," Bram said quietly. "Do not put her on the same list as the building. She is still breathing because you refused to let that beam have her."

Jhace looked down at his own hands. The bandages were stained with dust and a pale copper tint. Under the cloth, his fingers jerked in short, useless bursts.

"What do they say happened," he asked.

"Out there?" Bram jerked his chin toward the tunnel. "Depends who you ask. Some say a corner of Saint Merro learned to stand on its own. Some say the Piercing cracked the rules and the stone is waking. Some say there's a Forgemaster walking around with molten hands." His eyes dropped to Jhace's bandages. "Plenty say an Underface inspector held up a building."

Jhace's stomach tightened.

"They're asking for you," Bram went on. "The ones who were under that wall. Runners keep sticking their heads in. Want to know when the holder can walk."

"I held a short span," Jhace said. The word caught. "It did not fall because loads shifted. They ask for the wrong thing."

"Maybe," Bram said. "City's still cracked. Council's shouting in the Crown. Guilds are pointing at each other. Stone keeps failing in the middle of it. We have more to do than argue about what they call you."

Jhace swung his legs off the pallet. Cold floor met his feet. The shift set his arm muscles jumping.

Bram reached out. Jhace shook his head once and pushed himself up without taking the hand. The walls rolled past his eyes and then settled.

"Show me what failed," he said.

Bram studied him for a few breaths.

"Fine," Bram said at last. "But if you fall, try not to hit anything that holds weight."

Jhace loosened the wraps enough to let his fingers move. The cloth pulled at raw skin; pain bit up his forearms. The tremor did not care.

They stepped into the main tunnel.

The level had turned into a passage of constant motion. Runners carrying slates. Guild workers with coil and brace. Healers moving between makeshift nests with baskets of cloth and jars. Blood, hot metal, and root bitter thickened the usual damp-stone smell. The grid hum ran harsher, with edges where mains had been patched quick.

Heads turned as they passed.

"That's him," someone said behind them. "The holder from the kiln street."

Jhace kept his eyes forward.

"Where first," he asked.

"Nearest break we can reach," Bram said. "Two marry points took damage. One side support cracked. No spare crew for a full fix yet."

"Test," Jhace said. "We see if that thing listens without people under it."

The first junction had already been wedged and strapped. The main hummed steady. The brace that had sheared away vibrated on its own note. Jhace touched stone beside it.

Stress had moved into the emergency wedges. Imperfect. Stable enough.

"We move," he said.

The cracked side support sat deeper, in hotter air. Someone had tried to help by jamming broken stone under its base. Gaps showed. The line over it fed a crowded stretch of houses.

"No one on the floors above?" Jhace asked.

"Cleared after the first tremors," Bram said. "For now."

The fracture line at the base ran shallow but long. If it failed during the next Tide, the tunnel and part of the building wall would go with it.

Jhace set his palm on the split.

Stone met skin. Cool away from the crack. Warmer where it carried load. He felt the thin gap and its rough edges. His own shake blurred the reading.

Hold, he thought. Hold here. No one is under you yet, but they will be.

Nothing answered.

He pressed harder. Pain flared in his palm, then his wrist. The crack remained a crack. No heat surge. No copper-gold. Only the steady push of weight and his fingers jumping.

"Anything?" Bram asked.

"No," Jhace said.

"Good," Bram said. "I was half afraid you were about to say you can fix every hairline in this city by frowning at it."

Jhace pulled his hand away. His skin tingled. The stone stayed exactly as it was.

"Mark it," he said. "We reinforce it with tools."

They turned upslope.

They did not reach the surface before the next break found them.

A runner shot around a bend and nearly collided with them. A boy, sweat bright on his skin, breath tearing.

"You Jhace Torrins?" he gasped. "The one who held the building?"

Jhace's shoulders went tight.

"Yes," Bram said. "What's failing?"

"Shop near the Merro kiln corridor," the boy said. "Healers using it. Wall cracked. They say if we don't get you, we lose half the wounded when it drops."

Jhace started moving before Bram answered.

The kiln street looked worse than it had when they'd first climbed out.

Dust still hung in the air. A whole row of small fronts showed new cracks and missing corners. Someone had turned a narrow shop into a triage room; pallets and tables filled the inside space. The wall facing the street carried a thick fracture from lintel to ground.

People crowded the doorway. Two workers braced the wall with poles. Every shove made fine dust leak faster.

"Back," Jhace said.

His voice came rough and low. It did not clear the crowd.

Bram stepped up beside him.

"Back," Bram repeated, louder. "Give him the wall or it will take you with it."

The crowd shifted, grudging but real. Jhace stepped to the crack.

Closer, it was worse. The roof load had shifted onto two narrow contact points instead of the full length of wall. Inside, ceiling beams sat on that broken line. Several pallets lay under the center span.

Jhace placed both palms flat against the stone.

Cold outer face. Heat from wounded bodies and lamps inside. Heavy pressure from above.

Hold, he thought. They die if you do not hold.

Heat rose under his skin at once. Copper-gold slid across his hands and into the wall, fine streams chasing the fracture up and down. The glow reached the lintel, the base, then spread sideways into the adjoining blocks.

His jaw locked. His arms went rigid.

"Clear the center," Bram shouted. "Move the pallets to the sides. No arguments."

Voices and movement answered from inside. Cloth dragged. Someone cried out as they were shifted, then grunted and went quiet.

The wall pressed into his hands, the crack widening under load. He felt every point where load had narrowed, every place the stone barely held. His pulse beat into the contact, hard enough to make his fingers sting.

His shake worsened. Copper-gold lit the veins along his forearms, bright down to the elbow. Each heartbeat drove pain down those lines.

"Almost," Bram called. "Hold."

Jhace held.

The glow ran as far as it could find strain and stopped. It did not heal. It only pinned the worst angles in place.

"Clear," Bram shouted at last. "No one under the middle. Out."

Jhace counted three heartbeats more, then let go.

The copper-gold cut off. The wall shifted a short span and caught on a new lean. Dust dribbled. Loose chips fell and bounced on bare floor. The beams above groaned but stayed set.

Jhace's hands dropped. His fingers clawed and opened in fast, useless grabs.

"Out," Bram told the street. "No pallets under that span. You two—brace it with poles and proper wedges before next Pouring."

Jhace stepped back until his shoulders met the opposite wall. Sweat ran cold down his back. The edges of his vision went soft. His hands shook hard enough that his nails clicked together.

"Your arms lit," Bram said, low. "Vein-lines all the way up. You still with me?"

"For now," Jhace said. His tongue felt thick.

He looked down. Faint copper-gold lines still traced his forearms, pulsing with the tremor. They faded. The shake did not.

"Well," Bram said. "We learned the other half."

"What half," Jhace asked.

"Whatever this is," Bram said, "answers when people are about to get crushed. It ignores you when you want the nice clean fixes. And every time it answers, it takes more out of you."

Jhace stared at his hands until the last trace of color vanished, leaving raw skin and an unsteady twitch.

Someone cleared a throat.

The woman from the street stood near the cracked wall. Glass dust and copper shimmer threaded her hair. A logbook pressed against her chest. Her gaze moved from his hands to the stone.

"Jhace Torrins," she said.

He narrowed his eyes.

"You know my name," he said.

"You shouted it yesterday," she said. "Bram shouted it louder. Runners carried it. May I see where you touched?"

"No," Jhace said.

She watched him for a breath.

"I need to know what changed in the wall," she said. "Healers are using buildings you have… helped. If I know where your grip holds strongest, I can tell them where not to put beds."

That, Jhace could not argue with.

He turned back to the wall. One shaking finger rose to the point where the glow had felt thickest. The tremor made him miss twice. On the third try he hit the right strip.

"Here," he said. "Then down to here, and across."

She stepped closer, careful not to brush him. Her hand followed his path, then moved on, checking the stone in small presses.

"Hot band along your line," she murmured. "Cooler outside it. Stress is concentrated there. Load still carried, but the effective bearing length is small compared to the wall's full span. It should not stand on that narrow zone. But it does."

"It stands for now," Jhace said. "Do not trust it past that."

"I do not trust anything past that," she said. "But I prefer numbers to stories. Stories about you are already spreading."

"Stories are useless," Jhace said. "Either the brace holds or it does not."

"And if it only holds when you are here?" she asked.

He had no answer.

Her eyes dropped to his hands. The tremor still ran, fine and constant.

"Is that new?" she asked.

"Old injury," he said. "Tools."

She made a quiet sound that was not agreement.

"If you keep lying this badly," she said, "you make my work harder."

A voice called from farther up the street.

She glanced that way, then back.

"I am Tiffani Merrow," she said. "Glasswork and structural optics. I intend to keep people away from the first places your grips will fail. I need your routes and what you feel when you touch stone turned into marks."

"I have routes," Jhace said. "I do not have data."

"You have both," she said. "You just leave some of it in your bones."

She moved away, already reading the next damaged fronts. Bram watched her go.

"She will be trouble," he said.

Jhace did not answer.

A bent nail lay near his boot. He bent and reached for it.

His fingers missed twice. On the third try he caught it between thumb and forefinger. The tremor snapped. The nail jumped from his grip and tapped back onto stone.

He stared at it.

Bram's gaze slid to his hands.

Jhace straightened. He folded his hands behind his back, hiding the twitch.

"I am fine," he said.

Bram said nothing.

Across the street, Tiffani looked over. Her eyes dropped once to his hidden hands, then back to his face. She held his gaze for a breath, then turned away.

The grid hummed at its new pitch under their feet. The wall held. Jhace's grip did not.

***

## Chapter 5 – What Stays in Stone

The copper shimmer did not fade.

Tiffani crouched at the base of the wall Jhace Torrins had held the previous Tide and pressed her palm to the stone. Warmth bled through her skin. Not kiln heat. Not surface warming from the upper light. A band of elevated temperature ran along the fracture where his hands had been. Narrow, about the breadth of two knuckles, concentrated at the points where stress had peaked.

She pulled her hand back and logged the observation.

Tide 1, Pouring phase. Residual heat persists at contact points. Duration: minimum 18 phases post-activation. Pattern suggests localized energy retention in material structure.

Around her, the kiln corridor had turned into organized motion. Guild workers hauled broken stone into stacks. Healers moved between makeshift shelters. Runners carried slates between districts, their boots leaving new dust on old flagstones. The city was learning to function with fractures.

The fractures were not behaving in ways she trusted.

Tiffani stood and moved to the next marked site—a doorframe Jhace had stabilized during the night shift. The copper-gold traces here were fainter but still present, threading through mortar lines in patterns that followed load paths she knew from structural glass analysis. Where his hands had pressed, stress had shifted and settled into new paths. Where he had not touched, the cracks at the edges kept creeping.

She sketched the pattern in her logbook, comparing it to the previous site. The effective bearing zones were consistent: narrow bands of increased stability surrounded by long stretches of unchanged vulnerability. His contact had locked specific stress points in place without changing the broader load distribution.

A shadow fell across her page.

"Master Merrow."

She looked up.

Inspector Harlen stood with two guild officials, their vests marked with Merro Glass and Crown Engineering seals. Behind them, a younger clerk held a stack of blank slates.

"Inspector," Tiffani said. She did not rise. "I am in the middle of documentation."

"So I see." Harlen gestured at the chalk marks and her open book. "The RRO committee met during Kindling. Your name came up frequently. Master Corlis praised your precision. We need that precision applied to the formal survey."

"I am surveying," Tiffani said.

"Independently," one of the guild officials said. His tone treated the word as a flaw. "The committee is coordinating routes, assigning sectors, establishing reporting protocols. We cannot have scattered efforts."

Tiffani closed her logbook halfway, thumb holding her place.

"The fractures show where the stone failed," she said. "I am recording what they show. If your committee wants my data, I will share it. If you want me to stop collecting in order to fill out forms, the answer is no."

The clerk shifted his weight. The guild official's jaw tightened.

"The Crown wants counts," Harlen said, voice careful. "Compromised supports, households at risk, grid sections under threat. Your mapping is valuable, but we need it inside a larger frame. Join the committee officially. You will have assistants, access to archived plans, priority routing—"

"And constraints," Tiffani said. "Approved routes. Predetermined priorities. Reports that delay action while they pass through review."

"Guidelines," Harlen said. "We must manage panic as much as stone. If every craftsperson follows their own urgency, we lose coherence."

Tiffani looked past him to the wall she had just measured. The copper shimmer caught morning light, a thin band of something new written across old stone.

"Inspector Harlen," she said. "How many buildings has your committee stabilized since forming?"

His expression flickered.

"The committee coordinates," he said. "Others execute."

"I thought so." She turned a page and kept sketching. "When your framework produces results, show them to me. Until then, I will keep recording what the fracture patterns preserve before they stop showing anything at all."

The guild official drew breath to argue. Harlen raised a hand.

"Master Merrow," he said quietly. "The RRO appreciates independence. We also appreciate order. Consider the offer. If you change your mind, the committee meets each Kindling in the Crown administrative hall."

He turned. The officials followed. The clerk cast one backward glance at her chalk marks before hurrying after them.

Tiffani waited until their footsteps faded, then marked the wall again, this time noting the angle of copper inclusion relative to the street slope.

She was logging the third site when Pella found her.

"Master Tiffani." Her apprentice picked her way through rubble, a wrapped package in one hand and exasperation on her face. "You said you would be at the workshop during Pouring. Master Corlis has been asking for you. The Crown commission panes need final inspection before they cool past measurement range."

"The panes are stable," Tiffani said without looking up. "The temperature gradient was correct when I checked them at Kindling. They will hold tolerance."

"He wants your signature on the approval slate."

"Then he can wait two phases." Tiffani drew another stress line on the page. "Stone that is about to crush people takes priority over glass that is already behaving."

Pella set the package down—bread and cheese, from the smell—and crouched beside her. Her gaze followed the chalk on the wall, then moved to the marks in the logbook.

"What are you mapping?" Pella asked.

"Evidence," Tiffani said. "The man who held these walls left traces. Heat signatures. Copper residue. Stress redistribution patterns. If I can quantify where his contact stabilizes and where it does not, I can predict which held sections will fail first."

Pella's eyes widened.

"You mean the holder—Jhace Torrins—he is not fixing the stone?" she asked. "He only delays the collapse?"

"Correct." Tiffani tapped her pen against a marked node. "His contact creates narrow zones of temporary stability. The load paths beyond those bands do not change. The fractures do not seal. He is holding weight in place through direct force that does not match any mechanical principle I can name yet."

"That sounds dangerous."

"It is," Tiffani said. "For him, and for anyone who trusts a wall he has touched without understanding how short-term his intervention is. Which is why I am documenting the pattern before people start depending on it."

Pella nodded slowly.

"The RRO wants you to stop?" she asked.

"The RRO wants me to join a committee and report through channels." Tiffani took the bread, tore off a piece, and chewed. "By the time my observations pass through review, three more buildings will have collapsed and people will wonder why no one warned them."

"So you are refusing."

"I am choosing urgency over procedure." Tiffani met her apprentice's gaze. "There is a difference."

Pella's mouth curved.

"Everyone says you are impossible," she said.

"Everyone lacks precision," Tiffani said. She swallowed and reached for the cheese. "If you see Master Corlis, tell him I will inspect the panes before Shaping. If he cannot wait that long, tell him to find someone else whose standards he trusts less."

Pella shook her head but picked up the empty wrappings with care.

"I will tell him you are serving the public safety," she said. "He can argue with that if he wishes."

She walked away toward the main kiln corridor.

Tiffani returned to the wall.

The copper band here sat higher than at the first site, closer to the lintel. She pressed her palm against the stone. Warm. The same width. The same residual hum, just below hearing, traveling into her bones.

She wrote: Held zone narrow. Adjacent cracks remain active. Advise healers to avoid placing pallets within one band width of edge.

She was measuring the fourth site when she heard boots on broken stone and looked up to find Jhace Torrins standing three paces away, watching her work.

He looked worse than he had the previous Tide. Dark lines under his eyes. Dust ground into his coat. His hands, wrapped in fresh bandages, trembled faintly at his sides. His gaze stayed sharp, moving from her chalk marks to the wall to her open logbook.

"You are mapping my routes," he said.

It was not a question.

"Yes," Tiffani said. She did not apologize. "You are leaving evidence. I am recording it."

"Why."

"Because people are using buildings you held," she said. "If I know where your stabilization is weakest, I can warn them before the temporary hold fails."

He stared at her for a long moment, then moved closer and crouched beside the wall. His bandaged fingers hovered above the copper line without touching stone.

"This is where I pressed," he said quietly. "Here, and here." He indicated two points along the fracture. "The break was trying to widen. I felt it pushing."

"And you pushed back," Tiffani said.

"Yes."

"For how long?"

"Until they cleared the wounded." His jaw tightened. "Three phases, maybe four. I do not remember cleanly. It hurt."

Tiffani wrote. Duration correlates with residual heat intensity. Longer contact = stronger thermal signature. Cost to user: pain, tremor, memory blur.

She looked up again.

"How many sites have you held since the Piercing?" she asked.

He hesitated.

"Seven," he said. "Maybe eight. Some short. Two longer."

"And the tremor?"

His hands curled into loose fists, hiding the shake.

"Started after the second," he said. "Worsened after each one. It does not stop now."

Her pen scratched.

Progressive cost accumulation. No observed recovery between uses. Tremor baseline established. Predicted trajectory: continued deterioration with repeated activation.

"You are cataloging my deterioration," Jhace said. His tone stayed flat, but there was no heat in it.

"I am cataloging the pattern," Tiffani said. "Your deterioration is part of that pattern. If you are going to keep doing this—and you are—then someone needs to document what it costs. Otherwise you will spend yourself without knowing how near you are to failure."

"You think I am close," he said.

"I think you used this ability eight times in one Tide, and your hands have not been still since," she said. "That suggests you are approaching a threshold. I do not yet know where it lies. I am trying to find out before you cross it."

Silence settled around them. The corridor carried hammers, calling voices, the roughened thrum of the grid. Dust hung in narrow beams of light from a cracked upper vent.

"What do you need," Jhace asked at last.

The question surprised her more than any refusal would have.

"Your routes," she said. "Every site you held. Contact duration. Your sense of the load before and after. Any place where the glow started weaker, or refused you altogether. And—" She paused. "Leave markers when you plan to intervene next. I need to examine the walls before you touch them, not just after."

"You want to watch me fail," he said.

"I want to see the mechanism," Tiffani said. "Success and failure both produce data. If I only examine stable holds, I will miss half of what matters."

He looked back at the copper band.

"There is a section in the Underface," he said slowly. "Grid line under the Dregs. Main support is cracked. I marked it before the Piercing. It held then. It will not much longer. When it goes, that line ruptures. The district loses heat."

"When are you planning to reinforce it?" she asked.

"Next Kindling," he said. "If the braces hold that long."

"If they do not?"

"Then I move sooner," he said. "With or without you."

Tiffani considered the fracture in front of her, the heat under her palm, the steady shake in his bandaged hands. She pictured a support under the Dregs carrying more load than it should, trying to fail in the dark where no one from the RRO had drawn a neat grid yet.

"Leave word with Bram when you go," she said. "I will meet you at the ladder shaft before you drop."

He frowned.

"You want to be in the Underface," he said. "With a crack just before it fails."

"Yes," she said. "With instruments. With glass. With marks I can trust more than rumors."

"That is not how most people answer," he said.

"Most people did not spend three Anneals watching an oven whisper before it tore a wall down," she said. "Marisse asked if it worried me. I said no. I called signal noise. She died when it let go. I will not call this noise."

His gaze held on her face for several breaths, as if weighing the line between her words and her motives.

"You take a long path to say you care whether I break," he said.

"Care is a poor planning term," she said. "Break is measurable."

For the first time, his mouth twitched in something that almost reached amusement.

"You walk my routes," he said. "You see the worst points. Then you tell me where not to stand. That is the work you are asking for."

"Yes," she said. "And I tell healers where not to put beds. And I tell Harlen where his neat counts do not match what the fracture patterns preserve."

He pushed himself to his feet. The motion carried a small hitch, a moment where his balance checked before it settled.

"I keep the right to say no," he said. "If your questions ask for more than the wall does."

"You do," Tiffani said. "And I keep the right to tell you when your hands are past their safe load, even if you lie about it."

"How will you know I am lying," he asked.

She closed her logbook and slipped it under her arm.

"Glass resonates at specific pitches," she said. "When you start to crack, I will hear it."

He huffed a breath that might have been a laugh.

"You are impossible," he said.

"Everyone says so," she replied. "Most of them are still standing."

He turned toward the Underface ladder shaft. She watched the tremor in his hands as he walked, then dropped her gaze back to the copper bands on the wall.

The city's new hum ran under her feet, in the stone, in the glass, and, now, in the way Jhace Torrins's fingers refused to rest. One pattern. Many failure points.

She drew a line under the day's notes and wrote:

Working rule: Held walls remain dangerous. Use his grip as last resort, never first. Begin structured joint survey next Kindling.

She underlined last resort, then shut the book.

For now, the walls stayed up. They would not do so on their own.

***

## Chapter 6 – The Weight That Won't Yield

The Underface smelled wrong.

Not the usual damp stone and kiln exhaust. Something sharper. Metallic. The taste sat on Jhace's tongue and stayed. He had tasted it once before, in the span after the Piercing when dust rained copper and the upper bands of the sky warped.

He stood at the base of the cracked support and ran his palm along the fracture line. The stone vibrated under his touch at a pitch that had not been there two Tides ago. The main pipe overhead hummed higher than the lines to either side. Load that should have spread across three supports had settled into this one failing column.

Behind him, Tiffani measured the junction with a cord marked in hand-widths. Her logbook lay open on a dry ledge, weighted with a chunk of rubble. She had reached the ladder shaft before Kindling ended, carrying glass plates wrapped in cloth and a stub of chalk that put white on everything she touched.

"Fracture depth?" she asked without looking up.

"Three fingers at the widest," Jhace said. "Shallow at the base. Deeper near the brace."

"Load concentration?"

He pressed harder and read the push under his palm.

"High," he said. "The other supports are carrying less than they should. This one is taking the extra."

She made a mark. Crossed to the support and placed her own hand beside his, close without touching. Her fingers hovered a breath from the surface.

"Residual warmth," she said. "Faint. You touched this before."

"Last Anneal," Jhace said. "Before the Piercing. Hairline only. I marked it to watch."

"It is not for watching now," she said.

"No."

She pulled her hand back and sketched the fracture pattern. Pen strokes quick, exact.

"The crack runs up toward the pipe anchor and down into the foundation," she said. "If it fails at the anchor first, the pipe torques. Joints split. Heat vents into the tunnel and into the blocks above."

"I know," Jhace said.

"If it fails at the base, the support drops," she said. "The adjacent columns do not have slack to take the full load. Junction folds. Three mains rupture."

"I know," he said again.

His hands curled and opened. The tremor ran through the motion even when he tried to hold still.

Tiffani studied his fingers, then his face.

"How many phases since you last used the grip?" she asked.

"Eighteen. Maybe twenty," he said.

"And the tremor?"

"Constant."

She wrote. Set down the pen. Unwrapped one of the glass plates from her satchel. The plate was thin, etched with a grid of fine lines.

"What is that?" Jhace asked.

"Stress plate," Tiffani said. "If I place it against the stone while you work, the grid will distort where stress concentrates. It will show me where you are applying force and where the stone answers."

"You want to measure me," he said.

"I want to see the mechanism," she said. "If I know where your contact is most effective, I can tell you where to press next time. Less waste. Less cost."

"You cannot reduce this cost," Jhace said. "I have tried."

"You have tried alone," she said. She held his gaze. "Let me help."

Above them, the pipe gave a low groan. Grit sifted from the brace and pattered onto the floor.

Jhace looked at the fracture. At the load coming down from the Dregs. At the blocks overhead where people slept through a hum they did not understand.

Twenty-three names sat behind his teeth. He was not taking more.

"Fine," he said. "But if the column goes, you run. Do not stay to measure a fall."

"Agreed," Tiffani said.

She pressed the stress plate against the stone beside the fracture and held it steady with one hand.

"Proceed when ready," she said.

Jhace knelt and set his palms flat over the widest stretch of crack.

Stone met skin. Cold. Rough. Full of moving weight.

Hold, he thought. Long enough.

Heat built under his hands. At first it felt like friction. Then it sharpened and sank into bone. Copper-gold light spilled out from under his fingers and drove into the fracture. The glow traced the crack upward to the brace and downward into the foundation, following stress lines he could feel even if he did not know their names.

His arms locked. His jaw clenched. The tremor in his hands jumped into a hard shake that climbed his forearms.

Vein-lines lit under his skin, bright copper from wrist to elbow.

The stone pushed back.

Not as a shove he could see. Deeper than that. The load pressed to widen the crack, shifting where it had been held too long. He held it in place with heat and something that felt like his whole chest pressing through his hands.

"Grid distortion visible," Tiffani said. Her voice stayed level. "Stress concentrated at three nodes. Primary contact is strong. North side shows less distortion. You are giving too much there for too little gain."

Jhace could not spare breath to answer. Sweat ran into his eyes. Pressure beat behind them in time with his heart.

"Shift your left hand," Tiffani said. "Two hand-widths toward the brace. Put more into the upper node."

He tried.

His left hand stayed where it was, locked by strain. Copper-gold flared brighter. Pain shot along the arm in a tight, hot line.

"Jhace," Tiffani said. Her tone changed, sharp and close. "Your vein-lines are too bright. Pull back before—"

The pipe overhead shrieked.

Not the support. A joint three junction spans upslope. Pressure tore it open. Superheated air vented in a hard white jet that filled the tunnel with scalding mist.

Jhace's focus broke. The glow around his fingers stuttered. The support shifted a fraction under his hands.

"No," he forced out.

He drove his palms harder into the stone. Copper-gold surged back, brighter than before. His vision narrowed to contact points and light. Heat, pressure, weight. Sound dulled. The world reduced to contact and strain.

"Secondary rupture," Tiffani shouted. Her voice came through the roar of venting air. "The brace is pulling free. If it goes, the pipe drops onto the support. Then we lose all of it."

Jhace felt it in the stone. The brace pulling free of its seat. The pipe starting to sag. Load on the column doubling by the heartbeat.

He could not hold both. Support or pipe. Choose, and the other failed.

"Where," he shouted. His throat scraped.

"Not you," Tiffani said. "You keep the column. I mark the brace."

She left the stress plate in place and ran into the mist. She pressed her palm to the wall near the anchor, traced load with her fingers, then slashed an X in chalk on the stone.

"Here," she called. "Maximum strain at this point. If someone can lock this, the brace holds. The pipe stays."

Jhace stared at the mark. It sat three paces away. He could not reach it without letting go.

If he let go, the column failed. If he held the column, the brace failed. Either way put people under falling stone.

His hands shook so hard the copper glow trembled with the motion.

"I cannot reach both," he said.

"Then you hold the support," Tiffani said. "I brace the joint."

"You cannot—"

She was already moving. She snatched a length of broken pipe from the rubble, wedged it under the sagging brace, and leaned into it with her weight. Metal screamed. The wedge bit. The brace held, for the moment.

"This buys you thirty heartbeats," she shouted. "Make them count."

Jhace dragged his attention back to the crack.

The fracture had widened while his mind was split. Fine lines at the edges had opened. The stone was trying to fail faster now.

Hold.
