## Surgical fixes

### 1) “The city was learning to function with fractures.”

This shows up in **Chapter 5** (not 4), but your diagnosis is exactly right: “learning” gives agency.

**Before (Ch5):**

> Around her, the kiln corridor had turned into organized motion. Guild workers hauled broken stone into stacks. Healers moved between makeshift shelters. Runners carried slates between districts, their boots leaving new dust on old flagstones. **The city was learning to function with fractures.**

**After (Ch5):**

> Around her, the kiln corridor had turned into organized motion. Guild workers hauled broken stone into stacks. Healers moved between makeshift shelters. Runners carried slates between districts, their boots leaving new dust on old flagstones. **The city had begun to function with fractures.**

Same meaning, no “learning.”

---

### 2) “The stone was trying to fail faster now.”

Stone doesn’t try; it just fails.

**Before (Ch6):**

> The fracture had widened while his mind was split. Fine lines at the edges had opened. **The stone was trying to fail faster now.**

**After (Ch6):**

> The fracture had widened while his mind was split. Fine lines at the edges had opened. **The stone was failing faster now.**

Keeps the acceleration, drops the intent.

---

### 3) “The weight would not yield.”

“Would not” is a tiny whiff of refusal. You’re right it’s borderline, but easy to clean.

**Before (Ch6):**

> The weight would not yield. They would hold what they could, as long as they could, and write down what it took.

**After (Ch6):**

> The weight did not yield. They would hold what they could, as long as they could, and write down what it took.

Purely descriptive now.

---

## Cleaned Chapters 4–6 (drop-in text)

### Chapter 4 – After the Shudder

Jhace woke on his back with stone under his shoulders and boiled herbs in his throat.

For a few breaths sound came without shape. Then it sorted: low voices, cloth moving, metal in basins. Somewhere a child crying, then quiet. No collapse roar. Only the roughened hum of the grid under everything.

His hands hurt.

He tried to flex his fingers. Tremor ran from fingertips to elbow in a fine constant shake. Pain followed, a dull burn along bone. Rough cloth bound his palms. The edges rasped.

"Stay down," Bram said.

Jhace turned his head. He lay on a pallet in a side room off a wider tunnel. Ceiling higher than the work runs, stone blocks set regular. Two rows of makeshift beds on the floor. He recognized the top of a ladder shaft in the next chamber: Bram's level.

Bram sat on an overturned crate a pace away. Dust streaked his hair. A dark bruise marked his cheek. He held a clay cup in both hands.

"How long," Jhace said. The words scraped.

"Since late Striking," Bram said. "We're into Tide One now. You were out most of that stretch."

Jhace pushed himself up on an elbow. The room tilted once and steadied.

"Chloen," he said.

Bram's mouth twitched.

"Alive," he said. "Leg's in bad shape, but not gone. Healer says crushed bone, torn flesh, all the words. Some of the worst breaks were already knitting when they opened it. She's chewed enough root to sleep through a grid failure."

Jhace's gaze tracked past him.

Chloen lay three pallets down, under a cleaner blanket than his. Her leg was splinted from hip to ankle, wrapped in bandage cloth that showed dark stains along the middle. At the edges of the wrappings, a faint copper shimmer clung to the fibers.

Jhace's fingers twitched. Memory came in pieces: the beam, the heat in his hands, copper-gold streaking over bruised flesh.

"Do not do that," Bram said quietly. "Do not put her on the same list as the building. She is still breathing because you refused to let that beam have her."

Jhace looked down at his own hands. The bandages were stained with dust and a pale copper tint. Under the cloth, his fingers jerked in short, useless bursts.

"What do they say happened," he asked.

"Out there?" Bram jerked his chin toward the tunnel. "Depends who you ask. Some say a corner of Saint Merro learned to stand on its own. Some say the Piercing cracked the rules and the stone is waking. Some say there's a Forgemaster walking around with molten hands." His eyes dropped to Jhace's bandages. "Plenty say an Underface inspector held up a building."

Jhace's stomach tightened.

"They're asking for you," Bram went on. "The ones who were under that wall. Runners keep sticking their heads in. Want to know when the holder can walk."

"I held a short span," Jhace said. The word caught. "It did not fall because loads shifted. They ask for the wrong thing."

"Maybe," Bram said. "City's still cracked. Council's shouting in the Crown. Guilds are pointing at each other. Stone keeps failing in the middle of it. We have more to do than argue about what they call you."

Jhace swung his legs off the pallet. Cold floor met his feet. The shift set his arm muscles jumping.

Bram reached out. Jhace shook his head once and pushed himself up without taking the hand. The walls rolled past his eyes and then settled.

"Show me what failed," he said.

Bram studied him for a few breaths.

"Fine," Bram said at last. "But if you fall, try not to hit anything that holds weight."

Jhace loosened the wraps enough to let his fingers move. The cloth pulled at raw skin; pain bit up his forearms. The tremor did not care.

They stepped into the main tunnel.

The level had turned into a passage of constant motion. Runners carrying slates. Guild workers with coil and brace. Healers moving between makeshift nests with baskets of cloth and jars. Blood, hot metal, and root bitter thickened the usual damp-stone smell. The grid hum ran harsher, with edges where mains had been patched quick.

Heads turned as they passed.

"That's him," someone said behind them. "The holder from the kiln street."

Jhace kept his eyes forward.

"Where first," he asked.

"Nearest break we can reach," Bram said. "Two marry points took damage. One side support cracked. No spare crew for a full fix yet."

"Test," Jhace said. "We see if that thing listens without people under it."

The first junction had already been wedged and strapped. The main hummed steady. The brace that had sheared away vibrated on its own note. Jhace touched stone beside it.

Stress had moved into the emergency wedges. Imperfect. Stable enough.

"We move," he said.

The cracked side support sat deeper, in hotter air. Someone had tried to help by jamming broken stone under its base. Gaps showed. The line over it fed a crowded stretch of houses.

"No one on the floors above?" Jhace asked.

"Cleared after the first tremors," Bram said. "For now."

The fracture line at the base ran shallow but long. If it failed during the next Tide, the tunnel and part of the building wall would go with it.

Jhace set his palm on the split.

Stone met skin. Cool away from the crack. Warmer where it carried load. He felt the thin gap and its rough edges. His own shake blurred the reading.

Hold, he thought. Hold here. No one is under you yet, but they will be.

Nothing answered.

He pressed harder. Pain flared in his palm, then his wrist. The crack remained a crack. No heat surge. No copper-gold. Only the steady push of weight and his fingers jumping.

"Anything?" Bram asked.

"No," Jhace said.

"Good," Bram said. "I was half afraid you were about to say you can fix every hairline in this city by frowning at it."

Jhace pulled his hand away. His skin tingled. The stone stayed exactly as it was.

"Mark it," he said. "We reinforce it with tools."

They turned upslope.

They did not reach the surface before the next break found them.

A runner shot around a bend and nearly collided with them. A boy, sweat bright on his skin, breath tearing.

"You Jhace Torrins?" he gasped. "The one who held the building?"

Jhace's shoulders went tight.

"Yes," Bram said. "What's failing?"

"Shop near the Merro kiln corridor," the boy said. "Healers using it. Wall cracked. They say if we don't get you, we lose half the wounded when it drops."

Jhace started moving before Bram answered.

The kiln street looked worse than it had when they'd first climbed out.

Dust still hung in the air. A whole row of small fronts showed new cracks and missing corners. Someone had turned a narrow shop into a triage room; pallets and tables filled the inside space. The wall facing the street carried a thick fracture from lintel to ground.

People crowded the doorway. Two workers braced the wall with poles. Every shove made fine dust leak faster.

"Back," Jhace said.

His voice came rough and low. It did not clear the crowd.

Bram stepped up beside him.

"Back," Bram repeated, louder. "Give him the wall or it will take you with it."

The crowd shifted, grudging but real. Jhace stepped to the crack.

Closer, it was worse. The roof load had shifted onto two narrow contact points instead of the full length of wall. Inside, ceiling beams sat on that broken line. Several pallets lay under the center span.

Jhace placed both palms flat against the stone.

Cold outer face. Heat from wounded bodies and lamps inside. Heavy pressure from above.

Hold, he thought. They die if you do not hold.

Heat rose under his skin at once. Copper-gold slid across his hands and into the wall, fine streams chasing the fracture up and down. The glow reached the lintel, the base, then spread sideways into the adjoining blocks.

His jaw locked. His arms went rigid.

"Clear the center," Bram shouted. "Move the pallets to the sides. No arguments."

Voices and movement answered from inside. Cloth dragged. Someone cried out as they were shifted, then grunted and went quiet.

The wall pressed into his hands, the crack widening under load. He felt every point where load had narrowed, every place the stone barely held. His pulse beat into the contact, hard enough to make his fingers sting.

His shake worsened. Copper-gold lit the veins along his forearms, bright down to the elbow. Each heartbeat drove pain down those lines.

"Almost," Bram called. "Hold."

Jhace held.

The glow ran as far as it could find strain and stopped. It did not heal. It only pinned the worst angles in place.

"Clear," Bram shouted at last. "No one under the middle. Out."

Jhace counted three heartbeats more, then let go.

The copper-gold cut off. The wall shifted a short span and caught on a new lean. Dust dribbled. Loose chips fell and bounced on bare floor. The beams above groaned but stayed set.

Jhace's hands dropped. His fingers clawed and opened in fast, useless grabs.

"Out," Bram told the street. "No pallets under that span. You two—brace it with poles and proper wedges before next Pouring."

Jhace stepped back until his shoulders met the opposite wall. Sweat ran cold down his back. The edges of his vision went soft. His hands shook hard enough that his nails clicked together.

"Your arms lit," Bram said, low. "Vein-lines all the way up. You still with me?"

"For now," Jhace said. His tongue felt thick.

He looked down. Faint copper-gold lines still traced his forearms, pulsing with the tremor. They faded. The shake did not.

"Well," Bram said. "We learned the other half."

"What half," Jhace asked.

"Whatever this is," Bram said, "answers when people are about to get crushed. It ignores you when you want the nice clean fixes. And every time it answers, it takes more out of you."

Jhace stared at his hands until the last trace of color vanished, leaving raw skin and an unsteady twitch.

Someone cleared a throat.

The woman from the street stood near the cracked wall. Glass dust and copper shimmer threaded her hair. A logbook pressed against her chest. Her gaze moved from his hands to the stone.

"Jhace Torrins," she said.

He narrowed his eyes.

"You know my name," he said.

"You shouted it yesterday," she said. "Bram shouted it louder. Runners carried it. May I see where you touched?"

"No," Jhace said.

She watched him for a breath.

"I need to know what changed in the wall," she said. "Healers are using buildings you have… helped. If I know where your grip holds strongest, I can tell them where not to put beds."

That, Jhace could not argue with.

He turned back to the wall. One shaking finger rose to the point where the glow had felt thickest. The tremor made him miss twice. On the third try he hit the right strip.

"Here," he said. "Then down to here, and across."

She stepped closer, careful not to brush him. Her hand followed his path, then moved on, checking the stone in small presses.

"Hot band along your line," she murmured. "Cooler outside it. Stress is concentrated there. Load still carried, but the effective bearing length is small compared to the wall's full span. It should not stand on that narrow zone. But it does."

"It stands for now," Jhace said. "Do not trust it past that."

"I do not trust anything past that," she said. "But I prefer numbers to stories. Stories about you are already spreading."

"Stories are useless," Jhace said. "Either the brace holds or it does not."

"And if it only holds when you are here?" she asked.

He had no answer.

Her eyes dropped to his hands. The tremor still ran, fine and constant.

"Is that new?" she asked.

"Old injury," he said. "Tools."

She made a quiet sound that was not agreement.

"If you keep lying this badly," she said, "you make my work harder."

A voice called from farther up the street.

She glanced that way, then back.

"I am Tiffani Merrow," she said. "Glasswork and structural optics. I intend to keep people away from the first places your grips will fail. I need your routes and what you feel when you touch stone turned into marks."

"I have routes," Jhace said. "I do not have data."

"You have both," she said. "You just leave some of it in your bones."

She moved away, already reading the next damaged fronts. Bram watched her go.

"She will be trouble," he said.

Jhace did not answer.

A bent nail lay near his boot. He bent and reached for it.

His fingers missed twice. On the third try he caught it between thumb and forefinger. The tremor snapped. The nail jumped from his grip and tapped back onto stone.

He stared at it.

Bram's gaze slid to his hands.

Jhace straightened. He folded his hands behind his back, hiding the twitch.

"I am fine," he said.

Bram said nothing.

Across the street, Tiffani looked over. Her eyes dropped once to his hidden hands, then back to his face. She held his gaze for a breath, then turned away.

The grid hummed at its new pitch under their feet. The wall held. Jhace's grip did not.

---

### Chapter 5 – What Stays in Stone

The copper shimmer did not fade.

Tiffani crouched at the base of the wall Jhace Torrins had held the previous Tide and pressed her palm to the stone. Warmth bled through her skin. Not kiln heat. Not surface warming from the upper light. A band of elevated temperature ran along the fracture where his hands had been. Narrow, about the breadth of two knuckles, concentrated at the points where stress had peaked.

She pulled her hand back and logged the observation.

Tide 1, Pouring phase. Residual heat persists at contact points. Duration: minimum 18 phases post-activation. Pattern suggests localized energy retention in material structure.

Around her, the kiln corridor had turned into organized motion. Guild workers hauled broken stone into stacks. Healers moved between makeshift shelters. Runners carried slates between districts, their boots leaving new dust on old flagstones. The city had begun to function with fractures.

The fracture patterns were not matching any she trusted.

Tiffani stood and moved to the next marked site—a doorframe Jhace had stabilized during the night shift. The copper-gold traces here were fainter but still present, threading through mortar lines in patterns that followed load paths she knew from structural glass analysis. Where his hands had pressed, stress had shifted and settled into new paths. Where he had not touched, the cracks at the edges kept creeping.

She sketched the pattern in her logbook, comparing it to the previous site. The effective bearing zones were consistent: narrow bands of increased stability surrounded by long stretches of unchanged vulnerability. His contact had locked specific stress points in place without changing the broader load distribution.

A shadow fell across her page.

"Master Merrow."

She looked up.

Inspector Harlen stood with two guild officials, their vests marked with Merro Glass and Crown Engineering seals. Behind them, a younger clerk held a stack of blank slates.

"Inspector," Tiffani said. She did not rise. "I am in the middle of documentation."

"So I see." Harlen gestured at the chalk marks and her open book. "The RRO committee met during Kindling. Your name came up frequently. Master Corlis praised your precision. We need that precision applied to the formal survey."

"I am surveying," Tiffani said.

"Independently," one of the guild officials said. His tone treated the word as a flaw. "The committee is coordinating routes, assigning sectors, establishing reporting protocols. We cannot have scattered efforts."

Tiffani closed her logbook halfway, thumb holding her place.

"The fractures show where the stone failed," she said. "I am recording what they show. If your committee wants my data, I will share it. If you want me to stop collecting in order to fill out forms, the answer is no."

The clerk shifted his weight. The guild official's jaw tightened.

"The Crown wants counts," Harlen said, voice careful. "Compromised supports, households at risk, grid sections under threat. Your mapping is valuable, but we need it inside a larger frame. Join the committee officially. You will have assistants, access to archived plans, priority routing—"

"And constraints," Tiffani said. "Approved routes. Predetermined priorities. Reports that delay action while they pass through review."

"Guidelines," Harlen said. "We must manage panic as much as stone. If every craftsperson follows their own urgency, we lose coherence."

Tiffani looked past him to the wall she had just measured. The copper shimmer caught morning light, a thin band of new color across old stone.

"Inspector Harlen," she said. "How many buildings has your committee stabilized since forming?"

His expression flickered.

"The committee coordinates," he said. "Others execute."

"I thought so." She turned a page and kept sketching. "When your framework produces results, show them to me. Until then, I will keep recording what the fracture patterns preserve before they stop showing anything at all."

The guild official drew breath to argue. Harlen raised a hand.

"Master Merrow," he said quietly. "The RRO appreciates independence. We also appreciate order. Consider the offer. If you change your mind, the committee meets each Kindling in the Crown administrative hall."

He turned. The officials followed. The clerk cast one backward glance at her chalk marks before hurrying after them.

Tiffani waited until their footsteps faded, then marked the wall again, this time noting the angle of copper inclusion relative to the street slope.

She was logging the third site when Pella found her.

"Master Tiffani." Her apprentice picked her way through rubble, a wrapped package in one hand and exasperation on her face. "You said you would be at the workshop during Pouring. Master Corlis has been asking for you. The Crown commission panes need final inspection before they cool past measurement range."

"The panes are stable," Tiffani said without looking up. "The temperature gradient was correct when I checked them at Kindling. They will hold tolerance."

"He wants your signature on the approval slate."

"Then he can wait two phases." Tiffani drew another stress line on the page. "Stone that is about to crush people takes priority over glass that is already behaving."

Pella set the package down—bread and cheese, from the smell—and crouched beside her. Her gaze followed the chalk on the wall, then moved to the marks in the logbook.

"What are you mapping?" Pella asked.

"Evidence," Tiffani said. "The man who held these walls left traces. Heat signatures. Copper residue. Stress redistribution patterns. If I can quantify where his contact stabilizes and where it does not, I can predict which held sections will fail first."

Pella's eyes widened.

"You mean the holder—Jhace Torrins—he is not fixing the stone?" she asked. "He only delays the collapse?"

"Correct." Tiffani tapped her pen against a marked node. "His contact creates narrow zones of temporary stability. The load paths beyond those bands do not change. The fractures do not seal. He is holding weight in place through direct force that does not match any mechanical principle I can name yet."

"That sounds dangerous."

"It is," Tiffani said. "For him, and for anyone who trusts a wall he has touched without understanding how short-term his intervention is. Which is why I am documenting the pattern before people start depending on it."

Pella nodded slowly.

"The RRO wants you to stop?" she asked.

"The RRO wants me to join a committee and report through channels." Tiffani took the bread, tore off a piece, and chewed. "By the time my observations pass through review, three more buildings will have collapsed and people will wonder why no one warned them."

"So you are refusing."

"I am choosing urgency over procedure." Tiffani met her apprentice's gaze. "There is a difference."

Pella's mouth curved.

"Everyone says you are impossible," she said.

"Everyone lacks precision," Tiffani said. She swallowed and reached for the cheese. "If you see Master Corlis, tell him I will inspect the panes before Shaping. If he cannot wait that long, tell him to find someone else whose standards he trusts less."

Pella shook her head but picked up the empty wrappings with care.

"I will tell him you are serving the public safety," she said. "He can argue with that if he wishes."

She walked away toward the main kiln corridor.

Tiffani returned to the wall.

The copper band here sat higher than at the first site, closer to the lintel. She pressed her palm against the stone. Warm. The same width. The same residual hum, just below hearing, traveling into her bones.

She wrote: Held zone narrow. Adjacent cracks remain active. Advise healers to avoid placing pallets within one band width of edge.

She was measuring the fourth site when she heard boots on broken stone and looked up to find Jhace Torrins standing three paces away, watching her work.

He looked worse than he had the previous Tide. Dark lines under his eyes. Dust ground into his coat. His hands, wrapped in fresh bandages, trembled faintly at his sides. His gaze stayed sharp, moving from her chalk marks to the wall to her open logbook.

"You are mapping my routes," he said.

It was not a question.

"Yes," Tiffani said. She did not apologize. "You are leaving evidence. I am recording it."

"Why."

"Because people are using buildings you held," she said. "If I know where your stabilization is weakest, I can warn them before the temporary hold fails."

He stared at her for a long moment, then moved closer and crouched beside the wall. His bandaged fingers hovered above the copper line without touching stone.

"This is where I pressed," he said quietly. "Here, and here." He indicated two points along the fracture. "The break was trying to widen. I felt it pushing."

"And you pushed back," Tiffani said.

"Yes."

"For how long?"

"Until they cleared the wounded." His jaw tightened. "Three phases, maybe four. I do not remember cleanly. It hurt."

Tiffani wrote. Duration correlates with residual heat intensity. Longer contact = stronger thermal signature. Cost to user: pain, tremor, memory blur.

She looked up again.

"How many sites have you held since the Piercing?" she asked.

He hesitated.

"Seven," he said. "Maybe eight. Some short. Two longer."

"And the tremor?"

His hands curled into loose fists, hiding the shake.

"Started after the second," he said. "Worsened after each one. It does not stop now."

Her pen scratched.

Progressive cost accumulation. No observed recovery between uses. Tremor baseline established. Predicted trajectory: continued deterioration with repeated activation.

"You are cataloging my deterioration," Jhace said. His tone stayed flat, but there was no heat in it.

"I am cataloging the pattern," Tiffani said. "Your deterioration is part of that pattern. If you are going to keep doing this—and you are—then someone needs to document what it costs. Otherwise you will spend yourself without knowing how near you are to failure."

"You think I am close," he said.

"I think you used this ability eight times in one Tide, and your hands have not been still since," she said. "That suggests you are approaching a threshold. I do not yet know where it lies. I am trying to find out before you cross it."

Silence settled around them. The corridor carried hammers, calling voices, the roughened thrum of the grid. Dust hung in narrow beams of light from a cracked upper vent.

"What do you need," Jhace asked at last.

The question surprised her more than any refusal would have.

"Your routes," she said. "Every site you held. Contact duration. Your sense of the load before and after. Any place where the glow started weaker, or refused you altogether. And—" She paused. "Leave markers when you plan to intervene next. I need to examine the walls before you touch them, not just after."

"You want to watch me fail," he said.

"I want to see the mechanism," Tiffani said. "Success and failure both produce data. If I only examine stable holds, I will miss half of what matters."

He looked back at the copper band.

"There is a section in the Underface," he said slowly. "Grid line under the Dregs. Main support is cracked. I marked it before the Piercing. It held then. It will not much longer. When it goes, that line ruptures. The district loses heat."

"When are you planning to reinforce it?" she asked.

"Next Kindling," he said. "If the braces hold that long."

"If they do not?"

"Then I move sooner," he said. "With or without you."

Tiffani considered the fracture in front of her, the heat under her palm, the steady shake in his bandaged hands. She pictured a support under the Dregs carrying more load than it should, trying to fail in the dark where no one from the RRO had drawn a neat grid yet.

"Leave word with Bram when you go," she said. "I will meet you at the ladder shaft before you drop."

He frowned.

"You want to be in the Underface," he said. "With a crack just before it fails."

"Yes," she said. "With instruments. With glass. With marks I can trust more than rumors."

"That is not how most people answer," he said.

"Most people did not spend three Anneals watching an oven whisper before it tore a wall down," she said. "Marisse asked if it worried me. I said no. I called signal noise. She died when it let go. I will not call this noise."

His gaze held on her face for several breaths, as if weighing the line between her words and her motives.

"You take a long path to say you care whether I break," he said.

"Care is a poor planning term," she said. "Break is measurable."

For the first time, his mouth twitched in something that almost reached amusement.

"You walk my routes," he said. "You see the worst points. Then you tell me where not to stand. That is the work you are asking for."

"Yes," she said. "And I tell healers where not to put beds. And I tell Harlen where his neat counts do not match what the fracture patterns preserve."

He pushed himself to his feet. The motion carried a small hitch, a moment where his balance checked before it settled.

"I keep the right to say no," he said. "If your questions ask for more than the wall does."

"You do," Tiffani said. "And I keep the right to tell you when your hands are past their safe load, even if you lie about it."

"How will you know I am lying," he asked.

She closed her logbook and slipped it under her arm.

"Glass resonates at specific pitches," she said. "When you start to crack, I will hear it."

He huffed a breath that might have been a laugh.

"You are impossible," he said.

"Everyone says so," she replied. "Most of them are still standing."

He turned toward the Underface ladder shaft. She watched the tremor in his hands as he walked, then dropped her gaze back to the copper bands on the wall.

The city's new hum ran under her feet, in the stone, in the glass, and, now, in the way Jhace Torrins's fingers refused to rest. One pattern. Many failure points.

She drew a line under the day's notes and wrote:

Working rule: Held walls remain dangerous. Use his grip as last resort, never first. Begin structured joint survey next Kindling.

She underlined last resort, then shut the book.

For now, the walls stayed up. They would not stay that way without work.

---

### Chapter 6 – The Weight That Won't Yield

The Underface smelled wrong.

Not the usual damp stone and kiln exhaust. Something sharper. Metallic. The taste sat on Jhace's tongue and stayed. He had tasted it once before, in the span after the Piercing when dust rained copper and the upper bands of the sky warped.

He stood at the base of the cracked support and ran his palm along the fracture line. The stone vibrated under his touch at a pitch that had not been there two Tides ago. The main pipe overhead hummed higher than the lines to either side. Load that should have spread across three supports had settled into this one failing column.

Behind him, Tiffani measured the junction with a cord marked in hand-widths. Her logbook lay open on a dry ledge, weighted with a chunk of rubble. She had reached the ladder shaft before Kindling ended, carrying glass plates wrapped in cloth and a stub of chalk that put white on everything she touched.

"Fracture depth?" she asked without looking up.

"Three fingers at the widest," Jhace said. "Shallow at the base. Deeper near the brace."

"Load concentration?"

He pressed harder and read the push under his palm.

"High," he said. "The other supports are carrying less than they should. This one is taking the extra."

She made a mark. Crossed to the support and placed her own hand beside his, close without touching. Her fingers hovered a breath from the surface.

"Residual warmth," she said. "Faint. You touched this before."

"Last Anneal," Jhace said. "Before the Piercing. Hairline only. I marked it to watch."

"It is not for watching now," she said.

"No."

She pulled her hand back and sketched the fracture pattern. Pen strokes quick, exact.

"The crack runs up toward the pipe anchor and down into the foundation," she said. "If it fails at the anchor first, the pipe torques. Joints split. Heat vents into the tunnel and into the blocks above."

"I know," Jhace said.

"If it fails at the base, the support drops," she said. "The adjacent columns do not have slack to take the full load. Junction folds. Three mains rupture."

"I know," he said again.

His hands curled and opened. The tremor ran through the motion even when he tried to hold still.

Tiffani studied his fingers, then his face.

"How many phases since you last used the grip?" she asked.

"Eighteen. Maybe twenty," he said.

"And the tremor?"

"Constant."

She wrote. Set down the pen. Unwrapped one of the glass plates from her satchel. The plate was thin, etched with a grid of fine lines.

"What is that?" Jhace asked.

"Stress plate," Tiffani said. "If I place it against the stone while you work, the grid will distort where stress concentrates. It will show me where you are applying force and where the stone answers."

"You want to measure me," he said.

"I want to see the mechanism," she said. "If I know where your contact is most effective, I can tell you where to press next time. Less waste. Less cost."

"You cannot reduce this cost," Jhace said. "I have tried."

"You have tried alone," she said. She held his gaze. "Let me help."

Above them, the pipe gave a low groan. Grit sifted from the brace and pattered onto the floor.

Jhace looked at the fracture. At the load coming down from the Dregs. At the blocks overhead where people slept through a hum they did not understand.

Twenty-three names sat behind his teeth. He was not taking more.

"Fine," he said. "But if the column goes, you run. Do not stay to measure a fall."

"Agreed," Tiffani said.

She pressed the stress plate against the stone beside the fracture and held it steady with one hand.

"Proceed when ready," she said.

Jhace knelt and set his palms flat over the widest stretch of crack.

Stone met skin. Cold. Rough. Full of moving weight.

Hold, he thought. Long enough.

Heat built under his hands. At first it felt like friction. Then it sharpened and sank into bone. Copper-gold light spilled out from under his fingers and drove into the fracture. The glow traced the crack upward to the brace and downward into the foundation, following stress lines he could feel even if he did not know their names.

His arms locked. His jaw clenched. The tremor in his hands jumped into a hard shake that climbed his forearms.

Vein-lines lit under his skin, bright copper from wrist to elbow.

The stone pushed back.

Not as a shove he could see. Deeper than that. The load pressed to widen the crack, shifting where it had been held too long. He held it in place with heat and something that felt like his whole chest pressing through his hands.

"Grid distortion visible," Tiffani said. Her voice stayed level. "Stress concentrated at three nodes. Primary contact is strong. North side shows less distortion. You are giving too much there for too little gain."

Jhace could not spare breath to answer. Sweat ran into his eyes. Pressure beat behind them in time with his heart.

"Shift your left hand," Tiffani said. "Two hand-widths toward the brace. Put more into the upper node."

He tried.

His left hand stayed where it was, locked by strain. Copper-gold flared brighter. Pain shot along the arm in a tight, hot line.

"Jhace," Tiffani said. Her tone changed, sharp and close. "Your vein-lines are too bright. Pull back before—"

The pipe overhead shrieked.

Not the support. A joint three junction spans upslope. Pressure tore it open. Superheated air vented in a hard white jet that filled the tunnel with scalding mist.

Jhace's focus broke. The glow around his fingers stuttered. The support shifted a fraction under his hands.

"No," he forced out.

He drove his palms harder into the stone. Copper-gold surged back, brighter than before. His vision narrowed to contact points and light. Heat, pressure, weight. Sound dulled. The world reduced to contact and strain.

"Secondary rupture," Tiffani shouted. Her voice came through the roar of venting air. "The brace is pulling free. If it goes, the pipe drops onto the support. Then we lose all of it."

Jhace felt it in the stone. The brace pulling free of its seat. The pipe starting to sag. Load on the column doubling by the heartbeat.

He could not hold both. Support or pipe. Choose, and the other failed.

"Where," he shouted. His throat scraped.

"Not you," Tiffani said. "You keep the column. I mark the brace."

She left the stress plate in place and ran into the mist. She pressed her palm to the wall near the anchor, traced load with her fingers, then slashed an X in chalk on the stone.

"Here," she called. "Maximum strain at this point. If someone can lock this, the brace holds. The pipe stays."

Jhace stared at the mark. It sat three paces away. He could not reach it without letting go.

If he let go, the column failed. If he held the column, the brace failed. Either way put people under falling stone.

His hands shook so hard the copper glow trembled with the motion.

"I cannot reach both," he said.

"Then you hold the support," Tiffani said. "I brace the joint."

"You cannot—"

She was already moving. She snatched a length of broken pipe from the rubble, wedged it under the sagging brace, and leaned into it with her weight. Metal screamed. The wedge bit. The brace held, for the moment.

"This buys you thirty heartbeats," she shouted. "Make them count."

Jhace dragged his attention back to the crack.

The fracture had widened while his mind was split. Fine lines at the edges had opened. The stone was failing faster now.

Hold.

The copper-gold brightened until it washed detail away. It drove deeper into the break, into joints above and foundation below. His veins burned. His breath shortened. The hum in his ears turned to a solid, grinding tone.

Ten heartbeats.

The support slowed its shift.

Twenty.

The crack stopped moving. The glow traced each stress line and pinned it in place. The column still carried too much, but it held.

Thirty.

The brace groaned. Tiffani's wedge bent. She shifted her stance and put her shoulder into the metal, jaw clenched, boots sliding on wet stone.

"Jhace," she said. The strain pulled at her words. "I am losing it."

Forty.

The vented heat thinned. The roar eased down to a rough hiss. Pressure in the line dropped.

Fifty.

"Now," Tiffani said. "Let go now or it will take more than your hands."

Jhace tore his palms away from the stone.

The copper-gold cut out. The support settled in a slow, grinding drop and caught on a new, uneven seat. The brace over it shuddered once and stayed. The main pipe rocked and then held. A few shards of stone broke free and shattered on the tunnel floor, but nothing larger followed.

Jhace's arms gave out.

He dropped to his side. The tunnel spun, then steadied in a narrow circle around him. His hands clawed at the floor in fast, useless grabs. The tremor ran from fingertips to shoulders, sharp and continuous. Vein-lines still glowed under his skin, bright copper along each path.

Tiffani let the wedge take the load and pushed herself upright. Her own arms shook. She crossed to him and dropped to a crouch at his side.

"Vein-lines are still active," she said. Her voice stayed steady, but there was less air in it. "They are not fading."

"Usually fade in breaths," Jhace managed. "A few dozen at most."

"How many now?" she asked.

He tried to count back. The heartbeats blurred.

"Too many," he said.

She pulled off her outer tunic and wrapped it around his hands, covering the worst of the glow. The cloth darkened as copper seeped into it.

"You held past the point I warned you," she said. "Fifty when thirty would have been enough."

"The brace was failing," he said. "Could not let it go."

"The brace was my problem," she said. "The support was yours. You tried to solve both and nearly broke yourself."

He let out a rough breath that could have been a laugh.

"That is what I do," he said.

"Not anymore," Tiffani said. She shifted her grip and steadied his wrists. "You have a partner now. I mark the strain. You push where I tell you. We divide the work or we both fail."

The word caught him.

"Partner," he said. His tongue felt thick.

"Yes," she said. "You keep people from being crushed in the moment. I try to keep you from destroying your own hands fast enough that you drop them anyway."

The tremor in his arms eased by slow degrees. The copper lines under his skin lost brightness. They did not vanish. Faint bands remained along the veins, darker than they had been before this hold.

Tiffani watched them, jaw tight.

"Cost increase confirmed," she said. "Vein-mark persistence extended beyond prior uses. Baseline tremor worsened."

"You are still writing in your head," Jhace said.

"Always," she said.

He tried to push himself up. His elbows slipped. She caught his shoulder and helped him sit.

"I can manage," he said.

"I know," she said. "You do not have to."

He leaned back against the wall. The stone was warm where the stress had run through it. The crack no longer moved under his palm. It was still a break. It would still need wedges and braces if it was to hold past the next Tide.

"Support is stable for now," Tiffani said. She pressed her hand to the stone beside his. "The worst of the load is pinned. We will get proper braces in before we trust it with more."

"We," he said.

"Yes," she said.

He looked at her.

"You could have gone upslope," he said. "Left this to fail."

"So could you," she said. "You did not. That tells me enough about how you will act when I am not watching. I prefer to be watching."

He let his head rest against the stone.

"You are offering to help me ruin myself more slowly," he said.

"I am offering to help you hold more walls with the same ruin," she said. "There is a difference."

"You keep saying that."

"Because you keep pretending cost does not matter," she said. "I am going to write every one of your tremors down until you stop pretending."

He closed his eyes for a moment. The hum of the grid pressed through the floor into his spine. It carried the new pitch he had first felt at Junction Twelve and now knew in his bones.

"When I crack," he said, "what then?"

"Then we make sure the city is not balanced on only your hands," Tiffani said. "We train others to read what you read. We force the RRO to listen. We build other supports."

"You think they will listen," Jhace said.

"They will listen to failure counts," she said. "Harlen already does. Corlexi will pretend he is making the decisions, but he will follow the numbers."

"You trust them," Jhace said.

"I trust numbers," she said. "And I trust what I saw when you put your hands on that wall. You will keep doing it whether I plan for it or not. Better that I plan."

His hands still shook under the cloth. Not as violently. Enough that he doubted his grip on a wrench, let alone on another failing span.

"Can you stand?" she asked.

"Not well," he said.

"Good," she said. "We will go slow. I do not want you falling into a junction we just saved."

She rose and offered him her arm. After a moment, he took it. His weight leaned heavier on her than he wanted, but her stance stayed steady.

They started toward the ladder shaft.

Behind them, the support they had held hummed with trapped strain. Copper traces glowed faint along the stone where his hands had been. The crack remained, locked in a new, temporary balance that would need metal and effort to keep.

"The city is still breaking," Tiffani said as they walked. "You will keep trying to hold it. I will keep measuring the cost."

"You call that partnership," he said.

"I call that work," she said. "Partnership is the part where you listen when I say stop."

He almost smiled.

"We will see," he said.

Above, somewhere beyond the Underface ceiling, the sky carried a faint bright scar where the Piercing had passed. Below, in the tunnels, the grid kept its altered pitch. Between those two changed layers, they walked. One pair of hands already marked. One mind already counting.

The weight did not yield. They would hold what they could, as long as they could, and write down what it took.

Based on the project knowledge, here’s the **N01 Interlude Blueprint**:

## **N01: “The Grip That Holds” - Interlude Structure**

### **INTERLUDE I: “Dissolution and Care”**

**Placement:** After Chapter 3 (or possibly Chapter 5 - documents show variation)  
**Type:** Build-Up  
**God-Vessel Pairing:** Salee (Wholeness) + Eries (Entropy)

**Structure:**

```
SALEE (Wholeness) POV - ~400-650 words
[PAGE BREAK / SECTION DIVIDER]
ERIES (Entropy) POV - ~400-650 words
```

**Content:**

- **Salee Section:** In southern communes, connecting survivors after the Piercing, building networks, realizing she cannot save everyone, feeling the weight of care-obligation drowning her
- **Eries Section:** Watching a failing Fringe settlement, encouraging local groups to “walk away, let this die,” seeing beauty in controlled collapse, understanding destruction as redistribution

**Purpose:** Mirror Jhace/Tiffani’s choices - tension between saving everything vs. letting some things go. Shows Wholeness tempting dissolution and Entropy tempting justified collapse.

-----

### **INTERLUDE II: “Hinges”**

**Placement:** After Chapter 11  
**Type:** Break-Down  
**God-Vessel Pairing:** Corlexi (Form) + Mobel (Purity)

**Structure:**

```
CORLEXI (Form) POV - ~400-650 words
[PAGE BREAK / SECTION DIVIDER]
MOBEL (Purity) POV - ~400-650 words
```

**Content:**

- **Corlexi Section:** Crown infrastructure hub, reviewing stabilization reports with satisfaction, pleased at RRO formation, noting Jhace/Tiffani as “anomalies requiring future action” - they won’t fit her geometry
- **Mobel Section:** Entry-side cleansing rituals, participating in purity tests, sensing corruption spreading through infrastructure, awareness that “there are others like me” awakening

**Purpose:** Show Form’s institutional control emerging and Purity’s awakening awareness. Gods are waking because Jhace/Tiffani’s commitment is changing the planet’s resonance baseline.

-----

## **Key Structural Rules**

**Format per Interlude:**

- Total word count: 800-1,300 words
- Split between two god POVs (~400-650 each)
- Narrated by the Auditor (her voice frames both sections)
- Separated by page break/section divider

**Coverage Strategy:**

- **Interlude I:** Salee + Eries (street-level operators, Wholeness + Entropy)
- **Interlude II:** Corlexi + Mobel (institutional power, Form + Purity)
- **Result:** All four gods featured across N01
- **Epilogue:** Auditor tracks all four’s status post-Piercing

**Pairing Logic for N01:**

- Opposing/complementary dynamics: Wholeness (save everything) vs. Entropy (let things die), Form (order) vs. Purity (separation)
- Thematic mirror: Both interludes reflect the main story’s tension between holding vs. releasing

-----

## **Auditor’s Voice in Interludes**

The Auditor narrates all interlude content, framing the god-vessels’ experiences. Her tone:

- Observational, sardonic, knowing
- Not revealing her true identity yet
- Tracking divine awakening progression
- Notes limitations: “Salee holds too much. Eries lets go too easily. Neither is balanced.”

**Example internal assessment:**

> *Two gods are waking. Salee holds too much. Eries lets go too easily. Neither is balanced. Neither understands the other. When they collide, the mortals will be caught between save-everything and let-everything-die. That is the stage being set. Interesting.*

This establishes the pattern for all 30 novellas - each novella showcases all four god-vessels across two interludes, with the Auditor’s omniscient perspective framing their progression toward full divine awakening.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

[ASSISTANT]
Interlude I – Dissolution and Care  

⸻

Salee

By the first full Tide after the Piercing, Salee’s world had narrowed to three tasks: count who was still breathing, find who could help them breathe tomorrow, and do not break in the space between.

The southern communes did not crack in clean lines the way the Crown towers had. They frayed. Stone fronts slumped instead of falling. Irrigation channels clogged. Footpaths vanished under drifted dust. A dozen small failures spread across the slope instead of one grand collapse.

Salee moved between them with a slate, a bundle of binding cord, and a tongue that would not let her go quiet.

“You have grain,” she told a cluster of elders in a half-collapsed hall. “They have clean water and tools. Trade shifts, not pity. Three households from the low ditch move into your empty rooms. In return, your sons help clear their well.”  

She drew the lines on the slate so they could see the exchange—who owed hours, who owed food. No one left blank. No one written only on the “give” side.

A child watched from the doorway, eyes ringed in dust.

“Who writes for us?” the child asked when the elders turned away.

“You do,” Salee said. “You come to the next counting and say what you can carry and what you cannot.”

The child’s throat worked. No sound came.

Salee knew that feeling. The weight that sat where words should have come, thick and cold. She set the slate down, crouched, and took the child’s hand.

Copper-gold tingled at the edge of her skin. It wanted to move. She did not let it. This did not need that kind of breakage.

“You do not owe them strength,” she said. “You owe them truth. If you can only haul one bucket in a whole phase, say it. If you can watch smaller ones while others haul, say that. No one here stands alone and no one here stands for free.”

The child nodded, once. That was enough for now.

Salee stood and moved on.

By mid-Striking she had crossed four communes. Her slate carried marks for shared ovens, pooled tools, rotation schedules for watching the worst cracks during night phases. Every few spans, someone stopped her with a hand on her sleeve.

“My mother cannot stand.”

“My son is missing.”

“My neighbor is hoarding.”

Each tug on her sleeve woke the copper-gold again. Each time she held it back until she knew whether flesh or stone stood closest to failure.

At one field’s edge, the irrigation channel had collapsed into a narrow ravine. The water still ran, but in a path too deep for buckets. Three people stood staring down at the loss, hands empty.

Salee knelt at the broken lip. The clay walls had sheared away. The channel lining had split along its length. This would not be patched in a day.

She set both palms on the edge.

Hold, she thought. Not forever. Just long enough.

Heat answered. Copper-gold pulsed under her skin and into the cracked clay, tracing its weakness. The broken lip firmed under her hands. A rough span of wall stopped crumbling and took her weight.

It did not mend. It only stopped getting worse.

“Boards,” she said through clenched teeth. “From the storage shed. Lay them across while I keep this steady. You will not lift water from this cut, but you can cross it instead of losing a whole day circling.”

The three ran. Brought planks. Laid them in place. By the time the makeshift bridge held on its own, her hands shook and copper threads traced her forearms from wrist to elbow.

“You could have rebuilt the wall,” one of them said when he saw the faint glow.

“No,” Salee said. Her breath came shallow. “Not without dropping somewhere else. This buys you time. Use it.”

She walked away before they could call her a savior.

By Quench her legs ached, her hands burned, and her slate was full. Names, households, shifts, bridges, watch rotations. Each mark connected one person’s strain to another’s capacity. Each line meant she had stepped between one failure and the next.

At the edge of the last commune, she sat on a low stone and let her shoulders sag. Copper-gold still flickered under her skin in thin, restless threads. Every cry, every plea from the day clung to her. There were more roofs leaning than she had claws to catch. More empty beds than spare blankets.

She could feel other breaks, farther along the slope. Communities she had not reached yet. Pulls on the same copper threads that ran through her hands, all of them demanding: here, here, here.

She pressed her palms together hard enough that her fingers hurt.

“I cannot carry all of you,” she said under her breath. “But you do not get to fall for free.”

Somewhere beyond her reach a wall tipped from strain into failure. She felt the snap through the copper lines and flinched. Someone screamed. She did not know their name.

She would come tomorrow. Or the next Tide. She would mark what remained and tie it into what could still stand.

She would not leave a single life uncounted if she could help it.

She would also break if she kept answering every pull.

Two truths. Both solid. She held them in the same tired hands and did not yet know where to set either down.

The one watching her did.

*Salee’s problem is not that she cannot save enough,* the Auditor thought. *It is that she will not let anything dissolve without trying to stitch it back into whole. Even when the fibers are rotten. Even when the weight of the weave is cracking her own bones.*

*She calls it care. The word fits. It also hides the cost.*

⸻

Eries

Far north of Salee’s routes, on the outer edge of Saint Merro’s reach, a small settlement clung to bad ground.

It had never been a good place to build. Shallow soil. Old mining cuts half-filled with loose rubble. A heat line that barely brushed the edge of the district. The Piercing had not so much struck it as reminded it of everything it already was.

Three houses leaned inward where the old cuts had settled. One storage hall listed over an undermined corner. A meeting shed had lost half its roof. Nothing dramatic. Nothing that would draw a Crown inspector. Just a slow, certain sag toward collapse.

Eries stood on the only intact porch and watched the way the structures moved.

Every few breaths, a small shift. A beam sliding past its old seat. A stone settling deeper. Dust spilling from a fresh hairline.

“Can you fix it?” a woman asked behind him.

Her hands were raw. Rope burn. She had spent the morning hauling what she could from the listing storage hall.

“I can,” Eries said.

He did not say whether he would.

She stared past him at the buildings.

“My father put these up before I was born,” she said. “We added to them each Anneal. We buried three here. We cannot just abandon it.”

“You can,” Eries said. “The river path is four days north. There are communes with better soil and better feed heat. You can walk with what you can carry and start over on ground that does not slide under you.”

She flinched as if he had struck her.

“This is home,” she said.

He stepped down from the porch and crossed the packed dirt to the nearest failing house. Its corner sat over one of the old cuts. The earth under that stone had turned to powder. The house was already halfway gone; the rest just had not followed yet.

He pressed his palm to the wall.

Cracks ran through the stone and timber. Not new. Not Piercing-born. Old strain, old shortcuts, old patches. The ground under it shifted in faint, constant rolls as rubble settled in the dark.

He could hold it. Copper-gold stirred under his skin, hungry for a place to go. He could pour it into those cracks, pin them, give the family two more Anneals here. Maybe three.

Then the cut would widen. Or the next storm would bring the rest down at once. Or the heat line would hiccup and they would freeze in walls that did not hold warmth.

He took his hand away.

“No,” he said.

The woman grabbed his sleeve.

“You just said you could,” she said. “You people are holding up buildings in the Crown. I heard it. You set your hands and the walls stay. Why not here? Are we not worth your work?”

He faced her fully.

“You are worth more than this ground,” he said. “That is why you walk away from it.”

She shook her head, breathing hard.

“We have buried here,” she said. “We have planted. We have named these stones. How do we leave that in a heap?”

“By deciding their work is done,” Eries said. “You do not honor the dead by dying under the same failed beams.”

She looked past him again. At the tilt of the houses. At the storage hall, where a fresh crack had opened since she last checked. At the children sitting on an overturned crate with empty bowls in their hands.

“What do we eat on the road?” she asked. “What do we sleep under?”

“You eat what you can carry and what those ahead of you can spare,” Eries said. “You sleep under cloth and sky until you reach better walls. Or you stay, and you eat less each Tide and wake up one Striking with the roof on your chest.”

Her jaw clenched.

“You speak like someone who has already chosen for us,” she said.

“No,” Eries said. “I speak as someone who knows what these walls cost to keep standing. And what else that cost could buy if you let them fall.”

He walked to the storage hall.

Two men were bracing a leaning post with scavenged poles. Each shove sent a small shower of dust down from above.

“Stop,” he said.

One glared at him.

“If this goes, we lose half our grain,” the man said. “Who are you to tell us to leave it?”

“The one who can drop it without killing you,” Eries said.

He set his hand on the post. Copper-gold ran from his skin into the wood. The lines it traced were different from Jhace’s holds: not filling strain, but outlining it, marking each weak join.

“Pull the sacks out from that side,” he said, nodding to the safer corner. “Stack them by the road. Anything you cannot move in one phase, leave.”

They hesitated, then obeyed. Fear of collapse overruled pride.

When the last sack cleared the danger zone, Eries took a breath and pushed.

Not a hold, this time. A release.

He sent the copper-gold into the highest fracture, widened it, let gravity take what it already wanted. The post split. The overloaded roof frame dropped in stages instead of one crushing fall. Beams slid, caught, slid again, then settled in a broken heap. Dust billowed. No one stood under it.

The woman stared at the ruin.

“You destroyed our hall,” she said.

“It was already destroyed,” Eries said. “You were just still inside it.”

He wiped his palm on his coat. The copper lines under his skin dimmed, but did not fade entirely. Using the grip this way carried its own cost. His fingers shook once, then steadied.

“Walk north,” he said. “Today. Do not wait for another Tide. Carry what food you can. Leave the rest for the ground and the birds.”

“And if the communes turn us away?” one of the men asked.

“Some will,” Eries said. “Others will not. Some settlements are ready to dissolve and feed their neighbors. Some are ready to take in what breaks loose. That is how this slope survives.”

He looked over the small cluster of houses again. No structure here deserved another heartbeat of copper.

“There is nothing wrong with letting this die,” he said. “What you keep living is more important than what you keep standing.”

They did not thank him. They gathered children and packs in a hard, quiet rush. They did not look back often.

Eries watched them until they topped the low rise and started down the far side toward the river path.

He felt the empty structures behind him. The strain that had held them upright relaxed. Timber shifted, stone slumped. In another Anneal there would be only low piles and shallow depressions in the ground, and a few beams worth salvaging if someone passed by with a cart.

He felt no urge to shore any of it up.

Some things needed to run their full course into collapse so the pieces could move again.

*Eries is not cruel,* the Auditor thought. *He sees the end of things and refuses to pretend it is not coming. He cuts before the rot spreads. He makes space for what comes next.*

*Salee binds. Eries severs. They both think they are saving lives. They are both right. They are both wrong.*

*They have not met yet. When they do, entire districts will find themselves caught between their hands.*

Got you. No Auditor here, just the god-vessels in tight third.

Here’s **Interlude I – Dissolution and Care** rewritten as two tight POV segments, Salee then Eries, no omniscient frame, no Auditor voice.

---

### Interlude I – Dissolution and Care

#### Salee

By the first full Tide after the Piercing, Salee’s world had thinned to three tasks: count who still stood, find who could help them stand tomorrow, and stay on her own feet long enough to finish both.

The southern communes did not break in one clean fall. They slumped. A lean-to shed here, a cracked cistern there, a path washed out where a channel wall had given way. Nothing large enough to draw Crown attention. Enough small failures to pull at her from all sides.

She moved between them with a slate, a bundle of cord, and a throat gone raw from speaking.

“You have grain in the back room,” she told the elders in a half-collapsed meeting hall. “They have clean water and tools. You share. Three households from the low ditch move into your empty beds. In return, your younger ones help clear their well and reinforce the mouth.”

She showed it on the slate so they could see it, not just hear it. Names, marks, arrows. No household written only where it gave. No household written only where it took.

A child stood in the doorway, hand on the frame, eyes dark in a dust-pale face.

“Who writes for us?” the child asked when the elders turned away.

“You do,” Salee said.

The answer came without thought. It felt like the only solid thing in reach.

The child’s mouth tightened. No words followed.

Salee knew that silence. The weight that sat where speech should have been. She set the slate down, crouched, and took the child's hand gently between both of hers.

Copper-gold tingled along her palms. It wanted to move. It always did now, when skin met skin and someone pulled on her with need. She held it still.

“You do not owe them strength,” she said. “You owe them truth. If you can only haul one bucket in a whole phase, say one. If you can watch smaller ones while others haul, say that. No one here stands alone. No one here stands for free.”

The child nodded, once. Not much, but the shoulders loosened a fraction.

Salee stood and moved on.

By mid-Striking she had crossed four communes. Her slate carried new routes for shared ovens, pooled tools, rotations for watching the worst cracks through night phases. Every few spans someone caught her sleeve.

“My mother cannot walk to the well.”

“My son has not come back from the fields.”

“My neighbor is hiding sacks.”

Each voice woke that copper-gold again. Each time she had to decide: talk, or touch. Link hands and households, or lay her palms on stone and give in to the urge to press her heat into it.

At one irrigation run, the channel wall had fallen away into a narrow gash. Water still flowed, but deep and fast, out of reach. Three people stood staring down at the loss, shoulders bent.

Salee knelt at the break.

The clay lining had split and slumped. The edge crumbled under her thumb. This was not work for one phase and a few spare bricks.

She put both palms on the ragged lip.

Hold, she thought. Not forever. Just until they can cross.

Heat answered. Copper-gold rose under her skin and flowed into the clay. Fine lines of glow traced the hairline fractures, stalled their spread, stiffened the edge beneath her hands. The wall did not mend. It stopped giving way.

“Boards,” she said through tight teeth. “From the storage lean-to. Lay them here. You will not lift water from this cut, but you can reach the other side without losing half a day circling.”

They ran. Brought planks. Laid them in place. By the time the bridge carried its own weight, her hands shook and copper threads marked her forearms from wrist to elbow. The world leaned for a moment, then righted.

“You could have fixed it,” one of them said, staring at the faint glow fading in the clay where her palms had been.

“No,” Salee said. “I could have held it longer and dropped somewhere else. This gives you time. Use it.”

She did not stay for thanks. She did not want the way people looked at her when they saw the copper.

By Quench, her legs ached, her back burned, and the slate felt heavier than it should. Names, counts, shared tools, shared roofs. So many lines. So many small knots tied so nothing slipped free unnoticed.

At the edge of the last commune she reached that Tide, she sat on a low stone, set the slate down, and let her shoulders drop forward. Copper-gold still flickered faintly along the veins in her hands, restless, answering pulls she could not place.

She could feel other breaks farther downslope. An old roof beam somewhere had given up. A cistern lining had cracked. She felt the small releases as quick stings under her skin, little snaps in the copper that did not belong to any one body.

She pressed her palms together until her fingers hurt.

“I cannot reach all of you,” she said under her breath. “But I will not leave you untallied.”

If she could not hold the walls, she could at least make sure every person in their shadow was written down, linked to someone else, seen.

She knew she would break if she kept answering every tug with heat instead of cord and slate. Her body told her so: the ache in her bones, the shaking in her fingers when she reached for the next mark.

She still rose when someone called her name from farther down the lane.

There were more lists to make. More crossings to improvise. More people who needed to hear that they were not standing alone, even if all she could do for them that Tide was write their names in a column where someone else would see them.

Tomorrow, she promised herself. Tomorrow she would say no to at least one plea that wanted her hands instead of her words.

Tomorrow, the copper in her veins would pull again when someone screamed, and she did not know if the word would come.

She walked anyway.

---

#### Eries

The fringe settlement had never been a good place for houses, even before the Piercing.

Shallow soil. Old mining cuts that ran under half the street. A heat line that brushed the district’s edge instead of feeding its center. When the sky tore open, the commune did not shatter. It sagged further toward what it already was.

Three houses leaned over a buried cut. The storage hall had one corner hanging in air. The meeting shed had lost half its roof. Every wall held the memory of some past shortcut.

Eries stood on the storage hall’s threshold and watched the small motions. A post shifting the width of a fingernail. A beam sliding along its stone seat. Dust spilling from a new line above the door.

“Can you hold it?” a woman asked behind her.

The woman’s palms were raw. Rope marks. She had been dragging sacks out of the hall since first light.

“I can,” Eries said.

She did not move.

“We heard about what happened in the Crown,” the woman said. “People with copper light in their hands stopping walls mid-fall. You have that.” Her gaze dropped to Eries’s fingers. “I saw it when you steadied my boy after the aftershock. You set your hand on his shoulder and the shaking stopped.”

“That was his shaking, not the ground’s,” Eries said.

“Does it matter?” the woman asked. “You could do the same for the hall.”

Eries stepped off the threshold and crossed the packed dirt to the nearest leaning house. Its corner sat over one of the old cuts. The earth under the stones had turned to powder; each breath of wind sent a faint trickle into the dark.

She set her palm on the outer wall.

Cracks ran through the masonry, some new, most old. Load paths strained long before the Piercing. The ground beneath shifted in slow, uneven rolls. The house was not in danger of falling today, but its fall had already started; the pace had only changed.

Copper-gold stirred under her skin. It rose eagerly toward her palm, wanting a place to travel.

She could send it through the wall. Pin the cracks. Stiffen the corner. Buy them an Anneal or two on this bad ground. They would stand a little straighter, a little longer.

The cut would still be there. The soil would still be wrong. The heat line would still barely reach.

She took her hand away.

“No,” she said.

The woman caught her sleeve.

“You can,” she said. “You just said you could. So why not here? Are we less than the Crown?”

“You are worth more than this ground,” Eries said. “That is why you walk away from it.”

Her jaw tightened.

“My father raised these walls,” she said. “We added to them each Anneal. We buried four in this soil. We carved names into that bench.” She jerked her chin toward a cracked seat by the meeting shed. “How do we step away from that and say we still have a home?”

“You do not step away from them,” Eries said. “You step away from roofs that will kill you in your sleep.”

She walked to the storage hall.

Two men were bracing the worst-leaning corner with scavenged poles. Each time they shoved a pole into place, the corner dropped the width of a grain. Dust sifted from the upper join.

“Stop,” Eries said.

One of the men turned red with effort and anger.

“If this corner goes, half our food goes,” he said. “Unless you stand there with your hands and keep it up, we do not stop anything.”

“If I keep it up, you stay and die slow,” Eries said. “If I drop it right, you lose wood and stone and some sacks. You keep your children.”

The second man looked toward the hall’s shadow, where three small figures crouched with bowls in their laps.

“What do you mean, ‘drop it right’?” he asked.

“Get the sacks out from that side,” Eries said, pointing to the safer corner. “Stack them by the track. Anything you cannot move in one phase is not grain anymore. It is weight you do not carry north.”

They did not trust her. They still moved. Fear of a sudden fall pushed them faster than trust would have.

When the last sack cleared the danger zone, Eries stepped under the leaning beam. She set her hand on the main support and let the copper-gold come.

It flowed along the grain, found every split and weak knot. This time she did not use it to stiffen. She used it to see and choose.

There, at the highest break.

She pushed.

The crack widened. The weight above shifted all at once, then in stages. The support gave in a rough, controlled slide instead of a clean snap. The roof frame dropped in pieces. Beams slid off to either side. The corner folded inward instead of spilling out.

The hall collapsed into itself. Dust rolled. The children coughed but did not scream.

“You destroyed our hall,” the first man said, voice flat.

“It was already destroyed,” Eries said. “You were still standing under it.”

Copper threads still marked her forearms when she stepped away. Her fingers shook once, then eased. The strain of using the grip this way ran in a different pattern than a hold, but it still ran.

The woman from before stood staring at the broken timbers.

“All that work,” she whispered. “All those turns.”

Eries followed her gaze along the low houses, the sagging meeting shed, the bench with carved names washed pale by dust and time.

“You can carry the names north,” Eries said. “You can carry the work in your hands and in your backs. You cannot carry these walls anywhere. They are already sinking.”

“What do we eat on the way?” the woman asked. “What do we sleep under?”

“You eat what you can carry and what others along the track can spare,” Eries said. “You sleep under cloth and sky until you reach better stone. If communes turn you away, you walk farther until one does not. There are places that can take in those who leave bad ground. There are places that need new hands.”

“And if there are not?” the man asked.

“Then you still die standing on ground that had a chance,” Eries said. “Not under this.”

They did not thank her. They gathered what they could in a hard, narrow silence. Children were lifted to shoulders. Packs were tied. No one looked at the broken hall again for more than a heartbeat at a time.

When the last of them started along the track toward the river path, Eries stayed where she was and listened.

Without bodies and stores pressing on them, the remaining houses shifted more freely. Roof lines settled. A loose stone slid and bounced once. One wall leaned further, then stopped when it met its new balance. The place was turning from a living commune into salvage and memory.

She felt no pull to pour copper into any of it.

Some structures did not need another chance. They needed an end so the people who had clung to them could move.

Far downslope, where she had never walked, something else failed in a way that carried the stamp of hands like hers, but different. A hold instead of a severing. A building kept up that by rights should have fallen.

She did not know whose grip that was. She only knew that wherever they were, they had made a different choice.

She turned away from the empty frames and started along the track after the leaving line of people, far enough behind not to crowd them, close enough that if another beam somewhere decided to finish its fall while someone still stood under it, she would feel the strain in time to act.

She would not keep every wall standing. That was not her work.

She would make sure the worst ones fell before they took more than they were owed.
