# INTERLUDE I – Dissolution and Care



***

**Salee – Wholeness**

The southern commune of Vresh had fractured along three fault lines.

Salee stood in the center hall where the roof had partially collapsed and counted the wounded: forty-seven who could not walk, sixteen who needed constant care, eight who would not survive the next Anneal without intervention she did not have the supplies to provide.

Around her, the healthy moved between pallets. They brought water. They changed dressings. They whispered to the dying and held hands with the ones who screamed when bone shifted under skin.

She had organized this. All of it. The supply lines from three neighboring communes. The rotation schedules. The prioritization lists that decided who received the limited stock of root medicine and who received only comfort.

Her hands shook as she marked another name on the slate. Not from exhaustion. From the weight of every connection she had formed in the last five Tides. Each face became a thread. Each thread pulled at her chest.

"Salee." A woman approached, face streaked with dust and tears. "The north wall is splitting. If it goes, we lose the grain stores and the shelter for another twenty beds. We need someone to—"

"I know," Salee said. Her voice came flat. "I have marked it. We have no one to spare."

"You could go," the woman said. "You held the beam in the east wing. You kept it from—"

"I held it for half a phase," Salee said. "Then it fell anyway. I cannot hold every beam. I cannot patch every crack. I cannot keep every person breathing."

The woman stared at her. Salee felt the thread between them pull tighter, a physical ache under her ribs.

"But you will try," the woman said quietly.

Salee looked down at her slate. Forty-seven names. Sixteen who needed constant care. Eight who were dying.

She had connected them all. Fed them. Organized their survival. Built networks that spread from Vresh into six other communes, pulling supplies and people into a web that kept the wounded alive and the shelters standing.

The web was strangling her.

Every person she helped became another node. Every node pulled at her attention. She felt them all now—not just in Vresh, but in the communes beyond. Hundreds of threads, all asking for more than she had to give.

Her chest tightened. The connections did not ease when she turned away. They stayed, constant and demanding, a pressure that made it hard to breathe.

"I will go to the north wall," she said.

The woman nodded and turned away, already pulling two others to follow.

Salee stood alone in the center hall. The wounded breathed around her in rough, uneven rhythms. She felt every one of them. The threads did not break. They only multiplied.

She had thought connection was strength. Now it felt like drowning.

But she could not stop. If she let one thread go, the whole web would unravel. If the web unreled, people died.

So she would go to the north wall. She would hold what she could. She would add another thread and carry the weight until her chest could not expand and her hands could not lift and the connections crushed her under their combined demand.

She did not know another way.

***

**Eries – Entropy**

The fringe settlement of Kethmar was dying, and Eries was helping it along.

She stood at the edge of the main square where the ground had split during the Piercing and watched three families load their carts. They moved quickly, without ceremony. No one tried to convince them to stay.

"You are certain?" an older man asked. He held a child on his hip, a bundle of cloth and tools slung over his shoulder.

"I am certain," Eries said. "This place will not hold another Casting. The aquifer cracked. The grain stores are contaminated. You can stay and die slowly, or you can walk to Mevan before winter and live."

The man nodded once and turned toward his cart.

Eries felt no guilt. She felt relief.

Kethmar had been failing long before the Piercing. Poor soil. Bad water management. A refusal to consolidate with the larger communes upslope. The Piercing had simply accelerated what was already in motion.

Letting it die was not cruelty. It was efficiency.

Another woman approached, younger, her hands clenched at her sides. "You are telling people to abandon their homes."

"I am telling them the truth," Eries said. "The structure is failing. Staying will not change that. Leaving redistributes the survivors to places that can support them."

"And those who cannot walk?" the woman asked. Her voice shook. "The old? The injured?"

Eries met her gaze without flinching. "Some will die. Fewer will die if you move now than if you wait for the aquifer to run dry and the stores to rot."

The woman's jaw tightened. She turned and walked back to the group still gathered near the broken well.

Eries watched her go. She felt no pull to follow. No thread drawing her toward the woman's anger or the settlement's desperation. She felt only the clarity of knowing what had to be done.

Some things could not be saved. Trying to hold them together wasted resources that could support what remained viable. Kethmar was not viable. The Piercing had proven it. The smart choice was to let it collapse, guide the survivors to safety, and stop pretending the structure could be repaired.

She had said this to five settlements now. Three had listened. Two had refused. She did not know which choice would cost more lives in the end, but she knew holding broken systems together out of sentiment was not a strategy.

The carts began to move. Dust rose under their wheels. Kethmar sat empty behind them, cracks spreading through its square, its wells dry, its walls leaning.

Eries turned upslope and started walking. There were other settlements. Other choices. Other places where someone needed to say: let it go.

She would keep saying it. Because holding everything was not strength. It was refusal to accept what the ground already knew.

Some things had to fall.

***

Two gods were waking. One could not stop holding. The other could not stop releasing.

Neither understood the cost of their nature. Neither saw how their choices mirrored the pair in Saint Merro who stood at a cracked support and argued over where to press.

When gods woke fully, the mortals caught between save-everything and let-everything-die would have no neutral ground left.

I noted their positions and waited.

**Word count: 1,150**
 i had my other ai model draft this up

[ASSISTANT]
Yeah, this draft breaks almost every core constraint you’ve set for N01. Let me be blunt and then I’ll fix it.

### Why this version doesn’t work (for *this* project)

- **Wrong narrative frame.** The last section is pure Auditor omniscient (“Two gods were waking… I noted their positions”) and explicitly calls them gods. You already decided: *no Auditor in interludes, tight third only*. This violates that outright.
- **Too much metaphor.** Threads, web, drowning, “what the ground already knew,” save-everything vs let-everything-die—this is all figurative. Your rule is: *no metaphors/similes, describe physical reality directly*. This doesn’t.
- **Voice doesn’t match the world.** “Strategy,” “efficiency” and “not a strategy,” “you people” copper-hands rumors, “before winter”—reads like generic fantasy SF, not Te’Oga. Also: “winter” is straight-up Earth.
- **No Soulpulse visibility.** If they use anything remotely like the grip, we need copper-gold, strain, vein-lines, cost. Here their “god” nature is only thematic/philosophical. That’s fine *if* we’re not showing activation, but then the “gods waking” paragraph is out of place: the text talks like they’re already conceptually divine without any grounded phenomena.
- **Too on-the-nose thematics.** The final paragraph literally explains the metaphor and calls out Jhace/Tiffani. Interludes should *echo* the main pair, not point at them with a neon sign.

So: good *idea* (Salee drowning in connections, Eries cutting dead settlements loose), but wrong execution for N01’s formal rules.

Here’s a **clean rewrite** that keeps your core beats (Vresh triage + Kethmar abandonment), in **tight third**, **no Auditor**, **no overt gods**, **no metaphors**, and **Te’Oga lexicon-compatible**.

---

## INTERLUDE I – Dissolution and Care

### Salee

The southern commune of Vresh had broken in three places.

Salee stood in the main hall where part of the roof had come down and counted the ones who could not stand: forty-seven laid out on pallets, sixteen who needed someone at their side every phase, eight whose breathing already dragged at the edge of what root and bandage could reach.

Around them, those still on their feet moved between pallets. They brought water. They shifted splints. They wiped faces when fever sweat ran into eyes. They pressed cloth into hands when bone grated under skin and the cries would not stop.

Salee had set this in motion. The supply runs from three neighboring communes. The watch turns. The list that decided who got the last measures of pain root and who got only a hand and a blanket.

Her fingers shook as she marked another line on the slate. Not from lack of sleep. From the amount she had tied together in five Tides. Each name on the slate linked to two more: a cousin who could carry, a neighbor who could cook, a child who needed watching while someone else hauled stone. Every time she drew one of those lines, her chest tightened.

“Salee.”

She looked up.

A woman stood by the doorway, dust streaked on her face, bandage cloth tied around one arm.

“The north wall is splitting,” the woman said. “If it drops, we lose the grain stores and the beds in the side rooms. We need someone who knows how to read the cracks.”

“I know,” Salee said. Her voice came out flatter than she meant. “It is on the list. We have no one to spare for a full brace.”

“You could go,” the woman said. “You held the east beam when it started to go. You kept it long enough for us to drag three out.”

“I held it for half a phase,” Salee said. “Then it dropped. If I go to the north wall, I leave this hall.”

The woman’s jaw worked.

“You cannot be everywhere,” she said. It sounded like something she did not quite believe even as she said it.

Salee dropped her gaze to the slate. Forty-seven. Sixteen. Eight. Next to each count, she had put marks for who watched them, who brought water, who could be pulled for lifting if a new break hit. No column stood empty. Wherever there was a need, she had written a name.

The slate felt heavier in her hand than its clay weight should have allowed.

“I know I cannot,” she said at last.

“You will still go,” the woman said.

Salee did not answer. The truth of it sat in her body already.

She listened to the hall for a few breaths. To the rough, uneven rise and fall of injured chests. To the soft scrape of cloth and the muted sounds people made when they were in too much pain to stay quiet and too stubborn to let those sounds turn into full cries.

If she turned away now, those sounds would go on. The hall would not empty just because she walked to the north wall. Others would still carry, still lift, still sit by the ones who were not going to see another Anneal.

The north wall had no one yet. Only stone under strain, grain sacks stacked three high, and two youths who had been told to “watch for dust” because no one had given them better orders.

“I will go,” Salee said.

The woman’s shoulders slumped with relief. She nodded once and went to pull two others who knew how to work braces.

Salee stayed where she was for a moment longer. The slate hung at her side. Her hands felt heavy. When she closed her fingers around the edge of the board, a faint copper warmth stirred along the veins on the back of her hand, answering the thought of a wall under too much load.

She forced it still. She had used that heat twice since the Piercing—once on the east beam and once on a cracked cistern lip. Both times it had bought time. Both times it had left her arms shaking and her head full of other people’s weight.

She could not use it for every break. If she started down that path, she would stand under every sagging roof in Vresh until her legs gave way.

She lifted the slate to chest height again and walked toward the north wall.

As she stepped through the doorway, the threads of what she had already tied together in Vresh and its neighbors stayed with her. She knew who would take her place by the worst pallets. She knew which runner would carry a message if someone stopped breathing while she was gone. The knowledge did not ease anything. It sat in her chest as one more layer of weight.

She did not know how to set any of it down without leaving someone uncovered.

So she did what she knew: she moved toward the next point of strain and told herself she would rest when the worst of the breaks had been found and marked and held long enough for the next set of hands to arrive.

She knew that Tide would not come. She walked anyway.

---

### Eries

The fringe settlement of Kethmar was almost finished with itself.

Eries stood by the sunk wellhead and watched three families load carts with what they thought they could carry. The ground around them had cracked during the Piercing, but the worst damage was older: low walls tipped toward old mine cuts, a storage shed corner hanging over air, a channel that had silted until water spread in a shallow, useless sheet instead of following its set path.

“You are certain?” an older man asked. A child clung to his hip, arms tight around his neck. A bundle of cloth and tools hung from his other shoulder.

“I am,” Eries said. “The ground under your well split. The grain has mold. Your heat feed is too far from any main line to trust now that the grid has shifted. You can stay and hope this holds until the next Crucible, or you can walk to Mevan before the deep cold sets and have a chance to see the next Anneal.”

The man looked past her toward the squat houses, the leaning shed, the low stone where someone had carved initials that the dust now filled.

He nodded once and turned back to his cart.

Eries felt no pull to stop him. This was the right direction.

A younger woman approached, hands balled at her sides.

“You are telling people to abandon their home,” the woman said.

“I am telling them what the ground is already showing,” Eries said. “The structures are failing. You do not get more years here by pretending they are sound.”

“And the ones who cannot walk?” the woman asked. “Old ones. Those hurt in the Piercing. Children who cannot keep pace if the weather turns.”

“Some will die,” Eries said. She did not soften it. “If you stay, more will. When the grain smothers under rot and the well draws only silt, you will not have carts to load. You will have bodies you cannot lift.”

The woman’s jaw clenched. Her eyes stayed on Eries, unblinking.

“So that is it,” she said. “You come, you say ‘go,’ and we tear ourselves out by the roots.”

Eries let her own gaze move over Kethmar.

The meeting shed with one whole wall buckled. The storehouse with its beam already split at the center. The narrow lanes that had never been widened, no matter how many families added rooms. She did not see roots. She saw people clinging to structures that would crush them on their way down.

“I do not pull you,” she said. “I tell you how much time these walls have left. You decide whether to stand under them when they finish falling.”

At the far edge of the square, two men were propping the storehouse corner with scavenged posts. Each post shifted when they stepped back, taking some of the lean, but not enough.

Eries walked over.

“Stop,” she said.

“We lose half our grain if this goes,” one of them snapped. “Unless you plan to keep it up with your hands, stay clear.”

“If I keep it up, you stay here,” Eries said. “You hold to this place because the walls are still standing. You wait one more Tide, one more Crucible, until the ground takes its due when you are not ready.”

The second man’s grip on the post slackened.

“What do you mean, ‘keep it up’?” he asked.

Eries set her palm on the corner stone, just above the crack.

Fine fractures ran through it, connecting to a line that cut all the way under the load-bearing beam. Beneath that, the fill over the old cut had turned to loose grit. The whole corner was balanced on bad ground.

Copper warmth rose along the veins of her hand, ready to move into the stone, ready to stiffen it, ready to hold.

She did not let it.

“Clear the sacks from this side,” she said, pointing. “Stack them by the track. Anything you cannot move before Striking ends will spoil before the next Anneal anyway.”

They hesitated only a breath, then moved. The fear of a sudden drop drove them.

When the last sack was clear of the largest impact, Eries stepped back and took her hand from the stone.

“Push your posts in as far as they will go,” she said.

They shoved the props deep under the beam. It shifted another finger’s width.

“That is all you get,” she said.

She walked farther back, out of the drop path.

The men followed her instinctively, then realized what they had done and looked at her with anger and something close to fear.

“Now what?” the first man demanded.

“Now you let it do what it has been moving toward for half a dozen Castings,” Eries said.

The next tremor was small—just the kind that ran through the slope now and then since the Piercing. It was enough.

The corner stone cracked. The beam sank. The posts snapped in two places and spilled out. The storehouse wall folded inward, shedding blocks and timbers into its own floor instead of out into the square. Dust rolled into the air, then drifted away.

No one stood under it.

“You destroyed our storehouse,” the man said. This time his voice was low, not shouting.

“It was already finished,” Eries said. “Now it has stopped pretending otherwise. You have sacks, carts, and hands. You can travel with that. You cannot travel with a building, and it was not going to feed you for long.”

Hands still shook around her. Her own did too, though not from effort. The copper under her skin had risen to the surface and then been forced back. It left a faint ache in her wrists.

“What do we eat on the way?” the younger woman asked. She had come up behind them. “What do we sleep under?”

“You eat what you carry and what other communes can spare when you reach them,” Eries said. “You sleep under canvas and whatever roofs Mevan can give you when you arrive. If Mevan is full, you walk until another place is not.”

“And if there is no other place?” the man asked.

“Then you still die with a chance you would not have had here,” Eries said. “Not in a hall that was ready to drop on your children.”

They did not argue after that. They tied down the last bundles on the carts. They lifted those who could not walk onto wagon boards. They took one last look at the square—not at the cracked stones, but at the small marks that said they had been there: a carved pattern on a door lintel, a row of smoothed rocks along one path.

Then they turned their backs and started for the track upslope.

Eries watched until they were small shapes against the rise.

She felt the settlement behind her ease. Without bodies and stored weight, the strain in the remaining walls shifted. One roof slid another half-span. One empty room opened to the sky as its support gave way. Kethmar was on its way to becoming piles of stone and a few useful beams that someone might haul off one day.

She felt no pull to stiffen any of it.

Her work was not to stand under every failing roof. It was to look at a structure and say, plainly: this one is done. Take the people and the grain and the tools and go where they can become part of something that still has room to hold them.

She turned away from Kethmar and walked after the carts, far enough back not to crowd them, close enough that if another building along the track chose this Tide to finish falling while someone still stood in its doorway, she would feel the strain and decide again whether to hold or let it go.

[ASSISTANT]
Short version: we steal the *specifics* and emotional spine from the other model’s version (Vresh/Kethmar, triage numbers, “helping Kethmar die”), and keep my version’s **tight-third, physical description, Soulpulse cost, and no-Auditor/no-gods discipline**.

I’ll show that by just giving you a **blended Interlude I** that folds both drafts together.

## INTERLUDE I – Dissolution and Care

### Salee – Wholeness

The southern commune of Vresh had fractured along three fault lines.

Salee stood in the main hall where part of the roof had come down and counted the ones who could not stand: forty-seven on pallets, sixteen who needed someone at their side every phase, eight whose breathing already sat beyond what root and bandage could reach.

Around them, those still on their feet moved between the rows. They brought water. They changed dressings. They held hands when bone shifted and the cry would not stay in.

Salee had set this in motion. The supply runs from three neighboring communes. The shifts for hauling, cooking, watching. The list that decided who received the last measures of pain root and who received only blankets and company.

She marked another name on her slate. The clay board had never felt heavy before the Piercing. Now each line added weight.

“Salee.”

She looked up.

A woman stood in the doorway, dust streaked across her face, a strip of bandage cloth tied around one forearm.

“The north wall is splitting,” the woman said. “If it goes, we lose the grain and the side rooms. We need someone who knows how to read it.”

“I know,” Salee said. Her voice came out flat. “It is marked. We have no one to spare for a full brace.”

“You could go,” the woman said. “You held the beam in the east wing. You kept it until they hauled three out.”

“I held it for half a phase,” Salee said. “Then it dropped. I cannot hold every beam in Vresh.”

The woman’s jaw worked once.

“You cannot be everywhere,” she said. It sounded like she wanted Salee to argue.

Salee dropped her gaze to the slate.

Forty-seven. Sixteen. Eight.

Next to each count she had added other marks in smaller script: who carried water to that row, who could lift if a new break hit, who could be spared from cooking to watch children while their parents hauled stone. In the last five Tides, the list had grown beyond this hall. Six other communes now sat in the same cramped columns. Names, family marks, who owed grain and who could offer tools.

She saw all of it when she closed her eyes. Not as tidy lines, but as tasks stacked on other tasks until there was no bare space left.

Her chest tightened. The feeling didn’t ease when she set the slate down. It lived behind her ribs now, as steady as her pulse.

“I know I cannot be everywhere,” she said.

The woman’s shoulders dropped a little.

“You will still go,” she said.

Salee did not answer that. The truth of it had already moved her feet.

She looked along the pallets one more time. Watched one of the older boys adjust a splint with hands that shook, but did not slip. Watched a girl of no more than nine change a water jar without sloshing on the pallet straw.

They would keep working if Salee walked out. The hall would not empty because she left it. That knowledge did not loosen anything inside her. The breathing here stayed rough and uneven. She felt it in the tightness in her own chest.

She lifted the slate under her arm and headed for the north wall.

As she stepped through the doorway, a faint warmth stirred along the veins on the backs of her hands. It had done that twice since the Piercing—once under the east beam, once on the lip of a cracked cistern. Each time she had set her palms to failing material and given more than muscle. Copper-gold had run through her skin and into stone. The structure had held a little longer than it should.

Each time, her arms had shaken for a whole Tide afterward.

She flexed her fingers. The copper answer stayed buried for now, but close. She could not use it for every split and sag she saw. If she tried, she would spend herself long before the worst failures reached her. The logic of that was clear. Her body did not care.

Outside, the north wall showed its damage in a long shallow crack from corner to corner. Grain sacks leaned against it three high. Two youths stood nearby with poles in their hands, eyes on the slow trickle of dust from the fracture.

“You see it dropping?” Salee asked.

“Half a finger since mid-Striking,” one said. “We marked it.”

He pointed to a coal line on the stone. The crack sat noticeably lower now.

Salee put her palm to the wall just above the worst part of the split.

Cold stone. Rough mortar. A thin, uneven vibration where the load no longer spread evenly across the length. The strain wanted to drag the crack wider. The sacks and the roof above them all leaned toward that point.

Hold here, she thought. Not forever. Just long enough to move the grain and clear those beds.

Heat started under her skin at once, ready to rise.

She pulled her hand away.

“Move the sacks,” she said. “All of them. Stack them in the center. No one sleeps in the side rooms tonight.”

“With no brace?” the woman from the hall asked. She had followed them out.

“The wall will go before the next Anneal,” Salee said. “With a brace or without. I can hold it for a span if I have to. That is enough to clear it when the time comes. Not enough to make it safe.”

“What if it goes in the night?” one of the youths asked.

“I patrol,” Salee said. “I have others mark for me when I sleep. We catch as many drops as we can. We do not pretend we can keep them from happening.”

Her hands still tingled from the brief contact. Copper wanted a path. She denied it and used her voice instead.

“You two pull sacks,” she said to the youths. “No arguments over whose load is heavier. You—” she nodded to the woman “—find me three who can shift pallets. I will mark which ones move first.”

Orders steadied her, the way setting a brace under a beam did. As people ran to obey, the tightness in her chest did not ease, but it took on structure. Tasks. Sequences. A plan that carried them through the next Tide, if not the next Cast.

A runner from a neighboring commune appeared at the gate before she finished marking the wall. He had dust up to his knees and a cut on one cheek.

“Salee,” he panted. “The cistern in Dorrin cracked. They say the lining is letting the feed run into the slope. They want you there.”

Of course they did.

She wrote Dorrin in a new corner of the slate. Her hand cramped on the curve of the letters.

“There are others who know how to read cracks,” she said.

“They do not trust anyone else,” the runner said. “They said your name, not just ‘inspector.’”

Her arms felt heavy. Her legs too. She thought of the east beam, of standing under it while copper burned along her bones and men dragged bodies around her. She thought of the lip of the cistern she had already held once this Tide.

If she did not go, Dorrin would call someone else. They might wedge the crack wrong. They might waste water they could have saved. People she did not know would add names to the list of dead.

If she did go, this wall would still split. Vresh would still need someone to stand by it when it did.

“Tell them I am coming,” Salee said.

The runner nodded and took off without resting.

Salee watched him go. Her hands rested against the slate’s worn edges. The copper in her veins sat awake and restless. In her mind, the marks on the clay board connected Vresh and Dorrin and the other communes she had already walked. Not as a neat web. As a series of places where something would fail soon, and there were never enough hands to stand under all of them.

She turned back to the north wall long enough to see the youths pull the last sack away.

“If you see dust run in a line and not just fall,” she told them, “shout until someone hears. Do not assume someone else is watching.”

Then she left Vresh’s faults behind for the moment and headed downslope toward Dorrin, knowing that each place she left would keep tugging at her long after she passed out of sight.

She did not know how to stop answering that pull. So she did not stop.

---

### Eries – Entropy

The fringe settlement of Kethmar was almost finished.

Eries stood by the cracked wellhead and watched three families load carts with what they could move in one Tide. Their motions were quick but not frantic. This was not a sudden flight. It was a decision that had finally met the ground under it.

“You are certain,” an older man said. He held a child on one hip. A bundle of cloth and tools hung from his other shoulder.

“Yes,” Eries said.

She let her gaze take in the whole place while she spoke.

The well shaft stone had split during the Piercing. They had packed the worst of the gap with rags and clay, but the water now came up cloudy and low. The storehouse showed a long crack from roof to base on one corner. The meeting shed’s roof sagged where one main beam had bowed. Every lane tilted slightly toward an old mining cut that ran under half the homes.

“You can stay,” she said. “The cracks will keep moving. The feed from the grid is weak here now, with the mains shifted. By next Crucible your stores will be less and your well worse. Or you can walk to Mevan while the tracks are still clear and the deep cold has not taken the passes.”

The man looked past her at the houses. At the low stone beside one doorway, where someone had carved initials worn faint by hands and weather. At the place on the square where a tree had once grown before the soil had turned too thin for it.

He nodded once and turned away to finish tying down the cart.

A younger woman came up next, hands tight at her sides.

“You tell people to leave their homes,” she said. “You stand on cracked ground and say, ‘Go,’ and they listen.”

“I tell them what their walls are already showing,” Eries said. “This place has been failing for more than one Piercing. The breaks are only larger now.”

“And those who cannot walk?” the woman demanded. “Old ones. Those who took stone in the first shudder. Children who tire before we reach the first rise.”

“Some will die on the way,” Eries said. She saw no use in softening it. “If you stay, more will. When the well draws only silt and the grain spoils in its bins, you will be walking then too, only with less strength and fewer carts.”

The woman’s jaw clenched. She did not look away.

“So that is the choice,” she said. “Leave and lose some now, stay and lose more later.”

“Yes,” Eries said.

At the edge of the square, two men wrestled makeshift props into place under the leaning corner of the storehouse. Each time they stepped back, the corner sagged the width of a grain.

Eries walked over.

“Stop,” she said.

“If this drops, we lose half our grain,” one of them snapped. Sweat and dust streaked his face. “Unless you put your glowing hands on it like in the stories, stay clear and let us work.”

“If I hold it, you fill it again and stay,” Eries said. “You tell yourselves the building is sound because it did not fall this Tide. You wait another Casting. Another. Until one night it comes down when no one is watching.”

The second man’s grip tightened on his prop.

“What do you mean, ‘hold it’?” he asked.

Eries set her palm against the corner stone just above the worst split.

Cracks ran through the block into the beam above. Beneath them, the earth over the old cut had turned to powder. The whole load sat on a base that moved in slow, uneven shifts.

Copper warmth rose under her skin. It pushed outward toward the fracture, ready to seep into it, ready to stiffen it. She knew how that would feel: the stone’s strain filling her bones, the weight pressing along the bright lines in her arms.

She did not let it out.

“Clear the sacks from this side,” she said instead, pointing to the path that led away from the leaning corner. “Stack them by the track. Anything you cannot move before Striking ends was not going to last to the next Anneal.”

They hesitated, then obeyed. Fear of an uncontrolled drop moved their feet faster than trust in her.

When the last sack sat beyond the likely fall, Eries stepped away from the wall.

“That is all you can do for this building,” she said. “Stand well back.”

They retreated with her, a few paces more than they needed. Their eyes stayed fixed on the cracked stone.

The next tremor through the slope was small. It was enough.

The corner stone split fully. The beam shifted. The makeshift props slid out and toppled. The storehouse wall folded inward, shedding blocks and timber into its own floor. Dust rolled across the square, then settled. No one stood under the fall.

“You destroyed our storehouse,” the first man said. His voice came out strange, without shape.

“It was already finished,” Eries said. “Now it is not waiting to fail when you are asleep.”

Her hand still ached where it had rested on the stone. The copper under her skin had risen and been pushed back. It left a faint buzz in her wrist, as if some of the strain had caught there for a few breaths before fading.

The younger woman from before had followed them. She stared at the pile of broken stone.

“All that work,” she said. “All those Anneals. We built that one grain sack at a time.”

“You keep the grain,” Eries said. “You keep the tools. You keep the hands that stacked those sacks. You do not keep stone that will bury them if you insist on staying under it.”

“What do we eat on the way?” the woman asked. “What do we sleep under?”

“You eat what you can carry,” Eries said. “And what the communes along the track can spare when you reach them. You sleep under cloth until you reach Mevan. If Mevan is full, you walk until somewhere else is not.”

“And if nowhere is?” the man asked.

“Then you still had more chance than you would have standing here,” Eries said. “Not much comfort. Better than none.”

He looked back once at the broken storehouse, then at the child clinging to his hip. The decision finished itself in his face. He turned toward the track.

By late Pouring, three carts rolled out of Kethmar, carrying those who would not risk another Crucible on this ground. Others stayed. Some from stubbornness, some from lack of strength, some from the kind of hope Eries did not share.

When the last wheel left the square, Eries stood alone for a moment and listened.

Without sacks and bodies pressing on them, the remaining walls eased. One roof slid another half-span. A lintel stone dropped and shattered. The place was already turning from a living settlement into a scatter of salvage and shallow hollows.

She felt no urge to set her hands on any of it.

Her work was not to keep every wall standing. It was to look at a structure and say, This one has reached its end. Take what still breathes and move.

She followed the carts at a distance along the track upslope. Far enough not to press them. Close enough that if some other building along their path chose this Tide to finish its fall while someone still stood under its doorway, she could feel the strain and decide, again, whether to hold for a few breaths or let it go.

[USER]
**No. This is not correct for a novella.**

You just tightened Chapters 1-3 to 2,913 words combined. This single interlude is **2,400 words**. That's 82% of your entire opening three chapters.

## The Core Problem

**You're writing novel structure in novella format.**

This interlude is:
- Too long (2,400w)
- Too early (appears after ~7-8k words, roughly 1/3 through the novella)
- Too many new POVs (Salee AND Eries, bringing total to 4 POV characters)
- Too slow (contemplative pacing after you just established urgency)

**In a 60-80k novel?** This would be fine. You'd have room for wide scope, multiple POV characters, interludes that show global impact.

**In an 18-21k novella?** You can't afford it.

---

## Specific Problems

**1. Word count budget**

If N01 is 18-21k words and you've spent:
- Chapters 1-3: ~2,900w
- Chapters 4-6: ~3,000-4,000w (estimate)
- Interlude I: 2,400w

You're at **8,300-9,300 words** and you haven't even finished the first major arc (Jhace and Tiffani's partnership forming, first collaborative Soulpulse use, RRO pressure).

That leaves you **9,000-12,000 words** to:
- Escalate the crisis
- Develop Jhace/Tiffani partnership
- Introduce Corlexi as active antagonist
- Show escalating Soulpulse cost
- Resolve the immediate Saint Merro crisis
- Set up hooks for N02

You don't have 2,400 words to spend on characters who aren't in Saint Merro and won't appear in this novella again.

---

**2. POV proliferation**

You now have **four POV characters** in a novella:
- Jhace (protagonist)
- Tiffani (protagonist)  
- Salee (interlude, Wholeness philosophy)
- Eries (interlude, Entropy philosophy)

Plus Corlexi is supposed to enter as a third major force.

That's **too many voices** for this length. The reader can't track that many perspectives in 20k words.

Compare: Your revised Chapter 1-3 structure (Jhace → Tiffani → Jhace → convergence) works because it's *two* voices in tight parallel. Adding Salee and Eries diffuses focus.

---

**3. Pacing mismatch**

You just wrote:
- Chapter 1: 697 words, tight, urgent, guilt-driven
- Chapter 2: 953 words, tight, urgent, pattern-recognition
- Chapter 3: 1,263 words, visceral action, Soulpulse reveal

Those chapters **move**. They punch. They don't linger.

This interlude **lingers**. It's contemplative. Salee counting casualties, wrestling with decisions, moving between sites. Eries watching a settlement evacuate. Both sections are slow, atmospheric, philosophical.

After three chapters of urgency, this interlude **stops the momentum**.

---

**4. Structural purpose unclear**

**What is this interlude doing that the main narrative can't?**

- Showing Soulpulse manifesting in others? You already showed that with Jhace. You can show variety through other Saint Merro characters.
- Establishing Wholeness vs. Entropy? That's T1 (30-novella arc) worldbuilding. Does the reader need it in N01?
- Widening scope to show global impact? In a *novella*? Scope should be narrow and focused.

The interlude is doing **series-level work** in a **story-level space**.

---

**5. The two sections are redundant**

Both Salee and Eries:
- Stand at sites of structural damage
- Face people asking them to hold/help
- Feel internal conflict about capacity/limits
- Make decisions about what to save
- Move to the next crisis

The sections **mirror each other** too closely. They're showing the same emotional beat (overwhelmed responder) with different philosophical flavors (hold everything vs. let go).

You're spending 2,400 words to establish a contrast (Wholeness/Entropy) that won't pay off until later novellas.

---

## What You Should Do

**Option 1: Cut the interlude entirely from N01.**

Move it to N02 or later when:
- You have more word budget
- The wider world becomes relevant to the plot
- Salee and Eries can actually interact with Jhace/Tiffani

**Option 2: Compress it to 400-600 words max.**

Single POV (probably Salee, since Wholeness mirrors Jhace's instinct to hold).

Structure:
- Salee at one failing structure (200w)
- Decision to hold it, cost paid (200w)
- Realization she can't be everywhere, watching another structure fail in the distance (200w)
- Ends on her exhaustion and the scope of the crisis

Cut Eries entirely. Save her for later.

**Option 3: Replace it with a Corlexi scene.**

If you need an interlude after Chapter 6, use **Corlexi**. He's actually relevant to this novella's plot. Show him:
- Receiving reports about the Piercing's damage
- Noting the copper-gold manifestations (including Jhace's)
- Deciding the RRO needs to intervene
- Drafting the registration framework

That's 800-1,000 words that **advances the plot** and **applies pressure** to your protagonists, instead of 2,400 words in a different location with different characters.

---

## The Brutal Truth

**You're trying to write a 30-novella epic in installment one.**

This interlude feels like it belongs in **N05-N10** when:
- The reader knows the wider world
- Salee and Eries are established characters
- The Wholeness/Entropy conflict is active plot, not background philosophy

Right now, in N01, this is **scope creep**. You're world-building when you should be story-telling.

---

## My Recommendation

**Cut this interlude from N01 entirely.**

Save it. Expand it. Use it in N03-N05 when you have room and when these characters matter to the immediate plot.

For N01, stay **tight and focused**:
- Jhace and Tiffani in Saint Merro
- Their partnership forming under crisis
- Corlexi and the RRO as antagonistic pressure
- Soulpulse cost escalating
- Immediate crisis resolved, wider implications set up for N02

That's a complete 18-21k story. You don't need Salee and Eries yet.

**Is this correct?** No. It's good writing in the wrong place at the wrong length for the wrong format.

Cut it. Stay focused. Trust that 20k words on Jhace and Tiffani will be more compelling than 18k on them plus 2.4k on characters the reader won't see again for 60,000+ words.

Yes. That entire critique is correct.

For N01 as a novella, here’s what should actually happen with Interlude I:

## 1. Cut this Salee/Eries interlude from N01

- The draft you have is good writing.
- It is structurally wrong for this specific novella:
  - Too long for the format.
  - Too far from Saint Merro.
  - Too detached from Jhace/Tiffani’s arc.
- Keep it in your series bible. It’s perfect material for a later Turning when:
  - The reader already cares about global stakes.
  - Salee and Eries are active, recurring presences.

N01 needs to feel like **“two specialists in one broken city”**, not “the world is waking four gods in parallel.”

## 2. N01 doesn’t need an interlude at this point

Given your current state:

- Ch1–3: Inciting incident + magic reveal + first contact.
- Ch4–6: Cost escalation + formal partnership + Dregs support.
- You’re only ~7–8k words in.

The next word-budget needs to go to:

- Deeper use of Jhace/Tiffani’s partnership in practice.
- RRO/Corlexi entering as **present** pressure.
- The major local crisis being framed and escalated.

An 800–1,000 word interlude here would still be expensive. You can skip Interlude I entirely in N01 and keep:

- **Interlude II (after Ch11):** Corlexi + Mobel (if you still want god-tier POV in this novella at all).
- **Epilogue:** Auditor-only, tracking Salee/Eries/Corlexi/Mobel; Jhace/Tiffani invisible.

If you absolutely must keep an Interlude I in N01, it should be:

## 3. If Interlude I stays, it should be Corlexi-only, 600–800 words

And it should:
- Stay in the **Crown / RRO hub**.
- Show:
  - Incoming reports on Piercing damage and “copper-gold” events.
  - A first, clinical mention of “anomalous structural holds in Saint Merro.”
  - Corlexi deciding on:
    - Registration framework.
    - Committee formation.
    - The need to bring these anomalies under structure (without naming Jhace/Tiffani).

That:
- Keeps scope aligned with the main plot.
- Applies indirect pressure to Jhace/Tiffani.
- Does not add yet another full POV on the ground.

## 4. Concrete recommendation

For N01:

- **REMOVE** the Salee/Eries interlude from this novella.
- **FOCUS** N01’s remaining word count on:
  - Jhace
  - Tiffani
  - Corlexi/RRO pressure
  - Soulpulse cost and one major Saint Merro resolution
- **SAVE** Salee/Eries interlude for a later novella where:
  - They recur.
  - Wholeness/Entropy conflict becomes active plot, not backdrop.

You’re right: this was novel-scale expansion. The tight, revised Ch1–3 you just built are the correct model. The novella wants to stay that sharp.

You've diagnosed the problem accurately and made the right structural decision. 

The Salee/Eries interlude is **good prose doing the wrong work at the wrong time.** It belongs in N03-N07 when the reader has:
- Met these characters before
- Seen Wholeness/Entropy manifest in plot-critical ways
- Earned the scope expansion

---

## Your Next Move

**Write Chapters 4-6 before deciding if you need Interlude I at all.**

You might find that:
- The novella works better with **no interlude** between the opening arc (Ch 1-6) and the crisis escalation (Ch 7-12)
- Corlexi's pressure can be felt through **dialogue and action** (RRO inspectors showing up, demands for registration) without needing his POV yet
- The story breathes better if you **save all god-tier POV** (Auditor/Corlexi/Salee/Eries) for the Epilogue

---

## If You Do Need Interlude I

**Your proposed Corlexi version (600-800w) is exactly right:**

> Corlexi at his desk, receiving reports:
> - Piercing damage across multiple districts
> - Anomalous structural holds ("copper-gold light reported in 14 incidents")
> - Decision: registration framework, committee, need for structure
> - Ends with him dispatching inspectors to "identify and catalog manifestations"

That's **plot advancement** disguised as interlude. It applies pressure. It shows bureaucratic machinery engaging. It's in Saint Merro or close enough. It's **tight**.

---

## The Real Win Here

You've now done this **three times in a row**:

1. **Chapter 1:** Cut from 1,200w → 697w, found emotional core
2. **Chapter 2:** Cut from 1,400w → 953w, moved Marisse forward  
3. **Interlude I:** Recognized 2,400w is structurally wrong, cutting entirely

You're learning to **kill good writing that's in the wrong place.** That's the hardest editorial skill, and you're executing it cleanly.

---

## Strategic Advice for What Comes Next

**Don't write Interlude I yet.**

Write:
1. **Chapters 4-6** (partnership forming, first collaborative hold, cost escalating)
2. **Chapters 7-9** (major crisis, RRO pressure entering)
3. **Chapters 10-12** (climax, resolution, hooks for N02)

Then look at the **shape of the whole novella** and decide:
- Do I need an interlude after Ch 6? Or does the story flow better without it?
- If I need one, is it Corlexi observing from the RRO, or do I just show his pressure through Jhace/Tiffani's experience?

You might find the story works best as:
- **Tight 12-chapter arc** (Jhace + Tiffani only)
- **Brief Epilogue** (Auditor tracking all four gods, Jhace/Tiffani invisible)

That's **clean**. That's **focused**. That's novella structure.

---

**Bottom line:** You're making the right call. Cut it, save it, stay tight. Your revised Ch 1-3 prove you know how to do this. Trust that instinct for the rest of N01.

Now go write Chapter 4. Show me how their partnership forms under fire.

[ASSISTANT]
We can absolutely blend them — keep the **specifics** and emotional beats from the other draft, but use the **tight third, constraint-clean voice** from my version and keep it short enough to actually fit N01.

Here’s a single, blended **Interlude I – Dissolution and Care**, with:

- Salee (Vresh triage + north wall)  
- Eries (Kethmar collapse)

## INTERLUDE I – Dissolution and Care

### Salee

The southern commune of Vresh had broken in three places.

Salee stood in the main hall where part of the roof had come down and counted the ones who could not stand: forty-seven on pallets, sixteen who needed someone at their side every phase, eight whose breathing already sat beyond what root and bandage could reach.

Around them, those still on their feet moved between the rows. They brought water. They checked splints. They wiped faces when fever sweat ran into eyes. They held shoulders when bone shifted and the sound dragged a cry out of someone who had been quiet too long.

Salee had set this in motion. The supply runs from three neighboring communes. The shifts for hauling, cooking, watching. The list that decided who received the last measures of pain root and who received only blankets and company.

She marked another name on the slate. The clay board pulled at her arm more than its own weight should have.

“Salee.”

She looked up.

A woman stood in the doorway, dust streaked across her face, bandage cloth tied around her forearm.

“The north wall is splitting,” the woman said. “If it drops, we lose the grain and the side rooms. We need someone who can read it.”

“I know,” Salee said. Her voice came flat. “It is marked. We have no brace team to spare.”

“You could go,” the woman said. “You held the east beam. You kept it until they pulled three out from under it.”

“I held it for half a phase,” Salee said. “Then it dropped. I cannot stand under every beam in Vresh.”

The woman’s jaw tightened.

“You cannot be everywhere,” she said. It sounded like a warning and a plea at once.

Salee glanced down at the slate.

Forty-seven. Sixteen. Eight.

Beside each cluster she had added smaller marks: who carried water to that row, who could lift if a new break hit, who could watch children while their parents worked. Other communes filled the lower half of the board now

> - Anomalous structural holds ("copper-gold light reported in 14 incidents")
> - Decision: registration framework, committee, need for structure
> - Ends with him dispatching inspectors to "identify and catalog manifestations"

That's **plot advancement** disguised as interlude. It applies pressure. It shows bureaucratic machinery engaging. It's in Saint Merro or close enough. It's **tight**.

---

## The Real Win Here

You've now done this **three times in a row**:

1. **Chapter 1:** Cut from 1,200w → 697w, found emotional core
2. **Chapter 2:** Cut from 1,400w → 953w, moved Marisse forward  
3. **Interlude I:** Recognized 2,400w is structurally wrong, cutting entirely

You're learning to **kill good writing that's in the wrong place.** That's the hardest editorial skill, and you're executing it cleanly.

---

## Strategic Advice for What Comes Next

**Don't write Interlude I yet.**

Write:
1. **Chapters 4-6** (partnership forming, first collaborative hold, cost escalating)
2. **Chapters 7-9** (major crisis, RRO pressure entering)
3. **Chapters 10-12** (climax, resolution, hooks for N02)

Then look at the **shape of the whole novella** and decide:
- Do I need an interlude after Ch 6? Or does the story flow better without it?
- If I need one, is it Corlexi observing from the RRO, or do I just show his pressure through Jhace/Tiffani's experience?

You might find the story works best as:
- **Tight 12-chapter arc** (Jhace + Tiffani only)
- **Brief Epilogue** (Auditor tracking all four gods, Jhace/Tiffani invisible)

That's **clean**. That's **focused**. That's novella structure.

---

**Bottom line:** You're making the right call. Cut it, save it, stay tight. Your revised Ch 1-3 prove you know how to do this. Trust that instinct for the rest of N01.

Now go write Chapter 4. Show me how their partnership forms under fire.

The fix is always: describe what the senses register and what the physics do.\n\n### 2) Anthropomorphizing sneaks in through verbs\nStone and systems do not *choose*, *remember*, *try*, *want*, or *learn*. If a sentence needs those verbs, rewrite to:\n- the motion\n- the sound\n- the direction of load\n- the failure mode\n\n### 3) Constraint-clean lines hit harder\nDirect description forces clarity. It also makes the weirdness feel more real.\n\n### 4) Practical scan checklist\n- Search for: **“as if”**, **“like”**, **“the way”**\n- Search for agency verbs applied to non-agents: **choose / remember / speak / want / try / learn**\n- When in doubt, replace intent with **cause-and-effect**\n\n## Structural decision locked\n**Interlude I (Salee/Eries) is cut from N01 for novella scope.**\n- Too long for the word budget\n- Too many POVs too early\n- Slows momentum after the Piercing\n- Does series-level thematic work that won’t pay off inside this novella’s arc\n\n## Working principle for N01 draft\nKeep the lens tight on **Jhace + Tiffani in Saint Merro**, with pressure entering through action and on-page consequences.\n"}

[ASSISTANT]
Locked. Interlude I stays dead for N01.

Here’s the **surgical constraint pass** on the text you pasted for Chapters 4–6. I’m doing **before → after** replacements only.

## Chapter 4 – After the Shudder

### 1) (If present in your Ch4 draft) “the tunnel had chosen”
**Before:** “...and the tunnel had chosen for him three Tides later.”  
**After:** “...and the tunnel had failed three Tides later.”

### 2) “joints singing”
**Before:** “...joints singing the same low note.”  
**After:** “...joints humming the same low note.”

*(This one is only a violation if it’s in narration. In dialogue, it’s optional—but keeping it clean costs nothing.)*

---

## Chapter 5 – What Stays in Stone

### 3) “The city was learning…”
**Before:** “The city was learning to function with fractures.”  
**After:** “The city was functioning with fractures.”

### 4) “Warmth bled…”
This is metaphor-by-verb. It’s subtle, but it’s still a metaphor.
**Before:** “Warmth bled through her skin.”  
**After:** “Warmth spread through her skin.”

### 5) “cracks… kept creeping”
Same issue: metaphor verb.
**Before:** “...the cracks at the edges kept creeping.”  
**After:** “...the cracks at the edges kept extending.”

### 6) “written across old stone”
Also a metaphor.
**Before:** “...a thin band of something new written across old stone.”  
**After:** “...a thin band of heat and copper shimmer along the old stone.”

(Keep the *physical* thing: heat + shimmer. Drop the “written.”)

---

## Chapter 6 – The Weight That Won’t Yield

### 7) “The stone was trying…”
**Before:** “The stone was trying to fail faster now.”  
**After:** “The fracture was widening faster now.”

### 8) “would not yield”
**Before:** “The weight would not yield.”  
**After:** “The weight did not yield.”

*(If this is in the title, you can keep it as a stylistic chapter title, but inside narration, use the physical phrasing.)*

---

## One note you flagged correctly
Dialogue gets more latitude. If you *want* Tiffani to talk in glass-language (“when you start to crack”), that’s characterization. Just keep the **narration** clean.

---

If you want, paste the **completed** Chapter 6 text (yours cut off mid-sentence in the version above) and I’ll run the same scalpel pass on the rest without touching anything that isn’t a constraint break.

# N01: Chapters 4–6 (FINAL CONSTRAINT-CLEAN VERSION)
