[ASSISTANT]
## Chapter 7 – Terms of Load

Tiffani kept her hand on Jhace’s forearm until his tremor eased enough that he could take a full breath without his shoulders locking.

The copper lines under his skin had dimmed, but they were still there. Faint bands from wrist to elbow, darker than normal vein shadowing, set in the same paths the glow had taken during the hold.

“Look at me,” she said.

Jhace’s eyes tracked to her face. His pupils were still wide. His breathing had a hitch on the intake.

“Can you stand,” she asked.

“Yes.”

She waited.

He tried to push up. His elbow slipped once on damp stone. He caught himself and forced the motion through. The tremor spiked in his hands, then steadied into its baseline shake. He got his feet under him.

Tiffani kept her position close enough to catch his shoulder without grabbing him. She did not want him to mistake support for control. She wanted him upright and moving without pretending the cost was not rising.

“You are not going back to work in the tunnels,” she said.

He stared at her.

“There are more breaks,” he said.

“There will be more breaks in the next Tide and the next,” she said. “If you fall in the Underface, no one can drag you out fast. If your hands lock, you die where you drop.”

He flexed his fingers. The movement was small. The tremor remained.

“I can walk,” he said.

“I did not ask whether you can walk,” she said. “I asked whether you can stand. Those are different conditions.”

He looked past her, toward the ladder shaft. The tunnel air carried the harsh edge of vented heat. A thin mist still drifted in the lantern light. The support behind them held on its new seat, cracks still open, strain redistributed but not repaired.

Jhace’s jaw tightened.

“Bram will want to know,” he said.

“I will tell Bram,” Tiffani said. “You will go topside. You will sit down somewhere with light. I will examine your hands and arms while you can still feel them.”

His mouth twitched once. It was not amusement.

“You mean while you can still measure them,” he said.

“Yes,” she said. “That too.”

They started toward the ladder shaft at a slow pace. Jhace’s balance checked twice on uneven stone. Each time he corrected without reaching for the wall. Each time his shoulders stiffened afterward.

Tiffani counted his steps and watched his hands.

He kept them close to his body, fingers curled slightly, as if he could reduce the tremor by limiting range of motion. The copper lines showed most clearly when lantern light hit the inside of his forearms. They did not brighten. They did not fade. They stayed.

At the ladder, Jhace put one hand on the first rung and stopped.

“Wait,” Tiffani said.

He looked back, irritation already rising.

“You are not climbing first,” she said. “If your grip slips, you fall into me. If mine slips, I fall away from you.”

“I am fine,” he said.

Tiffani reached into her satchel and pulled out a narrow glass strip, wrapped in cloth. She unwrapped it with care and held it near his forearm without touching.

The glass trembled faintly in her fingers. Not from her own shake. The vibration came from him, transmitted through air and the ladder frame.

She watched the edge of the strip.

“Your tremor frequency has shifted,” she said. “Higher than it was in the kiln corridor this morning.”

“I do not know what that means,” he said.

“It means your baseline is not stable,” she said. “It means your body is not recovering between uses.”

Jhace’s eyes dropped to the strip. Then back to her face.

“Put it away,” he said.

Tiffani wrapped the glass again and returned it to the satchel.

“You climb,” she said.

He held her gaze for a beat, then turned to the ladder and went up.

Tiffani followed, counting rungs, keeping her body close enough to catch him if his legs failed and far enough back that he could not mistake her for a brace. She reached the top of the shaft with grit in her teeth and the grid’s altered hum pressing through the stone into her bones.

The surface air in the kiln district tasted of smoke and wet dust.

Saint Merro had not settled. It had reorganized into a constant state of motion. Workers moved broken stone. Healers moved bodies. Runners moved information. The street itself carried a new pattern of cracks, some filled with chalk marks, some with fresh mortar, none of them trustworthy.

Jhace stepped away from the shaft and paused, shoulders tightening as he took in the noise.

A runner saw him and changed direction immediately.

Tiffani moved to block the approach without stepping directly between them. She angled her body so the runner’s path would require effort. Most people took the easier route.

The runner did not.

“You,” the runner said, breath tearing. “Holder. They need—”

“Not now,” Tiffani said.

The runner blinked at her, then looked around her shoulder.

“He held a support under the Dregs,” the runner said. “They said he’s the only one—”

“He is injured,” Tiffani said. “Get Bram Torrins if you need an Underface crew. If you need a brace crew, get Crown Engineering. If you need a healer, get one with hands that do not shake.”

The runner’s face flushed.

“He’s right there,” he said.

Jhace’s eyes had gone flat. His hands were behind his back.

“You can see him,” Tiffani said. “You cannot use him.”

The runner looked between them, then swallowed and backed away, muttering under his breath.

Jhace exhaled once through his nose.

“They will keep coming,” he said.

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “Which is why we pick where you are when they arrive.”

She guided him toward a narrow side street where a half-collapsed awning cast a thin band of shade over a stable wall section. The stone there held without visible shear. She checked the mortar lines quickly, then nodded.

“Sit,” she said.

Jhace sat on a low stack of intact bricks. His posture stayed rigid, as if he expected the wall behind him to shift.

Tiffani knelt in front of him and reached for his bandages.

He flinched.

“Do not,” he said.

“I am not going to hurt you,” she said.

“You already did,” he said, voice low. Not accusing. Stating.

Tiffani paused with her fingers on the knot.

“I am not going to add to it,” she said. “Hold still.”

Jhace held still.

She unwound the cloth. The skin of his palms was raw. Not burned black, not blistered the way Marisse’s hands had blistered when the oven wall failed, but heat-stressed and scraped, copper tint sunk into the lines of his skin like fine dust pressed into wet clay.

She turned his hand and examined the fingers. The tremor pulled at each joint. The shake was not violent now. It was worse than before.

“How many phases between your first use and your second,” she asked.

He hesitated.

“I don’t know,” he said. “Short.”

“After the first use in the street,” she said. “The beam. Then the building.”

“Less than a phase,” he said.

“And since then,” she said, “you have not gone more than one full phase without touching a failing structure.”

His silence was confirmation.

Tiffani reached for the glass strip again, held it near his palm, then near the inside of his wrist. The vibration was stronger near the copper lines.

“Your body is carrying residue,” she said.

“Residue,” he repeated.

“Yes,” she said. “Not on the skin. In it.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened.

“Can you remove it,” he asked.

“I do not know,” she said. “Which is why I am measuring it.”

He gave her a look that would have read as impatience on anyone else. On him it read as fear held down by habit.

Tiffani tightened her wraps with fresh cloth from her satchel, not the used bandage Bram had tied earlier. She secured the knot, then moved to his forearm and traced one of the copper bands with a fingertip without pressing.

The skin there was warmer than the surrounding area.

“Pain here,” she asked.

“Yes,” he said. “All the way up.”

“Any numbness,” she asked.

“No.”

“Grip weakness,” she asked.

He tried to pinch thumb to forefinger. His tremor snapped the motion into a stutter.

“Yes,” he said.

Tiffani logged it in her head. She did not pull out her book yet. She wanted his eyes on her, not on the ink.

“You will not use the grip again this phase,” she said.

Jhace’s gaze went past her to the street. Another runner crossed the far end, glancing toward them, then moving on.

“There will be another collapse,” he said.

“Yes,” she said. “And if you answer every collapse, you will stop answering all collapses. Your hands will fail first, then your arms, then your breath.”

He stared at her.

“You think you can schedule stone failure,” he said.

“I think I can schedule you,” she said. “I think I can choose where you spend yourself instead of letting the loudest person decide.”

His eyes narrowed.

“Who decides now,” he asked.

“I do,” she said.

He barked a short breath.

“No,” he said. “We decide. Partner.”

The word landed harder in Tiffani’s chest than she expected.

“Fine,” she said. “We decide.”

Footsteps approached, measured and unhurried.

Tiffani looked up.

Inspector Harlen came down the side street with two RRO escorts behind him. The escorts wore plain vests but moved with a coordination that marked them as more than runners. Harlen’s slate case was strapped to his chest. Dust covered his boots.

His gaze took in Jhace first. Then Tiffani. Then the loose bandage ends in her hands.

“Master Merrow,” he said.

“Inspector,” she replied.

Harlen’s eyes dropped to Jhace’s wrapped palms.

“Jhace Torrins,” Harlen said.

Jhace did not rise.

“I have no business with the RRO,” Jhace said.

“You do now,” Harlen said. “You have been reported in seven separate incidents involving anomalous structural stabilization. Witnesses describe copper-gold light. Aftereffects include residual heat and copper inclusion in stone.”

Tiffani kept her face still.

“You have been collecting reports quickly,” she said.

Harlen’s mouth moved once, not quite a smile.

“We are collecting everything,” he said. “Because the city cannot afford improvisation without accounting.”

Jhace’s fingers twitched behind the cloth.

Harlen stepped closer, careful to keep his distance from Jhace’s space.

“We are forming a registration framework,” Harlen said. “It is voluntary at this stage. Support and medical oversight in exchange for documentation and coordination.”

Jhace’s gaze sharpened.

“Voluntary,” he repeated.

“For now,” Harlen said, not denying it.

Tiffani stood, slow and deliberate, blocking Harlen’s direct line to Jhace without making it a physical challenge.

“You are here to recruit him,” she said.

“I am here to keep him alive long enough to be useful,” Harlen said. “And to keep civilians from depending on unknown holds that could fail without warning.”

Tiffani’s voice stayed even.

“He is injured,” she said. “He will not be going anywhere with you.”

Harlen’s eyes flicked to her hands.

“And you,” he said, “are the one marking his routes and measuring his residual effects.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

“You refused committee assignment,” Harlen said.

“I refused delay,” Tiffani said.

Harlen inclined his head once.

“Then take this as an alternative,” he said. “A field designation. You remain independent in your methods. You submit daily reports. Jhace Torrins remains deployed only with a secondary observer present.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened.

“I do not need an observer,” he said.

Harlen’s gaze did not move from him.

“You collapsed in the street after your second activation,” Harlen said. “You were carried off. You collapsed again under the Dregs. If you collapse in the wrong place, people die.”

Jhace’s eyes went flat again.

Tiffani felt her own pulse jump once, then settle.

“I am already a secondary observer,” she said. “I am already documenting. You want to name it and file it.”

“I want accountability,” Harlen said. “If you will not join the committee, then this is the compromise. You keep your urgency. We get records we can act on.”

“And what do you get to order,” Tiffani asked.

Harlen’s eyes shifted toward the main street.

“There is a priority structure,” he said. “A support line under the Crown administrative hall. The Piercing stressed the main arch. It is stable now. It will not remain stable. If it fails, the RRO loses its coordination hub. The council loses its chamber. The medical stockpiles stored beneath it are buried.”

Jhace shifted on the bricks.

Tiffani heard the small scrape of stone under his boot. She saw the way his shoulders tightened.

Harlen’s voice stayed calm.

“We have brace crews,” Harlen said. “We have engineers. We do not have enough time to rebuild the load path before the next aftershock. We need a temporary hold during reinforcement.”

Jhace’s eyes lifted to Harlen.

“You want my hands,” he said.

“I want your hands,” Harlen said. “And I want Master Merrow’s marks telling you where to place them.”

Tiffani kept her expression neutral.

“His condition is deteriorating,” she said. “If you are asking for a hold, you will get strict limits on duration and contact points.”

Harlen nodded once.

“Put your limits in writing,” he said. “Bring them with you.”

Jhace’s fingers twitched behind his back.

“No,” he said.

Harlen did not react.

“You can refuse,” Harlen said. “And if you do, the brace crews will still work. If the arch shifts while they’re under it, they will die. If the arch drops, the hall is lost. The city loses coordination and stockpiles while the fractures are still active.”

Jhace’s jaw set.

Tiffani watched his face and read the decision forming before he spoke. She saw it the same way she saw a crack widen: the tension, then the shift.

He looked at her.

“Partner,” he said.

Tiffani nodded once.

“Yes,” she said. “Partner.”

She turned back to Harlen.

“He will do it,” she said. “Under conditions. I will be present. I will mark the highest-stress nodes. He will not exceed my count.”

Harlen’s eyes narrowed slightly.

“You will count for him,” he said.

“I will count for him,” Tiffani said. “He does not stop on his own.”

Jhace’s mouth tightened.

“I stop,” he said.

Tiffani did not look at him.

“You stop when you fall,” she said. “That does not qualify.”

Bram’s voice cut in from the mouth of the side street.

“That one’s true,” Bram said.

He approached at a quick walk, dust on his clothes, a coil of rope over one shoulder. His eyes went to Harlen and the escorts, then to Jhace’s newly wrapped hands.

“Inspector,” Bram said, not greeting. Not friendly.

Harlen gave him a glance.

“Journeyman Torrins,” he said.

“Bram,” Bram corrected. “He’s not going alone.”

“He is not going at all without me,” Tiffani said.

Harlen looked between them. Then nodded once.

“Good,” he said. “Then we have a team.”

Jhace pushed himself up from the bricks. The motion cost him. His balance wavered. He corrected, jaw tight, not looking at Tiffani.

Tiffani stepped back half a pace to give him room to stand without her hand on him.

She reached for her satchel and secured the glass strip and spare cloth. She took her logbook out, then paused.

No time to write in comfort.

She wrote one line, fast and hard.

*Crown hall arch: hold required during reinforcement. Conditions: marked nodes only. Duration limit enforced by count.*

She shut the book.

Harlen turned toward the main street.

“Now,” he said.

Jhace started forward.

Tiffani fell into step beside him, eyes already measuring the street slope, the crack lines, the nearest stable walls. Bram moved on Jhace’s other side, a half-step back, ready to catch him if his legs failed and ready to hit anyone who tried to grab him by force.

The crowd on the main street shifted as they merged into it. Heads turned. A few voices rose. The word *holder* carried through the dust.

Tiffani kept her gaze forward.

If the Crown hall arch failed, the next Tide would be worse. If Jhace’s hands failed, the Tide after that would kill him.

She had chosen measurement over noise once and buried the cost in the ground.

This time she would watch the pattern in full light, count it, and force it to stop at the limit she set.

They moved toward the Crown district, where the stone carried more weight than the street could see.

i may have went a different route
# N01: Chapter 7 – Method Takes Shape

**Tide:** 1 (Quench) | **Phase:** Analytical Engagement | **POV:** Jhace | **Beat:** BEAT04 (Recalibration) | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses | **Relationship:** Shared Fear

***

The tremor did not stop.

Jhace sat on the edge of a pallet in Bram's workshop, hands resting on his knees, and watched his fingers shake. Three Tides since the Piercing. Five since he had held the kiln street wall. The tremor had been constant for eighteen phases now, a fine rapid twitch that ran from fingertips to wrists without pause.

He flexed his right hand. The motion stuttered. His thumb missed the intended angle twice before he forced it closed.

"Still?" Bram asked from the workbench.

"Still," Jhace said.

Bram set down his hammer and crossed the small space. He crouched and studied Jhace's hands without touching them.

"Worse or same?" he asked.

"Same," Jhace said. "Maybe worse. Hard to measure."

"You need rest," Bram said.

"I rested," Jhace said. "Eighteen phases. It did not help."

Bram's jaw tightened, but he did not argue. He stood and moved back to the bench, picking up a bent nail and setting it aside with more care than it needed.

Jhace looked down at his hands again. The tremor made small shadows jump on his knees. He had tried holding tools yesterday—a chalk stick, a wrench, a measuring cord. All of them slipped from his grip within breaths. His hands no longer trusted themselves.

Footsteps sounded on the ladder. Jhace looked up as Tiffani emerged from the shaft, logbook under one arm, a rolled sheet of paper in the other hand. Dust streaked her hair. Her eyes found him first, then moved to his hands, then back to his face.

"You came," she said.

"You asked," Jhace said.

She crossed to the bench and spread the paper flat. It showed a rough sketch of a junction wall, marked with chalk lines and annotations in her tight script. Stress nodes labeled. Load paths traced. Points of maximum strain circled.

"I mapped the patterns from your last five holds," she said. "There are consistencies."

Jhace pushed himself up and joined her at the bench. His legs answered slowly but without fail. That, at least, still worked.

He studied the drawing. The circled points matched places where the copper-gold glow had felt thickest under his palms. The lines connecting them followed paths he had felt but not named.

"You are putting numbers to what I feel," he said.

"Yes," Tiffani said. "And I am finding where you waste effort."

That caught him.

"Where," he asked.

She tapped a marked zone on the sketch.

"Here," she said. "You applied force uniformly across the entire fracture. But the load concentration was here." She moved her finger two hand-widths to the left. "You spent energy stabilizing stone that was already stable while the actual failure point received less than half of what it needed."

Jhace frowned.

"It held," he said.

"It held," Tiffani agreed. "But it cost you more than it should have. If I can show you where to press before you press, you spend less. The tremor might ease."

"Might," Jhace said.

"Might," she confirmed. She did not soften it.

He looked at his hands. The tremor did not care about her might.

"What do you need from me," he asked.

"Permission to mark the next site before you touch it," she said. "I place indicators where the stress is highest. You follow them. We measure the difference."

"And if it does not work?" he asked.

"Then we learn what does not work," she said. "That is still data."

Jhace considered the sketch. The patterns she had drawn made sense in a way his instinct did not. His body knew when to push. Her marks knew where.

"When," he asked.

"Next failure," she said. "Whenever that is. Bram sent word there is a cracked beam in the lower Dregs. No one under it yet, but the load is shifting."

"I tried holding stone without people under it," Jhace said. "In Chapter Four. The glow did not answer."

"Because there was no urgency," Tiffani said. "This beam is different. It will fail within a Tide. People will move back under it when they think it is safe. Your grip knows that."

Jhace did not like the certainty in her voice, but he could not argue with the logic.

"Fine," he said. "We test it."

Tiffani rolled the paper and tucked it under her arm.

"Good," she said. "Now sit. You look like you are about to fall."

"I am fine," Jhace said.

"You are shaking so hard I can hear your nails clicking," she said. "Sit."

He sat.

She moved to Bram's water jug, poured a cup, and brought it to him. He took it with both hands. The tremor made the surface ripple. He drank carefully, watching the water to keep it from spilling.

When he finished, she took the cup and set it aside, then crouched in front of him so their eyes were level.

"How much sleep did you get last night?" she asked.

"Enough," he said.

"How much," she repeated.

He did not answer.

Her gaze stayed on his face, patient and unyielding.

"Two phases," he admitted. "Maybe three."

"Why," she asked.

"Could not stop thinking about Junction Fourteen," he said. "The harmonic is still wrong. If it fails during cold phase, the entire lower kiln quarter loses heat. People die."

"So you lay awake counting deaths that have not happened yet," she said.

"Yes," he said.

She was quiet for a breath.

"I do the same," she said.

That surprised him.

"You?" he asked.

"Every night," she said. "I count the cracks I did not measure. The walls I did not mark. The patterns I might have missed. Then I calculate how many people die if I was wrong."

Her voice stayed level, but something raw moved under it.

Jhace stared at her.

"You hide it better than I do," he said.

"I have practice," she said. "Three Anneals of it. Since Marisse."

The name hung between them.

"Your apprentice," Jhace said. He remembered Bram mentioning her in passing.

"My friend," Tiffani corrected. "She asked if the oven readings worried me. I said no. I called it noise. She died when the wall let go."

Her hands curled into fists on her knees. The motion was tight, controlled, deliberate.

"Twenty-three," Jhace said quietly.

She looked up.

"That is how many died when I ignored the hum at Junction Seven," he said. "I felt the wrongness. I marked it for monitoring. I did not push for immediate action. The committee delayed. The tunnel failed."

He met her gaze.

"I know all their names," he said.

"So do I," she said. "Marisse's. And the six others who were in the shop when it went."

Silence settled. Bram had gone still at the bench, hammer forgotten in his hand.

"We are the same," Tiffani said at last. "You feel the cracks. I see the patterns. We both carry the dead we did not save."

"Yes," Jhace said.

"Then we stop pretending we can do this alone," she said. "You push where I mark. I mark where you feel. We divide the cost or we both break trying to carry it solo."

Jhace looked down at his shaking hands.

"What if dividing it is not enough," he asked.

"Then we fail together," she said. "But at least we will know we tried everything."

The tremor ran through his fingers in waves. He thought of Junction Fourteen's wrong harmonic. Of the kiln quarter losing heat. Of names he did not want to learn.

"All right," he said.

She nodded once, stood, and moved back to the bench. She picked up the rolled sketch and tucked it into her satchel with the logbook.

"Next failure," she said. "I mark. You push. We log the cost and the result."

"And if someone else wants to learn this?" Jhace asked. "If they have the glow too?"

Tiffani paused.

"Then we teach them the method," she said. "Before they spend themselves the way you are spending yourself."

Bram cleared his throat.

"That is a dangerous idea," he said. "Teaching people to break themselves for strangers."

"Teaching them to waste less while they do it," Tiffani corrected. "The breaking will happen either way. At least this way they might last longer."

Bram shook his head but did not argue further.

Jhace stood. His legs held. The tremor in his hands did not ease, but it did not worsen either.

"When do we start?" he asked.

"Now," Tiffani said. "The Dregs beam will not wait for us to feel ready."

She headed for the ladder. Jhace followed.

Behind them, Bram watched them go, his expression caught between pride and resignation.

The grid hummed at its altered pitch under their feet. The city fractured around them. Somewhere below, a beam carried too much weight and prepared to fail.

They would meet it with marks and grip and the hope that method could buy them a few more breaths before the cost took everything.

***

- **Relationship Milestone:** "Shared Fear" phase—both realize they're driven by terror of preventable deaths, decide to divide the burden

# CHARACTER VOICE ARCHITECTURE
## OmniMeta Saga: Te'Oga World

**Purpose:** Complete reference for character speech patterns, metaphor frameworks, and voice differentiation across all character tiers.

**Constraint Reminder:**  
- **Narration:** No similes, no metaphors, no anthropomorphizing  
- **Dialogue:** Natural speech with idioms, metaphors, and personification allowed

---

## CHARACTER DOSSIER TEMPLATE

**Age/Build:**  
**Dress:**  
**Speech Rhythm:**  
**Vocabulary Tendencies:**

**Humor Style:**  
**Verbal Tics/Patterns:**

# TIER 1: PROTAGONISTS

## JHACE TORRINS

**Craft/Role:** Grip-holder, Underface inspector, Soulpulse manifestation (Wholeness)  
**Age/Build:** Mid-30s, lean, wiry strength from years in tunnels  
**Dress:** Work coat (oil-stained, patched), heavy boots, bandages on hands (post-Piercing), tool belt with worn leather

**Speech Rhythm:** Short. Clipped. Drops subjects. Uses fragments under stress.  
- "Work first." Not "I'll work first."  
- "Hold. Long enough." Not "I need to hold it long enough."

**Vocabulary Tendencies:**  
- Tactile verbs: grip, press, settle, shift, carry  
- Load/weight language: "too much," "can't take it," "holds"  
- Avoids abstractions - everything is concrete, physical  
- Uses exact trade terms without explaining them

**Metaphor Framework:**  
Draws from **stone, load, structure, failure points**  
- "That argument has cracks in it."  
- "You're putting weight where it won't hold."  
- "Your logic is settling wrong."  
- Does NOT elaborate metaphors - states and moves on

**Humor Style:** Dry. Deadpan. Often unintentional. Doesn't realize he's funny.

**Verbal Tics/Patterns:**  
- Counts things under stress (heartbeats, names, breaths)  
- Repeats "Not again" as internal mantra  
- Says "I know" when defensive  
- Uses names rarely (only when absolutely necessary)  
- Answers questions with minimal words: "Yes." "No." "Maybe."

**How Others React:**  
- Bram teases him for being too serious  
- Tiffani finds his directness refreshing (no wasted words)  
- People who want reassurance find him cold  
- Bureaucrats find him frustrating (won't elaborate)

**Sample Dialogue:**  
> "The wall holds or it doesn't. Talking won't change the load."

> "Work first."

> "I know."

---

## TIFFANI MERROW

**Craft/Role:** Glassworker, structural optics, precision measurement, Soulpulse manifestation (Form)  
**Age/Build:** Late 20s, lean, hands always slightly stained with glass dust  
**Dress:** Work apron over practical tunic, hair tied back tight, carries logbook everywhere, ink stains on fingers, chalk dust on sleeves

**Speech Rhythm:** Precise. Complete sentences. Technical accuracy even in casual talk. Doesn't soften.  
- "The deviation is measurable." Not "I think something's off."  
- Uses exact numbers: "Eighteen phases" not "a while"  
- Never hedges or softens bad news

**Vocabulary Tendencies:**  
- Measurement language: quantify, assess, calibrate, tolerance  
- Glass/optics terms: refraction, clarity, distortion, stress patterns  
- Turns emotions into data: "elevated concern" instead of "worry"  
- Corrects imprecise language: "Not 'broken.' Fractured."

**Metaphor Framework:**  
Draws from **glass, light, measurement, precision instruments**  
- "Your argument has too many distortions to be clear."  
- "That reasoning is refracting wrong - you're bending around the real issue."  
- "You're measuring with a warped lens."  
- Uses optical/material science metaphors even for emotions

**Humor Style:** Cutting. Analytical. Often doesn't realize she's being harsh.

**Verbal Tics/Patterns:**  
- Corrects imprecise language mid-conversation  
- Says "That's not accurate" frequently  
- Adds qualifiers: "approximately," "within tolerance," "measurable"  
- Uses "I do not" instead of "I don't" (formal contractions

## BRAM

**Craft/Role:** Journeyman grip-worker, Jhace's work-partner  
**Age/Build:** Early 40s, broader build, gray threading his hair  
**Dress:** Similar work gear to Jhace but better maintained, fewer patches, clean enough to show he cares

**Speech Rhythm:** Conversational. Warm. Uses full sentences. Teasing edge.  
- Speaks in paragraphs where Jhace speaks in fragments  
- Asks questions to make points instead of stating them directly  
- Fills silences with talk

**Vocabulary Tendencies:**  
- Folk sayings and idioms  
- Softer language than Jhace: "things are rough" vs "grid's failing"  
- Uses people's names frequently (connection-building)  
- Avoids technical precision in favor of relatable descriptions

**Metaphor Framework:**  
**PRIMARY METAPHOR CHARACTER**  
Draws from **everything** - often awkwardly, often mixing domains  
- "You're wound tighter than a spring in a glass jar... wait, that doesn't work."  
- "She measures things like... like a person who measures things a lot." *(Jhace: "Shut up, Bram.")*  
- "The grid's singing a new song, and I don't like the tune." *(Actually lands.)*  
- Starts metaphors, realizes they're failing, commits anyway  
- Sometimes brilliant, often terrible, always trying

**Humor Style:** Self-deprecating. Tries to lighten tension. Sometimes succeeds, sometimes makes it worse.

**Verbal Tics/Patterns:**  
- Starts metaphors, realizes they're bad, keeps going anyway  
- Says "Listen" before making a point  
- Asks rhetorical questions: "You know what I mean?"  
- Calls Jhace by name constantly (Jhace almost never uses names)  
- Uses "we" even when talking about Jhace's solo work

**How Others React:**  
- Jhace tolerates him (barely) - "Bram." as a full warning  
- Tiffani ignores his metaphors entirely, responds only to facts  
- Chloen thinks he's funny  
- When his metaphors fail: awkward silence, someone telling him to stop  
- People like him even when he's annoying

**Sample Dialogue:**  
> "You're like a... a beam that's forgotten how to bend. No wait, that's— Look, all I'm saying is you need to let people help before you crack like an over-fired—"  
> Jhace: "Bram."  
> "Right. Shutting up."

> "Listen, the way I see it, we're all under pressure here. Like stones in a... no, that's not it either."

# N01: Constraint Recognition and Editing Lens (Locked)

This note captures the pattern-recognition that emerged during the Chapters 1–6 revision pass.
It is meant as an internal reference for future chapter drafts and line-edits.

## 1) “As if” is the escape hatch
When confidence in direct description drops, the draft tends to reach for comparison.

**Flag phrases**
- “as if …”
- “like …”
- “the way …”
- “as … as …”

**Fix principle**
Replace comparison with **named physical behavior**:
- what changed (sound / motion / heat / pressure / visibility)
- where it changed (location / surface / depth)
- by how much (relative scale, not Earth units)
- what it implies (risk, load path, instability)

## 2) Anthropomorphizing sneaks in through verbs
Agency slips in via action words assigned to inanimate systems.

**High-risk subjects**
- stone, wall, beam, brace, tunnel
- grid, pipe, joint
- city, district, street
- weight, load, pressure

**High-risk verbs**
- choose, decide, remember, speak, learn, want, try, refuse, insist, punish, lie

**Fix principle**
Swap “intent” for **mechanics**:
- “wanted to fall” → “load shifted / crack widened / support dropped”
- “the tunnel chose” → “failure propagated / collapse followed”
- “the city learned” → “the city organized / crews moved / routines formed”

## 3) Constraints that strengthen voice
The constraint isn’t a limiter; it forces precision.

### Narration rule (absolute)
- No similes/metaphors.
- No anthropomorphizing.
- No Earth-specific frames (music theory steps, modern idioms, measurements).

Narration should read like a **field report written by a person with a pulse**:
clean, sensory, tactical, consequence-forward.

### Dialogue rule (character-voice governed)
Dialogue can use:
- folk idioms and compressed phrasing
- metaphor/personification as *human misattribution*
- craft-based shorthand (Tiffani’s material language, Underface slang)

**Guardrails**
- Keep idioms **in-world** (stone/heat/grid/load/Tides/Phases), not Earth-culture imports.
- Use metaphor as characterization, not as authorial decoration.
- If a metaphor appears in dialogue, narration must still describe the physical reality directly.

## 4) Operational checklist for each chapter pass
1. Search for: **“as if”, “like”, “the way”, “as … as”**
2. Search for inanimate subject + agency verb:
   - stone/wall/grid/city + choose/remember/speak/learn/want/try/refuse
3. Scan for Earth frames:
   - musical steps, modern units, modern idioms
4. If a line fe

[ASSISTANT]
Yep — this route works **and** it fits what you tagged (BEAT04 recalibration, 0 uses, “Shared Fear”). It also does exactly what Chapter 7 needs to do in a tight novella: method + emotional alignment + a forward-moving decision at the end.

There are a few **must-fix** items, though — mostly continuity and a handful of narration-only constraint slips.

## Non-negotiables to fix

### 1) Timeline continuity error
**Current**
> “Three Tides since the Piercing. Five since he had held the kiln street wall.”

This contradicts your locked Ch4 (“We’re into Tide One now…”) and collapses the internal clock.

**Fix (minimal, keeps your intent)**
> “Since late Striking, the tremor had been constant for eighteen phases now.”

(You already give the “eighteen phases” number; let that do the work and drop the Tide-counting.)

---

### 2) Meta reference to “Chapter Four”
**Current (dialogue)**
> “In Chapter Four.”

That’s out-of-world language. Needs to become in-scene memory.

**Fix**
> “Earlier. In the tunnel.”

---

## Narration-only constraint violations I’d clean

### A) Anthropomorphizing
**Current**
> “His hands no longer trusted themselves.”

**Fix**
> “His grip no longer held.”

---

### B) Anthropomorphizing
**Current**
> “The tremor did not care about her might.”

**Fix**
> “The tremor did not change.”

---

### C) Figurative phrasing (narration)
**Current**
> “The name hung between them.”

**Fix**
> “After the name, neither of them spoke for a breath.”

---

### D) Intent/agency drift (narration)
**Current**
> “a beam carried too much weight and prepared to fail.”

**Fix**
> “a beam carried too much weight and was close to failure.”

---

### E) “Buy breaths” metaphor (narration)
**Current**
> “the hope that method could buy them a few more breaths…”

**Fix**
> “the hope that method could reduce the cost…”

That keeps the meaning and stays inside the physical/analytic lens.

---

# Chapter 7 – Method Takes Shape (constraint-clean pass)

The tremor did not stop.

Jhace sat on the edge of a pallet in Bram's workshop, hands resting on his knees, and watched his fingers shake. Since late Striking, the tremor had been constant for eighteen phases now, a fine rapid twitch that ran from fingertips to wrists without pause.

He flexed his right hand. The motion stuttered. His thumb missed the intended angle twice before he forced it closed.

"Still?" Bram asked from the workbench.

"Still," Jhace said.

Bram set down his hammer and crossed the small space. He crouched and studied Jhace's hands without touching them.

"Worse or same?" he asked.

"Same," Jhace said. "Maybe worse. Hard to measure."

"You need rest," Bram said.

"I rested," Jhace said. "Eighteen phases. It did not help."

Bram's jaw tightened, but he did not argue. He stood and moved back to the bench, picking up a bent nail and setting it aside with more care than it needed.

Jhace looked down at his hands again. The tremor made small shadows jump on his knees. He had tried holding tools yesterday—a chalk stick, a wrench, a measuring cord. All of them slipped from his grip within breaths. His grip no longer held.

Footsteps sounded on the ladder. Jhace looked up as Tiffani emerged from the shaft, logbook under one arm, a rolled sheet of paper in the other hand. Dust streaked her hair. Her eyes found him first, then moved to his hands, then back to his face.

"You came," she said.

"You asked," Jhace said.

She crossed to the bench and spread the paper flat. It showed a rough sketch of a junction wall, marked with chalk lines and annotations in her tight script. Stress nodes labeled. Load paths traced. Points of maximum strain circled.

"I mapped the patterns from your last five holds," she said. "There are consistencies."

Jhace pushed himself up and joined her at the bench. His legs answered slowly but without fail. That, at least, still worked.

He studied the drawing. The circled points matched places where the copper-gold glow had felt thickest under his palms. The lines connecting them followed paths he had felt but not named.

"You are putting numbers to what I feel," he said.

"Yes," Tiffani said. "And I am finding where you waste effort."

That caught him.

"Where," he asked.

She tapped a marked zone on the sketch.

"Here," she said. "You applied force across the entire fracture. But the load concentration was here." She moved her finger two hand-widths to the left. "You spent output stabilizing stone that was already stable while the actual failure point received less than half of what it needed."

Jhace frowned.

"It held," he said.

"It held," Tiffani agreed. "But it cost you more than it should have. If I can show you where to press before you press, you spend less. The tremor might ease."

"Might," Jhace said.

"Might," she confirmed. She did not soften it.

He looked at his hands. The tremor did not change.

"What do you need from me," he asked.

"Permission to mark the next site before you touch it," she said. "I place indicators where the stress is highest. You follow them. We measure the difference."

"And if it does not work?" he asked.

"Then we learn what does not work," she said. "That is still data."

Jhace considered the sketch. The patterns she had drawn made sense in a way his instinct did not. His body knew when to push. Her marks knew where.

"When," he asked.

"Next failure," she said. "Whenever that is. Bram sent word there is a cracked beam in the lower Dregs. No one under it yet, but the load is shifting."

"I tried holding stone without people under it," Jhace said. "Earlier. In the tunnel. The glow did not answer."

"Because there was no urgency," Tiffani said. "This beam is different. It will fail within a Tide. People will move back under it when they think it is safe. Your grip knows that."

Jhace did not like the certainty in her voice, but he could not argue with the logic.

"Fine," he said. "We test it."

Tiffani rolled the paper and tucked it under her arm.

"Good," she said. "Now sit. You look like you are about to fall."

"I am fine," Jhace said.

"You are shaking so hard I can hear your nails clicking," she said. "Sit."

He sat.

She moved to Bram's water jug, poured a cup, and brought it to him. He took it with both hands. The tremor made the surface ripple. He drank carefully, watching the water to keep it from spilling.

When he finished, she took the cup and set it aside, then crouched in front of him so their eyes were level.

"How much sleep did you get last night?" she asked.

"Enough," he said.

"How much," she repeated.

He did not answer.

Her gaze stayed on his face, patient and unyielding.

"Two phases," he admitted. "Maybe three."

"Why," she asked.

"Could not stop thinking about Junction Fourteen," he said. "The harmonic is still wrong. If it fails during cold phase, the entire lower kiln quarter loses heat. People die."

"So you lay awake counting deaths that have not happened yet," she said.

"Yes," he said.

She was quiet for a breath.

"I do the same," she said.

That surprised him.

"You?" he asked.

"Every night," she said. "I count the cracks I did not measure. The walls I did not mark. The patterns I might have missed. Then I calculate how many people die if I was wrong."

Her voice stayed level, but something raw moved under it.

Jhace stared at her.

"You hide it better than I do," he said.

"I have practice," she said. "Three Anneals of it. Since Marisse."

After the name, neither of them spoke for a breath.

"Your apprentice," Jhace said. He remembered Bram mentioning her in passing.

"My friend," Tiffani corrected. "She asked if the oven readings worried me. I said no. I called it noise. She died when the wall let go."

Her hands curled into fists on her knees. The motion was tight, controlled, deliberate.

"Twenty-three," Jhace said quietly.

She looked up.

"That is how many died when I ignored the hum at Junction Seven," he said. "I felt the wrongness. I marked it for monitoring. I did not push for immediate action. The committee delayed. The tunnel failed."

He met her gaze.

"I know all their names," he said.

"So do I," she said. "Marisse's. And the six others who were in the shop when it went."

Bram had gone still at the bench, hammer forgotten in his hand.

"We are the same," Tiffani said at last. "You feel the cracks. I see the patterns. We both carry the dead we did not save."

"Yes," Jhace said.

"Then we stop pretending we can do this alone," she said. "You push where I mark. I mark where you feel. We divide the cost or we both break trying to carry it solo."

Jhace looked down at his shaking hands.

"What if dividing it is not enough," he asked.

"Then we fail together," she said. "But at least we will know we tried everything."

The tremor ran through his fingers in waves. He thought of Junction Fourteen's wrong harmonic. Of the kiln quarter losing heat. Of names he did not want to learn.

"All right," he said.

She nodded once, stood, and moved back to the bench. She picked up the rolled sketch and tucked it into her satchel with the logbook.

"Next failure," she said. "I mark. You push. We log the cost and the result."

"And if someone else wants to learn this?" Jhace asked. "If they have the glow too?"

Tiffani paused.

"Then we teach them the method," she said. "Before they spend themselves the way you are spending yourself."

Bram cleared his throat.

"That is a dangerous idea," he said. "Teaching people to break themselves for strangers."

"Teaching them to waste less while they do it," Tiffani corrected. "The breaking will happen either way. At least this way they might last longer."

Bram shook his head but did not argue further.

Jhace stood. His legs held. The tremor in his hands did not ease, but it did not worsen either.

"When do we start?" he asked.

"Now," Tiffani said. "The Dregs beam will not wait for us to feel ready."

She headed for the ladder. Jhace followed.

Behind them, Bram watched them go, his expression caught between pride and resignation.

The grid hummed at its altered pitch under their feet. The city fractured around them. Somewhere below, a beam carried too much weight and was close to failure.

They would meet it with marks and grip, and they would measure whether method reduced the cost.

***

If you want, I can do the same kind of **constraint scan** on your next Chapter 8 draft (the Dregs beam test) before you lock it—because that scene will be where “method takes shape” has to cash out in results.

[USER]
# N01: Chapter 8 – Where It Answers

**Tide:** 2 (Kindling) | **Phase:** Functional Partnership | **POV:** Jhace | **Beat:** BEAT05 (Training) | **Soulpulse:** 1 use (efficiency test) | **Relationship:** Work and argue as a unit

***

The beam in the lower Dregs had three phases left before it failed.

Jhace felt it the moment his palm touched the cracked wood. The fracture ran diagonal through the grain, shallow at one end, deep at the other. Above it, the ceiling carried weight from two floors and a roof. Below it, a workshop sat empty, cleared by guild order but not yet braced.

"People will move back in before next Tide," Tiffani said beside him. She crouched near the base, measuring the angle with a cord marked in hand-widths. "The guild posted a closure order, but no one enforces it. Too many displaced already."

Jhace withdrew his hand and stepped back. The tremor in his fingers jumped higher for a breath, then settled into its constant rhythm.

"Where do I press," he asked.

Tiffani pulled chalk from her satchel and moved along the beam. She touched three points in sequence, pausing at each to check the load above.

"Here," she said, marking an X on the wood near the deepest part of the crack. "Primary stress concentration. Maximum load transfer."

She moved a hand-width to the left.

"Here. Secondary node. Carries half what the first does but critical for distribution."

A third mark, near the shallow end.

"Here. Tertiary. Ignore it unless the first two hold and you have output left."

Jhace studied the pattern. The marks did not follow the visible crack. They sat where stress converged, not where the break showed.

"You are certain," he said.

"The load paths do not lie," Tiffani said. "Your instinct will tell you to spread force across the whole fracture. Do not. Focus here." She tapped the primary mark. "Then here. Ignore everything else unless I tell you otherwise."

Jhace flexed his hands. The tremor made the motion stutter.

"And if it does not answer," he asked.

"Then we learn the method does not work," she said. "Better to know now than after you waste yourself on ten more holds."

He moved to the beam and set both palms over the primary mark. Cold wood. Rough grain. The weight above pressed through it in a pattern he felt but could not see.

Hold, he thought. They will return. They always return.

Heat rose under his palms.

It came slower than before. Not the sharp surge he had felt when Chloen screamed under the timber. Not the desperate flood when the kiln wall tried to fold. This built like warmth in a closed room, gradual and steady.

Copper-gold light seeped from his hands into the wood.

The glow followed the grain, then dove toward the crack. It found the stress concentration Tiffani had marked and pooled there, bright and thick. The beam shuddered once. Load shifted through the contact.

"Secondary," Tiffani said.

Jhace moved his left hand to the second mark without breaking contact with his right. The glow spread sideways, threading between the two points. His forearms lit with vein-lines, faint copper from wrist to elbow.

The beam settled.

"Tertiary?" he asked. His voice came tight.

"No," Tiffani said. "Hold where you are. The first two are carrying it."

Jhace held.

The copper-gold glow stayed narrow, concentrated at the marks. It did not chase every splinter. It did not flood the entire beam. It pressed where Tiffani had told him to press and stopped.

Pain climbed his arms in slow, steady waves. The tremor in his hands jumped higher. Sweat ran into his eyes.

"Fifteen heartbeats," Tiffani said. She was counting, slate in hand. "The distribution is stable. You can let go."

"It is not sealed," Jhace said.

"It does not need to be sealed," she said. "It needs to hold long enough for the guild to brace it properly. Let go."

He let go.

The copper-gold cut out. The beam shifted a fraction and caught. A few splinters fell and tapped the floor. The crack remained, but the load above stayed where it was.

Jhace's hands dropped to his sides. The tremor ran hard, making his nails click against his belt. Pain sat bright in his palms and wrists. The vein-lines on his forearms pulsed twice more, then faded.

He flexed his fingers. They answered, barely.

"Faster fade," Tiffani said. She was writing. "Vein-lines cleared in under thirty heartbeats. Tremor elevated but not worse than baseline. Beam stable at marked nodes."

She looked up.

"That was half the output you used on the kiln wall," she said. "And it held just as well."

Jhace stared at the beam.

"Half," he said.

"Approximately," she confirmed. "Focused application. No wasted distribution. The method works."

He sat down hard on the workshop floor. His legs had stopped holding him without asking permission first.

Tiffani crouched beside him and pressed the back of her hand to his forehead.

"No fever," she said. "Tremor elevated but recoverable. How do you feel?"

"Used," he said. "But not spent."

"Good," she said. She sat beside him, back against the wall, and kept writing. "We have confirmation. Marked stress points reduce cost. You can do this more often if you do it correctly."

"More often still means more cost," Jhace said.

"Yes," she said. "But slower accumulation. That matters."

Footsteps sounded on the ladder behind them. Jhace turned his head.

A man climbed into view. Young, maybe twenty-five Turns. Guild vest marked with mason's tools. His hands carried fresh chalk dust. He stopped when he saw them, gaze moving from Jhace to the beam to the faint copper shimmer still clinging to the wood.

"You held it," the man said.

"For now," Jhace said. "Guild needs to brace it."

"I know," the man said. He stepped closer, eyes on Jhace's hands. "I felt the hum. In the stone. Two Tides ago. During a wall repair."

Jhace went still.

"Felt what," he asked.

"Heat under my palms," the man said. "The stone pushed back. Like it was listening. Then copper light. Just for a breath. I thought I was sick."

Tiffani stopped writing.

"Did it cost you," she asked.

The man held out his hands. They shook, faint but visible.

"Started after," he said. "Has not stopped since."

Jhace recognized that shake. He knew the exhaustion behind it. The confusion.

"What is your name," he asked.

"Kylar," the man said. "Journeyman mason. I heard people talking about a holder who could touch cracks and make them stay. I came to see if it was real."

"It is real," Jhace said.

"Can you teach me how to do it without breaking myself," Kylar asked.

Jhace looked at Tiffani. She met his gaze, then looked back at Kylar.

"We are learning that ourselves," she said. "But yes. We can try."

Kylar's shoulders dropped. Relief and fear crossed his face together.

"When," he asked.

"Now," Tiffani said. She stood and brushed dust from her trousers. "If you want to learn, you start by learning what not to do."

Jhace pushed himself up. His legs held this time. The tremor in his hands had not eased, but it had not worsened either.

"There is a practice wall three levels up," he said. "Cracked but not critical. No one under it. We mark it. You try to follow the marks. We log what happens."

"And if I fail," Kylar asked.

"Then you fail where no one dies from it," Jhace said. "Better than learning on a live site."

Kylar nodded slowly.

"All right," he said.

They climbed.

The practice wall was older than most in the Dregs, part of a storage hall that had emptied after the Piercing. The crack ran vertical through three blocks, shallow but persistent. Tiffani measured it with her cord, then marked two stress points with chalk.

"Press here," she said to Kylar. "Not across the whole crack. Just here. Let the material tell you where it needs support."

Kylar set his palms over the mark. His hands shook worse than Jhace's.

"How do I make it answer," Kylar asked.

"You do not make it," Jhace said. "You ask. And you mean it."

Kylar closed his eyes. His breathing slowed. Sweat beaded on his forehead.

Nothing happened.

"It is not answering," Kylar said.

"Because there is no urgency," Tiffani said. "The wall is not about to fail. No one is under it. Your grip does not activate without need."

"Then how do I practice," Kylar asked.

Tiffani looked at Jhace. He saw the same question in her eyes.

"We need a different test," Jhace said slowly. "Something that feels urgent without being dangerous."

"I do not know how to make that," Tiffani said.

"Neither do I," Jhace admitted.

Kylar pulled his hands away from the wall. Frustration sat tight on his face.

"So I wait until something is about to crush someone," he said. "And then I find out if I can stop it. That is the training?"

"That is what I did," Jhace said. "It nearly killed me. We are trying to find a better way."

"There might not be one," Kylar said.

The words sat in the air between them. True and bitter.

Tiffani closed her logbook.

"We will find one," she said. "Or we will design one. But not today."

Kylar stared at the wall for a long moment, then nodded and turned toward the ladder.

"Send word when you do," he said.

He climbed down.

Jhace and Tiffani stood in the empty hall. The practice wall held its crack without help. The city hummed around them at its altered pitch.

"He is right," Jhace said. "There might not be a better way."

"Then we make one," Tiffani said. "Because sending people to learn by almost dying is not a method. It is triage."

She moved to the ladder.

Jhace followed.

The Dregs beam had held with half the cost. The method worked. But teaching it to others meant solving a problem neither of them had an answer for yet.

They climbed toward the surface, carrying the question with them.

***

## Narration constraint violations and fixes

### 1) Simile (narration)
**Before:**  
> “This built like warmth in a closed room, gradual and steady.”

**After:**  
> “This built gradually, steady and controlled.”

---

### 2) “Dove” + “found” (figurative/agency in narration)
**Before:**  
> “The glow followed the grain, then dove toward the crack. It found the stress concentration…”

**After:**  
> “The glow followed the grain, then ran toward the crack. It gathered at the stress concentration…”

---

### 3) “Chase” (anthropomorphic verb in narration)
**Before:**  
> “It did not chase every splinter.”

**After:**  
> “It did not spread into every splinter.”

---

### 4) “Pain climbed” (figurative narration)
**Before:**  
> “Pain climbed his arms in slow, steady waves.”

**After:**  
> “Pain spread up his arms in slow, steady waves.”

---

### 5) Legs “asking permission” (anthropomorphizing narration)
**Before:**  
> “His legs had stopped holding him without asking permission first.”

**After:**  
> “His legs failed without warning.”

---

### 6) Expression “crossed his face” (mild figurative; optional but keeps the baseline strict)
**Before:**  
> “Relief and fear crossed his face together.”

**After:**  
> “Relief and fear showed on his face together.”

---

### 7) “Words sat in the air” (figurative narration)
**Before:**  
> “The words sat in the air between them.”

**After:**  
> “After the words, none of them spoke for a breath.”

---

### 8) Wall “held its crack” (anthropomorphizing narration)
**Before:**  
> “The practice wall held its crack without help.”

**After:**  
> “The crack remained without change.”

---

### 9) “Carrying the question” (figurative narration)
**Before:**  
> “They climbed toward the surface, carrying the question with them.”

**After:**  
> “They climbed toward the surface with the question still unanswered.”

## Chapter 8 – Where It Answers (constraint-clean narration)

The beam in the lower Dregs had three phases left before it failed.

Jhace felt it the moment his palm touched the cracked wood. The fracture ran diagonal through the grain, shallow at one end, deep at the other. Above it, the ceiling carried weight from two floors and a roof. Below it, a workshop sat empty, cleared by guild order but not yet braced.

"People will move back in before next Tide," Tiffani said beside him. She crouched near the base, measuring the angle with a cord marked in hand-widths. "The guild posted a closure order, but no one enforces it. Too many displaced already."

"Where do I press," he asked.

Tiffani pulled chalk from her satchel and moved along the beam. She touched three points in sequence, pausing at each to check the load above.

"Here," she said, marking an X on the wood near the deepest part of the crack. "Primary stress concentration. Maximum load transfer."

She moved a hand-width to the left.

"Here. Secondary node. Carries half what the first does but critical for distribution."

A third mark, near the shallow end.

"Here. Tertiary. Ignore it unless the first two hold and you have output left."

Jhace studied the pattern. The marks did not follow the visible crack. They sat where stress converged, not where the break showed.

"You are certain," he said.

"The load paths do not lie," Tiffani said. "Your instinct will tell you to spread force across the whole fracture. Do not. Focus here." She tapped the primary mark. "Then here. Ignore everything else unless I tell you otherwise."

Jhace flexed his hands. The tremor made the motion stutter.

"And if it does not answer," he asked.

"Then we learn the method does not work," she said. "Better to know now than after you waste yourself on ten more holds."

He moved to the beam and set both palms over the primary mark. Cold wood. Rough grain. The weight above pressed through it in a pattern he felt but could not see.

Hold, he thought. They will return. They always return.

Heat rose under his palms.

It came slower than before. Not the sharp surge he had felt when Chloen screamed under the timber. Not the desperate flood when the kiln wall tried to fold. This built gradually, steady and controlled.

Copper-gold light seeped from his hands into the wood.

The glow followed the grain, then ran toward the crack. It gathered at the stress concentration Tiffani had marked and pooled there, bright and thick. The beam shuddered once. Load shifted through the contact.

"Secondary," Tiffani said.

Jhace moved his left hand to the second mark without breaking contact with his right. The glow spread sideways, threading between the two points. His forearms lit with vein-lines, faint copper from wrist to elbow.

The beam settled.

"Tertiary?" he asked. His voice came tight.

"No," Tiffani said. "Hold where you are. The first two are carrying it."

Jhace held.

The copper-gold glow stayed narrow, concentrated at the marks. It did not spread into every splinter. It pressed where Tiffani had told him to press and stopped.

Pain spread up his arms in slow, steady waves. The tremor in his hands jumped higher. Sweat ran into his eyes.

"It is not sealed," Jhace said.

"It does not need to be sealed," she said. "It needs to hold long enough for the guild to brace it properly. Let go."

He let go.

The copper-gold cut out. The beam shifted a fraction and caught. A few splinters fell and tapped the floor. The crack remained, but the load above stayed where it was.

Jhace's hands dropped to his sides. The tremor ran hard, making his nails click against his belt. Pain sat bright in his palms and wrists. The vein-lines on his forearms pulsed twice more, then faded.

He flexed his fingers. They answered, barely.

She looked up.

Jhace stared at the beam.

"Half," he said.

"Approximately," she confirmed. "Focused application. No wasted distribution. The method works."

He sat down hard on the workshop floor. His legs failed without warning.

Tiffani crouched beside him and pressed the back of her hand to his forehead.

"No fever," she said. "Tremor elevated but recoverable. How do you feel?"

"Used," he said. "But not spent."

"Good," she said. She sat beside him, back against the wall, and kept writing. "We have confirmation. Marked stress points reduce cost. You can do this more often if you do it correctly."

"More often still means more cost," Jhace said.

"Yes," she said. "But slower accumulation. That matters."

Footsteps sounded on the ladder behind them. Jhace turned his head.

A man climbed into view. Young, maybe twenty-five Turns. Guild vest marked with mason's tools. His hands carried fresh chalk dust. He stopped when he saw them, gaze moving from Jhace to the beam to the faint copper shimmer still clinging to the wood.

"You held it," the man said.

"For now," Jhace said. "Guild needs to brace it."

"I know," the man said. He stepped closer, eyes on Jhace's hands. "I felt the hum. In the stone. Two Tides ago. During a wall repair."

Jhace went still.

"Felt what," he asked.

"Heat under my palms," the man said. "The stone pushed back. Like it was listening. Then copper light. Just for a breath. I thought I was sick."

Tiffani stopped writing.

"Did it cost you," she asked.

The man held out his hands. They shook, faint but visible.

"Started after," he said. "Has not stopped since."

Jhace recognized that shake. He knew the exhaustion behind it. The confusion.

"What is your name," he asked.

"Kylar," the man said. "Journeyman mason. I heard people talking about a holder who could touch cracks and make them stay. I came to see if it was real."

"It is real," Jhace said.

"Can you teach me how to do it without breaking myself," Kylar asked.

Jhace looked at Tiffani. She met his gaze, then looked back at Kylar.

"We are learning that ourselves," she said. "But yes. We can try."

Kylar's shoulders dropped. Relief and fear showed on his face together.

"When," he asked.

"Now," Tiffani said. She stood and brushed dust from her trousers. "If you want to learn, you start by learning what not to do."

Jhace pushed himself up. His legs held this time. The tremor in his hands had not eased, but it had not worsened either.

"There is a practice wall three levels up," he said. "Cracked but not critical. No one under it. We mark it. You try to follow the marks. We log what happens."

"And if I fail," Kylar asked.

"Then you fail where no one dies from it," Jhace said. "Better than learning on a live site."

Kylar nodded slowly.

"All right," he said.

They climbed.

The practice wall was older than most in the Dregs, part of a storage hall that had emptied after the Piercing. The crack ran vertical through three blocks, shallow but persistent. Tiffani measured it with her cord, then marked two stress points with chalk.

"Press here," she said to Kylar. "Not across the whole crack. Just here. Let the material tell you where it needs support."

Kylar set his palms over the mark. His hands shook worse than Jhace's.

"How do I make it answer," Kylar asked.

"You do not make it," Jhace said. "You ask. And you mean it."

Kylar closed his eyes. His breathing slowed. Sweat beaded on his forehead.

Nothing happened.

"It is not answering," Kylar said.

"Because there is no urgency," Tiffani said. "The wall is not about to fail. No one is under it. Your grip does not activate without need."

"Then how do I practice," Kylar asked.

Tiffani looked at Jhace. He saw the same question in her eyes.

"We need a different test," Jhace said slowly. "Something that feels urgent without being dangerous."

"I do not know how to make that," Tiffani said.

"Neither do I," Jhace admitted.

Kylar pulled his hands away from the wall. Frustration sat tight on his face.

"So I wait until something is about to crush someone," he said. "And then I find out if I can stop it. That is the training?"

"That is what I did," Jhace said. "It nearly killed me. We are trying to find a better way."

"There might not be one," Kylar said.

After the words, none of them spoke for a breath. True and bitter.

Tiffani closed her logbook.

"We will find one," she said. "Or we will design one. But not today."

Kylar stared at the wall for a long moment, then nodded and turned toward the ladder.

"Send word when you do," he said.

He climbed down.

Jhace and Tiffani stood in the empty hall. The crack remained without change. The city hummed around them at its altered pitch.

"He is right," Jhace said. "There might not be a better way."

"Then we make one," Tiffani said. "Because sending people to learn by almost dying is not a method. It is triage."

She moved to the ladder.

Jhace followed.

The Dregs beam had held with half the cost. The method worked. But teaching it to others meant solving a problem neither of them had an answer for yet.

They climbed toward the surface with the question still unanswered.

***

If you want the same treatment on Chapter 7 (your newer route) to keep Ch7–8 perfectly aligned in tone and constraints, paste it and I’ll do a narration-only sweep the same way.

[USER]
# N01: Chapter 9 – What He Needs

**Tide:** 2 (Pouring) | **Phase:** Functional Partnership | **POV:** Jhace | **Beat:** BEAT06 (Debate/Resolution) | **Soulpulse:** 1 use (crisis, near-Stage 2) | **Relationship:** Survive failure together

***

"You are wrong," Tiffani said.

Jhace looked up from the marked stone. They stood in Bram's workshop, three Tides after Kylar had walked away from the practice wall. The training problem had not solved itself.

"Explain wrong," Jhace said.

"Simulated urgency will not work," Tiffani said. She tapped her logbook. "The activation requires genuine protective desperation. You cannot fake that. Kylar's grip will know the difference."

"So we do nothing," Jhace said. "We let him wait for a real crisis and hope he does not kill himself trying."

"No," Tiffani said. "We teach him load recognition first. Stress mapping. Pattern identification. When the crisis comes, he will know where to press. That reduces the waste."

"He needs to know how it feels," Jhace said. "The heat under his palms. The pull. If he has never activated on purpose, the first time will be chaos."

"And if he tries to force activation when there is no real need, he wastes output on nothing," Tiffani countered. "That is worse than waiting."

Jhace flexed his hands. The tremor made the motion stutter. It had not stopped for eighteen Tides now. Baseline, Tiffani called it. Permanent, he thought but did not say.

"I learned by doing," he said.

"You nearly died learning by doing," she said. "Three times. I counted."

"And I am still here," Jhace said.

"Barely," she shot back.

The door opened. Kylar climbed into view, dust on his vest and frustration on his face.

"I felt it again," he said without greeting. "In the Downslope market hall. A wall with the wrong resonance. But I could not make the glow come. I pressed until my hands cramped and nothing answered."

"Because no one was under it," Tiffani said.

"Someone will be," Kylar said. "Eventually. And when they are, I will not know what to do because I have never done it when it mattered."

He looked at Jhace.

"Teach me how to make it answer," he said.

Jhace opened his mouth to respond.

A runner burst through the door, breathing hard.

"Holder," the runner said. "Underface junction. Arch is folding. Fifty people trapped on the lower side."

Jhace was moving before the runner finished speaking.

Tiffani grabbed her satchel. Kylar followed without asking permission.

They ran.

***

The Underface junction was older than most of the grid, one of the original spoke-passages that connected lower terraces to the vertical shafts. The arch had carried foot traffic for six generations. Now it sagged in the middle, stone grinding against stone with a sound Jhace felt in his teeth.

Fifty people pressed against the far side, where the passage narrowed into the climb. No way out except through the arch. No time to dig another route.

"How long," Jhace asked the site lead.

"Three phases," the lead said. "Maybe four. The crown stone is cracked. When it goes, the whole arch comes down."

Jhace moved to the arch. His hands shook so hard he had to press them against the stone to steady them. The tremor had escalated the moment he saw the arch. His body knew what was coming.

"Where," he asked Tiffani.

She was already measuring, cord stretched across the span. She marked two points on the crown stone, then a third near the base of the right column.

"Primary stress at the crown," she said. "But the column is taking uneven load. You need both or the whole structure twists."

Jhace set his palms on the crown stone. Cold. Dense. Ancient. The weight above it pressed down like a hand on his chest.

Hold, he thought. Fifty people. Hold.

Heat surged under his palms.

It came fast and hard, sharper than the Dregs beam, hotter than the kiln wall. Copper-gold light flooded from his hands into the stone. The glow spread through the arch like water through cloth, fast and wild.

Pain hit his arms in waves. The vein-lines lit bright from wrist to shoulder, copper threads under his skin. The tremor in his hands jumped to a full shake.

"Column," Tiffani said. Her voice came from far away.

Jhace kept his right palm on the crown and reached for the column with his left. His arm did not want to move. He forced it. His palm hit stone and the glow split, threading from the crown down into the column base.

The arch shuddered. Stone scraped stone. Dust fell in streams.

Jhace held.

The copper-gold glow sank deep, deeper than he had pushed before. He felt the grain of the stone, the cracks in it, the places where weight pressed too hard. His grip spread through all of it, holding it together by will alone.

His vision narrowed. Sound faded. All he knew was heat and pressure and the grinding weight trying to collapse under his hands.

"Clear," someone shouted. Distant. Irrelevant.

"Jhace," Tiffani said. Closer. Urgent. "They are through. Let go."

He could not let go. The arch was not stable. If he let go, it would fold. Fifty people would die.

"They are through," Tiffani repeated. Her hand closed on his wrist. "No one is under it. Let go."

He let go.

The copper-gold cut out. The arch groaned and settled. Cracks spread through the crown stone like lightning, but the structure held. Barely.

Jhace's legs failed. He went down hard on one knee. The tremor in his hands had become a full convulsion. His arms shook from shoulder to fingertips. The vein-lines pulsed bright, then faded to faint tracery that did not disappear.

Pain sat in his chest like a weight. Each breath came shallow and fast.

Tiffani crouched in front of him. Her hands framed his face, forcing him to focus on her.

"Look at me," she said.

He looked.

"How many fingers," she asked, holding up three.

"Three," he managed.

"Good," she said. "Breathe. Slow. Count with me."

He counted. His breathing slowed. The pain in his chest eased but did not leave.

The tremor in his hands did not stop.

Kylar stood a span away, staring at Jhace with an expression caught between awe and horror.

"That is what it costs," Kylar said quietly.

"Yes," Jhace said.

"Every time," Kylar said.

"Yes," Jhace confirmed.

Kylar looked at his own hands. They shook, faint but constant. After a long moment, he looked back at Jhace.

"I do not know if I can do that," Kylar said.

"Neither did I," Jhace said. "The first time."

***

They did not return to the workshop. Tiffani led them to a quiet corner of the lower market instead, where the stalls had emptied after midphase. She bought three cups of broth from a vendor and handed them out without asking if anyone wanted them.

Jhace drank because his hands needed something to do. The tremor made the cup rattle against his teeth.

Kylar held his cup without drinking. After a long silence, he spoke.

"You were both arguing about how to train me," he said. "Before the runner came."

"Yes," Tiffani said.

"What were you arguing about," Kylar asked.

Jhace and Tiffani exchanged a glance.

"I think you need controlled practice before a real crisis," Tiffani said. "Learn the theory first. Stress mapping. Load paths. Pattern recognition. So when it activates, you know where to press."

"I think you need to feel it activate at least once before the crisis," Jhace said. "So your body knows what to expect. Otherwise the first time will be chaos."

"You are both right," Kylar said.

They looked at him.

"I need to know where to press," Kylar said. "But I also need to know what it feels like when the glow answers. Because right now I know neither."

He set his cup down.

"And neither of those things will help me with what I actually do not know," he said.

"What do you not know," Tiffani asked.

Kylar looked at Jhace.

"How to stop when it is enough," he said. "You held that arch until someone made you let go. If Tiffani had not been there, would you have let go on your own?"

Jhace did not answer.

"That is what I need to learn," Kylar said. "Not how to activate. Not where to press. How to know when to stop before I spend myself the way you just did."

After the words, none of them spoke for a breath.

"I do not know how to teach that," Jhace said finally. "I am not good at it myself."

"Then we learn it together," Kylar said. "Because if you cannot teach me to stop, and I cannot figure it out on my own, then training me just means creating another person who will break themselves for strangers."

Tiffani was writing. Her hand moved fast across the page.

"Threshold recognition," she said. "We need metrics. Physical markers that indicate when you are approaching your limit. Before you cross it."

She looked up.

"I can track that," she said. "Heartbeat, tremor intensity, glow saturation, duration. We map the progression. When you hit a certain combination of factors, that is the signal to disengage."

"And if I cannot disengage," Kylar asked.

"Then I pull you off," Tiffani said. "The way I pulled Jhace off today."

Kylar nodded slowly.

"All right," he said. "But I still need to know what it feels like. At least once. In a situation where it matters enough to activate but not enough to kill me if I fail."

"That is a narrow window," Jhace said.

"Yes," Kylar agreed. "Find it."

He stood, drained his broth in three swallows, and set the cup on the stall counter.

"Send word when you do," he said.

He walked away.

Jhace and Tiffani sat in silence. The market hummed around them. Jhace's hands still shook. The vein-lines on his forearms remained faintly visible even without the glow.

"He is right," Tiffani said at last. "We were arguing about the wrong problem."

"Yes," Jhace said.

"We need to teach him to stop," she said. "Which means we need to understand when you should stop."

"I stopped today," Jhace said.

"You stopped when I made you stop," she corrected. "That is not the same thing."

Jhace looked at his hands. The tremor ran through them in waves.

"I do not know how to stop when people need me to hold," he said quietly.

"Then we figure it out," Tiffani said. "Together. Before you or Kylar cross a threshold you cannot come back from."

She closed her logbook and stood.

"Come on," she said. "You need rest."

"The arch needs bracing," Jhace said.

"The arch will get bracing," she said. "You need rest more than it needs you to watch it."

She offered her hand. After a moment, he took it. She pulled him to his feet. His legs held, barely.

They walked back toward the surface together. The city hummed at its altered pitch. Somewhere below, the arch Jhace had held groaned under weight it could no longer carry on its own.

He did not look back.

***

Here’s the **surgical narration-only constraint pass** on your Chapter 9 draft.

I did **not** restructure, tighten, or “improve” prose. I only changed lines that break the **strict narration baseline** (no similes, no metaphors, no anthropomorphizing of objects/forces/problems). Dialogue is left as-is.
