## N01: Chapter 7 — Recalibration

**Tide:** 1 (Quench) | **POV:** Jhace | **Seismic texture:** Rattle | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses

Jhace could not tie the knot.

The cord was dry hemp, rough against his fingertips. He looped it once, crossed it, pulled—his fingers jerked, the tremor throwing the tail out of his pinch. The loop collapsed. The cord slipped through and snapped back against his knuckles.

He tried again.

The tremor ran constant from fingertip to wrist. Fine, fast. His nails clicked against each other when he forced his hands close.

Bram watched from the bench without speaking. A brace plate sat half-drilled under his lamp. The workshop smelled of wet dust, kiln exhaust, and the copper tang that stayed in the back of Jhace’s mouth no matter how much water he drank.

Jhace looped the cord a third time and used his teeth to hold the tail while his fingers worked. He pulled the knot tight with wrist pressure instead of finger pinch. The cord bit into skin.

It held.

He released it and stared at his hands. The tremor did not change.

Bram finally spoke. “That’s one way.”

Jhace didn’t answer. He set the cord aside and picked up a slate from the stack beside the bench. The edge scraped his palm. The tremor made the slate buzz lightly against his skin.

Outside the workshop, runners moved through the corridor with slates held close. A brace crew dragged timber over uneven stone. Someone shouted a street name. Someone else shouted back that the street had shifted again during the last aftershock.

Bram drilled two more holes in the brace plate and set it down. “You slept?”

“Two phases,” Jhace said.

Bram’s jaw tightened. “That’s not sleep. That’s shutting your eyes until you hear another crack.”

Jhace stared at the slate. “I heard the support groan even when I wasn’t there.”

“That’s your head,” Bram said.

“It’s also the stone,” Jhace said.

Bram did not argue. He set the drill aside and reached for another tool, then paused when a faint rattle ran through the bench. A small vibration, quick. The hanging lamp chain trembled. Dust sifted from a seam in the ceiling and drifted down in a thin line.

Jhace counted on instinct. One. Two. Three heartbeats.

The tremor passed.

No one in the corridor shouted. No runner stopped. They moved through it as if it were weather. As if it had always been like this.

Bram exhaled through his nose. “Quench is supposed to be quiet.”

“Not anymore,” Jhace said.

He stood, slate in hand, and stepped to the workshop entrance. He looked upslope through the passage. Light from above came down in a dull wash through dust. On clear spans, the seam in the sky showed faintly between rooflines, a bent-light line that did not belong. It looked like flawed glass set across the upper air.

Jhace closed his eyes for one breath and opened them again. The seam stayed.

He went back to the bench.

Tiffani Merrow arrived without announcing herself. Her footsteps were clean, fast. She carried her logbook and a slate strapped to a board, edges protected with cloth. Chalk dust streaked one sleeve. Copper-gold particulate glittered faintly in the folds of her hair when she moved through lamplight.

Her eyes went to Jhace’s hands first. Then his face.

“You did not die,” she said.

Jhace’s mouth twitched without reaching amusement. “Not yet.”

“That is data,” she said, and set her board on Bram’s bench.

Bram shifted to make space without complaint. “You bring more bad news?”

“I bring measurements,” Tiffani said.

She opened the board strap and lifted the top slate. Jhace saw columns in tight script: heartbeats, tremor notes, glow width, contact points. A crude sketch of a seam line on stone with chalk marks labeled.

Jhace leaned in despite himself.

Tiffani tapped one column with her pen. “Your hold at the junction span. Ten heartbeats commanded. Ten heartbeats attempted. Contact broken at ten plus one.”

Jhace stared at the number.

“I did not—” he started.

“You did,” Tiffani said. Her voice stayed level. “Your body did not obey the instruction at ten. I broke contact.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. The memory sat sharp: the aftershock, the flare, his hands refusing to lift.

Bram watched Jhace’s face and said nothing.

Tiffani turned the slate slightly so Jhace could see the next line. “Your tremor increased from baseline at heartbeat seven. Approached convulsive threshold at nine. Glow saturation spiked during the aftershock. The spike compressed your markers.”

Jhace nodded once. He did not like that she had words for it. He needed that she did.

Tiffani lifted her gaze to his hands. “Your tremor baseline remains elevated. Your fine control is reduced. You missed the nail twice yesterday.”

Jhace didn’t react. Bram did.

Bram’s eyes narrowed. “You watching him in my workshop now?”

“I observed,” Tiffani said. “Observation is not ownership.”

Bram huffed once, but he did not push.

Jhace’s fingers jerked against his thigh. He forced them still by pressing his palm flat to the fabric. The tremor ran through pressure anyway.

“You came to tell me I’m worse,” Jhace said.

“I came to tell you your markers are consistent enough to build a protocol,” Tiffani said. “You are not worse than yesterday. You are worse than pre-Piercing. That distinction matters.”

Jhace stared at the slate. “Protocol doesn’t stop the ground.”

“No,” Tiffani agreed. “But it can stop you from chasing failure points when the ground shifts.”

Bram leaned back on his stool. “If you can teach him to stop chasing, I’ll carve you a shrine out of scrap.”

Tiffani did not look at Bram. “Do not threaten me with religion.”

Bram’s mouth twitched. “Fair.”

Tiffani turned another slate. A sketch showed a support with two chalk Xs at base and midline. A third mark in a circle with an arrow.

“Node pairs,” she said. “Two contact points per structure. Not wherever the crack expresses. Where the load concentrates. If the ground moves and the load path shifts, the marks must be redrawn.”

Jhace looked at the sketch. His body recognized it. The push under his boots. The way the stone pressed wrong.

“You can mark fast,” he said.

“I can mark fast enough if you do not touch until I finish,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “People die while you finish.”

“They also die if you waste your output on the wrong stone,” Tiffani said.

Bram made a small sound. Not agreement. Not disagreement. He was listening.

Tiffani’s pen tapped the slate again. “We need a rig.”

Jhace blinked. “A rig.”

“A controlled test structure,” Tiffani said. “Not a practice wall that is too stable to trigger anything. Not a live corridor with people trapped. A loaded brace with predictable failure points. We can generate real urgency without crushing someone.”

Bram frowned. “You want to build a wall that’s about to fall?”

“I want to build a brace that will fail unless held,” Tiffani said. “A brace that can be allowed to fail without killing anyone if control is lost. The difference matters.”

Jhace’s hands flexed. The tremor ran.

“And Kylar,” Bram said, looking toward the ladder shaft.

As if the name summoned him, Kylar stepped into the workshop from the corridor. He looked tired. Dust ground into the seams of his vest. His hands stayed close to his body. When he held still, a faint tremor showed in his fingers.

He stopped when he saw the slates on the bench.

“You’re measuring him,” Kylar said. It wasn’t accusation. It was a statement.

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

Kylar’s gaze moved to Jhace’s hands, then away. “I want that.”

Jhace’s mouth tightened. “You want a tremor.”

“I already have one,” Kylar said. He held his hands out. The shake was small, but it was there.

Tiffani’s pen paused. She looked at Kylar’s hands without softening. “It began after the Piercing.”

“Yes,” Kylar said. “I touched a wall that was sliding. Heat for one breath. Copper shimmer. Then nothing. The tremor stayed.”

Jhace watched Tiffani mark a line on her slate.

Kylar’s voice came quieter. “I keep waiting for another crisis. I keep thinking I’ll fail when it comes.”

Jhace looked at him. “You will if you chase the glow.”

Kylar flinched. “Then teach me not to.”

Bram set his tool down. “We teach you where to press. And we teach you to obey the count.”

Kylar’s eyes flicked to Tiffani. “If I can’t let go?”

Tiffani lifted her wrist. The cord loop sat there, plain and practical. “Then someone breaks your contact.”

Kylar swallowed.

Jhace heard his own voice come out before he chose it. “He learns with us. Not alone.”

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on Jhace for one breath, then she nodded once. “Agreed.”

Bram pushed the brace plate aside and cleared space on the bench. “Tell me what you need for your rig.”

Tiffani named items without hesitation. “Timber lengths. A brace hinge. Two wedge stacks. A load weight we can control. Dust trays. Vibration cord.”

Bram raised his eyebrows at “dust trays,” but he did not argue.

Kylar asked, “Where do we build it?”

“Near the south market,” Bram said. “Salee can keep people out of the way. She has room and bodies.”

Tiffani looked at Jhace. “And the RRO.”

Jhace frowned. “What about them.”

Tiffani slid a thin notice slate across the bench. It had been copied clean, ink still sharp.

**REGISTRATION OF ANOMALOUS STABILIZERS**  
**All individuals demonstrating copper-gold manifestations in structural emergencies will report to the RRO intake office within three phases.**  
**Unauthorized interventions will be recorded.**

Jhace read it once. His fingers twitched against the edge.

“They want names,” he said.

“They want control,” Tiffani replied.

Bram’s voice went flat. “They can come down here and wedge a post if they want control.”

No one laughed.

Jhace set the notice down. “We build the rig.”

Kylar nodded. “When.”

Tiffani closed her logbook. “Next Kindling. Tide Two. We need one calm span to build it. Calm is not guaranteed.”

Jhace looked at his hands. The tremor kept moving. Calm was not guaranteed anywhere now.

“Next Kindling,” he said.

Outside, another faint rattle ran through the corridor. A tool clinked somewhere upslope. Dust drifted in a thin line past the workshop opening.

Jhace counted without meaning to.

The ground did not stop moving.

Neither would they.

---

## N01: Chapter 8 — The Rig

**Tide:** 2 (Kindling) | **POV:** Jhace | **Seismic texture:** Shift | **Soulpulse:** 1 use (Jhace #4)

The rig sat under a half-collapsed awning in the south market.

Salee’s people had cleared the space with rope lines and blunt voices. No one argued. They routed foot traffic around the perimeter as if it were another broken stair throat. Barrels of water stood nearby, chalk slashes on the side marking distribution counts. Wedges and timber lay stacked on a cloth tarp, sorted by size.

Bram knelt at the base of the rig and checked the hinge alignment with a straightedge. The hinge was scavenged from a door brace, iron pins worn but intact. The timber frame around it was new-cut from salvage beams.

Tiffani stood at the side with cord and chalk, marking the ground where the frame legs met stone. Two Xs at the base. One higher mark on the upright where load would concentrate if the hinge slipped.

Kylar hovered a span away, hands tight at his sides.

Jhace stood closer than he wanted to. His hands shook in front of him. The tremor did not stop when he held them behind his back, so he kept them visible.

Tiffani finished her marks and stepped back. “This is the rule. You do not touch until marks exist.”

Kylar nodded. “I understand.”

“You understand the words,” Tiffani said. “We will see if you understand the behavior.”

Kylar’s jaw tightened. “I am here.”

“That is behavior,” Bram said dryly. “Good start.”

Tiffani lifted a dust tray—just a shallow pan with fine sifted grit—and set it near the hinge. “If an aftershock hits, the pattern in the tray changes. The tray tells us if vibration shifts load.”

Bram grunted. “Or the tray tells us the ground shook.”

“It tells us where it shook hardest,” Tiffani said. She did not look up. “That distinction matters.”

Jhace watched her hands. No shake. Clean marks. She moved like the world would obey if she measured it enough.

The world would not. But the marks mattered anyway.

Salee stepped in from the rope line and looked at the rig. “If it fails, what happens.”

Bram pointed. “The upright drops. It hits that sand pile. No one stands under it.”

Salee nodded once. “Then we keep no one under it.”

Kylar looked between them. “How does this make the glow answer.”

“It might not,” Jhace said.

Kylar’s eyes narrowed. “Then what is the point.”

“The point is placement and stop,” Tiffani said. “Activation is not the skill. Activation happens when it happens. Placement and stop determine whether you live long enough to activate again.”

Kylar swallowed. He stepped closer to the upright and stared at the timber grain where Tiffani’s chalk Xs sat.

“Try,” Tiffani said.

Kylar set his palms on the primary mark.

Nothing happened.

He stood there for ten heartbeats, then twenty. His hands began to sweat. The tremor in his fingers increased.

His breathing shortened.

Jhace saw his shoulders tighten. The urge to force contact. To press harder. To scrape.

“Kylar,” Jhace said.

Kylar did not answer. He pressed harder. His palms slid slightly on the chalked timber.

Tiffani’s voice cut in. “Do not chase. Do not grind.”

Kylar’s hands jerked. His right palm slid again. A thin splinter caught under his skin. He hissed.

“Stop,” Tiffani said.

Kylar did not stop.

He shifted his weight forward and shoved.

The upright jumped a fraction on its hinge. The brace pin clacked. The timber frame creaked.

Bram swore once and lunged, catching the upright with his forearm before it swung farther.

Salee’s nearest runner stepped in fast and grabbed the frame leg to steady it.

A child on the rope line had leaned in to watch. Salee’s hand snapped out and yanked the child back by the shoulder without looking away from the rig.

Kylar froze as if he had just realized what he had done.

Jhace’s chest tightened.

That was not a collapse. But it could have broken someone’s arm. It could have crushed a foot. It could have killed a child if the upright had swung wrong and someone had been under it.

“Hands off,” Bram said, voice hard.

Kylar stepped back two paces. His hands shook. His face had gone pale.

“I—” he started.

Tiffani looked at the splinter in his palm. “You injured yourself while trying to force an answer.”

Kylar’s voice came thin. “It didn’t answer.”

“No,” Tiffani said. “And you made the rig unsafe.”

Kylar’s eyes flicked to the child Salee had pulled back. Shame tightened his mouth.

Jhace heard his own voice come rough. “Do you understand now.”

Kylar looked at him. “I understand that I’m useless.”

“That is not what happened,” Tiffani said.

Kylar laughed once, sharp and wrong. “What happened then.”

Jhace stepped forward. The tremor in his hands made the motion look like anger. He kept his voice flat.

“You tried to make it answer,” Jhace said. “You tried to force the world to do what you wanted. You almost dropped timber on a child.”

Kylar flinched. “I didn’t mean—”

“Intent doesn’t change load,” Jhace said.

Kylar’s mouth opened, then closed. His breath shook.

Bram set the upright back against its seat and checked the hinge pin. “Rig’s still intact. But no one touches it unless Tiffani says.”

Tiffani’s gaze stayed on Kylar. “This is the chapter where you learn restraint.”

Kylar’s voice cracked. “I don’t know how.”

Jhace looked at Tiffani. He felt the urge in his own bones: to show Kylar what power looked like so he would stop chasing it blindly. To make him afraid enough to listen.

Tiffani read his posture before he moved.

“No,” she said, to Jhace.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “He needs to see.”

“He needs to obey,” Tiffani said. “If you demonstrate glow, he will chase glow. That is not what we teach.”

Kylar looked between them, eyes wide. He was breathing fast.

Bram said quietly, “Don’t turn this into a contest.”

Jhace’s hands shook harder. The tremor always looked like agitation now, even when he felt nothing but fatigue.

“He will die,” Jhace said.

Tiffani’s voice stayed level. “He will die faster if he thinks glow is the point.”

Jhace stared at her. He did not like being stopped. He needed being stopped.

Salee spoke from the rope line. “If you want him alive, teach him to listen.”

Jhace turned his head. Salee’s gaze stayed steady. No awe. No fear. Just systems thinking in a human body.

Jhace exhaled once through his nose.

“Fine,” he said.

Tiffani nodded once. “Good.”

She stepped to the rig and rechecked her marks. Then she looked at Bram.

“Pre-load,” she said.

Bram moved to the weight sling—a sandbag bundle suspended by cord through a pulley. He raised it a hand-span and set a catch pin so the load pulled against the upright. The timber frame complained in a low creak.

Tiffani watched the dust tray. Fine lines formed in the grit, radiating from one corner toward the hinge.

“Load is concentrated,” she said. “Primary node is the lower hinge seam. Secondary is the upright midline. If the hinge slips, the upright will swing.”

Kylar swallowed. “So if it swings—”

“It hits the sand pile,” Bram said. “But it can take an arm if someone’s in the path.”

Salee said, “No one will be in the path.”

Tiffani’s eyes went to Kylar. “You will not be in the path.”

Kylar nodded, small and fast.

Tiffani looked at Jhace. “This is not a demonstration of power. This is a demonstration of obedience. I will call the count. You will break when I say.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. “Yes.”

She looped the cord around her wrist twice. The other end she held in her palm. A simple leash. A contact-break tool.

Jhace stepped to the rig.

He placed his palms on the two marked points—lower hinge seam and upright midline. Cold timber. Rough grain. Load pulling against the hinge.

He did not want to activate. He wanted the rig to hold on wedges and pins and Bram’s clean joints.

The rig did not care what he wanted.

He heard the child on the rope line whisper something. Salee answered with a low hush. The child stayed quiet.

Protective urgency rose in Jhace’s chest anyway. Not pride. Not performance. Just the knowledge that if he failed to hold when it mattered, the world took someone and did not return them.

Hold, he thought. Long enough for them to reset. Long enough for Kylar to see what “stop” costs.

Heat built under his palms.

Copper-gold light seeped out from his skin into the timber grain. Narrow threads. Not the broad flood from the day the world opened. It followed the hinge seam line first, then ran up toward the midline node. It stayed tight to stress, as if it could see what Tiffani had marked.

Vein-lines lit faint under his forearms. The tremor in his hands jumped from fine to hard.

Tiffani’s fingers pressed to his wrist. “Heartbeat elevated,” she said. “Tremor escalating.”

The upright stopped complaining. The hinge creak eased into a steady pressure noise.

Bram adjusted the catch pin. The load pulled harder for a breath, then steadied. The rig stayed in place.

Kylar stared at Jhace’s hands. His own tremor ran in his fingers. Jhace could see Kylar trying not to reach for the glow, trying not to step closer, trying not to do anything except watch.

“Four,” Tiffani counted. “Five.”

Jhace’s breath shortened. Pain climbed his wrists in a hot band. The glow stayed narrow, locked to the nodes.

A faint rattle ran through the market. Tools on the tarp clicked. Dust shifted in the tray, lines changing direction by a small degree.

Tiffani’s eyes snapped to the tray. “Minor aftershock,” she said. “Marks drifted a hand-width.”

Jhace felt it as a shift in resistance under his left palm. The hinge seam pushed harder.

The instinct to chase surged up his arms. Move your hand. Find the new failure point. Press more. Hold longer.

Tiffani’s voice cut it. “Do not chase.”

Jhace held where the mark had been. The glow tightened further, as if compressing to match the shift without moving his hands.

“Six,” Tiffani said. “Seven.”

Jhace’s tremor spiked. His fingers wanted to lock.

“Break,” Tiffani said.

Jhace did not argue. He tore his palms away from the timber.

The copper-gold cut out instantly.

The rig creaked and shifted a fraction, then Bram dropped the catch pin fully and let the load settle back into its safe position. The upright stayed upright.

Jhace’s hands shook hard. Pain flooded in behind the tremor. He breathed through his teeth.

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on his forearms. “Vein-lines fading under twenty heartbeats. Tremor elevated, not convulsive.”

Jhace swallowed. “Managed,” he said, the word bitter in his mouth.

“Managed,” Tiffani confirmed.

Kylar’s voice came low. “You stopped.”

“Yes,” Jhace said.

“Because she told you,” Kylar said.

Jhace looked at Tiffani. “Yes.”

Kylar’s face tightened. He looked at the rig, then his hands.

“I don’t get the glow,” Kylar said. “But I can do the rest.”

Tiffani stepped to him and held out the chalk. “Then do the rest.”

Kylar took it with shaking fingers.

Tiffani pointed at the dust tray. “Aftershock drifted the concentration. Redraw the primary mark based on the tray line.”

Kylar crouched. His tremor made the chalk line stutter at first. He slowed. He used wrist pressure instead of finger pinch the way Jhace had with the knot. The second line came cleaner.

“Secondary?” Tiffani asked.

Kylar looked at the upright midline. He pressed two fingers to the timber and paused, as if listening through skin.

“Here,” he said, and marked it.

Bram watched with a craftsman’s expression. “Good enough.”

Kylar looked up. “I didn’t feel heat.”

“No,” Tiffani said. “You felt load.”

Kylar blinked. “That counts?”

“It counts more than glow without control,” Tiffani said.

Jhace’s hands shook. He clenched them until his nails cut skin.

Kylar looked at Jhace. “I hate that this feels like progress.”

Jhace heard his own voice come flat. “Progress feels like restraint now.”

A faint rattle ran through the market. Someone on the rope line muttered and kept moving. The city did not stop failing. It only paused between shifts.

Tiffani closed her logbook with a sharp sound. “We run this again next Pouring,” she said. “Same rig. Same drift checks. Then we take it to the field.”

Jhace nodded once.

Kylar stared at the chalk marks he had made. They were not perfect. They were real.

Salee stepped back toward her rope line. “If you’re done with your rig, I have a stair throat that needs wedges.”

Bram grabbed his kit without hesitation.

Tiffani looked at Jhace. “No more uses today.”

Jhace’s mouth tightened. He wanted to argue. He did not.

“Fine,” he said.

His hands shook as he followed them out of the market space, the tremor constant, the cost visible.

The rig stayed behind under the awning, held by pins and marks and the thin line between control and collapse.

---

## N01: Chapter 9 — The Count Breaks

**Tide:** 2 (Pouring) | **POV:** Jhace | **Seismic texture:** Failure | **Soulpulse:** 1 use (Jhace #5)

The crisis came while Kylar was holding chalk.

They were at the rig again, running drift checks after a shallow rattle had shifted the dust tray. Kylar redrew the primary mark with less stutter than yesterday. Tiffani watched his hand and nodded once, approving a line without praising it.

Jhace stood a span back, hands behind him, tremor visible anyway.

He had not touched the timber since Kindling. His palms still carried phantom heat memory. His wrists still ached when he flexed.

A runner cut through Salee’s rope line and nearly tripped over the tarp.

“Holder,” the runner gasped. “Lower terrace junction throat. Span is folding. People trapped on the far side. Aftershocks narrowed the passage. They can’t clear.”

Bram was moving before the runner finished.

Tiffani snapped her logbook shut and looped the cord around her wrist without looking at it. The motion was practiced now.

Kylar dropped the chalk into the tray and followed without being told.

Jhace followed last, because he always did, because the city kept handing him places where people stood under weight that wanted to fall.

They ran.

---

The junction throat sat under a row of older terrace houses, stone packed tight, built before anyone understood how the grid would hum through foundation. The passage was narrow, with a bend that forced bodies into single file. The support at the bend carried load from two streets and a stairwell above. The crack at its base looked like a thin pale thread.

Jhace felt the wrongness in his teeth before he saw it.

The grid hum was strained here, higher than it should be. The stone under his boots vibrated on a doubled note.

People clustered on the far side of the bend, visible through the gap. They did not push. They did not panic. They waited with faces tight, bodies held still, as if movement would make the support decide to fail.

A site lead stood near the support with two brace workers. His hands were chalked. His eyes were raw.

“We wedged it after last shake,” he said. “It still groans. Another wave and it goes.”

Tiffani stepped in front of Jhace and held up a hand. “Do not touch yet.”

Jhace stopped. His hands flexed at his sides. The tremor buzzed through his fingers.

Tiffani moved fast. Cord out. Two measurements. Chalk marks placed at the base seam and a higher offset node where a joint line met a load-bearing block.

“Primary here,” she said. “Secondary here. You do not press the visible crack. You press where the load concentrates.”

Jhace stared at the marks. His body agreed with them.

Bram moved to the bend and raised his voice to the trapped cluster. “One at a time. When I tell you. No rush.”

Kylar crouched near the brace kit and began sorting wedges by size without being told. His hands shook. He worked anyway.

Tiffani stepped close to Jhace and pressed two fingers to his wrist. “Baseline,” she said. “Heartbeat elevated. Tremor present and consistent.”

Jhace heard the authority in her voice again.

“When I tell you to disengage,” she said, “you disengage.”

“Yes,” Jhace said.

“If you do not,” she said, “I break contact.”

“Yes,” Jhace repeated.

Kylar looked up from the wedges. “Can you—”

“Yes,” Tiffani said, without looking at him.

Jhace stepped in and set his palms on the two marked nodes.

Cold stone. Rough surface under dust. Load pressing wrong under his skin.

He heard breathing on the far side of the bend. He heard someone whisper a name. He heard Bram tell them to wait for his hand signal.

Hold, Jhace thought. Long enough for them to clear.

Heat built under his palms.

Copper-gold light seeped into the seam lines. It traced the base node first, then ran up into the secondary mark, threading through joint lines like water following the easiest path.

Vein-lines lit along Jhace’s forearms. Faint at first. Then brighter.

The tremor in his hands jumped from fine to hard.

Tiffani’s fingers stayed on his wrist. “Heartbeat spiking,” she said. “Breathing shortening. Tremor escalating.”

The support stopped shifting by degrees. The groan eased into a steady pressure noise.

Bram waved the first trapped person through the bend.

A woman moved sideways, shoulder pressed to wall, breath held. She passed under the support and cleared into the open span behind Jhace.

Jhace held.

Two more followed, slower than they wanted to move.

“Five heartbeats,” Tiffani counted. “Six. Seven.”

Jhace’s tremor thickened. His jaw clenched. The glow stayed narrow, locked to stress.

Then the failure wave hit.

It was not a small snap. It was not a brief rattle. It rolled through the junction like a shove that moved the entire span sideways by a fraction and left it there.

Dust burst from seams in a thick sheet. Lantern flames bent. The stone under Jhace’s knees shifted hard enough to make his stomach rise.

The crack under his left palm widened.

The copper-gold flared brighter in a single heartbeat.

Jhace’s tremor spiked toward convulsion. It felt like his hands were trying to tear themselves away and lock down at the same time.

Tiffani’s voice cut sharp. “Markers compressing. Glow saturation spiking. Tremor accelerating.”

Jhace tried to breathe. His breath came short.

Bram shouted, “Hold position. No one rush the bend.”

A brace wedge near the base clacked against stone as load shifted. Kylar snatched it and shoved a thicker wedge in behind it, using shoulder weight. His tremor made the wedge chatter, but it seated.

Jhace felt the support try to fail anyway. A shove under his palms. A shift he could not see but could feel in bone.

The instinct to chase surged. Move your right hand. Find the new failure point. Press harder. Hold longer.

Tiffani’s hand tightened on his wrist. “Do not chase,” she said. “Hold the nodes. Hold.”

Jhace held.

The quake eased after a handful of heartbeats. The corridor stayed vibrating.

The support groaned louder than before.

On the far side of the bend, someone made a small sound that might have been a sob.

Tiffani counted again, voice steady despite the drift. “Eight. Nine.”

Jhace’s tremor crossed into convulsive edge. His fingers started snapping in short uncontrolled jerks against stone even while his palms stayed flat. Pain spiked up his forearms along the vein-lines as if the glow had turned to heat wire under skin.

“Ten,” Tiffani said. “Disengage.”

Jhace stared at the seam line. It was not stable. The crack was still there. The load had shifted. If he let go, he could not predict the direction of failure.

“Not—” he forced out. The word broke. “Not yet.”

Tiffani’s voice stayed level. “No one is under the center. Disengage.”

Bram shouted, “Last two, now. Move.”

Two bodies slipped through the bend in fast steps, guided by Bram’s hands and voice.

Jhace’s arms shook so hard his shoulders burned. The glow flared again, too bright, too wide. His vision narrowed. Sound dulled. The world reduced to pressure and contact.

The last trapped person cleared the bend.

Tiffani said, “Now.”

Jhace told his hands to release.

They did not.

His palms stayed pressed flat. The glow brightened. The convulsive tremor snapped up his forearms and into his shoulders.

Tiffani’s other hand closed hard on his wrist. “Contact break,” she said.

She yanked.

Jhace’s left palm peeled off stone. The copper-gold cut out instantly where contact broke. The glow along the base seam stuttered and dimmed.

His right hand stayed for one breath longer, locked by strain.

Tiffani yanked again, harder.

His right palm broke contact.

The glow cut out completely.

Jhace staggered backward two steps. His hands convulsed in short hard snaps. The vein-lines on his forearms stayed visible, bright for too long, then faded to faint tracery that did not disappear.

He bent forward, breathing fast. His chest tightened. Pain flooded his wrists and forearms as if the heat had left raw nerve behind.

Behind him, stone ground on stone.

Jhace snapped his head up.

The support did not collapse cleanly.

It shifted and dropped one short span. The upper seam cracked open with a sharp sound. A section of the bend wall sheared inward—controlled by the wedges Kylar had forced in, guided by Bram’s shouted directions.

Dust slammed into the passage.

A brace post splintered.

No one was under it.

But the passage narrowed further, a chunk of stone now blocking half the throat. The route was not usable until someone dug and braced and carried rubble out by hand.

Property loss. Access loss. More work added to a city that already had too much.

Lives kept.

Jhace’s throat tightened until it hurt.

He looked at the fallen stone and felt something inside him pull toward anger, sharp and immediate.

Tiffani stepped between Jhace and the support, her body a barrier without touching him. Her eyes went to the fallen section, then to his hands, then to his face.

“You are alive,” she said.

Jhace’s voice came rough. “The throat is gone.”

“The people are not,” Tiffani said.

Jhace’s hands convulsed again. He pressed his palms together to stop them. The tremor ran through both, constant.

Bram’s voice carried from the bend. “Everyone’s clear. No bodies under it.”

Kylar stood near the wedge stack, dust on his hair, breathing hard. His hands shook in front of him like he was watching a future he did not want.

Tiffani crouched in front of Jhace. Her voice lowered, precise. “Look at me.”

Jhace looked.

“Count,” she said.

He forced air in. “One.”

“Again,” she said.

“Two.”

His breath slowed in small steps. The convulsive edge eased. The tremor stayed.

Tiffani watched the vein-lines along his forearms. “Faint persistence at rest,” she said. “That is new.”

Jhace swallowed. “Write it.”

“I will,” Tiffani said.

He stared past her at the broken throat. The city would pay for this. People would reroute. Supplies would detour. Someone would curse the loss and not know the reason it happened.

Jhace’s jaw clenched. “You pulled me early.”

“I pulled you at ten,” Tiffani said.

“It didn’t feel like enough,” Jhace said.

“It rarely will,” Tiffani replied.

Bram stepped closer, dust on his shoulders. “We can dig this. We can shore it. It’s stone and time.”

Jhace looked up at Bram. “It didn’t have to fall.”

Bram’s expression stayed flat. “It didn’t have to kill.”

That landed harder.

Jhace looked at Tiffani again. His voice came low. “In the moment, it felt like you took my hands off the only thing holding.”

Tiffani did not flinch. “In the moment, your hands stopped obeying you. That is the difference between holding and dying.”

Kylar took one step closer, then stopped as if a rope held him back.

“How,” Kylar asked quietly, “do I learn to stop when it feels wrong to stop.”

Jhace looked at him. His hands shook in front of his chest. He did not hide them.

“You don’t,” Jhace said. “Not alone.”

Kylar’s mouth tightened.

Tiffani turned her head toward Kylar. “You learn it as a protocol,” she said. “And you learn it with someone who will break your contact when you cannot.”

Kylar nodded slowly. His eyes flicked to the cord loop still tight on Tiffani’s wrist.

Jhace watched the understanding land in Kylar’s face. Not admiration. Not thrill. Fear and clarity together.

Tiffani stood and opened her logbook. She wrote with fast strokes despite the dust.

Jhace looked at the broken throat again. The loss sat heavy. The lives sat heavier.

He did not apologize. Neither did she.

Bram raised his voice toward the brace crew. “Wedges here. Timber there. Clear rubble out of the center. No one under the remaining seam.”

Work started again. Hammers sounded. Stone scraped.

Jhace’s hands trembled as if they would never be still again.

Tiffani’s pen scratched on paper as if the only way to keep anyone alive in this world was to write down what it cost to hold it up for ten heartbeats at a time.

---

## N01: Chapter 10 — The Revision

**Tide:** 2 (Shaping) | **POV:** Jhace | **Seismic texture:** Shift | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses

They rebuilt the failure on purpose.

Not the people trapped in the throat, not the groan in the stone, not the moment Jhace’s hands refused to lift. They rebuilt the conditions around it: load on a hinge, drift under vibration, marks that stopped matching what the structure was doing.

The rig was back under the south market awning, reinforced now. Bram had added a second brace leg and an extra catch pin so the upright could be allowed to slip by controlled degrees instead of swinging free. Salee’s rope lines held the perimeter, her runners rotating through without complaint, keeping the curious at a distance.

Tiffani had added more trays.

Fine dust in shallow pans at each leg. A strip of glass plate strapped to the upright midline to show distortion when the load concentrated. A cord line stretched across the base with knots at hand-width intervals.

Kylar stood at the edge of the tarp with chalk in one hand and the cord in the other, staring at the marks from yesterday like they had betrayed him.

Jhace sat on an upturned crate with his hands on his knees.

He was not touching anything.

His tremor ran constant. Fine in his fingers, heavier in his wrists when he tried to hold still. The faint copper tracery under his forearms showed even without glow now, as if the last hold had stained him from the inside.

Bram checked the hinge and looked at Jhace once. “You sure you’re not going to ‘help’?”

Jhace did not look up. “No touch today.”

Bram grunted. “Good.”

Tiffani crouched at the dust tray nearest the hinge. She lifted it, tilted it, let the grit slide. “We begin with drift mapping,” she said. “No contact. No heat. No hero reflex.”

Kylar’s mouth tightened at the word reflex. He didn’t argue.

Tiffani ran the cord tight, checked the knots, then marked three new Xs with chalk where the tray lines indicated stress concentration. They were not where yesterday’s Xs had been.

“You see,” she said, without looking up. “The rig hasn’t moved. The ground has. The load path changed.”

Kylar stared at the new marks. “So the marks are always wrong.”

“The marks are always provisional,” Tiffani corrected.

Bram muttered, “She does that on purpose.”

Tiffani’s pen scratched across her logbook. “Now we simulate the wave.”

Bram moved to the hinge and loosened the catch pin by a fraction, just enough that the upright would complain under load. The frame creaked. The glass plate on the upright showed a faint distortion line.

Kylar flinched.

“Hold it,” Bram said, to the rig, as if it could obey.

It did not. It creaked again.

Tiffani looked at Kylar. “Redraw the primary mark based on the distortion line, not the crack expression.”

Kylar took two steps forward and crouched. His tremor made the chalk touch down twice before he committed to a line. He forced his hand slower, using wrist pressure, not finger pinch.

The X landed. Slightly crooked. In the right place.

Tiffani nodded once. “Secondary.”

Kylar shifted and marked the second X. His hand shook less by the time he finished. He exhaled, a small release he didn’t seem to notice.

Jhace watched him do it. Watched the learning happen in small movements, not in light.

Tiffani stood and walked to Jhace.

She didn’t crouch. She didn’t soften. She just stopped in front of him and looked down at his hands on his knees.

“You will say the count aloud,” she said.

Jhace blinked once. “For drills.”

“For everything,” Tiffani said. “When a wave hits, your body loses track of heartbeats. You will anchor it in your voice. If you go quiet, you are no longer in control.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened. The thought of speaking while holding felt wrong. Too exposed. Too close to panic.

“Fine,” he said.

Tiffani’s eyes moved up to his face. “And you will tell me the truth.”

Jhace did not answer.

Tiffani waited.

The market noise carried around them—footsteps, low voices, a barrel being rolled. A faint rattle ran through the awning beams and dust sifted from a seam.

Jhace counted without meaning to. One. Two.

It passed.

Tiffani’s gaze did not.

“What truth,” Jhace said.

“The truth that when I pulled you off yesterday, you were relieved,” Tiffani said.

Jhace’s breath caught.

Bram’s hammer paused midair. Kylar looked up sharply.

Jhace’s tremor thickened for a breath, then returned.

“That’s not—” he started.

“It is,” Tiffani said. “And it matters. If you can name relief as a marker, you can stop before your hands refuse.”

Jhace stared at her. The cord of his throat felt too tight.

He forced the words out. “Yes.”

Tiffani nodded once, as if he had delivered a measurement. “Good.”

Kylar watched them with an expression caught between fear and hunger for instruction.

Tiffani turned toward the rig again. “Now. We run the drill under wave conditions.”

Bram loosened the catch pin another fraction.

The upright shifted by a finger-width.

The glass plate distortion line jumped. The dust tray grit lines changed angle.

Kylar stepped in immediately and re-marked without being told. Chalk squeaked fast. His lines were messier than before, but they tracked the drift.

Tiffani’s voice stayed level. “Good. Again.”

Bram shifted the catch pin. The upright creaked. The load concentrated elsewhere.

Kylar marked again.

Jhace watched the repetition build something in Kylar that didn’t rely on glow. He watched Tiffani’s hand flick to her wrist cord, always present, always ready. He watched Bram’s expression settle into the calm of doing a job that could be done, even with the world moving under it.

After the fifth drift, Kylar’s shoulders sagged.

“I can do this forever,” Kylar said, voice thin.

“No,” Tiffani said. “You can do it until fatigue makes you sloppy. Then you stop. That is also protocol.”

Kylar’s mouth tightened. “Stopping again.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

She looked at Jhace. “Your turn.”

Jhace’s stomach tightened. “I’m not touching.”

“You’re not,” Tiffani said. “You are repeating the count.”

Bram adjusted the hinge to simulate a sharper slip. The upright shifted in a quick snap.

Kylar flinched and marked fast. Tiffani watched his hand and shook her head once.

“Too far left,” she said. “Correct. Don’t panic-mark.”

Kylar swallowed and corrected.

Tiffani looked back at Jhace.

“Count,” she said.

Jhace forced air in. “One.”

Bram shifted the hinge again. Another snap.

“Two,” Jhace said.

The rig creaked.

“Three.”

The market awning rattled with a shallow aftershock. Dust fell in a thin line and landed in the trays, changing patterns mid-count.

Kylar froze for half a heartbeat.

Tiffani’s voice cut. “Update marks. Don’t stare.”

Kylar moved.

“Four,” Jhace said, voice rough.

The count anchored him. It also made him feel exposed. Like everyone could hear how close to the edge he always was now.

Tiffani’s eyes flicked to his hands. The tremor ran constant. She didn’t comment.

When the drift drill ended, Bram locked the catch pins and the rig settled back into quiet.

Kylar wiped his forehead with the back of his wrist, leaving a streak of chalk. “That was… loud,” he said.

“It is always loud now,” Jhace said quietly, mostly to himself.

Tiffani closed her logbook. “You did not touch the rig. That is correct.”

Jhace exhaled through his nose. “Correct,” he echoed, and this time the word didn’t taste as bitter.

Bram picked up his hammer again and said, almost casual, “You two are building a craft.”

Jhace looked at him.

Bram met his gaze. “Not a miracle. A craft. That means it can be taught.”

Kylar’s throat bobbed.

Tiffani’s shoulders eased by a fraction. “Yes,” she said. “That is the point.”

A faint rattle ran through the awning again. Dust drifted. The world reminded them it didn’t care.

Tiffani looked at Jhace and held out her hand—not to comfort, not to steady. A simple functional offer to stand.

Jhace hesitated, then took it.

Her grip was firm. Steady. She pulled him up without comment.

His hands shook anyway.

But he didn’t pull away from her hand.

---

## N01: Chapter 11 — Lines Drawn

**Tide:** 3 (Kindling) | **POV:** Jhace | **Seismic texture:** Rattle | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses

The summons came on slate, delivered by a runner who wouldn’t meet Jhace’s eyes.

**RRO COMMITTEE INTAKE**  
**MIDPHASE**  
**RE: STABILIZATION PROTOCOLS / ANOMALOUS INTERVENTIONS**

Bram read it over Jhace’s shoulder, then grunted. “Back stairs.”

Tiffani arrived from the Underface with chalk on her hands and a new smear of copper dust on her sleeve. She read the slate once and said, “They’re late.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “They’re here now.”

They took the back route upslope.

The committee chamber had been a guild archive before the Piercing. Stone walls. High benches. Slates stacked in neat piles that did not match the city’s actual shape anymore. Clerks at a long table wrote without looking up. Guards at the doors stood with hands near batons, not weapons—close enough to be a threat without calling it one.

Corlexi sat at the raised bench. Paper arranged in front of him like the world still held to columns. Harlen sat to his right, committee vest over dust-streaked clothes. Two guild representatives filled the ends.

Jhace’s tremor ran through his hands as he sat. He kept them on his knees where everyone could see.

Corlexi’s eyes went to Jhace’s hands first.

Then to Tiffani.

Then to the slate in front of him.

“The committee has recorded thirteen unassigned interventions since Tide Zero,” Corlexi said. His voice was calm, practiced, as if the number itself could contain the reality behind it. “Five in the lower terraces. Four in Underface. Four in the Dregs.”

Jhace spoke without lifting his gaze. “Stone fails faster than your routes.”

Harlen leaned forward. “Unregulated holds create secondary risk. If a stabilizer overextends—”

Tiffani cut in. “That word does not match the data.”

Corlexi’s fingers tapped a page. “Copper-gold manifestations. Persistent vein-line traces. Structural shifts following temporary stabilization.”

Tiffani opened her logbook and set it on the bench in front of her. “We record every activation. Heartbeats, tremor escalation markers, contact points, visible saturation. You have rumor summaries. We have sequences.”

A clerk’s pen slowed. Then resumed.

Corlexi’s expression didn’t change. “Registration is required. All known stabilizers will report to this office. Deployments will occur under assigned supervision. A maximum engagement duration has been established.”

Jhace lifted his eyes. “Ten heartbeats.”

Harlen nodded once, pleased. “Correct. Standard threshold. Improves safety.”

Tiffani’s pen hovered, then pressed down hard enough to dent paper. “Ten heartbeats is a disengagement signal. It is not a quota. It is not a deployment rule. The markers compress under seismic wave conditions. The threshold depends on site and phase.”

Corlexi leaned forward slightly. “We set thresholds for beams. For arches. For brace angles. This follows the same logic.”

“Beams don’t panic,” Jhace said. “People do.”

The guards at the doors shifted weight. The sound of boots on stone was small, but it landed.

Harlen’s voice tightened. “The committee coordinates relief distribution, heat routing, water access. Stabilizers must fit within that structure.”

Tiffani’s gaze stayed on Corlexi. “Your structure assumes the world is stable enough to be managed. It isn’t.”

A faint rattle ran through the chamber, quick as a breath. Inkwells trembled. A clerk steadied a stack of slates with one hand and kept writing with the other.

No one reacted beyond that. The rattle was normal now. It made the whole chamber feel like denial built out of stone.

Corlexi waited until the vibration passed. Then: “A secondary stabilizer has been observed. Kylar. Unregistered. That constitutes uncontrolled expansion.”

Jhace’s mouth went tight. “Kylar is learning to stop. That’s more than most of your committee has done.”

Harlen’s jaw flexed. “Under whose authority is he being trained.”

“Ours,” Tiffani said. “Baseline measured. Escalation tracked. Disengagement enforced. He is safer with us than alone in a collapsing stair throat.”

Corlexi’s gaze stayed steady. “Authority is not personal. It is institutional.”

Jhace’s hands shook harder for a breath. He forced his fingers still by pressing them into his knees.

“Your institution couldn’t stop the Piercing,” Jhace said. “It can’t stop aftershocks. It can’t stop stone from failing. What it can do is delay action until someone dies under a stamp.”

A clerk’s pen paused.

Corlexi’s voice stayed calm. “Refusal will be recorded.”

Tiffani’s tone sharpened without rising. “Record it. Also record that we will provide aggregated findings. Verified. Without names. Without routes. Without giving you a list of people to control.”

Harlen’s eyes narrowed. “The committee requires raw logs.”

“Then you require deaths,” Tiffani said.

Corlexi held her gaze. “The heat-grid council meets in two Tides. Crown infrastructure requires priority stabilization. You will attend. You will bring your findings.”

Jhace stood.

His tremor made the movement look like anger. It wasn’t anger. It was his body.

“We will stabilize what needs stabilizing,” Jhace said. “Send sites if you want. Don’t send guards.”

Harlen’s voice followed, a step behind. “Refusal carries penalties.”

Jhace didn’t turn back.

Tiffani stood beside him as they walked out, close enough that their sleeves brushed. Not tender. Deliberate. A public line drawn without speech.

Outside, Bram waited in the corridor with his hands in his pockets like he wasn’t ready to hit someone.

“Well,” Bram said.

“They want names,” Jhace said.

“They want control,” Tiffani corrected.

Bram nodded once. “And you said no.”

Jhace’s hands shook at his sides. “We said no.”

Tiffani’s gaze flicked to his hands, then back to his face. “Watchers will follow.”

“Let them,” Jhace said.

They went downslope.

Behind them, the committee kept writing as if writing could hold the world together.

---

## N01: Chapter 12 — We Hold Together

**Tide:** 3 (Shaping) | **POV:** Jhace | **Seismic texture:** Failure | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses

The Crown request arrived the next morning.

Stamped slate. Priority seal. Heat-grid node in the upper terrace. “Urgent.” Committee clearance attached.

Bram set it on his bench like it was dirty.

Jhace read it once. The tremor in his hands made the slate edge buzz against his palm.

Tiffani read it over his shoulder and didn’t bother hiding her expression. “That’s the fourth Crown ‘urgent’ since Tide Zero.”

Jhace looked at the stack of slates beside it—unsealed, hand-scratched, delivered by runners who couldn’t afford ink. Dregs stair throats. Underface braces. Market walls. People sleeping under weight they didn’t understand.

“How many Dregs sites marked for collapse within this phase,” Jhace asked.

Tiffani flipped pages in her logbook fast. “Nine.”

“How many have committee crews assigned,” Jhace asked.

Tiffani didn’t look up. “One.”

Jhace set the Crown slate down. “We go to the Dregs.”

Tiffani’s pen paused mid-page. Then: “Corlexi will record that.”

“Let him,” Jhace said.

Bram grabbed his brace kit without comment. “Salee sent word late Kindling. Foundation wall under the south market. Families leaning against it.”

Jhace’s mouth tightened. “We go.”

---

The south market in the Dregs smelled of damp stone and cooked root and copper dust.

People had patched lean-tos against the old foundation wall—cloth, salvaged wood, sheets of thin metal. Seventeen families, Salee had said. Displaced from upslope after the Piercing. Nowhere else to go.

The wall ran three stalls long, stone blocks stacked without mortar in places, old construction meant for a world that held still.

It did not hold still now.

Salee stood near the wall with rope over one shoulder and two runners beside her. She didn’t greet with ceremony.

“It shifts every phase,” she said. “They won’t leave. They’ve already moved three times.”

Jhace stepped to the wall and pressed his palm flat against stone.

Cold at the surface. Warm in a narrow band where load pressed through. The push felt uneven. The lower course was sagging at two points, concentrating weight where it shouldn’t.

His hands shook against the stone. The tremor blurred his reading, but he could still feel where it wanted to fail.

Tiffani measured with cord and chalk, quick and exact. She marked three points—primary stress, secondary node, corner risk.

Jhace watched her marks and felt his body agree.

Kylar stepped into view from behind a stall, dust on his vest, hands held slightly away from his sides as if he didn’t trust them.

Tiffani’s eyes flicked to him. “You’re here.”

Kylar nodded once. “Salee told me. The wall is bad.”

Bram muttered, “Everything’s bad.”

Salee ignored Bram. “If it goes, it takes the shelters. People get crushed. Or they scatter and lose each other and we find bodies later.”

Jhace’s throat tightened.

He looked at the wall. He looked at the lean-tos. He looked at children sitting on a cloth bundle with copper dust in their hair like it was normal.

He did not want to use the glow here. He did not want to pay that price. He also didn’t want to say “not today” and watch it fold during the next wave.

Tiffani caught his hesitation.

“No use,” she said quietly, before he could speak. “Not unless it begins to fail with bodies under it. We brace first.”

Jhace’s jaw clenched. Part of him wanted to argue. The larger part knew she was right.

Salee’s runners brought timber. Bram’s kit came open on the ground. Wedges were laid out by size. Rope lines went up to clear a work lane.

Kylar took wedges without being told and started setting them where Tiffani pointed, not where cracks looked dramatic. His hands shook. He worked anyway.

Jhace steadied a timber with both palms. The tremor made the wood buzz. He held it until Bram pinned it with rope and wedges.

A faint rattle ran through the market. People paused mid-breath. Dust sifted from stall roofs.

Then the failure wave hit.

Not the deep planetary shove from Tide Zero. A shorter, sharper roll that moved through the foundation like the ground had slipped on wet stone.

The wall groaned.

Not loud. A low grinding sound that made Jhace’s teeth buzz.

A wedge popped loose and clacked across stone.

Someone screamed.

Jhace’s body surged forward on instinct—hands lifting, palms ready to press.

Tiffani’s hand snapped to his wrist, hard. “No,” she said. “Not yet.”

The wall shifted by a fraction and caught.

Bram shouted, “Re-seat wedges! Now!”

Kylar lunged for the loose wedge and slammed it back in with the heel of his hand because his tremor made hammer swings dangerous. The wedge seated. The timber brace line took load.

The wall stopped moving.

For one breath, everything held.

Jhace’s hands shook in front of him. The urge to press didn’t vanish; it just had nowhere to go.

Salee stepped into the cleared lane and raised her voice to the families. “You stay back from the wall. You don’t lean on it. You don’t hang cloth on it. You give us space or it takes you.”

People moved. Not far. But enough.

Tiffani re-measured. The chalk marks had drifted a hand-width. The load path changed with the wave. She corrected without complaint, chalk squeaking fast.

“Shift the primary brace right,” she said. “Two hand-widths. Now.”

Bram obeyed. Kylar obeyed. Jhace held timber steady while they moved it.

They worked like a network, not a miracle.

When the bracing line finally set, the wall made no new sound.

Tiffani pressed her knuckle to the stone at the primary node and held it there for a breath. “Stable,” she said. “For now.”

Salee’s shoulders eased by a fraction. “How long.”

“An Anneal,” Jhace said. “Maybe two. If the aftershocks don’t hit it wrong.”

Salee nodded. “Then we brace it again next Tide. We don’t pretend it’s fixed.”

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on Salee. “The Crown wanted us upslope.”

Salee’s gaze didn’t change. “The Crown has crews. The Dregs has us.”

Jhace looked at the braced wall. At the families now keeping a hand’s distance. At Kylar wiping chalk from his palm, tremor visible, still alive.

He turned his head toward upslope, toward the districts with seals and slates and a belief that priority stamps mattered more than who actually died.

“We’re staying here,” Jhace said.

Tiffani nodded once. “Yes.”

Bram exhaled like he’d been holding breath for days. “Good.”

They walked back toward Bram’s workshop as lamplight faded.

Behind them, the braced wall held. Not because of glow. Because people did the work.

In the workshop, Tiffani sat on the bench beside Jhace and opened her logbook. She wrote for a span without speaking.

Jhace watched her hand move. Steady. Precise. The opposite of his.

Outside, another faint rattle ran through the corridor. Dust drifted. The world reminded them it was still broken.

Tiffani closed the book and set it aside.

“Corlexi will escalate,” she said.

“Let him,” Jhace replied.

She was quiet for a breath, then spoke with less edge than usual—still exact, but softer in the way she chose words.

“This partnership,” she said. “It’s more than marks and counts.”

Jhace stared at his hands. The tremor ran constant. The faint copper tracery under his skin caught lamplight in thin lines.

“Yes,” he said.

Tiffani’s hand rested on the bench between them. Ink-stained fingers. Chalk under nails.

“I can’t hold stone the way you do,” she said. “I can’t make it answer. But I can measure. I can mark. I can stop you when you won’t stop.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. He didn’t look away from his hands.

“That’s enough,” he said.

Tiffani’s fingers moved from the bench to his shaking palm. She closed her hand around his, firm and steady. The tremor didn’t stop, but it had somewhere to go that wasn’t isolation.

“We should name what we’re doing,” she said.

Jhace turned his head to look at her.

“Name it how,” he asked.

Tiffani held his gaze. Exhaustion and resolve sat together in her face.

“We hold together,” she said.

Jhace repeated it once, low. “We hold together.”

Tiffani nodded. “Not just stone. Each other. The cost. The choice.”

Jhace swallowed. “Together,” he said.

Outside, the city hummed at its altered pitch. Somewhere upslope, a stamped slate waited. Somewhere downslope, a braced wall held for one more phase.

Jhace’s hands shook.

Tiffani did not let go.

---
