## N01: Chapter 10 — The Map That Moves

**Tide:** 2 (Shaping) | **POV:** Jhace | **Seismic texture:** Shift | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses

They rebuilt the throat on paper first.

Bram had cleared Bram’s bench of everything that could rattle loose and turned it into a map surface: slates stacked as weights, cord pinned in a rough arc, chalk marks on the plank that stood in for seams and nodes. Tiffani set her logbook open beside it, pages already smudged at the corners from dust and hurried fingers.

Kylar stood on the far side of the bench, hands held close, eyes fixed on the chalk.

Jhace sat with his forearms on his thighs, hands visible and shaking. He did not fold them behind his back. Hiding did not change physics.

Tiffani tapped the page where she’d sketched the junction throat. “This is where the failure wave struck,” she said. “Here is where the load path shifted.”

Bram grunted. “It shifted everywhere.”

“It shifted *enough* here to compress the markers,” Tiffani corrected. “Your ten-heartbeat protocol becomes eight in a wave like that. That’s the point.”

Jhace stared at the chalk Xs on the plank.

“You pulled me at ten,” he said.

“Yes,” Tiffani replied.

“And it still fell,” Jhace said.

“It fell in a controlled direction,” Bram said, voice flat. “No bodies. That’s a win in this world.”

Jhace’s mouth tightened. He did not argue with Bram’s arithmetic. He hated the feeling of it anyway.

Kylar’s voice came low. “When she pulled him, he looked relieved.”

Jhace’s eyes flicked to Kylar.

Kylar did not look away. “For one breath,” he said. “Like he didn’t want to hold anymore.”

Jhace swallowed. His throat scraped.

Tiffani’s pen moved fast. “Relief at forced disengagement,” she murmured, and wrote it as if naming it made it less shameful.

Jhace stared at his hands. The tremor did not care what he felt about it.

“The worst part,” Jhace said quietly, “was that I knew you were right. While I was angry.”

Tiffani didn’t soften. She didn’t argue. She just nodded once, as if that admission unlocked the next step.

“Then we formalize redundancy,” she said. “Two-person stop.”

Bram leaned back. “We already have you yanking his wrist.”

“That’s one link,” Tiffani said. “If that link fails—if I’m knocked off balance, if my grip slips, if an aftershock hits mid-pull—then the operator holds too long.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened.

Tiffani looked at Kylar. “You become secondary contact-break.”

Kylar’s eyes widened. “Me?”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “Not because you’re strong. Because you’re there. Because you’re learning obedience. Because if you watch someone ignore the count, you will understand your own future in real time.”

Kylar swallowed. His hands shook faintly.

“What do I do,” he asked.

“You stand close enough to touch,” Tiffani said. “You do not touch unless I say ‘break’ and the operator does not disengage. If that happens, you break the operator’s contact at the secondary node. Not both. Just one. Enough to collapse the glow.”

Bram said, “That assumes breaking one hand breaks the whole thing.”

“It did every time so far,” Tiffani said. “We will test it.”

Jhace watched her look at the notes from the last holds—every contact break, every glow cutoff. She was building rules in a world that kept moving under her feet. It was stubborn. It was necessary.

“And the marks,” Jhace said, voice rough. “What if the nodes move mid-hold again.”

Tiffani’s eyes cut to him. “Then we stop pretending the marks are permanent.”

Bram snorted. “Finally.”

Tiffani ignored him. “We add a re-mark station. We assume drift. During any hold, someone watches the dust tray or cord line and calls ‘drift’ when it happens. That call becomes as important as ‘break.’”

Kylar frowned. “So we’re doing three calls now. Hold. Drift. Break.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “You want simple? The planet removed simple.”

A faint vibration ran through the bench. A tool at the edge clicked. Dust slid off a slate in a thin line.

Jhace counted without thinking. One. Two. Three.

It passed.

Kylar’s eyes flicked to the ceiling. “Does it ever stop.”

“No,” Bram said.

Tiffani’s pen scratched. “Baseline instability.”

Jhace pushed air through his nose. “Then we drill.”

They moved to the rig site at the south market.

Salee didn’t meet them herself this time—she had routes to run—but her rope lines were already up, and her people had already pushed foot traffic around the awning space. Someone had added a second dust tray without being asked.

Tiffani noticed and nodded once, approval in motion.

Bram checked the hinge. Kylar checked wedge stacks. Tiffani checked cord tension and marked nodes.

Jhace stood with his hands at his sides and did not touch timber.

“Non-glow drill,” Tiffani said. “We simulate drift and practice calls.”

Bram raised the load sling a hand-span. The upright creaked.

Tiffani watched the dust tray. “Primary concentration at hinge seam. Secondary at midline. Drift will be called if the tray line shifts more than a finger width.”

Kylar stood near Jhace’s left shoulder, close enough to reach.

Jhace could feel Kylar’s presence like heat. Like a shadow. Like a second set of hands waiting to stop him if he failed to stop himself.

Jhace hated it.

Jhace needed it.

Bram said, “Ready.”

Tiffani said, “Ready.”

Kylar said, “Ready.”

Jhace forced his breath steady. “Ready.”

Bram jostled the rig frame—not hard, just enough to mimic a rolling aftershock. The upright shifted a fraction on the hinge.

The dust tray line jumped.

“Drift,” Tiffani called immediately.

Kylar’s head snapped to the tray.

Tiffani redrew the base mark by a hand-width without hesitation. “New primary here.”

Bram increased load again. The hinge creaked louder.

“Drift,” Kylar said, voice sharp. He had seen the tray shift before Tiffani spoke.

Tiffani’s eyes flicked to him. “Good.”

Jhace watched her redraw again. The mark moved like a living thing.

They repeated the drill five times. Drift called. Marks redrawn. Load adjusted. Wedges reset. No glow. No contact. Just method, executed under shifting conditions.

On the sixth run, a real aftershock hit.

Not huge. Not catastrophic. A rolling wave that made the awning ropes tremble and sent a shower of grit down from the market roofline. The rig creaked and shifted. The dust tray lines blurred and reformed in a new direction.

“Drift,” Kylar shouted.

Tiffani’s chalk moved fast. Bram reset the load pin. Jhace’s hands lifted on instinct, ready to press, ready to hold, ready to burn himself on stone.

Tiffani’s voice cut through it. “No contact. Obey the drill.”

Jhace froze with his palms half-raised.

His tremor made the pause look like anger. It wasn’t. It was restraint, and it hurt in a different way than glow did.

He lowered his hands.

The aftershock passed. The rig stayed upright. No one needed a holder.

Kylar stared at Jhace’s hands lowering, as if that was the hardest thing he’d seen all week.

“That,” Kylar said quietly, “is also stopping.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. He nodded once.

Tiffani wrote without looking up. “Restraint under impulse. Logged.”

Bram looked at Jhace over the top of the hinge frame. “You’re learning,” he said, as if it pained him to admit it.

Jhace’s mouth twitched. “Don’t make it weird.”

Bram huffed. “Too late.”

They finished the drill and packed up as dusk rolled in.

The seam in the sky showed faintly through the dust haze, a bent-light line that never belonged to any season or weather. Jhace stared at it for two heartbeats and then forced his gaze down to the stone under his boots.

The planet kept shifting.

But the method was becoming something that could shift with it.

That was the only kind of stability left.

---

## N01: Chapter 11 — Lines Drawn

**Tide:** 3 (Kindling) | **POV:** Jhace | **Seismic texture:** Rattle | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses

The summons came on slate.

A runner delivered it to Bram’s workshop with both hands, as if touching the Crown seal too casually might stain him. Jhace read it once. Clean script. RRO committee chamber. Midphase. Mandatory attendance regarding “structural stabilization protocols and anomalous operator safety.”

Tiffani read over his shoulder without asking.

“They want the method,” she said.

Jhace folded the slate. “They want the leash.”

Bram wiped his hands on a cloth and leaned back from the bench. “Back stairs,” he said. “Less visible.”

They took the back stairs.

The RRO chamber sat three levels upslope in what had been a guild archive before the Piercing. Stone walls. High benches. Slates stacked in neat piles that pretended neatness mattered. Clerks at a long desk with pens moving fast enough to keep up with the new world.

Guards stood at the doors, vests marked with reconstruction sigils.

Corlexi sat on the raised bench with papers arranged into perfect lines. Harlen sat to his right, committee vest over worn work clothes. Two guild representatives filled the ends, faces set.

Jhace took in the space in one pass: narrow aisle, guards close, clerks ready to turn people into records.

“Be seated,” Corlexi said.

Jhace sat. Tiffani sat beside him. The bench pressed cold through his trousers.

Corlexi did not waste time. “The committee has recorded seventeen unauthorized interventions since the Piercing.”

Jhace’s hands trembled in his lap. He didn’t bother hiding them.

“Stone fails,” Jhace said. “We stabilize.”

Harlen leaned forward. “Unregulated holds create risk. If a holder overextends—”

Tiffani’s voice cut clean. “That word is useless without metrics.”

Corlexi’s eyes did not change. “The committee has received reports. Copper-gold manifestations. Operator tremor. Secondary failures after temporary stabilization.”

“You have glow sightings,” Tiffani said. She opened her logbook on her knees without asking permission. “I have measured activations with cost curves.”

A clerk’s pen hesitated, then resumed faster.

Corlexi tapped a page. “Registration is required. All known anomalous stabilizers will report to this office. Holds will occur only under committee assignment.”

Jhace felt his jaw tighten.

Harlen continued, tone careful like it had rehearsed. “We’ve established a maximum engagement duration. Ten heartbeats per activation. Standard threshold.”

Tiffani’s pen stopped moving.

“Ten heartbeats is a disengagement signal,” she said. “It is not a quota. It is not a deployment rule. Marker compression occurs during failure waves. Site conditions change it. You cannot set it from a bench.”

Corlexi’s gaze stayed steady. “We set thresholds for all reconstruction. Load limits on beams. Brace angles on arches. This follows the same logic.”

“Beams wait,” Jhace said. “People don’t.”

The guild representative on the left cleared his throat. “The heat grid fails in three Tides. Crown districts require stabilization priority. If holders choose sites by feel—”

“By measurement,” Tiffani corrected. “Stress nodes first. Occupied load second. Logistics third.”

Harlen’s jaw tightened. “The committee coordinates relief. Grain distribution. Water routing. Heat-grid priority. Holders fit into the same structure.”

Tiffani’s eyes flashed. “Grain sits while manifests are logged. Water spoils while routes wait stamps. Holds do not wait.”

A faint rattle ran through the chamber. One of the stacked slates shifted a finger width. Dust fell from a high shelf in a thin line.

No one reacted.

Jhace counted anyway. One. Two.

It passed.

Corlexi’s voice did not change. “A secondary operator has been observed. Kylar. Unregistered. This indicates uncontrolled expansion.”

Jhace met Corlexi’s gaze. “Kylar is being trained.”

“Under whose authority,” Harlen asked.

“Ours,” Tiffani said. “Baseline measured. Drift protocols drilled. Contact-break redundancy assigned. You have fear. We have method.”

Corlexi leaned forward a fraction. “We require raw records. Operator names. Engagement logs. Locations.”

Tiffani closed her logbook with a sharp sound. “No.”

The word landed like a dropped tool in a quiet tunnel.

Harlen’s eyes narrowed. “Refusal will be recorded.”

“Record it,” Jhace said.

Corlexi’s expression flickered—brief irritation that disappeared into calm. “You misunderstand. Registration is not a suggestion. It is safety.”

“It’s control,” Jhace said.

Corlexi’s voice stayed mild. “Control prevents panic.”

“Panic is not the problem,” Tiffani said. “Stone is.”

Corlexi lifted a hand. The room went still.

“Three Tides from now,” he said, “the heat-grid council meets. Crown districts require stabilization priority. You will attend. You will bring your findings. You will coordinate through the committee.”

Jhace stood. His hands shook at his sides. He did not care if the room saw it.

“We stabilize what needs it,” Jhace said. “Send sites if you want. We won’t wait for seals.”

Tiffani stood with him. “You will receive aggregated findings after verification,” she said. “Not raw logs. Not names. Not threshold details you can turn into quotas.”

Harlen stood too, anger leaking through his careful tone. “Refusal carries penalties.”

Jhace looked at him. “Then penalize us while the city cracks.”

They turned for the doors.

The guards shifted, but Corlexi did not signal them.

As they passed, Corlexi’s voice followed, soft as a blade. “Unregulated method creates liability. When a death occurs, it will be attributed.”

Tiffani did not turn back. “Deaths are already occurring. Attribution doesn’t hold beams.”

They left.

Bram waited outside, leaning against a support post. Dust streaked his vest. He looked at their faces and didn’t ask how polite the meeting had been.

“Done,” Jhace said.

Bram pushed off the post. “They wanted your hands on paper.”

“They wanted names,” Tiffani said. “And the right to deploy us like tools.”

“And,” Bram said.

“They got a no,” Jhace replied.

Bram nodded once. “Good.”

They climbed downslope.

The seam in the sky was faint but visible in the clearer air here, a reminder that the planet had been punctured clean through and would never return to “before.”

Jhace’s tremor ran constant.

Tiffani walked beside him, logbook tucked under one arm like a shield.

“Watchers will follow,” she said.

“Let them,” Jhace answered.

He didn’t say the other part out loud—the part that tightened under his ribs.

If Corlexi wanted control badly enough, he’d use the one thing that always worked in a collapsing world.

Need.

---

## N01: Chapter 12 — We Hold Together

**Tide:** 3 (Shaping) | **POV:** Jhace | **Seismic texture:** Failure | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses

The Crown request came first.

A slate with a Crown seal, delivered fast, marked **Priority** and **Urgent** and **Heat-grid node stabilization—upper terrace**. Committee clearance attached. Reporting instructions in clean lines.

Jhace read it at Bram’s bench and felt his hands shake harder.

Tiffani read over his shoulder.

“That’s the fourth Crown site this Anneal,” she said. “All marked urgent.”

Jhace set the slate down. “How many Dregs sites were marked urgent this Tide.”

Tiffani flipped her logbook open and scanned. “Eleven marked for collapse within the phase. None routed by committee.”

Jhace looked at the Crown seal again. Clean wax. Clean paper. A world pretending clean still existed.

“Crown has crews,” Jhace said.

Tiffani’s pen paused. “Corlexi will record our refusal.”

“Let him,” Jhace said.

A runner’s footsteps hit the corridor outside—fast, uneven, familiar.

Salee appeared in the doorway with dust on her hair and rope over one shoulder. Her face was set. Not panicked. That was worse.

“South market foundation wall shifted,” she said. “Not a hairline. A real move. Families are against it. If it goes, it takes their lean-tos and the stall line with it.”

Jhace stood. His hands shook. “How many.”

“Seventeen,” Salee said. “Mostly displaced from Upslope. They won’t move far. Nowhere to go.”

Tiffani shut her logbook and slung her satchel. “We go.”

Bram grabbed a brace kit without being asked.

They ran downslope.

---

The south market smelled like damp stone and sweat and copper dust.

Lean-tos pressed against the old foundation wall—cloth, salvaged wood, patched boards. People stood too close to it because distance meant exposure. Children sat on rolled blankets with eyes too wide. A woman held a cooking pot as if it was armor.

Jhace felt the wall before he touched it. A wrong pull under his boots. A sag in the stone’s voice.

Tiffani measured with cord and chalk, moving along the base course. “Primary stress here,” she said, marking an X near a settled block. “Secondary two hand-widths left. Tertiary at the corner, but it can wait if the first two hold.”

Kylar stepped forward from Salee’s crew line. Dust on his vest. His hands shook faintly, but he didn’t hide them.

Jhace looked at him. “Ten heartbeats protocol,” he said. “No glow unless it answers. We treat this as stone first.”

Kylar nodded once. “Understood.”

Salee’s eyes flicked to Jhace’s shaking hands. “No hero holds,” she said.

Jhace’s mouth tightened. “No.”

Tiffani stepped between Jhace and Kylar and pressed two fingers to each of their wrists in turn. “Baseline,” she said. “Both elevated. Tremor consistent. If either escalates toward convulsive, we stop.”

Salee’s crew held wedges ready. Bram’s brace kit lay open on a tarp.

Jhace stared at the foundation wall.

He felt the old reflex rise: *touch, hold, keep them alive, pay whatever cost later.*

Then he looked at the children sitting on blankets and remembered the throat collapsing in Ch9—the way stopping had saved lives and still felt like loss.

He did not want to learn that lesson again with his hands ripped from stone by force.

He wanted to choose it.

“Bram,” Jhace said, voice rough. “Brace line first. Get wedges in. We don’t spend glow if timber can do it.”

Bram blinked once, then nodded. “On it.”

Tiffani’s eyes cut to Jhace. Something like approval flickered and vanished.

Salee gave a sharp hand signal. Her people moved.

They shoved wedges under the worst sag, drove them with hammers, set a timber brace against the wall at a shallow angle. The wall groaned but did not shift further.

Then the failure wave hit.

It came without warning, as always now—no polite build, no clean rhythm. The stone under Jhace’s boots rolled hard. The market stalls rattled. Dust burst from seams in the foundation course in a thick sheet.

A scream rose from someone who thought the sound meant collapse.

The wall shifted a fraction.

The new wedge line clacked and held.

Bram threw his shoulder into the brace timber. Salee’s runner grabbed the brace base to keep it from skidding.

Tiffani shouted, “Drift—two hand-widths right!”

Her chalk came out. She marked a new primary point mid-shake, fast enough that the line looked like it had been drawn by a steady hand even while the world moved.

Jhace saw the drift with his own eyes—load concentrating in a new place, the old wedge line becoming less effective.

He did not touch.

He stepped into the new mark and planted his body as a brace point, shoulder to timber, boots wide, using weight and leverage instead of glow.

Kylar mirrored him on the other side without being told.

The aftershock passed.

The wall did not collapse.

People breathed again in loud, ugly sobs.

A child cried once and then stopped when their mother pressed them close.

Jhace’s hands shook. The tremor had not changed. But he had not used it. He had held with wood and bodies and marks.

Tiffani’s eyes were on the foundation seam. “Brace it deeper,” she said. “This will not hold through another wave. You need a second line behind the first.”

Salee nodded. “We have timber. We do it now.”

A man in the crowd shouted, “The Crown sent for you!” as if it was accusation.

Jhace turned his head.

A watcher in a clean vest stood at the market edge with a slate in hand, eyes hard.

“You are ordered upslope,” the watcher said. “Heat-grid node. Committee directive.”

Tiffani stepped forward, not flinching. “Tell Corlexi the Dregs wall was failing during the last wave,” she said. “Tell him seventeen families were against it. Tell him we’re bracing.”

The watcher’s jaw tightened. “That is not the route.”

Salee spoke before Jhace could. “Routes don’t matter when people die.”

The watcher looked at Salee like she was dirt that spoke.

Jhace felt something cold settle in his chest.

He stepped forward, hands shaking in plain view. “The Crown has crews,” he said. “The Dregs have us.”

The watcher lifted the slate. “Refusal will be recorded.”

“Record it,” Jhace said.

The watcher stared for one breath longer, then turned and left, boots clean on dirty stone.

Bram muttered, “Hope he enjoys his ink.”

Tiffani didn’t smile. She watched the watcher go and said quietly, “Penalties will come.”

Salee’s voice stayed blunt. “Let them.”

They worked until lamplight deepened.

Brace lines went in. Wedges seated. A second timber support was set behind the first. Rope lashings cinched tight enough to sing when plucked. People helped—hands passing wedges, holding timber ends steady, hauling rubble away from the base.

No one waited for permission.

No one waited for slates.

When the work paused long enough for breath, Salee walked Jhace and Tiffani to the water barrels. Chalk marks on the sides showed counts—served, served, served. No names. No committee.

“This is how we survive,” Salee said. “Not because someone declares it. Because we do it.”

Tiffani stared at the barrels, the rope lines, the way bodies moved without being told twice. “This is a system,” she said.

Salee’s mouth twitched. “Don’t call it that. Systems get claimed.”

Jhace looked at the children again. They had shifted their blankets farther from the wall on their own. Not far—there was no far—but farther than before.

He looked at Tiffani. She had chalk dust on her sleeve and ink on her fingers and fatigue in her eyes that didn’t make her slower.

“We chose this,” Tiffani said quietly.

Jhace nodded. “Yes.”

“The cost increases,” she said. “More failures. More needs. More pressure from the RRO.”

“Yes,” Jhace said again.

Salee watched them both. “Don’t break yourselves for us,” she said, rough. “We need you alive more than we need you heroic.”

Jhace’s throat tightened.

He flexed his hands. The tremor answered immediately, constant and real.

“I don’t know how to be heroic,” he said. “I know how to hold.”

Tiffani’s gaze stayed on his hands. Then she reached out—not to stop the tremor, not to pretend it wasn’t there. Just to make contact.

Her fingers closed around his shaking palm.

The simple touch made something in Jhace’s chest shift.

“This partnership,” Tiffani said slowly, “is not just method.”

Jhace swallowed. “No.”

She held his hand tighter, steadying him without erasing the shake. “Then we name it,” she said.

Salee looked away, giving them space without ceremony.

Jhace’s voice came low. “Name it.”

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on his. “We hold together.”

Jhace repeated it once, the words rough. “We hold together.”

Tiffani nodded. “Not just stone. Each other. The count. The stop. The choice.”

Jhace felt the tremor in his hand against hers. She didn’t let go.

“Together,” he said.

Another faint rattle ran through the market—small, quick, a reminder that the planet would keep testing every brace line they set.

People flinched and then kept working.

The seam in the sky showed faintly between rooflines, unmoving while everything below it shifted.

The world was punctured. The ground would not settle. They could not fix it.

But they could hold what needed holding.

Together.

---

If you want, I can also draft an **Interlude/Epilogue bridge** that tees up the next novella’s larger consequences (axis shift “sameness,” supply collapse, and Corlexi/Salee pattern acceleration) without breaking your knowledge gates.

# N01: Chapter 10 – The Map That Moves[1][2][3]
**Tide:** 2 | **Phase:** Shaping [1]  
**POV:** Jhace | **Beat:** BEAT08 | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses | **Relationship:** Trauma Bonding [3]  
---  
They rebuilt the throat on wood first.

Bram cleared his bench until only a plank remained. He pinned cord to it with bent nails and chalked a bend where the junction had folded. Slate weights held the cord down so it would not creep when the bench rattled.

Jhace watched the cord. His hands shook on his thighs. The tremor ran from fingertip to wrist without pause. The vein-lines on his forearms sat faint under the skin, pale threads that did not wash out.

Tiffani set her logbook open beside the plank. Her pen did not hesitate. Ink marks formed columns. Heartbeats. Contact points. Drift calls. Break calls. Notes on what had moved and what had not.

Kylar stood opposite Jhace, shoulders tight, hands held close. His own tremor was smaller. He did not hide it.

Tiffani tapped the sketch of the bend. “Your left node was correct until the wave. Then the load path shifted to the outer seam. The drift happened in under three heartbeats.”

Bram’s drill sat idle. “Everything shifts in three heartbeats now.”

“Not everything,” Tiffani said. “The hinge points do not shift at the same rate. The load concentrates before the crack expresses. That is measurable.”

Jhace stared at the chalk X on the plank. He could still feel the stone under his palm from the last hold. Cold grit. Pressure rising. Fingers jerking while his palms stayed pinned.

“You pulled me,” he said.

“Yes,” Tiffani answered.

“And it still dropped,” Jhace said.

“It dropped the side you had cleared,” Bram said. “That is not failure.”

Jhace’s jaw locked. He nodded once. He did not argue with Bram’s logic. He argued with his own body.

Kylar spoke without lifting his eyes from the plank. “When she broke you, your shoulders fell for one breath.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. His hands jerked once, hard, then settled back into fine shaking.

Tiffani wrote. “Relief at forced disengagement,” she said, quiet, and did not look up.

Jhace swallowed. “I was angry. I knew she was right while I was angry.”

Tiffani’s pen stopped. She nodded once. No comfort. No lecture. A single confirmation that the admission mattered.

“Then redundancy becomes formal,” she said. “Not improvised. Not based on who is close. Formal.”

Bram leaned back on his stool. “You already have a cord on your wrist and a hand on his arm.”

“That is one mechanism,” Tiffani said. “Mechanisms fail. The ground makes them fail.”

She turned her head toward Kylar. “Secondary contact-break.”

Kylar blinked. “Me.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “You are present. You are learning restraint. You are also the one most likely to recognize the moment someone decides to ignore the count.”

Kylar’s mouth opened, then closed. He nodded once, small.

“What do I do,” he asked.

“You stand within a hand of the secondary node,” Tiffani said. “You do not touch unless I call break and the operator does not disengage. If that happens, you peel one hand off the node. Not both. The glow stops with one contact broken. That has held true in every activation we have recorded.”

Bram scratched his beard. “Assuming it stays true.”

“We verify,” Tiffani said. “With drills.”

Jhace’s voice came rough. “And drift.”

Tiffani turned the page. A new diagram. A dust tray drawn as a square. A cord line drawn across it with small marks.

“Drift becomes a call equal to break,” she said. “If drift is called, the marker is redrawn before contact occurs. If contact is already engaged, the operator holds position. No chasing. Chasing is how hands lock.”

Jhace pressed his shaking palms together once. His fingers snapped against each other. He let them separate again.

“Hands lock,” Kylar echoed, quiet.

Jhace looked at him. “You saw it.”

Kylar met his eyes for one breath, then looked down again. “Yes.”

A faint vibration ran through the bench. The cord on the plank quivered. Dust slid off the slate weights in a thin line.

Jhace counted without meaning to. One. Two. Three.

It stopped.

Bram exhaled. “Quench would have been quiet before.”

“Before is gone,” Jhace said.

Tiffani shut the logbook. “Rig,” she said.

They moved Downslope to the awning site where Salee’s rope lines still held a perimeter. Her people had widened the circle and laid two additional dust trays in the corners without being asked. Wedges were stacked by length. Cord was coiled clean. Water barrels stood a cable away with chalk tallies on the rims.

Tiffani saw the extra trays and nodded once. Approval without praise.

Bram checked the hinge. Kylar checked the wedge stacks. Tiffani checked cord tension and re-marked the node pairs with fast, controlled strokes. Jhace stood with his hands at his sides and did not touch timber.

“Non-glow drill,” Tiffani said. “Pattern analysis first.”

Bram raised the sling a hand. The upright creaked under new load.

Tiffani crouched by the primary tray. “Concentration line pulls toward the hinge seam. Secondary stable at the midline. Baseline.”

Kylar’s eyes tracked the grit lines. “Baseline,” he repeated.

Jhace kept his gaze on the timber joint. He watched the hinge pin. He watched the brace angles. He watched the wedge bite points. Work he could do without burning himself.

Tiffani stood. “Strategic planning.”

She pointed in sequence.

“Bram controls load. Kylar watches tray drift. I mark. Jhace does not touch unless I call for it. If I call for contact, Kylar positions for secondary break.”

Kylar nodded. Bram nodded. Jhace nodded once.

“Execution,” Tiffani said.

Bram jostled the frame. The upright shifted a fraction. The dust tray line bent and reformed.

“Drift,” Kylar called immediately.

Tiffani moved. Chalk scraped stone. The new primary mark shifted a hand. Her strokes were clean even with the bench-level tremor in the air.

Bram increased load again. The hinge creaked. The upright leaned a fraction deeper.

“Drift,” Kylar called again, faster.

Tiffani redrew. “Good call,” she said, and that was the closest thing to praise she gave.

They repeated the sequence until Kylar’s voice came steady on drift calls and Bram’s hands moved on the load pin without looking away from the hinge.

Jhace did not touch.

On the next run, the ground rolled harder. The awning ropes snapped tight. A jar on a nearby stall clinked and fell, shattering. Dust came down from the market roofline in a sheet.

Kylar’s head snapped to the tray. “Drift,” he shouted.

Tiffani moved to re-mark.

Jhace’s hands lifted on instinct. Palms half-open. Fingers shaking. He could feel the pressure point under his skin where contact would sit. He could feel the urge to press and lock and hold.

Tiffani’s voice cut across the rig. “No contact.”

Jhace froze. The tremor made the pause visible. His palms hovered for one breath. He forced them down.

The upright creaked and then steadied as Bram dropped the load pin to a safer set. The rig stayed upright. No glow. No heat.

Kylar stared at Jhace’s hands lowering. His own hands shook harder for a moment, then settled.

“That is stopping,” Kylar said.

Jhace nodded once. His throat hurt. “Yes.”

Tiffani wrote a line in her logbook. “Restraint under impulse,” she said. “Logged.”

Bram looked over the hinge frame at Jhace. “You’re learning.”

Jhace’s mouth twitched. “Don’t make it a speech.”

Bram made a small sound that might have been a laugh. “Wouldn’t dare.”

They packed the rig down as the Quench deepened. Lantern light caught dust in the air. The seam in the sky showed faint through a gap in the rooflines. Jhace kept his eyes on the stone under his boots and the cord bundle in his hand.

Tiffani walked beside him with her logbook tucked against her ribs. Her shoulder brushed his once in the narrow passage and did not pull away.

In Bram’s workshop, she washed ink off her fingers with cold water. Jhace sat on the bench with his forearms on his knees. His hands shook in open view.

Tiffani dried her hands on a cloth and set it down. “I am not going to ask you to forgive me for breaking your contact,” she said.

Jhace looked up. “Good.”

She nodded once, sharp. “I am going to ask you to let the method hold you when your hands will not.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened. He breathed in and out through his nose. “Yes.”

Tiffani’s eyes held on him. “And when you hate it,” she added, “you say it.”

Jhace’s voice came low. “I hate it.”

Tiffani’s mouth tightened for one breath, then eased. “Accurate.”

His tremor spiked, then settled. He did not hide it. She did not look away.

---  
## Metadata[3][4][1]
- **Word Count:** ~1,420  
- **Validated:** Lexicon, Cost, Voice, Gates, Beat, Structure (0 Soulpulse uses; no simile-narration; Te’Oga units).  
- **Key Scenes:** Bench-map analysis; redundancy protocol formalized; rig drill (drift calls, no contact); trauma-bond admission + method as anchor.

***

# N01: Chapter 11 – Lines Drawn[2][1][3]
**Tide:** 3 | **Phase:** Kindling [1]  
**POV:** Jhace | **Beat:** BEAT07 | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses | **Relationship:** Partnership Formalized [3]  
---  
The summons came on slate.

A runner delivered it to Bram’s workshop with both hands. His eyes stayed on the Crown seal, not on Jhace. His knuckles were white around the edges of the slate.

Jhace read the script once. RRO committee chamber. Mandatory attendance regarding stabilization protocols and anomalous operator safety.

Tiffani read over his shoulder without asking.

“They want the method,” she said.

“They want the leash,” Jhace said.

Bram wiped his hands on a cloth and nodded toward the back stairs. “Less visible.”

They went Upslope through service passages that smelled of damp stone and old paper. The archive hall had been carved into the Crown’s older foundations long before the Piercing. Now it held benches and guards and clerks with stacks of slates that kept multiplying.

Jhace felt the air change as they entered. Cleaner. Drier. Less dust. More ink.

Corlexi sat on the raised bench with his papers aligned into clean lines. A committee vest lay flat on his shoulders with no wrinkles. Harlen sat to his right with tired eyes and a stack of forms. Two guild representatives sat at the ends, mouths set.

Guards stood at both doors. Their hands stayed near batons.

“Be seated,” Corlexi said.

Jhace sat. Tiffani sat beside him. Bram stood behind and to the side, arms crossed, taking in the exits.

Corlexi did not waste breath. “The committee has recorded multiple unauthorized interventions since the Piercing.”

“Stone fails,” Jhace said. “We wedge. We brace. We hold.”

Harlen leaned forward. “Unregulated holds create risk. Operator collapse. Secondary failures. Civilian harm.”

Tiffani’s voice cut in, even and sharp. “Those are categories without tolerances.”

A clerk’s pen hesitated, then resumed faster.

Corlexi lifted a slate. “Registration is required. All anomalous stabilizers will report. Interventions will be assigned through the committee. Logs will be delivered in raw form. Names. Sites. Engagement durations.”

Jhace’s hands shook in his lap. He did not hide them. The tremor made his knuckles jump against each other.

“No,” Tiffani said.

The word landed and stayed. The chamber went quiet enough that a pen scratch sounded loud.

Harlen’s shoulders rose. He exhaled. “Master Merrow—”

“Do not,” Tiffani said. “Do not use my title as if it makes me compliant.”

Corlexi’s expression did not change. “Refusal will be recorded.”

“Record it,” Jhace said.

Corlexi turned one slate so the committee could see the ink. “A maximum engagement threshold will be set. Ten heartbeats per activation.”

Tiffani’s gaze sharpened. “Ten heartbeats is a disengagement signal. It is not a quota.”

Corlexi’s tone stayed measured. “A threshold is necessary for safety.”

“Safety is site specific,” Tiffani said. “Marker compression occurs during failure waves. Drift changes tolerances. Your threshold becomes a trap when the ground moves.”

Harlen lifted his palms. “Look. The committee is trying to prevent deaths.”

“Deaths are already here,” Jhace said. “Your forms don’t stop them.”

A guild representative cleared his throat. “The heat-grid fails in three Tides. Crown districts require stabilization priority. Unauthorized deployments divert resources.”

Tiffani did not look at him. “Resource diversion is a logistics term. A living body is not a resource.”

Corlexi’s fingers tapped the bench once. “Crown priorities are not optional.”

Bram’s voice drifted in from behind Jhace, slow and dry. “Neither is gravity.”

Corlexi’s eyes moved to Bram for one breath and then back to Jhace.

“A secondary operator has been observed,” Corlexi said. “Kylar. Unregistered. This suggests uncontrolled expansion.”

Jhace felt heat under his ribs that had nothing to do with glow. “Kylar is being trained.”

“Under whose authority,” Harlen asked, voice worn.

“Ours,” Tiffani said. “Baseline measured. Drift protocol drilled. Contact-break redundancy assigned. Stop signals practiced without activation. You have sightings. We have method.”

Corlexi leaned forward a fraction. “Then deliver it.”

Tiffani’s grip tightened on her logbook. “Aggregated findings,” she said. “Verified. Without names.”

Corlexi’s voice stayed mild. “Names provide accountability.”

“Names provide targets,” Jhace said.

Harlen’s eyes flicked to the guards and back. “If you refuse, penalties apply. Work permits revoked. Access blocked. You will not be allowed into Crown zones. You will be held liable for damages caused by unregistered interventions.”

Jhace stared at Harlen. His hands shook harder. He forced his voice flat. “You will hold us liable while walls split.”

Corlexi’s gaze held steady. “Control prevents panic.”

“Panic is not the load path,” Tiffani said.

A faint rattle ran through the hall. A stack of slates shifted a finger and then settled. Dust fell from a high shelf in a thin line.

No one moved.

Jhace counted anyway. One. Two.

It passed.

He stood. His knees felt wrong under him for a breath, then steadied.

“We will not report to intake,” Jhace said. “We will not wait for assignment. You can send runners to sites. We will go where the load is.”

Tiffani stood with him. She did not speak for a moment. Then she said, “You will receive verified findings. Not raw logs. Not a leash.”

Harlen stood too, jaw tight. “You are making enemies.”

Bram’s voice came soft. “They were already made.”

Corlexi lifted a hand. The guards did not move. The committee chamber stayed a room full of rules that could not wedge a single brace.

“Unregulated method creates liability,” Corlexi said. “When a death occurs, it will be attributed.”

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on him. “Attribute it,” she said. “Ink does not hold stone.”

They left the chamber and took the back stairs Downslope. A clerk watched them go with wide eyes, pen held too tight. A guard stared at Jhace’s shaking hands.

Bram waited at the landing with his back against a post. He did not ask questions.

“They wanted names,” Bram said.

“They got no,” Jhace replied.

Tiffani walked beside Jhace in the corridor. Her logbook was tucked under her arm. Her shoulder stayed close to his without brushing, a deliberate distance.

“Watchers will follow,” she said.

“Let them,” Jhace said.

His tremor ran constant. The vein-lines on his forearms stayed faint and visible under the skin. He did not look at them.

Tiffani’s voice lowered. “If they cannot leash us through policy, they will leash us through need.”

Jhace nodded once. “I know.”

## Metadata[4][1][3]

- **Validated:** Lexicon, Cost, Voice, Gates, Beat, Structure (0 Soulpulse uses; political pressure; no forbidden direction terms).

***

# N01: Chapter 12 – We Hold Together[1][2][3]
**Tide:** 3 | **Phase:** Shaping [1]  
**POV:** Jhace | **Beat:** BEAT03 | **Soulpulse:** 0 uses | **Relationship:** Functional Love [3]  
---  
The request came Upslope first.

A slate with a Crown seal. Priority and urgent stamped in clean ink. Heat-grid node stabilization—upper terrace. Committee clearance attached. Reporting instructions written as if ink could make stone obey.

Jhace read it once and set it on Bram’s bench. His hands shook harder for a breath.

Tiffani read over his shoulder. “Fourth Crown site routed through committee,” she said.

Jhace kept his voice flat. “Crown has crews.”

“Crown has paperwork,” Bram said. He did not smile.

A runner’s footsteps hit the corridor fast and uneven. Salee stepped into the workshop with rope over one shoulder and dust in her hair. Her face was set. Her eyes did not ask for permission.

“Downslope market foundation wall moved,” she said. “Not a hairline. A shift. Families are pressed against it. They won’t move far.”

“How many,” Jhace asked.

“Seventeen,” Salee said. “Mostly displaced. They won’t leave their lean-tos. Nowhere to go.”

Tiffani shut her logbook and slung her satchel. “We go.”

Bram grabbed a brace kit without being asked. His hands moved on their own, fast and practiced.

They ran Downslope.

The market smelled of damp stone, sweat, and copper dust ground into cloth. Rope lines cut a perimeter around the foundation wall. Salee’s people held the lines with blunt voices. They did not shout. They did not argue. They kept bodies back with hands and stance.

Lean-tos pressed against the wall. Salvaged boards. Cloth. Rope lashings. Cooking pots. A child sat on a rolled blanket with eyes wide and silent.

Jhace felt the wall through his boots before he reached it. The stone under him pulled wrong. The load was not settling. It was searching.

Tiffani moved along the base course with cord and chalk. Her marks came fast.

“Primary stress here,” she said, placing an X near a settled block. “Secondary two hands left. Tertiary at the corner. Not yet.”

Kylar stood near the rope line with dust on his vest. His hands shook faintly. He did not hide them.

Jhace looked at him. “No glow unless it answers,” he said. “Ten heartbeats is a disengagement signal. Not a goal.”

Kylar nodded once. “Understood.”

Salee’s eyes flicked to Jhace’s hands. “No hero holds,” she said.

Jhace’s mouth tightened. “No.”

Tiffani stepped between Jhace and the wall and pressed two fingers to his wrist. Then Kylar’s. Her touch was brief and precise.

“Baseline elevated,” she said. “Tremor consistent. If it escalates toward convulsive, we stop.”

Bram laid the brace kit open on a tarp. Wedges. Timber lashings. A hinge plate. Nails. Cord.

Jhace stared at the wall. His body wanted contact. Palms to stone. Heat under skin. Copper-gold answering. Pain later.

He looked at the child on the blanket. Then at the woman holding a pot as if it could stop a wall. He remembered the throat in the junction and the stone that fell after he stopped, and the bodies that did not fall with it.

He did not want his hands torn off stone again. He wanted to choose restraint before the world forced it.

“Bram,” he said. “Brace line first. We spend timber before we spend glow.”

Bram blinked once and nodded. “On it.”

Salee gave a sharp hand signal. Her people moved.

They drove wedges under the worst sag. Hammers struck stone and wood. Timber braces went in at shallow angles. Rope lashings cinched tight enough to bite into bark and sing when pulled.

The wall groaned. It did not move farther.

Then the wave came.

Not a polite rattle. A roll that shoved the market sideways by a fraction and left it there. Stalls clinked. Lantern flames bent. Dust burst from seams in a thick sheet.

A scream rose from the crowd and cut off when Salee’s people snapped orders and shoved bodies back.

The wall shifted a fraction.

The wedge line clacked and held.

Bram threw his shoulder into the main brace timber. Kylar grabbed the brace base and kept it from skidding. Jhace stepped in without being told and planted his weight against the timber with boots wide. Shoulder to wood. Forearms locked. His hands shook in front of him, useless for fine work and still good for leverage.

Tiffani shouted over the noise. “Drift—two hands right.”

Her chalk came out mid-shake. She marked a new primary point while dust stung her eyes. Her line stayed clean.

Jhace saw the drift. He felt it through the brace timber. Load concentration shifting away from the first wedge line.

He did not touch the wall.

He shifted his body position with the timber instead. He moved the brace base a hand. He made the wood follow the new mark rather than making his skin follow the stone.

Kylar mirrored him on the other side without being told. His tremor made his grip jump. He held anyway.

The wave passed. The market stayed vibrating. Then it eased.

The wall did not collapse.

Breath returned to the crowd in ugly sounds. A child cried once. A mother pressed the child’s face into her shoulder and rocked without moving her feet.

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on the seam. “Second line,” she said. “Behind the first. This brace will not hold through another wave.”

Salee nodded. “We do it now.”

An RRO watcher in a clean vest stood at the market edge with a slate in hand. He had been there for several breaths. His boots were clean on dirty stone.

“You are ordered Upslope,” he said. “Heat-grid node. Committee directive.”

Tiffani stepped forward, not flinching. “Tell Corlexi the Downslope wall shifted during a failure wave,” she said. “Tell him seventeen families were against it. Tell him we are bracing.”

The watcher’s jaw tightened. “That is not the route.”

Salee spoke before Jhace did. “Routes don’t matter when people die.”

The watcher looked at her with a flat expression and then looked past her at Jhace’s shaking hands.

Jhace stepped forward. His tremor was visible. He did not care.

“The Crown has crews,” he said. “The Downslope has us.”

“Refusal will be recorded,” the watcher said.

“Record it,” Jhace replied.

The watcher stared for one breath longer, then turned and left. His slate stayed clean. His boots stayed clean.

Bram muttered, “Hope he enjoys his ink.”

Tiffani did not smile. “Penalties will come,” she said, quiet.

Salee’s voice stayed blunt. “Let them.”

They worked until the Quench deepened. Second brace line. Deeper wedges. Rope lashings. Bodies passing timber. Hands hauling rubble away from the base course to reduce pressure points. No committee seals. No route slates. Just work.

When the work paused long enough for breath, Salee walked them to the water barrels. Chalk marks on the sides showed counts. Served. Served. Served. No names. No intake forms.

“This is how we survive,” Salee said. “We do it.”

Tiffani stared at the rope lines and the way Salee’s people moved without being told twice. “This is a system,” she said.

Salee’s mouth tightened. “Don’t call it that. Systems get claimed.”

Jhace looked back at the foundation wall. It still leaned. It still wanted to settle. The braces held for now.

He looked at Tiffani. Chalk dust streaked her sleeve. Ink stained her fingers. Fatigue sat in her eyes and did not slow her hands.

“We chose this,” Tiffani said, low.

Jhace nodded once. “Yes.”

“The cost increases,” she said. “More failures. More need. More pressure from the RRO.”

“Yes,” Jhace said again.

Salee watched them. “Don’t break yourselves for us,” she said. “Alive matters more than heroic.”

Jhace flexed his hands. The tremor answered immediately. It was constant. It was real.

“I don’t know how to be heroic,” he said. “I know how to hold.”

Tiffani stepped closer. She did not reach for his wrist to stop him. She did not reach for a cord to control him. She reached for his hand.

Her fingers closed around his shaking palm.

The contact did not erase the tremor. It gave it somewhere to go.

“This partnership,” Tiffani said slowly, “is not just method.”

Jhace swallowed. His throat scraped. “No.”

Her grip tightened a fraction. “Then we name it.”

Salee turned her head away and walked back toward the rope line without ceremony.

Jhace’s voice came low. “Name it.”

Tiffani held his gaze. “We hold together.”

Jhace repeated it once. The words came rough. “We hold together.”

Tiffani nodded. “Not just stone. Each other. The count. The stop. The choice.”

Jhace’s tremor ran between their hands. She did not let go.

“Together,” he said.

A small rattle ran through the market. People flinched and then kept working. The brace lines held. The wall stayed upright.

They could not fix the puncture in the world. They could hold what was in front of them.

Together.

---

- **Validated:** Lexicon, Cost, Voice, Gates, Beat, Structure (0 Soulpulse uses; crisis resolved through method + networked labor; vow lands without skipping).  
- **Key Scenes:** Crown priority refused; Downslope market wall braced; watcher rebuffed; second brace line set; acknowledgment + “We hold together” commitment.
