# N01: The Grip That Holds (Seismic Revision)

## Chapter 1 — Something Wrong

**Tide:** 0 | **Phase:** Kindling | **POV:** Jhace

Jhace stood at Junction Seven and pressed his palm to the stone where the wall had been.

They had rebuilt it two Castings ago. New blocks. Fresh mortar. The grid hum ran steady here now—no rasp, no chatter. The marry point held its load.

He still checked it every Tide.

Twenty-three people had died here because he had heard the hiss and called it settling. Because he had written *monitor, non-urgent* in his ledger and moved on. Because the tunnel had failed three Tides later and the list of names had been read out loud until his throat closed.

He knew all their names. He carried them in the tight place under his ribs where breath should have gone easier.

The stone under his hand was cool. Stable. It would not fail today.

He moved on.

The Underface narrowed as he went deeper. The air tasted of damp iron and kiln exhaust. His lantern threw a tight circle of light over pipe braces and riveted plates. The grid’s vibration ran through his boots in a constant low pulse he had learned to read through soles and bone.

At Junction Twelve the hum shifted.

Not here. Somewhere else. A wrongness riding under the usual vibration, faint but persistent. He stopped and set his palm flat against the main pipe.

The metal carried heat downslope into the Dregs. It carried the grid’s vibration with it. Normally that voice ran even—steady pressure, steady flow, joints carrying the same low note.

Not now.

The note doubled. A harmonic that should not exist. Not a loose bracket. Not a mild strain from cold phase. A pattern that meant something had moved without permission.

Jhace pulled his ledger and made the mark.

Sector Fourteen, third branch. Pull for inspection. Immediate.

The foreman would push back. The guild would complain. He would take their complaints and shut the line down anyway, because *monitor, non-urgent* had killed twenty-three people and he would not write those words again.

He capped his ink.

A drip fell somewhere in the dark. A runner’s footsteps passed above, faint through stone. The Underface around him kept humming, but the wrong harmonic stayed under it like a second pulse.

Jhace pressed his ear closer to the pipe. The metal vibrated hard enough to buzz against cartilage. The harmonic was not random. It had rhythm.

He was still counting it when the floor gave a small jerk.

Not a full tremor. A single sharp shift, like the stone had been pushed sideways by a giant hand and released. Dust shook loose from a seam overhead and fell into his lantern light in thin streaks.

Jhace steadied himself with one hand on the pipe. His lantern swung once and settled.

He waited.

No crack. No hiss. No shout of failing brackets.

The grid hum returned to its baseline pitch—then climbed a fraction, strained.

Jhace swallowed. The taste in his mouth turned metallic for a breath. Not blood. Not root. Just a sharp tang that had no source.

He wrote again.

*Kindling. Junction Twelve. Pulse shift. Dust drop. Harmonic persists.*

His hand did not shake. Not yet.

He should go up. Report. Tell someone with a seal and a neat desk that the Underface had moved in a way it should not.

He did not go up.

He followed the harmonic deeper, tracking it by touch. Palm to pipe, palm to support, listening through skin. The wrongness strengthened toward Sector Fourteen, toward the third branch where the heat line split toward the lower terraces.

A crew worked there—three guild men in soot-dark vests, sleeves rolled, sweating in the warm air. They had a brace plate open on a joint and were arguing over whose fault the misalignment was.

Jhace stopped at the edge of their light.

"Inspector," one of them said, seeing his vest mark. His tone held impatience before Jhace spoke. "We already filed a request for a replacement collar. We have work to do."

"Stop," Jhace said.

The nearest man blinked. "We cannot stop. The line feeds the kiln quarter—"

"It stops," Jhace said. "Now."

The man opened his mouth to argue.

Jhace stepped past him and put his palm on the joint collar.

The harmonic screamed under his skin.

Not loud in the air—loud in bone. The collar was taking load it should not. The support plate behind it had settled wrong. The bolt pattern was misaligned by less than a finger width, but the strain it created ran through the whole span like a crooked tooth.

Jhace pulled his hand away.

"Brace failure is coming," he said. "Within this Tide."

"That is not possible," another worker said. "We just tightened—"

Jhace looked at him. "You tightened against a mis-seat. You made it worse."

The first man scowled. "You do not have that data."

Jhace held up his ledger. "I have enough."

He moved to the control valve and turned it down a notch. The pipe voice dropped.

The workers swore in unison.

"You shut this line and you freeze people," the first man said.

"You leave it and you burn them," Jhace said. "Or you crush them. Choose."

They stared at him. Two heartbeats. Three.

A second jerk hit the stone. Sharper than the first. The crew’s tools clinked against the brace plate. Dust fell again, thicker.

The argument stopped because their bodies stopped it. Hands went still. Breath held.

Jhace felt the vibration jump through the pipe. The harmonic shifted. It thickened.

He did not like that.

"Brace it," he said. "Now. Temporary wedges under the collar. Two on the east side, one on the south. You—" He pointed at the youngest worker. "Run upslope. Get Bram Kelter. Tell him Sector Fourteen is settling wrong."

The young man hesitated.

Jhace repeated, "Run."

The boy ran.

The first man’s jaw clenched. "We cannot wedge without committee clearance."

Jhace looked at him, then at the joint collar.

"Do you want clearance," he said, "or do you want the line to stay in one piece."

The man cursed and knelt. He pulled wedge slats from a tool bag and began shoving them into the gap under the collar.

Jhace moved back to the pipe and pressed his palm flat against it again.

The harmonic was still there. It was not coming from the joint alone.

It was coming from deeper.

From below the Underface. From under the foundation.

Something was pushing through the planet’s mass, and the stone was responding before anyone on the surface knew to be afraid.

Jhace wrote one more line.

*Not settling. Not local. Pressure under foundation. Not again.*

He looked toward the ladder shaft that led up into Saint Merro.

The stone under his boots gave a tiny shiver, like a warning tap.

Jhace started moving.

---

## Chapter 2 — Glass That Listens

**Tide:** 0 | **Phase:** Pouring | **POV:** Tiffani

Tiffani set the theodolite to Seven-Three and leaned into the eyepiece.

The star should have met the crosshair where it had met every previous pass for the last six Tides. Steady. Predictable.

It missed.

Not by a fraction. By enough that she checked the angle twice and reset the instrument a third time before she trusted her own eyes.

She straightened.

The last time she had dismissed a persistent pattern as noise, Marisse died.

The oven wall had whispered for three Anneals—temperature drift, tiny stress fractures, heat pooling where it should have spread. Marisse had asked if it worried her. Tiffani had said no. She had not wanted to stop production. She had not wanted to argue with Master Corlis about delays.

The wall had torn itself open mid-firing. Marisse had been standing beside it when stored heat let go.

Tiffani had heard the crack from the observatory platform, then the scream.

She did not assume again.

She checked the instrument base—all three feet flat on stone, no grit, no wobble. Checked the lens—no smear. Reset to Seven-Three and sighted again.

Same offset. Repeatable.

She pulled her logbook close and wrote with more pressure than necessary.

*Tide 0, Pouring phase. Deviation at Seven-Three increased beyond prior tolerance. Instrument confirmed stable. Pattern persistent. Conclusion: external source.*

She underlined *external* once, then wrote the adjacent offsets and the curve the star drift implied.

The air on the platform tasted of metal. Faint. Sharp. No smoke source. No kiln vent nearby. She swallowed and the tang stayed.

She rotated the theodolite through the upper band. Seven-Three was not alone. Adjacent stars showed smear by small degrees, brightness stretched as if light itself had been pulled sideways.

The curve was coherent. It had direction. It pointed—if she extended it—toward the far side of the city, beyond Saint Merro’s tight streets and leaning rooflines.

Tiffani wrote the projection in a separate column.

Her glass plates began to hum.

Not loudly. A pitch just below hearing, traveling through the prisms in their cradles, the spare lens plates stacked on the lower shelf, even the ink bottle at her elbow. The sound climbed into her teeth.

She set the pen down and pressed her palm to the stone under the instrument’s feet.

Cool. No tremor. The platform was stable.

The vibration was in the glass.

One prism jumped in its cradle and scraped the metal lip. She caught it with her palm and held it down. The vibration ran through her hand and into her wrist.

This was not normal resonance from wind. The air was still.

She looked back through the eyepiece.

Seven-Three drifted again, a tiny movement that should not have happened between breaths.

Tiffani wrote faster.

*Glass resonance onset. Metallic taste present. Optical displacement accelerating. No local tremor detected at platform. Source remains external.*

She heard the first shout from below a breath later.

Not words. A sound that carried panic up stairwells. She stepped to the platform edge and looked down across rooftops.

The city’s grid hum—always present if you listened for it—shifted pitch. A low note climbed into a strained higher one, like a line forced beyond its intended load.

Then a pressure change hit her chest.

Not wind. Not a weather front. A blunt force that pushed air outward from the city’s far edge. The shock made her lantern flame bend and her logbook page lift and slap back down.

She turned toward the curve she had projected.

A column of dust rose from beyond the Saint Merro district line. It climbed fast—too fast—expanding outward as if the ground itself had burst open.

A second later, the platform shook.

Not a gentle tremor. A hard lurch that sent theodolite feet scraping against their marks. The glass plates on the shelf clinked. The prism she held tried to jump again.

Gravity went wrong for a breath.

Her stomach rose like she had stepped off a ledge. Her feet felt light, then heavy, then normal again. She grabbed the instrument frame to keep from falling.

The sound came with it—a deep roar that wasn’t thunder and wasn’t collapse. It was pressure moving through the world’s mass.

She looked back toward the dust column.

For a heartbeat she saw something inside it.

Not a building. Not debris.

A dark spindle shape moving upward at impossible speed, visible only as a moving absence against bright dust. She did not see ends. She did not see full length. The thing was gone before her eyes finished focusing.

The air around its path bent light. Rooftops near the column warped, edges bending as if seen through thick glass.

Then the dust expanded and hid everything.

Copper-gold shimmer fell through sunlight.

Not sparks. Not ash. Fine particulate that caught light and turned it metallic. It drifted down in slow sheets, coating roof tiles, catching in hair, sticking to her sleeves.

The platform shook again. A second aftershock. Smaller but sharp.

Tiffani’s hands moved without her permission: she closed the logbook, strapped it, grabbed her satchel.

Pella burst onto the platform stairs at a run, face pale, breath tearing.

"Master," she gasped. "The street—Saint Merro—"

"I saw," Tiffani said.

"Was that—" Pella swallowed. "Was that the sky—"

"Do not label it," Tiffani said. "Pack the field kit. We are going down."

Pella blinked. "Down? But—"

"People are being crushed," Tiffani said. "Bring chalk. Cord. Plates. The spare slate. If the glass continues resonating, I want it recorded at the failure sites, not in a tower."

Another shake hit the platform. Tools rattled. The theodolite shifted a finger width.

Pella flinched and grabbed the railing.

Tiffani counted without thinking. One. Two. Three heartbeats. The tremor eased.

"This is not one quake," Tiffani said. "This is the beginning of a sequence."

Pella stared at her.

Tiffani tightened the satchel strap until it bit her shoulder.

"Move," she said.

They ran down.

---

## Chapter 3 — The Piercing

**Tide:** 0 | **Phase:** Striking | **POV:** Jhace

Jhace was in Saint Merro when the world opened.

He had made it to the surface two streets downslope from the Underface ladder shaft. The air above ground tasted wrong—metal and dust, sharp on the tongue. People moved fast around him, not running yet, but walking with that stiff urgency that meant they felt something in their bones and did not want to admit it.

He pushed through a covered market corridor, roofed in patched wood and old cloth. Stalls lined both sides—tools, dried grain, scrap metal, cheap glass trinkets that caught light.

A fruit vendor argued with a woman about weight. A child cried over a cracked cup. A runner shoved past with a slate held up like a shield.

The ground gave a small shiver.

Jhace stopped in the middle of the corridor. His lantern flame bent, then steadied.

People kept moving. No one paused long enough to look at the floor.

Jhace looked up at the roof beams. A long timber ran the length of the corridor, carrying more load than it should. He saw an old split at one end—existing damage, not new. He marked it in his head anyway.

He moved on.

The next lurch hit harder.

Stall poles rattled. Hanging goods swung. Grain sacks shifted on their stacks. A clay bowl slid off a counter and shattered, the crack sharp in the corridor’s enclosed air.

Jhace grabbed a support post with one hand and steadied himself.

This was not a local settling.

This was deep.

He started toward the open end of the corridor where street light spilled in.

A sound hit him from below.

Not a bang. Not a crack.

A low roar that came through stone and into bone, like the planet itself had taken a breath and forced it out. His teeth buzzed with it. His chest vibrated.

Then gravity went wrong.

For a breath his weight vanished. His stomach floated. His boots lost contact with the stone by a fraction and came down again too hard, knees buckling.

People screamed because their bodies understood before their minds did.

Jhace looked toward the corridor exit.

Outside, beyond rooflines, the ground erupted.

Not a building collapse. Not a sinkhole.

The street far ahead bulged upward, then split open with a violence that threw debris into the air in a widening cone. Stone blocks, roof tiles, dust—everything lifted at once, as if a force from under the foundations had punched straight through.

A dark shape shot up through the center of the plume.

Jhace saw only a section of it—smooth, spindle-curved, coated in dust and light-bent air. It moved too fast to track. It did not wobble. It did not slow. It was gone into the sky before his eyes finished counting its length.

The dust column kept rising without it.

Copper-gold particulate rained through the corridor opening.

It drifted into the covered space, catching in cloth roof seams and on shoulders and hair. It glinted when it turned in the light. It stuck to skin. It tasted like metal when it hit his lips.

The shockwave arrived a heartbeat later.

Air slammed into the corridor. Stalls bucked. A roof beam groaned. People fell.

Jhace hit the wall shoulder-first. Pain flashed. His lantern swung hard, spilling light across faces twisted in panic.

A second shock followed, weaker but sharp enough to make the roof creak again.

Then the structures began to fail.

A building front outside the corridor made a grinding sound and folded inward. Stone blocks slid like they had lost friction. People shouted names. Someone yelled to run upslope.

Inside the corridor, the long timber Jhace had marked shifted.

Not much. A finger width. Enough.

He saw it happen and moved before he chose to.

"Back," he snapped.

His voice cut through close panic. Two vendors froze. A woman hauling a sack stumbled backward.

The timber dropped.

One end tore loose from its seat and slammed down across the corridor at knee height. The impact shook dust loose from the roof cloth. The timber rolled half a span and pinned a girl who had been running under it.

She went down with a sound that was more breath than voice.

Jhace was on his knees beside her.

Apprentice age. Hair full of copper-gold dust that glittered in the lantern spill. Her leg was trapped under the timber above the knee, crushed against stone.

Her hands clawed at the ground. Her eyes found his.

Jhace put both palms on the timber and tried to lift.

It did not move.

The load above it pressed down through roof beams and stall frames. The corridor’s roof had shifted; everything had settled wrong.

Another tremor hit—short, violent. The timber bit deeper into her leg. She screamed now, loud and raw.

Jhace’s throat tightened.

Not again.

He stopped trying to lift.

He pressed both palms flat against the timber.

Hold, he thought. Long enough.

Heat built under his skin.

Not from friction. Not from effort. A rising pressure that started under his palms and drove upward into his wrists, like something inside him had been struck and was ringing.

Copper-gold light seeped from his hands.

It spilled into the timber grain in narrow threads, tracing along splinters and stress lines. It did not burn his skin. It did not warm the air. It moved as if the wood could drink it.

The timber vibrated under his palms.

Not shaking loose—answering.

The weight changed.

It did not become light. It became held.

Jhace pushed down with his hands and felt resistance meet him—an invisible brace, like the timber had been pinned in place by something that was not wedge or rope.

"Pull her," he said, voice rough.

Hands grabbed the girl’s shoulders. Someone cursed and yanked. Her leg came free in a wet rush of torn cloth and blood.

She screamed again and then choked on it, face going pale.

Jhace held for two more heartbeats to make sure the timber did not roll after her.

Then he let go.

The copper-gold cut out.

The timber dropped a fraction and settled, no longer held by anything but the wrong-leaning roof. It did not roll further.

Jhace stared at his palms.

No burns. No marks.

His fingers began to shake.

Fine at first. Then harder. A rapid tremor from fingertips to wrist that he could not stop by clenching.

He swallowed. The metallic taste in his mouth thickened.

The girl lay on her back on the far side of the corridor, breathing in short gasps. Someone pressed cloth to her leg. Someone else shouted for a healer.

A fresh roar came from outside—another building folding, stone grinding on stone.

Jhace pushed himself up.

His legs answered, but his hands shook in front of him like they belonged to someone else.

He stepped out of the corridor into the street.

Saint Merro had turned into a moving fracture.

Dust hung in the air in thick curtains. Copper-gold particulate glittered inside it. People ran in broken streams, not organized, not directed—away from collapses, toward voices, toward children.

Upslope, a tower leaned and did not fall. Downslope, two fronts had already dropped into their own foundations.

Far beyond the nearest rooftops, the dust column still rose from the eruption site. It expanded outward, drifting on wind that had not been there before.

Jhace looked for the dark shape again.

He did not see it. It was already gone into sky.

Another quake hit.

This one rolled, not snapped. The street rippled under his boots. A wall to his left made a cracking sound along a mortar line and started to separate.

People were in its doorway. A knot of them, clustered under a lintel that was suddenly carrying too much.

"Move," Jhace said.

His voice came hoarse, but it landed.

Two people turned. One froze. A man tried to pull a cart through the doorway, insisting the cart held all he had left.

The wall cracked again.

Jhace ran.

His tremoring hands reached the doorway post. He put both palms on stone.

Cold. Rough. Dust-coated.

The post was failing at its base. Load had shifted onto a narrow contact point. If it went, the wall above would fold into the doorway and crush everyone under it.

Hold, he thought. Long enough.

Heat surged under his palms.

Faster this time. Hotter. Copper-gold light burst into the stone, threading through cracks he could not see until they lit. It traced load paths up the post and into the lintel.

His forearms tightened. His shoulders locked.

The world narrowed to pressure and contact.

The post tried to fail anyway. He felt it like a shove under his hands. The light pressed back.

"Out," he forced out. "Out now."

Someone shouted behind him and people finally moved. The cart scraped and caught. The man cursed and abandoned it. Feet pounded over stone.

The quake eased. The post remained under his palms, held by something that was not wood wedge or brace.

Jhace counted without meaning to.

One. Two. Three heartbeats.

He could not hold it forever. He did not know how long he could hold it at all.

The last person cleared the doorway.

Jhace tore his hands away.

The copper-gold cut out instantly.

The post dropped a fraction and caught in a wrong lean. A few blocks above it shifted and held. Dust spilled down in a steady stream.

Jhace staggered backward.

His hands shook hard now—fast enough that his nails clicked against each other when his fingers flexed. Pain flooded in behind the shaking, a deep burn along wrist and forearm as if the heat had left something raw inside.

He bent forward and tried to breathe through it.

Another aftershock hit, smaller. Dust fell again. Someone screamed nearby.

Jhace looked up through the thinning curtain of airborne grit.

Above the city, the sky showed a new line.

Not the spindle. Not the moving object.

A scar.

A faint bent-light seam that ran across the upper air, visible only where dust made the light catch. It looked like a distortion—like looking through flawed glass.

Jhace stared at it for one heartbeat, two, trying to make sense of a line in the sky that should not exist.

Then a voice cut through the noise, close.

"What did you do?"

Jhace turned.

A woman stood a span away, hair tied tight, sleeves streaked with glass dust and copper-gold particulate. A logbook pressed to her chest with one hand. Her eyes moved fast: his hands, his forearms, the doorway post, the crack pattern, the last fading copper shimmer in the stone.

No awe. No prayer. No soft fear.

Demand.

Jhace tried to close his fingers into fists. The tremor made the motion stutter.

"Held it," he said.

Her gaze flicked back to the post. She stepped closer, careful not to touch him, and leaned in to look at the stone where his palms had been.

"It should have fallen," she said.

"It will," Jhace said. "Brace it."

She didn’t look away from the crack.

"Your contact points are narrow," she said, as if speaking to herself. "The load did not redistribute; it paused. Residual heat is visible at the surface."

Jhace stared at her.

"You have a name," he said, not a question. "You are the one who measures."

Her eyes cut to his face.

"You know about me," she said.

"Guild gossip," Jhace said. "Glass platform. Counts stars. Tells people to stop work for tolerances."

"That is not accurate," she said. Then, after a breath: "Yes. I measure."

Another tremor ran through the street. Smaller. Enough to make the seam in the sky shimmer as dust shifted.

The woman steadied herself by planting her feet, not grabbing a wall. Her eyes stayed on his hands again.

"Your tremor began after you did that," she said.

Jhace did not answer.

She opened her logbook with her free hand, pen already out.

"State your name," she said.

"No," Jhace said.

Her pen paused.

"Then I will write what I can without it," she said. "Two interventions observed. Copper-gold manifestation through direct palm contact. Material response: temporary stabilization under load. Immediate cost: tremor onset in operator’s hands."

Jhace’s throat tightened.

"People are dying," he said. "Write later."

"I write now," she said. "If I do not, it becomes rumor. Rumor kills as fast as stone."

She looked past him toward the street where another wall had begun to lean.

"That one will go in the next aftershock," she said, already moving. "If you touch it, I need to see where."

Jhace watched her move—fast, precise, not running blind, stepping over cracks and debris as if she could read the ground.

His hands shook in front of him. He tried to stop them by pressing his palms together. The tremor ran through both.

A deep roar came again from far below, like the planet shifting its weight.

Jhace tasted metal and dust and fear.

He looked once more at the seam in the sky. Then back to the street-level failures, the people trapped in doorways, the wrong-leaning posts.

He moved.

---

## Chapter 4 — After the Shudder

**Tide:** 1 (Kindling) | **POV:** Jhace

Jhace missed the nail twice.

The bent head sat on the edge of Bram’s bench, half in lamplight, half in shadow. Jhace reached for it with thumb and forefinger. His fingers closed on air. The tremor ran through his hand in a fine, constant vibration.

He tried again. Missed again.

Bram did not comment. He only held the nail steady with the tip of his own finger until Jhace caught it on the third attempt and pinned it to the plank.

Jhace’s grip slipped anyway. The nail skittered and tapped stone.

Bram’s jaw tightened. “Leave it.”

Jhace drew a breath through his nose. The air in the workshop carried wet dust and kiln exhaust and the copper tang that never seemed to clear since the Piercing. He could still taste it on the back of his tongue.

“I can do it,” Jhace said.

“You can do it slow,” Bram replied. “And today the city is doing everything fast.”

Jhace looked past the bench to the corridor outside. Runners moved through with slates. A brace crew pushed a timber cart, wheels grinding over uneven stone. Someone shouted an address. Another voice shouted back that the street no longer existed as it had.

Jhace flexed his hand. The tremor did not stop.

“Where first,” he asked.

Bram nodded toward a stack of marked slates. “Triage span by the kiln corridor. Two supports cracked. One brace line shifted during Pouring.”

Jhace picked up the top slate. The edge scraped his palm. The tremor made the slate buzz lightly against his skin.

He hated the sensation. It was small. It was constant. It was his.

They moved.

---

The kiln corridor had become a route instead of a place. Cloth dividers hung between damaged fronts. Pallets lined the walls in two rows. Healers worked in the center aisle, hands moving without pause. The grid hum ran underneath everything at a pitch that never settled into the old note.

Jhace stepped into the corridor and felt the load through his boots. Not the weight of stone—he knew that. The shift. The unevenness. Places where blocks carried more than they should.

A brace post stood under a lintel near the center. Someone had wedged it with cracked stone. The wedges were already powdering under pressure.

Jhace crouched and pressed two fingers to the brace base. Cold wood. Grit under nail. A slight vibration that did not match the grid.

“Loose,” he said.

“Mark it,” Bram answered.

Jhace took chalk from his pocket. His hand jerked once as he raised it. The chalk line came out crooked.

He redrew it, slower. The second mark was clear enough.

A healer pushed a pallet past his shoulder. Jhace leaned away to give space. The motion sent a short wave through his arms, as if his muscles had to remember how to hold himself still.

He stood and moved on.

The second support crack sat near the far end, where the corridor narrowed into an older Underface passage. The fracture ran from the base of a column up into a joint line. The crack did not gape. It did not hiss. It looked like every hairline he had ever marked and left alone.

Jhace put his palm against it anyway.

Stone met skin. Cool at the surface. Warmer in a narrow band where load pressed through.

He waited for heat. He waited for the copper-gold to answer.

Nothing.

His tremor buzzed against the stone and came back through his bones.

He pressed harder until his wrist hurt.

Nothing answered.

Bram’s voice came from above him. “Anything?”

“No,” Jhace said.

Bram let out one breath. “Good.”

Jhace pulled his hand away. The crack stayed a crack. The column stayed loaded. The city stayed unstable.

He stared at his palm. No glow. No burn. Just the shake.

“It only answers when people are about to die,” Bram said quietly, as if saying it softer would make it less true. “Not when you want to be tidy.”

Jhace did not respond. His jaw was tight.

A small sound of scraping came from farther upslope. A brace pole shifted under its wedges. Someone cursed.

Then the aftershock hit.

It was not the main violence of the Piercing. It was not the deep surge that had made the world feel wrong under his ribs. This was smaller and faster, like a hand shaking the whole corridor once, hard.

Lantern flames bent. Dust fell in a sudden sheet. The brace pole near the healer pallets jumped against its wedges and dropped a finger-width.

Healers froze. A few patients made small sounds and went silent again.

Jhace felt it in his teeth and behind his eyes. His knees flexed on instinct. His hands lifted, empty, ready.

The shake passed within a handful of heartbeats.

No one spoke for one breath. Then work resumed, faster.

Bram was already moving. “That pole. Reset it.”

Jhace reached for a wedge stone. His fingers missed once. He caught it on the second try and slid it under the brace base. The tremor made the stone chatter against wood.

Bram leaned in with shoulder weight and set the pole back where it belonged. Jhace drove the wedge in with the heel of his hand because the hammer felt too risky with his shake.

The wedge seated.

The brace stopped shifting.

The corridor noise returned.

Jhace’s tremor did not stop.

---

They climbed back toward the workshop on the back route, keeping to the less crowded passages. The air felt cooler here. The copper tang still sat in Jhace’s mouth.

He had tried twice now—once in the corridor, once earlier at a wall edge near the street—and both times the glow had refused him. It had answered on the day of the Piercing like it had been waiting. Now it stayed absent when he wanted it most.

He did not know whether that was a mercy.

At the workshop ladder shaft, someone stepped into view above them.

Tiffani Merrow climbed down with her logbook under one arm and a cloth bundle in the other. Glass plates inside, wrapped tight. A stub of chalk was tucked behind her ear.

Her eyes went to Jhace first, then to his hands.

He did not hide them this time. His fingers shook at his sides.

“You’re still standing,” she said.

“Yes,” Jhace replied.

“That is not the same as stable,” she said, and stepped past him toward the bench.

Bram watched her cross his space without asking. Bram’s expression stayed careful.

Tiffani set her bundle down. “Where were the last failures?”

Bram tilted his chin. “Kiln corridor. Underface near the old spoke.”

Tiffani turned her head toward Jhace again. “And you tried?”

Jhace did not ask what she meant. “Twice. Nothing.”

Tiffani nodded once, as if marking a result.

“Show me the crack,” she said.

Jhace moved before he decided to.

---

The Underface spoke passage was older stone, darker, more worn by hands and boots. The crack line in the column base was still there. The aftershock had dusted it clean, making the seam look sharper.

Tiffani measured with her cord, fingers moving fast and exact. She marked two points with chalk, one above the crack, one to the side where the joint line met a load-bearing block.

Jhace watched her mark.

“Why there,” he asked.

“Because this is where load concentrates,” she said. “The crack is where failure expresses. It is not where force is best applied.”

He heard the difference. He did not argue.

Tiffani pressed her palm flat against the marked point and went still for a breath. Her eyes narrowed.

“No residual warmth,” she said. “No copper trace.”

“Because it did not answer,” Jhace said.

Tiffani looked at him. “Or because you did not have the condition.”

“What condition,” Bram asked.

Tiffani did not take her gaze off Jhace. “Protective desperation.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. He did not deny it.

“In the corridor,” he said, “people were under braces.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “But not under *this* crack when you touched it. Not with imminent failure.”

Jhace turned his palm over and stared at the skin. His tremor made the light jitter on his fingers.

“So I cannot use it to prevent,” he said.

“You can prevent with wedges and braces,” Tiffani answered. “This is not a tool for tidy.”

Bram made a small sound that was almost agreement.

Jhace looked at the crack again. It sat there, clean and quiet, as if it had never threatened anyone.

A runner’s footsteps echoed from upslope. Fast. Unsteady.

The runner appeared in the passage opening, breath tearing.

“Holder,” he gasped. “Downslope market stair. Support shifted during the last shake. People trapped in the stair throat. They can’t clear without it folding.”

Jhace was moving before the runner finished.

Tiffani’s chalk went back into her pocket. Her logbook snapped shut.

Bram grabbed a coil of cord and a brace kit without a word.

They ran.

---

## Chapter 5 — Where to Press

**Tide:** 1 (Pouring) | **POV:** Jhace

The stair throat was a bad place to hold anything.

Narrow stone, no side slack, load carried in two tight lines. Jhace could feel it as he moved down—how the vibration in the stone changed from step to step. A high note in the left wall. A duller pressure in the right.

People packed the lower landing, pressed against each other. Not panicking. Not calm. Waiting for the stone to decide.

Salee was there, rope over one shoulder, two others with her carrying wedges and a timber length.

She saw Jhace and stepped out of the crowd without hesitation. “It shifted again during Pouring. The brace line is wrong. If we move people through in a rush, it folds.”

Jhace looked at the overhead span. The support post at the stair bend had a hairline split near its base. Someone had wedged it. The wedges were powdering.

Tiffani moved straight to the post. “Clear the immediate landing,” she said. Not loud, but her voice cut through.

Salee gestured and the crowd backed by small degrees, making space without argument.

Tiffani measured with cord and chalk. Two marks, quick. A third.

Jhace watched her hands. Ink stains. Calluses. No shake.

Kylar stood two spans back, half in shadow. Dust on his vest. His hands were held close to his body as if he didn’t trust them to hang free.

Jhace had seen him twice since the practice wall attempt. Kylar had not asked again to “make it answer.” He had only followed.

Jhace did not know whether that was respect or fear.

Tiffani finished her marks and looked at Jhace. “You do not touch yet.”

“I wasn’t going to,” Jhace said.

“You were,” she replied. “Your shoulders already moved.”

Jhace did not deny it.

Tiffani pointed at the post base. “Primary stress is here. Not across the visible split. Here. Secondary is here.”

Jhace stepped closer but kept his hands off.

Kylar’s voice came low. “You can fix it without… without the glow?”

“We can stabilize with wedges,” Bram said. “We can brace and reroute. The glow is not for everything.”

Kylar swallowed. “My hands shake all the time now.”

Jhace looked at him. The faint tremor was visible. Not as bad as Jhace’s. Not yet.

“When did it start,” Jhace asked.

Kylar’s eyes flicked to the post, then down to his own hands. “After the Piercing. I touched a wall that was sliding. I felt heat for one breath. Then nothing. The shake stayed.”

Tiffani wrote without looking up. “That matters. But not here.”

Salee leaned in. “We need passage. People need to get up to water. And down to shelter. We can’t keep this stair closed.”

Tiffani nodded. “We brace it. No glow. No contact. We treat this as stone.”

Bram and Salee’s crew set wedges. Jhace held a timber end steady because his hands were bad for hammer work. The tremor made the timber buzz under his palm, but he kept it in place.

They worked fast.

Then the aftershock came.

It rolled instead of snapping. A longer motion that shifted weight through the stair throat in a slow wave.

Jhace felt it in his knees first, then in his teeth.

Dust fell. A small shower of grit pattered down the steps. The brace timber they were setting shifted a finger-width. The chalk marks on the post base did not align with the cord line anymore.

Tiffani cursed once under her breath. Not loud.

“Stop,” she said. “Hold everything. No strikes.”

Everyone froze.

The shake passed.

Tiffani pulled her cord back into tension and remeasured. Her earlier marks were wrong now by a small amount. The load path had moved.

“We redo,” she said. “Two hand-widths right on the primary.”

Salee’s crew looked at each other and then followed her instruction without complaint.

Bram glanced at Jhace. “That’s what we’re in now,” he said quietly. “Marks move under us.”

Jhace watched Tiffani redraw, chalk squeaking. He watched her write the correction as if it mattered as much as the first measurement.

Kylar stared at the shifting marks. “So you can’t learn a wall once,” he said. “You have to keep learning it.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “The planet is not holding still.”

Jhace’s mouth went tight. He did not argue with that.

They reset the brace with the new marks. Salee’s crew drove wedges. Bram anchored the timber line.

This time, when the crowd moved through, the post did not complain.

People went up. People went down. The stair throat stayed open.

Tiffani closed her logbook and finally looked at Kylar. “You want training. Training is not making it answer. Training is learning where to press when it answers, and learning to stop when someone tells you.”

Kylar’s throat bobbed. “I don’t know how to stop.”

Jhace heard the honesty in that. It matched what he had not said yet.

“We start with where,” Jhace said. “Then we learn stop.”

Kylar’s eyes narrowed slightly. “How.”

Tiffani turned the chalk in her fingers. “Tomorrow, we use a stable wall and map stress. During a shake, we re-map. You do it until your marks match what the load is doing, not what you want it to do.”

Kylar looked uncertain. “That doesn’t—”

“It does not look like power,” Tiffani cut in. “Correct.”

Jhace watched Kylar’s hands. The tremor was faint. It was there. It would become a constant if Kylar kept chasing answers.

Kylar nodded once, slow. “All right.”

Jhace did not know if “all right” meant acceptance. It was enough to proceed.

Tiffani looked back at the stair throat and then at Jhace. “No glow today. Good.”

Jhace stared at his own shaking hands. “Good,” he echoed, without meaning it.

Above them, the grid hum ran at its altered pitch. Dust hung in the air like a thin fog.

The planet did not settle.

---

## Chapter 6 — Stop

**Tide:** 1 (Shaping) | **POV:** Jhace

The call came from Underface again.

A junction span under the lower terraces. Old stone. High load. A brace line that had survived Kindling and Pouring and then shifted during the last rolling shake.

“People are still inside,” the runner said. “They tried to clear. The throat narrowed. A support groaned. They stopped moving.”

Jhace was already on the ladder when Bram answered.

Tiffani followed without waiting for permission, logbook tight against her ribs. Kylar came last, silent, face drawn.

Jhace did not tell him to stay back. He did not tell him to come closer. He did not have time to decide.

---

The junction span smelled of wet stone and copper dust.

The corridor was narrower here, walls older, joints packed tight. The support in question sat at the bend where two passages met. The crack ran along the base and up into a seam line like a pale thread.

People clustered on the far side of the bend, visible through a gap. They were not crushed yet. They were waiting for the corridor to decide if it would hold.

A site lead stood with two brace workers. His hands were chalked. His eyes were raw. “After the last shake, the base shifted. We wedged it. It still groans.”

Jhace moved closer and looked up.

The overhead load did not sit centered. It rode slightly to one side, pressing into the cracked base as if leaning on a bad leg.

Tiffani stepped in front of Jhace and held up a hand.

“Do not touch yet,” she said.

Jhace stopped. His hands flexed at his sides. The tremor buzzed through his fingers.

Tiffani measured with cord and chalk. She did not hurry. She moved fast, but each motion completed cleanly.

“Primary node,” she said, marking an X at the base seam. “Secondary node,” another X higher, offset. “If you press the crack line itself, you waste output. You press here and here.”

Jhace stared at the marks. They matched the push he felt through his boots.

Tiffani turned and looked at him. “You listen.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “I listen.”

She stepped close and pressed two fingers to his wrist. “Baseline,” she said. “Heartbeat elevated. Tremor present and consistent.”

Kylar made a small sound behind them. Jhace did not look back.

Tiffani’s gaze stayed on Jhace’s face. “When I tell you to disengage, you disengage.”

Jhace heard the edge. Not anger. Authority.

He did not like it. He needed it.

“Yes,” he said.

“If you do not,” she continued, “I will pull you off.”

Bram shifted weight nearby. Salee was not here. No one else would stop Jhace if he refused.

Kylar asked quietly, “Can you pull him off?”

Tiffani did not glance at Kylar. “Yes.”

Jhace spoke before he could soften it. “Do it if I don’t.”

Tiffani nodded once. No warmth. No hesitation.

“Proceed,” she said.

Jhace stepped in and set his palms on the marked points.

Cold stone. Rough grain. Load pressing wrong.

People were still on the far side. He could hear breathing. Someone whispered a name. Someone else told them to stay quiet.

Hold, Jhace thought. Long enough.

Heat built under his palms.

It was not slow. It was not polite. It rose like a surge that pushed into bone and locked his arms in place.

Copper-gold light seeped out from under his fingers and drove into the seam lines. It threaded into the crack, then into the blocks above, tracing the load paths Tiffani had marked.

Vein-lines lit along his forearms, faint at first, then brighter. The tremor in his hands jumped from fine to hard.

Tiffani’s fingers stayed on his wrist. “Heartbeat spiking,” she said. “Breathing shortening. Tremor escalating.”

Jhace did not answer. He could not spare breath.

The support stopped shifting by degrees. The groan eased into a steady pressure noise.

“Move,” the site lead called to the far side. “One at a time. Keep weight centered.”

Boots scraped. One person moved through the bend, body pressed tight to the wall.

Jhace held.

Two more followed.

“Five heartbeats,” Tiffani counted aloud. “Six. Seven.”

The stone under Jhace’s left palm vibrated differently for one breath. A subtle change in resistance.

Then the aftershock hit.

Not a small snap. Not a rattle. A hard failure wave that rolled through the junction like the planet was trying to shift its skin.

Jhace felt it in his teeth. The corridor pitched under his knees. Dust burst from seams in a thick sheet. The overhead load moved sideways by a fraction.

The crack under his palm widened.

The copper-gold glow spiked brighter in response, flaring along the seam lines. His tremor jumped toward convulsion in a single step.

He wanted to move his right hand to chase the new failure point. The urge was immediate and physical.

“Do not chase,” Tiffani said sharply. “Hold the nodes. Hold the nodes.”

Jhace’s breath tore. His hands felt locked to the stone by pressure.

The quake passed in a handful of heartbeats, leaving the corridor ringing.

The support groaned again, louder than before.

A brace wedge near the base shifted and clacked against stone.

Someone on the far side made a small sound that might have been a sob.

Jhace held.

Tiffani’s fingers tightened on his wrist. “Eight,” she counted. “Nine. Tremor approaching convulsive threshold. Disengage at ten.”

Jhace stared at the seam. It was not stable. The crack was still there. The load had shifted.

If he let go now, he did not know what would happen.

“Tiffani—” he managed.

“Ten,” she said. “Disengage.”

Jhace did not let go fast enough.

He tried. He told his hands to release.

They stayed pressed flat. The glow brightened. Pain spiked up his arms.

Tiffani’s other hand closed hard around his wrist.

“Contact break,” she said, and pulled.

Jhace’s left palm peeled off the stone. The copper-gold cut out at once where contact broke. The glow along the seam line stuttered and dimmed.

His right hand stayed for one breath longer, then she yanked it free as well.

The glow cut out completely.

Jhace staggered backward two steps, arms shaking hard. His hands convulsed in short, uncontrolled snaps. The vein-lines on his forearms pulsed bright and then faded, slower than before.

He bent forward, breathing fast. His chest tightened. The tremor ran through him like it had been turned up.

Behind him, the corridor groaned.

Jhace snapped his head up.

The support did not collapse.

It shifted a fraction and caught. The new brace wedge took load. The overhead pressure redistributed slightly, settling into the adjacent blocks with a sound like grinding teeth.

People moved through the bend in a rush now, guided by the site lead’s hands and voice. One stumbled. Another caught them. No one went down under the support.

Within a short count of heartbeats, the far side was empty.

The corridor still held.

Barely.

Jhace’s legs threatened to fold. He put his back against the wall and slid down until he was crouched. His hands shook so hard his nails clicked against his belt buckle.

Tiffani crouched in front of him. Her eyes were on his pupils first, then his hands, then the vein-lines fading along his forearms.

“Look at me,” she said.

Jhace looked.

“Breathe,” she said. “Slow.”

He pulled one breath in. Then another. The tremor did not stop, but the convulsive edge eased.

Tiffani’s voice softened only in precision, not comfort. “You held through a failure wave and did not chase. You disengaged at ten. I had to pull you. That is correct.”

Jhace let out one breath that could have been a laugh if he had air to spare. “Correct,” he echoed.

Kylar stood a span away, staring at Jhace’s hands as if the tremor was contagious.

“That’s… that’s what stopping looks like,” Kylar said quietly.

Tiffani did not look away from Jhace. “Stopping looks like obeying the count.”

Jhace swallowed. His throat scraped.

“If you hadn’t pulled me,” he said, voice low, “I would’ve held until it broke.”

“Yes,” Tiffani replied. “That is why I pulled you.”

Bram shifted closer. “Support’s still groaning.”

Tiffani stood. “Brace it now. Full wedges. No one under it. The hold bought time, nothing more.”

The brace crew moved in. Their hammers sounded sharp in the tight space.

Jhace stayed crouched. His hands shook in front of his face, visible proof of what it had cost.

Kylar took one step closer, then stopped as if he didn’t know whether he was allowed.

“How do I learn that,” Kylar asked. “How do I learn to stop when it feels wrong to stop?”

Jhace looked up at him. His own breath was still rough.

“You don’t,” Jhace said, blunt as stone. “Not alone.”

Kylar’s face tightened.

Tiffani’s gaze flicked to Kylar for the first time. “You learn it with a protocol,” she said. “And with someone who will pull you off.”

Kylar nodded slowly, eyes fixed on the cord looped around Tiffani’s wrist.

Jhace watched him understand.

The planet had punctured. The ground would keep moving. They would not get a clean world back.

If holders existed now, they needed a way to survive long enough to keep working.

Jhace flexed his hands. The tremor answered immediately, constant and real.

Tiffani stood beside him, logbook already open.

“Record,” she said.

Jhace did not argue.

They recorded.

---

## Chapter 7 — Recalibration

**Tide:** 1 (Quench) | **POV:** Jhace

Jhace could not tie the knot.

The cord was dry hemp, rough against his fingertips. He looped it once, crossed it, pulled—his fingers jerked, the tremor throwing the tail out of his pinch. The loop collapsed. The cord slipped through and snapped back against his knuckles.

He tried again.

The tremor ran constant from fingertip to wrist. Fine, fast. His nails clicked against each other when he forced his hands close.

Bram watched from the bench without speaking. A brace plate sat half-drilled under his lamp. The workshop smelled of wet dust, kiln exhaust, and the copper tang that stayed in the back of Jhace’s mouth no matter how much water he drank.

Jhace looped the cord a third time and used his teeth to hold the tail while his fingers worked. He pulled the knot tight with wrist pressure instead of finger pinch. The cord bit into skin.

It held.

He released it and stared at his hands. The tremor did not change.

Bram finally spoke. “That’s one way.”

Jhace didn’t answer. He set the cord aside and picked up a slate from the stack beside the bench. The edge scraped his palm. The tremor made the slate buzz lightly against his skin.

Outside the workshop, runners moved through the corridor with slates held close. A brace crew dragged timber over uneven stone. Someone shouted a street name. Someone else shouted back that the street had shifted again during the last aftershock.

Bram drilled two more holes in the brace plate and set it down. “You slept?”

“Two phases,” Jhace said.

Bram’s jaw tightened. “That’s not sleep. That’s shutting your eyes until you hear another crack.”

Jhace stared at the slate. “I heard the support groan even when I wasn’t there.”

“That’s your head,” Bram said.

“It’s also the stone,” Jhace said.

Bram did not argue. He set the drill aside and reached for another tool, then paused when a faint rattle ran through the bench. A small vibration, quick. The hanging lamp chain trembled. Dust sifted from a seam in the ceiling and drifted down in a thin line.

Jhace counted on instinct. One. Two. Three heartbeats.

The tremor passed.

No one in the corridor shouted. No runner stopped. They moved through it as if it were weather. As if it had always been like this.

Bram exhaled through his nose. “Quench is supposed to be quiet.”

“Not anymore,” Jhace said.

He stood, slate in hand, and stepped to the workshop entrance. He looked upslope through the passage. Light from above came down in a dull wash through dust. On clear spans, the seam in the sky showed faint through a gap in the rooflines. It looked like flawed glass set across the upper air.

Jhace closed his eyes for one breath and opened them again. The seam stayed.

He went back to the bench.

Tiffani Merrow arrived without announcing herself. Her footsteps were clean, fast. She carried her logbook and a slate strapped to a board, edges protected with cloth. Chalk dust streaked one sleeve. Copper-gold particulate glittered faintly in the folds of her hair when she moved through lamplight.

Her eyes went to Jhace’s hands first. Then his face.

“You did not die,” she said.

Jhace’s mouth twitched without reaching amusement. “Not yet.”

“That is data,” she said, and set her board on Bram’s bench.

Bram shifted to make space without complaint. “You bring more bad news?”

“I bring measurements,” Tiffani said.

She opened the board strap and lifted the top slate. Jhace saw columns in tight script: heartbeats, tremor notes, glow width, contact points. A crude sketch of a seam line on stone with chalk marks labeled.

Jhace leaned in despite himself.

Tiffani tapped one column with her pen. “Your hold at the junction span. Ten heartbeats commanded. Ten heartbeats attempted. Contact broken at ten plus one.”

Jhace stared at the number.

“I did not—” he started.

“You did,” Tiffani said. Her voice stayed level. “Your body did not obey the instruction at ten. I broke contact.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. The memory sat sharp: the aftershock, the flare, his hands refusing to lift.

Bram watched Jhace’s face and said nothing.

Tiffani turned the slate slightly so Jhace could see the next line. “Your tremor increased from baseline at heartbeat seven. Approached convulsive threshold at nine. Glow saturation spiked during the aftershock. The spike compressed your markers.”

Jhace nodded once. He did not like that she had words for it. He needed that she did.

Tiffani lifted her gaze to his hands. “Your tremor baseline remains elevated. Your fine control is reduced. You missed the nail twice yesterday.”

Jhace didn’t react. Bram did.

Bram’s eyes narrowed. “You watching him in my workshop now?”

“I observed,” Tiffani said. “Observation is not ownership.”

Bram huffed once, but he did not push.

Jhace’s fingers jerked against his thigh. He forced them still by pressing his palm flat to the fabric. The tremor ran through pressure anyway.

“You came to tell me I’m worse,” Jhace said.

“I came to tell you your markers are consistent enough to build a protocol,” Tiffani said. “You are not worse than yesterday. You are worse than pre-Piercing. That distinction matters.”

Jhace stared at the slate. “Protocol doesn’t stop the ground.”

“No,” Tiffani agreed. “But it can stop you from chasing failure points when the ground shifts.”

Bram leaned back on his stool. “If you can teach him to stop chasing, I’ll carve you a shrine out of scrap.”

Tiffani did not look at Bram. “Do not threaten me with religion.”

Bram’s mouth twitched. “Fair.”

Tiffani turned another slate. A sketch showed a support with two chalk Xs at base and midline. A third mark in a circle with an arrow.

“Node pairs,” she said. “Two contact points per structure. Not wherever the crack expresses. Where the load concentrates. If the ground moves and the load path shifts, the marks must be redrawn.”

Jhace looked at the sketch. His body recognized it. The push under his boots. The way the stone pressed wrong.

“You can mark fast,” he said.

“I can mark fast enough if you do not touch until I finish,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “People die while you finish.”

“They also die if you waste your output on the wrong stone,” Tiffani said.

Bram made a small sound. Not agreement. Not disagreement. He was listening.

Tiffani’s pen tapped the slate again. “We need a rig.”

Jhace blinked. “A rig.”

“A controlled test structure,” Tiffani said. “Not a practice wall that is too stable to trigger anything. Not a live corridor with people trapped. A loaded brace with predictable failure points. We can generate real urgency without crushing someone.”

Bram frowned. “You want to build a wall that’s about to fall?”

“I want to build a brace that will fail unless held,” Tiffani said. “A brace that can be allowed to fail without killing anyone if control is lost. The difference matters.”

Jhace’s hands flexed. The tremor ran.

“And Kylar,” Bram said, looking toward the ladder shaft.

As if the name summoned him, Kylar stepped into the workshop from the corridor. He looked tired. Dust ground into the seams of his vest. His hands stayed close to his body. When he held still, a faint tremor showed in his fingers.

He stopped when he saw the slates on the bench.

“You’re measuring him,” Kylar said. It wasn’t accusation. It was a statement.

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

Kylar’s gaze moved to Jhace’s hands, then away. “I want that.”

Jhace’s mouth tightened. “You want a tremor.”

“I already have one,” Kylar said. He held his hands out. The shake was small, but it was there.

Tiffani’s pen paused. She looked at Kylar’s hands without softening. “It began after the Piercing.”

“Yes,” Kylar said. “I touched a wall that was sliding. Heat for one breath. Copper shimmer. Then nothing. The tremor stayed.”

Jhace watched Tiffani mark a line on her slate.

Kylar’s voice came quieter. “I keep waiting for another crisis. I keep thinking I’ll fail when it comes.”

Jhace looked at him. “You will if you chase the glow.”

Kylar flinched. “Then teach me not to.”

Bram set his tool down. “We teach you where to press. And we teach you to obey the count.”

Kylar’s eyes flicked to Tiffani. “If I can’t let go?”

Tiffani lifted her wrist. The cord loop sat there, plain and practical. “Then someone breaks your contact.”

Kylar swallowed.

Jhace heard his own voice come out before he chose it. “He learns with us. Not alone.”

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on Jhace for one breath, then she nodded once. “Agreed.”

Bram pushed the brace plate aside and cleared space on the bench. “Tell me what you need for your rig.”

Tiffani named items without hesitation. “Timber lengths. A brace hinge. Two wedge stacks. A load weight we can control. Dust trays. Vibration cord.”

Bram raised his eyebrows at “dust trays,” but he did not argue.

Kylar asked, “Where do we build it?”

“Near the south market,” Bram said. “Salee can keep people out of the way. She has room and bodies.”

Tiffani looked at Jhace. “And the RRO.”

Jhace frowned. “What about them.”

Tiffani slid a thin notice slate across the bench. It had been copied clean, ink still sharp.

**REGISTRATION OF ANOMALOUS STABILIZERS**
**All individuals demonstrating copper-gold manifestations in structural emergencies will report to the RRO intake office within three phases.**
**Unauthorized interventions will be recorded.**

Jhace read it once. His fingers twitched against the edge.

“They want names,” he said.

“They want control,” Tiffani replied.

Bram’s voice went flat. “They can come down here and wedge a post if they want control.”

No one laughed.

Jhace set the notice down. “We build the rig.”

Kylar nodded. “When.”

Tiffani closed her logbook. “Next Kindling. Tide Two. We need one calm span to build it. Calm is not guaranteed.”

Jhace looked at his hands. The tremor kept moving. Calm was not guaranteed anywhere now.

“Next Kindling,” he said.

Outside, another faint rattle ran through the corridor. A tool clinked somewhere upslope. Dust drifted in a thin line past the workshop opening.

Jhace counted without meaning to.

The ground did not stop moving.

Neither would they.

---

## Chapter 8 — The Rig

**Tide:** 2 (Kindling) | **POV:** Jhace

The rig sat under a half-collapsed awning in the south market.

Salee’s people had cleared the space with rope lines and blunt voices. No one argued. They routed foot traffic around the perimeter as if it were another broken stair throat. Barrels of water stood nearby, chalk slashes on the side marking distribution counts. Wedges and timber lay stacked on a cloth tarp, sorted by size.

Bram knelt at the base of the rig and checked the hinge alignment with a straightedge. The hinge was scavenged from a door brace, iron pins worn but intact. The timber frame around it was new-cut from salvage beams.

Tiffani stood at the side with cord and chalk, marking the ground where the frame legs met stone. Two Xs at the base. One higher mark on the upright where load would concentrate if the hinge slipped.

Kylar hovered a span away, hands tight at his sides.

Jhace stood closer than he wanted to. His hands shook in front of his chest. The tremor did not stop when he held them behind his back, so he kept them visible.

Tiffani finished her marks and stepped back. “This is the rule. You do not touch until marks exist.”

Kylar nodded. “I understand.”

“You understand the words,” Tiffani said. “We will see if you understand the behavior.”

Kylar’s jaw tightened. “I am here.”

“That is behavior,” Bram said dryly. “Good start.”

Tiffani lifted a dust tray—just a shallow pan with fine sifted grit—and set it near the hinge. “If an aftershock hits, the pattern in the tray changes. The tray tells us if vibration shifts load.”

Bram grunted. “Or the tray tells us the ground shook.”

“It tells us where it shook hardest,” Tiffani said. She did not look up. “That distinction matters.”

Jhace watched her hands. No shake. Clean marks. She moved like the world would obey if she measured it enough.

The world would not. But the marks mattered anyway.

Salee stepped in from the rope line and looked at the rig. “If it fails, what happens.”

Bram pointed. “The upright drops. It hits that sand pile. No one stands under it.”

Salee nodded once. “Then we keep no one under it.”

Kylar looked between them. “How does this make the glow answer.”

“It might not,” Jhace said.

Kylar’s eyes narrowed. “Then what is the point.”

“The point is placement and stop,” Tiffani said. “Activation is not the skill. Activation happens when it happens. Placement and stop determine whether you live long enough to activate again.”

Kylar swallowed. He stepped closer to the upright and stared at the timber grain where Tiffani’s chalk Xs sat.

“Try,” Tiffani said.

Kylar set his palms on the primary mark.

Nothing happened.

He stood there for ten heartbeats, then twenty. His hands began to sweat. The tremor in his fingers increased.

His breathing shortened.

Jhace saw his shoulders tighten. The urge to force contact. To press harder. To scrape.

“Kylar,” Jhace said.

Kylar did not answer. He pressed harder. His palms slid slightly on the chalked timber.

Tiffani’s voice cut in. “Do not chase. Do not grind.”

Kylar’s hands jerked. His right palm slid again. A thin splinter caught under his skin. He hissed.

“Stop,” Tiffani said.

Kylar did not stop.

He shifted his weight forward and shoved.

The upright jumped a fraction on its hinge. The brace pin clacked. The timber frame creaked.

Bram swore once and lunged, catching the upright with his forearm before it swung farther.

Salee’s nearest runner stepped in fast and grabbed the frame leg to steady it.

A child on the rope line had leaned in to watch. Salee’s hand snapped out and yanked the child back by the shoulder without looking away from the rig.

Kylar froze as if he had just realized what he had done.

Jhace’s chest tightened.

That was not a collapse. But it could have broken someone’s arm. It could have crushed a foot. It could have killed a child if the upright had swung wrong and someone had been under it.

“Hands off,” Bram said, voice hard.

Kylar stepped back two paces. His hands shook. His face had gone pale.

“I—” he started.

Tiffani looked at the splinter in his palm. “You injured yourself while trying to force an answer.”

Kylar’s voice came thin. “It didn’t answer.”

“No,” Tiffani said. “And you made the rig unsafe.”

Kylar’s eyes flicked to the child Salee had pulled back. Shame tightened his mouth.

Jhace heard his own voice come rough. “Do you understand now.”

Kylar looked at him. “I understand that I’m useless.”

“That is not what happened,” Tiffani said.

Kylar laughed once, sharp and wrong. “What happened then.”

Jhace stepped forward. The tremor in his hands made the motion look like anger. He kept his voice flat.

“You tried to make it answer,” Jhace said. “You tried to force the world to do what you wanted. You almost dropped timber on a child.”

Kylar flinched. “I didn’t mean—”

“Intent doesn’t change load,” Jhace said.

Kylar’s mouth opened, then closed. His breath shook.

Bram set the upright back against its seat and checked the hinge pin. “Rig’s still intact. But no one touches it unless Tiffani says.”

Tiffani’s gaze stayed on Kylar. “This is the chapter where you learn restraint.”

Kylar’s voice cracked. “I don’t know how.”

Jhace looked at Tiffani. He felt the urge in his own bones: to show Kylar what power looked like so he would stop chasing it blindly. To make him afraid enough to listen.

Tiffani read his posture before he moved.

“No,” she said, to Jhace.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “He needs to see.”

“He needs to obey,” Tiffani said. “If you demonstrate glow, he will chase glow. That is not what we teach.”

Kylar looked between them, eyes wide. He was breathing fast.

Bram said quietly, “Don’t turn this into a contest.”

Jhace’s hands shook harder. The tremor always looked like agitation now, even when he felt nothing but fatigue.

“He will die,” Jhace said.

Tiffani’s voice stayed level. “He will die faster if he thinks glow is the point.”

Jhace stared at her. He did not like being stopped. He needed being stopped.

Salee spoke from the rope line. “If you want him alive, teach him to listen.”

Jhace turned his head. Salee’s gaze stayed steady. No awe. No fear. Just systems thinking in a human body.

Jhace exhaled once through his nose.

“Fine,” he said.

Tiffani nodded once. “Good.”

She stepped to the rig and rechecked her marks. Then she looked at Bram.

“Pre-load,” she said.

Bram moved to the weight sling—a sandbag bundle suspended by cord through a pulley. He raised it a hand-span and set a catch pin so the load pulled against the upright. The timber frame complained in a low creak.

Tiffani watched the dust tray. Fine lines formed in the grit, radiating from one corner toward the hinge.

“Load is concentrated,” she said. “Primary node is the lower hinge seam. Secondary is the upright midline. If the hinge slips, the upright will swing.”

Kylar swallowed. “So if it swings—”

“It hits the sand pile,” Bram said. “But it can take an arm if someone’s in the path.”

Salee said, “No one will be in the path.”

Tiffani’s eyes went to Kylar. “You will not be in the path.”

Kylar nodded, small and fast.

Tiffani looked at Jhace. “This is not a demonstration of power. This is a demonstration of obedience. I will call the count. You will break when I say.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. “Yes.”

She looped the cord around her wrist twice. The other end she held in her palm. A simple leash. A contact-break tool.

Jhace stepped to the rig.

He placed his palms on the two marked points—lower hinge seam and upright midline. Cold timber. Rough grain. Load pulling against the hinge.

He did not want to activate. He wanted the rig to hold on wedges and pins and Bram’s clean joints.

The rig did not care what he wanted.

He heard the child on the rope line whisper something. Salee answered with a low hush. The child stayed quiet.

Protective urgency rose in Jhace’s chest anyway. Not pride. Not performance. Just the knowledge that if he failed to hold when it mattered, the world took someone and did not return them.

Hold, he thought. Long enough for them to reset. Long enough for Kylar to see what “stop” costs.

Heat built under his palms.

Copper-gold light seeped out from his skin into the timber grain. Narrow threads. Not the broad flood from the day the world opened. It followed the hinge seam line first, then ran up toward the midline node. It stayed tight to stress, as if it could see what Tiffani had marked.

Vein-lines lit faint under his forearms. The tremor in his hands jumped from fine to hard.

Tiffani’s fingers pressed to his wrist. “Heartbeat elevated,” she said. “Tremor escalating.”

The upright stopped complaining. The hinge creak eased into a steady pressure noise.

Bram adjusted the catch pin. The load pulled harder for a breath, then steadied. The rig stayed in place.

Kylar stared at Jhace’s hands. His own tremor ran in his fingers. Jhace could see Kylar trying not to reach for the glow, trying not to step closer, trying not to do anything except watch.

“Four,” Tiffani counted. “Five.”

Jhace’s breath shortened. Pain climbed his wrists in a hot band. The glow stayed narrow, locked to the nodes.

A faint rattle ran through the market. Tools on the tarp clicked. Dust shifted in the tray, lines changing direction by a small degree.

Tiffani’s eyes snapped to the tray. “Minor aftershock,” she said. “Marks drifted a hand-width.”

Jhace felt it as a shift in resistance under his left palm. The hinge seam pushed harder.

The instinct to chase surged up his arms. Move your hand. Find the new failure point. Press more. Hold longer.

Tiffani’s voice cut it. “Do not chase.”

Jhace held where the mark had been. The glow tightened further, as if compressing to match the shift without moving his hands.

“Six,” Tiffani said. “Seven.”

Jhace’s tremor spiked. His fingers wanted to lock.

“Break,” Tiffani said.

Jhace did not argue. He tore his palms away from the timber.

The copper-gold cut out instantly.

The rig creaked and shifted a fraction, then Bram dropped the catch pin fully and let the load settle back into its safe position. The upright stayed upright.

Jhace’s hands shook hard. Pain flooded in behind the tremor. He breathed through his teeth.

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on his forearms. “Vein-lines fading under twenty heartbeats. Tremor elevated, not convulsive.”

Jhace swallowed. “Managed,” he said, the word bitter in his mouth.

“Managed,” Tiffani confirmed.

Kylar’s voice came low. “You stopped.”

“Yes,” Jhace said.

“Because she told you,” Kylar said.

Jhace looked at Tiffani. “Yes.”

Kylar’s face tightened. He looked at the rig, then his hands.

“I don’t get the glow,” Kylar said. “But I can do the rest.”

Tiffani stepped to him and held out the chalk. “Then do the rest.”

Kylar took it with shaking fingers.

Tiffani pointed at the dust tray. “Aftershock drifted the concentration. Redraw the primary mark based on the tray line.”

Kylar crouched. His tremor made the chalk line stutter at first. He slowed. He used wrist pressure instead of finger pinch the way Jhace had with the knot. The second line came cleaner.

“Secondary?” Tiffani asked.

Kylar looked at the upright midline. He pressed two fingers to the timber and paused, as if listening through skin.

“Here,” he said, and marked it.

Bram watched with a craftsman’s expression. “Good enough.”

Kylar looked up. “I didn’t feel heat.”

“No,” Tiffani said. “You felt load.”

Kylar blinked. “That counts?”

“It counts more than glow without control,” Tiffani said.

Jhace’s hands shook. He clenched them until his nails cut skin.

Kylar looked at Jhace. “I hate that this feels like progress.”

Jhace heard his own voice come flat. “Progress feels like restraint now.”

A faint rattle ran through the market. Someone on the rope line muttered and kept moving. The city did not stop failing. It only paused between shifts.

Tiffani closed her logbook with a sharp sound. “We run this again next Pouring,” she said. “Same rig. Same drift checks. Then we take it to the field.”

Jhace nodded once.

Kylar
The file is too long and its contents have been truncated.

def extract_ch(n):
    # split on chapter headers
    pattern = rf"## Chapter {n}\b.*?(?=\n## Chapter {n+1}\b|\n## Epilogue\b|\Z)"
    m = re.search(pattern, text, flags=re.S)
    return m.group(0) if m else None
ch7 = extract_ch(7)
ch8 = extract_ch(8)
ch9 = extract_ch(9)

def find_activation_markers(ch):
    markers = []
    for phrase in ["Heat built under his palms", "Heat surged under his palms", "Copper-gold light", "The copper-gold cut out", "vein-lines"]:
        if phrase.lower() in ch.lower():
            markers.append(phrase)
    return markers

for n in [7,8,9,12]:
    ch = extract_ch(n)
    print("CH",n, find_activation_markers(ch)[:10])
